recent_2_synopsis: Using observational shadowing and nursing focus groups, the study found that guideline-compliant needleless-connector disinfection occurred in only 77% of access events, with mean scrub times of 5.5 seconds versus the recommended 15 seconds. The human factors analysis attributed non-compliance primarily to workload, time pressure, environmental design, and false assurance conferred by alcohol-impregnated caps.
recent_3: Dorsan, E., Drews, F. A., et al. (2025). A qualitative study of health care professionals' attitudes and practices of mobile equipment cleaning. American Journal of Infection Control. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ajic.2025.xx (in press/online first).
recent_3_synopsis: Fifteen focus groups conducted at three skilled nursing facilities examined why staff often skip or inadequately perform cleaning of shared mobile patient equipment. Themes identified systemic barriers — unclear ownership, competing workload demands, and insufficient training — that a purely procedural compliance approach fails to address.
seminal_1: Strayer, D. L., Drews, F. A., & Crouch, D. J. (2006). A comparison of the cell phone driver and the drunk driver. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 48(2), 381–391. https://doi.org/10.1518/001872006777724471
seminal_1_synopsis: Using a high-fidelity driving simulator, this study demonstrated that hands-free cell phone conversations impaired driving performance to a degree statistically indistinguishable from driving at the 0.08% blood-alcohol legal limit. The striking equivalence to drunk driving became one of the most widely cited findings in distracted-driving science and strongly influenced policy debates around hands-free legislation; it has accumulated nearly 1,000 citations.
seminal_2: Strayer, D. L., Drews, F. A., & Johnston, W. A. (2003). Cell phone-induced failures of visual attention during simulated driving. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Applied, 9(1), 23–52.
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper provided the mechanistic explanation for cell-phone distraction by demonstrating inattention blindness: drivers fixated roadway objects yet failed to encode them in memory during hands-free phone conversations, implicating a centrally limited attentional resource rather than manual phone manipulation. With over 1,500 citations, it remains the foundational cognitive science account of why both handheld and hands-free phones impair driving.
expertise_overview: Applied cognitive psychology, human factors, cognitive ergonomics across driving safety, healthcare patient safety, attention and workload. Hands-free cell-phone conversations impair driving to a degree indistinguishable from the 0.08% blood-alcohol legal limit, via inattention blindness in which drivers fixate roadway objects yet fail to encode them, implicating a centrally limited attentional resource rather than manual manipulation. ICU workflow, task interruptions, medical device usability, infection-prevention compliance; low needleless-connector disinfection and inadequate cleaning of shared mobile equipment attributed to workload, time pressure, environmental design, unclear ownership. Occupational ergonomics in high-risk industries: haul-truck operator fatigue, subcutaneous injection-device force requirements via benchtop testbeds. High-fidelity simulation combined with observational field methods including shadowing and focus groups.
notes: Identification is unambiguous: Frank A. Drews is a named Professor of Psychology at the University of Utah (Google Scholar user ID qCR0Nh0AAAAJ, h-index 52, >13,000 citations) with decades of publication in Human Factors journal specifically, and he serves on the journal's Editorial Board. The third recent citation (Dorsan et al., 2025, AJIC) lists Drews as a co-author per Google Scholar and the AJIC record; full author order and page numbers were not retrievable due to paywall but the article existence is confirmed. The Dorsan et al. DOI suffix is provisional (online first); caller should verify final volume/page data. The two seminal papers are verified via SAGE (Human Factors) and confirmed citation counts from Google Scholar.

name: Feng, Jing
recent_1: Bai, X., & Feng, J. (2025). Awakening the disengaged: Can driving-related prompts engage drivers in partial automation? Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208251314248
recent_1_synopsis: This study tested whether conversational prompts (audio cues + text questions about driving conditions) could re-engage drivers who had become disengaged during Level 2 partially automated driving. Driving-related prompts significantly improved take-over readiness under low cognitive load, but were ineffective when drivers were engaged in high-demand secondary tasks, highlighting a fundamental limitation of prompt-based engagement strategies.
recent_2: Cui, L., Bai, X., & Feng, J. (2025). The interplay between driver dynamic trust and their self-confidence in monitoring automation. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting.
recent_2_synopsis: Examined how drivers' moment-to-moment trust in automated driving systems interacts with their self-confidence in their ability to monitor automation. The work extends driver-automation interaction theory by jointly modeling trust calibration and metacognitive confidence as dual determinants of supervisory attention behavior.
recent_3: Shoffner, L. D., & Feng, J. (2024). Simultaneous remote monitoring of multiple automated vehicles. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting. https://doi.org/10.1177/10711813241269256
recent_3_synopsis: Investigated how many remotely operated autonomous vehicles a single human operator can effectively supervise, finding that monitoring performance remained adequate for two vehicles but degraded substantially with four. The findings inform staffing and interface design requirements for remote driving centers, which are now mandated in several jurisdictions.
seminal_1: Feng, J., Spence, I., & Pratt, J. (2007). Playing an action video game reduces gender differences in spatial cognition. Psychological Science, 18(10), 850–855. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2007.01990.x
seminal_1_synopsis: Demonstrated that just 10 hours of action video game training virtually eliminated the gender gap in spatial attention and substantially reduced the disparity in mental rotation ability, with women benefiting more than men. This highly cited paper (over 1,100 citations) is foundational to debates about the trainability of spatial cognition and the attentional mechanisms underlying gender differences, directly informing human factors research on training, skill acquisition, and individual differences.
seminal_2: Spence, I., & Feng, J. (2010). Video games and spatial cognition. Review of General Psychology, 14(2), 92–104. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0019491
seminal_2_synopsis: Provided the first systematic review of mechanisms by which action video game play modifies spatial cognitive processes, synthesizing evidence on attention, mental rotation, and transfer of training. The paper established a theoretical framework — linking basic attentional capacity to higher-level spatial skills — that is widely referenced in human factors literature on training design, cognitive engineering, and applied cognitive psychology.
expertise_overview: Spatial, selective, and sustained attention, individual differences, age-related decline, and brain plasticity, applied to driving and transportation safety, driver distraction, and driver-automation interaction. Hazard detection in older drivers, attentional failures as crash-risk predictors, visual behavior under in-vehicle display demands. Conversational prompts improve partial-automation take-over readiness only under low cognitive load; trust and metacognitive self-confidence jointly govern supervisory attention; limits on how many remotely operated vehicles one operator can supervise; action video game training reduces gender gaps in spatial attention and mental rotation. Attention and workload, aging, automation and autonomy, displays and controls, human-vehicle interaction, external pedestrian communication signals, training and skill acquisition.
notes: Identification is confident: Jing Feng (Ph.D. University of Toronto, 2011) is a Professor in the Human Factors and Applied Cognition Program, Department of Psychology, NC State University. Her CV (July 2021) confirms membership on the Human Factors editorial board from 2021, consistent with the AE role code provided (she may have moved from EB to AE after 2021). The 2007 Psychological Science paper citation count (~1,100+) is from Semantic Scholar; exact current count was not directly confirmed from a live Google Scholar page. The Shoffner & Feng (2024) and Cui et al. (2025) papers appear in HFES Annual Meeting Proceedings rather than a standalone journal; the Bai & Feng (2025) paper is published in Human Factors journal proper. The Feng et al. (2026) proactive remote operation chapter was listed on her ISE profile but publication details (venue/DOI) were not fully confirmed and was therefore excluded from the recent list.

name: Funke, Gregory J.
recent_1: Driskell, T., Funke, G., Tolston, M., Capiola, A., & Driskell, J. E. (2024). Fluid teams. Military Psychology, 37(6), 544–557. https://doi.org/10.1080/08995605.2024.2413726
recent_1_synopsis: This article provides a framework for understanding fluid teams — temporary cross-domain assemblies that must perform immediately without prior familiarity — identifying their four defining characteristics (rapid assembly, cross-domain expertise, immediate time-critical work, post-task disbanding). It proposes evidence-based performance support strategies such as role clarity, leader support, and pre-performance briefings, with direct application to military Joint All-Domain Operations and healthcare contexts.
recent_2: Duan, W., Zhou, S., Scalia, M. J., Freeman, G., Gorman, J., Tolston, M., McNeese, N. J., & Funke, G. (2025). Understanding the processes of trust and distrust contagion in human–AI teams: A qualitative approach. Computers in Human Behavior, 165, Article 108560.
recent_2_synopsis: Drawing on interviews with 36 participants in three-member human–AI teams, this study empirically maps the social and cognitive mechanisms by which trust or distrust spreads within human-AI teams, finding that a trustworthy spreader can catalyze trust contagion. It provides one of the first empirical accounts of specific pathways of (dis)trust transmission in human-autonomy teaming contexts.
recent_3: Driskell, T., Funke, G., Tolston, M., Capiola, A., & Driskell, J. E. (2024). Composition considerations for fluid teams: a review. Frontiers in Psychology, 15, Article 1302022. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2024.1302022
recent_3_synopsis: This systematic review synthesizes team composition literature — surface-level and deep-level attributes — specifically applied to fluid teams, concluding that surface-level task-related diversity (particularly functional background) provides greater performance benefits in this context than for traditional teams. It offers practical selection principles for organizations that rely on rapidly assembled expert groups.
seminal_1: Funke, G. J., Knott, B. A., Salas, E., Pavlas, D., & Strang, A. J. (2012). Conceptualization and measurement of team workload: a critical need. Human Factors, 54(1), 36–51. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720811427901
seminal_1_synopsis: This article provided the first systematic theoretical framework for team workload as a construct distinct from individual workload, reviewing subjective, performance, physiological, and strategy-shift measurement approaches and showing that simply aggregating individual workload measures fails to capture team-level demands. With over 130 citations, it anchored a new measurement agenda and directly stimulated the development of team-specific tools such as the Team Workload Questionnaire (TWLQ).
seminal_2: Strang, A. J., Funke, G. J., Russell, S. M., Dukes, A. W., & Middendorf, M. S. (2014). Physio-behavioral coupling in a cooperative team task: Contributors and relations. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, 40(1), 145–158. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0033125
seminal_2_synopsis: This study documented and characterized physio-behavioral coupling (PBC) — the spontaneous synchronization of physiological and behavioral responses between teammates — in dyadic cooperative task performance, comparing cross-correlation, cross-recurrence quantification analysis, and cross-fuzzy entropy as measurement approaches. With over 140 citations, it established PBC as a viable real-time index of teamwork quality and opened a neuroergonomics research program linking neurophysiological synchrony to team trust, cohesion, and performance.
expertise_overview: Teams and macrocognition: team workload theory and measurement, fluid and virtual teams, team composition, adaptive aiding. First systematic theoretical framework distinguishing team workload from individual workload, reviewing subjective, performance, physiological, and strategy-shift measures and the Team Workload Questionnaire. Human-autonomy teaming and trust, mapping trust calibration, trust contagion, and cooperation in human-AI teams and tracing empirical pathways of (dis)trust transmission. Neuroergonomics via physio-behavioral coupling, EEG synchrony, and cerebral hemodynamics as real-time indices of team state, cohesion, and performance. Performance-support strategies for rapidly assembled cross-domain fluid teams; vigilance; human factors of cyber operations, including interface design for cyber defenders and social engineering in cyber-mediated environments.
notes: Identification is confident: Gregory J. Funke is a well-documented researcher at AFRL with a consistent publication record in Human Factors, Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, Frontiers in Psychology, Military Psychology, and HFES Proceedings — all squarely within the Human Factors journal's scope. Google Scholar (user=MTlMxosAAAAJ) confirms his identity and affiliation. The 2012 Human Factors paper (DOI confirmed via PubMed PMID 22409101) is his most cited work in the journal itself, making his Editorial Board role natural. The DOI 10.1037/a0033125 for the Strang et al. (2014) JEP:HPP paper is inferred from PubMed record 23750969 and standard APA formatting; the citation details are reliable but the DOI should be verified if needed. For the Duan et al. (2025) Computers in Human Behavior paper, the article number 108560 and volume 165 are sourced from the Arizona State University PURE repository entry and the ScienceDirect listing. The 2026 Applied Ergonomics paper on "spread of trust and distrust" listed in Google Scholar sortby=pubdate could not be fully confirmed via direct fetch due to paywalls; it is omitted from the three recent citations in favor of fully confirmed papers.

name: Gallagher, Sean
recent_1: Rungere, D.J., Dondi, A.C., Bellacov, R., Omoifo-Irefo, M., Taheri, A., Ahire, A., ... Gallagher, S., Jorgensen, M.J., & Davis, K.G. (2026). A comprehensive evaluation of job rotation: Biomechanical risk, body discomfort, and psychosocial demands. Human Factors. PMID: 41919738.
recent_1_synopsis: This study evaluated the effectiveness of specific job rotation schemes in reducing musculoskeletal disorder risk, integrating measures of biomechanical exposure, body discomfort, and psychosocial demands into a comprehensive assessment framework. It provides evidence-based guidance on the conditions under which job rotation is genuinely protective versus merely redistributing risk.
recent_2: Nail-Ulloa, I., Zabala, M., Pool, N., Sesek, R., Thiese, M., Schall, M.C., Jr., & Gallagher, S. (2025). A fatigue failure framework for the assessment of highly variable low back loading using inertial motion capture — a case study. Ergonomics, 69(2), 292–308. PMID: 39955671.
recent_2_synopsis: This paper applied a fatigue failure-based framework to estimate cumulative lumbar loading under variable occupational conditions using inertial motion capture and inverse dynamics, with Rainflow cycle counting and Palmgren-Miner damage accumulation tested on eight automotive workers across 108 industrial trials. It demonstrates the practical feasibility of translating laboratory-based biomechanical risk models into real-world field settings.
recent_3: Capanoglu, M.F., Barim, M.S., Sesek, R.F., Sesek, R.M., Schall, M.C., & Gallagher, S. (2023). Exploring the addition of torso flexion to the LiFFT analysis tool. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting, 67. DOI: 10.1177/21695067231199687.
recent_3_synopsis: This work extended the Lifting Fatigue Failure Tool (LiFFT) by incorporating trunk flexion angle into the cumulative load calculation, addressing a known limitation of the original tool that omitted the contribution of the lifter's own body mass to spinal loading. The modification preserves the simplicity of the original tool while improving its biomechanical completeness.
seminal_1: Gallagher, S., & Heberger, J.R. (2013). Examining the interaction of force and repetition on musculoskeletal disorder risk: A systematic literature review. Human Factors, 55(1), 108–124. DOI: 10.1177/0018720812449648.
seminal_1_synopsis: This systematic review of epidemiological studies demonstrated a consistent multiplicative interaction between force and repetition across a wide range of musculoskeletal disorders, providing the key empirical evidence that MSD etiology follows a fatigue failure process. It is among the most cited papers in Human Factors (384+ citations per Google Scholar) and received the IEA/Liberty Mutual Medal in Occupational Safety and Ergonomics for 2013, fundamentally reshaping how MSD risk assessment tools are designed.
seminal_2: Gallagher, S., Sesek, R.F., Schall, M.C., Jr., & Huangfu, R. (2017). Development and validation of an easy-to-use risk assessment tool for cumulative low back loading: The Lifting Fatigue Failure Tool (LiFFT). Applied Ergonomics, 63, 142–150. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2017.04.016.
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper introduced LiFFT, a practitioner-accessible tool that estimates daily cumulative lumbar damage dose using only three inputs (load weight, horizontal load distance, daily repetitions), grounded in material fatigue failure theory and validated against two epidemiological databases explaining 72–95% of deviance in low-back disorder rates. LiFFT has been widely adopted and extended (to exoskeletons, upper extremity, shoulder) and became the foundation for an entire family of fatigue failure-based ergonomic assessment tools.
expertise_overview: Occupational ergonomics, work-related musculoskeletal disorder etiology, biomechanics, and physical ergonomics. Developed and validated fatigue failure theory as a mechanistic explanation of musculoskeletal disorder causation: repetitive loading damages tissue through the same cumulative fatigue processes that cause structural failure in engineering materials, evidenced by a multiplicative force-by-repetition interaction across disorders. Low-back loading, shoulder and upper-extremity risk, and quantitative risk-assessment tools including LiFFT, which estimates daily cumulative lumbar damage dose from load weight, horizontal distance, and repetitions, and DUET. Extends tools with trunk flexion angle; validates field-deployable cumulative-load estimation via inertial motion capture, inverse dynamics, Rainflow counting, and Palmgren-Miner damage accumulation. Job rotation effectiveness integrating biomechanical exposure, discomfort, and psychosocial demands.
notes: Identification is unambiguous: Sean Gallagher at Auburn University is a Fellow of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society (HFES), has published in Human Factors journal itself (Gallagher & Heberger, 2013; Rungere et al., 2026), and is indexed on Google Scholar (user=ChXgzNAAAAAJ) with 4,400+ citations and an h-index of 34. The 2026 Human Factors paper (PMID 41919738) confirms continued engagement with the journal. The third recent citation (Capanoglu et al., 2023) appeared in the Proceedings of the HFES Annual Meeting (SAGE), not a standalone journal article — this is noted for transparency. Citation counts for seminal works are drawn from Google Scholar as of mid-2025 and may have grown since.

name: Gutzwiller, Robert S.
recent_1: Lee, J. R., & Gutzwiller, R. S. (2025). Do the eyes have it? A review of using eye tracking for automation trust measurement. Human Factors, 67(12), 1290–1311. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208251348395
recent_1_synopsis: This literature review systematically examines whether eye-tracking metrics validly represent trust in automation, critically evaluating the widespread assumption that reduced visual attention signals increased trust. The review identifies boundary conditions and gaps, providing guidance for researchers on when and how eye tracking can be used as an objective trust measure.
recent_2: Johnson, C. J., Gutzwiller, R. S., Holder, E., Amazeen, P. G., & Cooke, N. J. (2025). Exploring the relationship between team workload and communication in action teams. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making. https://doi.org/10.1177/15553434251328835
recent_2_synopsis: Using a simulated wildland firefighting command-and-control task, this study manipulated task demand and team interdependence across 40 three-person teams to identify communication signatures of team workload. High demand conditions produced longer utterances, slower speech rates, more time spent communicating, and greater centralization in high-interdependence scenarios, informing communication-based measures of team workload.
recent_3: Gutzwiller, R. S., Rheem, H., Ferguson-Walter, K. J., Lewis, C. M., Johnson, C. K., & Major, M. (2024). Exploratory analysis of decision-making biases of professional red teamers in a cyber-attack dataset. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making, 18(1), 37–51. https://doi.org/10.1177/15553434231217787
recent_3_synopsis: This paper applies dual-process theory (thinking fast and slow) to an empirical dataset of professional red teamers conducting cyber attacks, finding exploratory evidence of systematic decision-making biases such as anchoring and sunk-cost effects. The work extends behavioral cybersecurity research by characterizing attacker cognition using established human factors frameworks.
seminal_1: Wickens, C. D., McCarley, J. S., & Gutzwiller, R. S. (2022). Applied Attention Theory (2nd ed.). Taylor & Francis / Routledge. ISBN 9780367533557.
seminal_1_synopsis: This graduate-level text synthesizes decades of attention research — including multiple-resource theory, salience-effort-expectancy-value (SEEV) models, and workload — and bridges laboratory findings to real-world system design (warnings, driving, aviation, VR/AR). It is a canonical reference in applied cognitive psychology and human factors education, with nearly 1,000 citations on Google Scholar, and Gutzwiller's co-authorship of this second edition marks a career-defining contribution to the theoretical foundation of the field.
seminal_2: Wickens, C. D., Gutzwiller, R. S., & Santamaria, A. (2015). Discrete task switching in overload: A meta-analysis and a model. International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 79, 79–84. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhcs.2015.01.002
seminal_2_synopsis: Integrating 31 experiments on applied task switching, this meta-analysis quantified consistent human tendencies under overload — specifically the bias to avoid switching and to preferentially switch toward less difficult, more salient, and higher-priority tasks — and organized these into a predictive model. With approximately 180 citations, it is a foundational reference for multi-task management, workload scheduling, and supervisory control interface design.
expertise_overview: Attention, mental workload, and multi-task management; meta-analytic task-switching overload model showing systematic biases to avoid switching and to favor easier, more salient, higher-priority tasks for supervisory control and workload scheduling. Trust in automation and human-machine teaming, including psychometric critique of widely used trust scales, eye-tracking-based trust measurement and its boundary conditions, and interactive machine learning. Cybersecurity via situation awareness for cyber defense and oppositional human factors, characterizing red-team attacker cognition and decision biases such as anchoring and sunk-cost effects via dual-process theory. Team workload and communication in action teams, identifying communication signatures of demand and interdependence in simulated command-and-control. Multiple-resource theory, SEEV models, and workload, bridging laboratory findings to warnings, driving, aviation, and VR/AR system design.
notes: Identity is confirmed with high confidence. Gutzwiller is a listed editorial board member for Human Factors (HFES) and his research profile is well-documented across ASU's institutional repository, Google Scholar (h-index 27, ~2,900 citations), and SAGE journal pages. The two seminal works selected are the highest-impact outputs: the Applied Attention Theory textbook (2nd ed., ~956 Google Scholar citations) and the task-switching meta-analysis (~180 citations). Note that the textbook citation count on Google Scholar aggregates both editions; Gutzwiller joined as co-author on the 2nd edition (2022). The three recent publications are confirmed from SAGE journal DOI pages and the ASU Pure institutional repository. The 2025 Human Factors paper (Lee & Gutzwiller) was published online June 2025 (confirmed PubMed record PMID 40498518). The 2024 JCEDM paper DOI is confirmed from SAGE. The 2025 JCEDM team workload paper is confirmed from SAGE (DOI 10.1177/15553434251328835). A 2026 Human Factors review on team workload (Johnson, Gutzwiller, Cohen, Cooke, Human Factors 68(6)) was identified in the ASU Pure repository but could not be independently confirmed via a SAGE DOI page, so it was not included as a recent publication to avoid any risk of inaccuracy.

name: Helton, William S.
recent_1: Esau, A., & Helton, W. S. (2025). Mind-wandering or task-unrelated thought reports may be a response to performance not a cause of performance: Using forced errors to impact thought content reports. Human Factors. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208251410809
recent_1_synopsis: This study experimentally manipulated task performance by inducing forced errors to test whether retrospective self-reports of mind-wandering reflect a cause of poor performance or a post-hoc response to it. Findings challenge the prevailing assumption that task-unrelated thought drives vigilance failures, suggesting instead that performance outcomes shape self-reported thought content.
recent_2: Helton, W. S. (2023). Will the real resource theory please stand up! Vigilance is a renewable resource and should be modeled as such. Experimental Brain Research, 241(5), 1263–1270. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00221-023-06604-x
recent_2_synopsis: This theoretical paper argues that vigilance (sustained attention) should be conceptualized and modeled as a renewable rather than depletable resource, proposing a quantitative model of resource depletion and renewal that generates performance patterns consistent with empirical data from both human and non-human observers. The paper offers a significant reframing of resource-based accounts of the vigilance decrement.
recent_3: Ward, M. D., Smith, S. L., & Helton, W. S. (2023). Dual-task interference while receiving information on a head mounted display, visually on the same depth plane, or audibly. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting. https://doi.org/10.1177/21695067231192613
recent_3_synopsis: This study compared the dual-task costs of receiving secondary word information via a head-mounted display (HMD), on the same visual plane as a tracking task, or audibly, while participants performed a primary manual tracking task. Both HMD and auditory word presentation impaired tracking performance relative to single-task conditions, informing wearable interface design for operational contexts.
seminal_1: Helton, W. S., & Warm, J. S. (2008). Signal salience and the mindlessness theory of vigilance. Acta Psychologica, 129(1), 18–25. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2008.04.002
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper tested whether the vigilance decrement results from 'mindless' withdrawal of attention or from limited attentional resources, showing that both overall detection performance and the rate of decline were modulated by signal salience — a result inconsistent with the mindlessness account and supportive of resource-based theories. With approximately 276 citations (Semantic Scholar), it is a cornerstone empirical contribution to the theoretical debate on why sustained attention fails over time.
seminal_2: Helton, W. S., & Russell, P. N. (2015). Rest is best: The role of rest and task interruptions on vigilance. Cognition, 134, 165–173. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2014.10.001
seminal_2_synopsis: Using an abbreviated visuospatial vigilance task, this paper demonstrated that complete rest breaks were more effective than task-switching interruptions at counteracting the vigilance decrement, establishing 'rest' as the benchmark countermeasure condition. With approximately 108 citations, it has become a standard reference in human factors research on vigilance countermeasures, and was followed by a direct replication and extension (Rest Is Still Best, Human Factors, 2017).
expertise_overview: Sustained attention and vigilance: vigilance decrement, signal detectability, and theoretical accounts of performance degradation over time-on-task. Argues vigilance should be modeled as renewable rather than depletable, showing signal salience modulates both overall detection and rate of decline inconsistent with mindlessness accounts and supportive of resource theories. Complete rest breaks outperform task-switching interruptions as vigilance countermeasures; challenges assumptions that retrospective mind-wandering reports cause poor performance. Cognitive-physical dual-task interference during climbing, kayaking, and running; response inhibition; wearable interface and head-mounted display design, showing head-mounted and auditory secondary information impairs manual tracking. Working dog ergonomics as an extension of human-systems integration. Attention and workload, vigilance theory, displays, applied cognition.
notes: Identification is confident: William S. "Deak" Helton is a well-known, uniquely identifiable human factors researcher at George Mason University; no disambiguation is required. The 2023 Experimental Brain Research paper co-author from Molde University College, Norway, is likely Jorn Wen based on the resource theory framing, but the full author list could not be confirmed from accessible sources — the citation is listed with Helton as sole confirmed named author. The 2025 Human Factors paper (Esau and Helton) was online-first as of the search date; volume/issue/page numbers were not yet indexed. Citation counts for seminal works are approximate figures from Semantic Scholar as of mid-2026. The Engineering Psychology and Human Performance textbook (5th ed., 2022) is a major career contribution but is a book, not a journal article, so it was not included in the seminal works slots; it is noted in the expertise overview instead.

name: Hollands, Justin G.
recent_1: Lam, T. K., Vartanian, O., & Hollands, J. G. (2022). The brain under cognitive workload: Neural networks underlying multitasking performance in the multi-attribute task battery. Neuropsychologia, 174, 108350.
recent_1_synopsis: Using fMRI, the study identified functional connectivity patterns — particularly between default mode and dorsal attention networks — that distinguish multitasking from single-tasking in a simulated aviation environment. The findings advance neuroergonomic understanding of cognitive workload and point toward neurostimulation as a potential intervention for multitasking performance.
recent_2: Vasquez, H. M., Hollands, J. G., Jamieson, G. A., & Agnew, M. J. (2022). A mirror in the sky: The effects of map format and user expertise on navigation performance and mental workload. Ergonomics, 65(4), 604–617.
recent_2_synopsis: This study compared an augmented reality 'Mirror in the Sky' (MitS) display against conventional north-up and track-up maps in a virtual military navigation task, finding that both soldier experts and civilian novices achieved better navigation outcomes with standard map formats than with MitS. The results highlight how display compatibility and operator expertise interact in determining the effectiveness of novel AR navigation interfaces.
recent_3: Reiner, A. J., Vasquez, H. M., Jamieson, G. A., & Hollands, J. G. (2022). Comparing an augmented reality navigation display to an electronic map for military reconnaissance. Ergonomics, 65(1), 78–90.
recent_3_synopsis: Twenty-four soldiers performed a VR reconnaissance scenario comparing the MitS augmented reality head-up display to a conventional electronic map; participants made better reroute decisions and showed higher threat-location recall with the north-up map, though threat detection and mental workload did not differ between conditions. The study contributes empirical evidence on the limitations of egocentric AR overlays relative to established exocentric map formats for tactical navigation.
seminal_1: Wickens, C. D., & Hollands, J. G. (2000). Engineering Psychology and Human Performance (3rd ed.). Prentice Hall. [Subsequent editions: 4th ed. with Banbury & Parasuraman, Pearson, 2013; 5th ed. with Helton & Banbury, Routledge, 2022.]
seminal_1_synopsis: This foundational textbook — now in its fifth edition and cited over 12,000 times on Google Scholar — synthesizes human information processing theory (perception, attention, memory, decision-making, manual control, stress) with practical implications for the design of human-machine systems. It has served as the primary graduate-level reference in engineering psychology and applied human factors for more than two decades and is consistently cited as the field's definitive overview text.
seminal_2: Lipkus, I. M., & Hollands, J. G. (1999). The visual communication of risk. JNCI Monographs, 1999(25), 149–163.
seminal_2_synopsis: This highly cited review (approximately 789 citations) systematically examined the effectiveness of graphical formats — pie charts, bar graphs, histograms, icon arrays, risk ladders — for conveying health-risk information to the public, synthesizing principles from psychophysics, graphical perception, and health communication. It established a framework for evidence-based design of risk graphics that has been widely adopted in medical, public health, and risk communication research.
expertise_overview: Displays and controls: graph perception, 2D and 3D terrain displays, and augmented reality navigation interfaces, demonstrating that conventional north-up and track-up maps can outperform egocentric AR head-up overlays for tactical reroute decisions and threat recall. Automation and trust via appropriate reliance on imperfect automated combat identification and the role of reliability disclosure. Neuroergonomics of multitasking, using fMRI to identify default-mode and dorsal-attention connectivity patterns distinguishing multitasking from single-tasking. Psychophysical models of proportion and risk judgment; reviews of graphical formats, pie charts, bar graphs, icon arrays, and risk ladders, for communicating health risk. Human information processing, perception, attention, memory, decision-making, manual control, and stress, with implications for human-machine system design; statistical methods for within-subjects designs.
notes: Identity is confidently established: Justin G. Hollands (PhD, Psychology, University of Toronto, 1993) is a Defence Scientist at DRDC Toronto, an adjunct professor at University of Toronto, and has served as Associate Editor for Human Factors (confirmed via ResearchGate profile as of October 2017 and his SAGE Publications author page). Google Scholar shows ~15,000 citations total with h-index 24. The three most recent publications listed are peer-reviewed journal articles from 2022 (Neuropsychologia; Ergonomics x2); no confirmed peer-reviewed journal papers from 2023–2026 appeared in systematic search, though a DRDC technical report (DRDC-RDDC-2024-R002, with Nakaza, Apostoli, Bray-Miners) was referenced but its title and full content could not be verified from public sources. The 5th edition of the Engineering Psychology textbook (Wickens, Helton, Hollands, Banbury, Routledge, 2022) also represents a significant recent contribution. The Wickens & Hollands citation figure (12,026 on Google Scholar) aggregates across multiple editions; the 3rd edition (2000) is the most commonly cited standalone entry. The Lipkus & Hollands (1999) citation count of ~789 is from Google Scholar data. The Hollands & Dyre (2000) Psychological Review paper on the cyclical power model (249 citations) and the Wang, Jamieson & Hollands (2009) Human Factors paper on trust and reliance (237 citations) are also highly influential career works, but the two seminal slots were allocated to the textbook and the risk communication review as the highest-impact contributions by citation count and field influence.

name: Horrey, William J.
recent_1: Gaspar, J. G., Tefft, B., Carney, C., & Horrey, W. J. (2025). The impact of self-initiated breaks during drowsy driving. Sleep, 48(9), zsaf150. https://doi.org/10.1093/sleep/zsaf150
recent_1_synopsis: Ninety sleep-restricted drivers completed a 150-mile simulated highway drive; voluntary breaks cut lane departures by ~50% immediately but performance returned to pre-break impairment within ~40 minutes, while caffeine during breaks provided the most sustained benefit. The study highlights that breaks are insufficient to fully counteract drowsiness and that nearly half of drivers chose not to stop at all.
recent_2: Gaspar, J. G., Tefft, B., Carney, C., & Horrey, W. J. (2024). Predicting drowsy driver break taking during long drives. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208241293707
recent_2_synopsis: Using the same 150-mile simulator paradigm with sleep-restricted drivers, this companion paper identified individual and situational factors predicting whether drivers chose to stop at rest areas. The findings inform countermeasure design by clarifying who is least likely to take restorative breaks when most impaired.
recent_3: Biondi, F. N., Horrey, W. J., & Donmez, B. (2024). Preface to the special issue on assessment and effectiveness of driver monitoring systems. Human Factors, 66(9), 2161–2165. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208231206073
recent_3_synopsis: This guest editorial frames the scientific and policy context for a special section of Human Factors dedicated to driver monitoring systems (DMS), noting that automation is shifting drivers from operators to supervisors and that DMS technology is the critical safeguard against disengagement and drowsiness. The preface synthesizes the current state of DMS accuracy research and outlines open questions addressed by the issue's empirical papers.
seminal_1: Horrey, W. J., & Wickens, C. D. (2006). Examining the impact of cell phone conversations on driving using meta-analytic techniques. Human Factors, 48(1), 196–205. https://doi.org/10.1518/001872006776412135
seminal_1_synopsis: This meta-analysis of 23 studies established with high quantitative precision that hands-free cell-phone conversations substantially impair driver reaction time while hands-free or handheld status makes little difference — overturning a common public and policy assumption. With nearly 900 citations, it became a foundational reference in the distracted-driving literature and directly shaped hands-free legislation debates.
seminal_2: Horrey, W. J., Wickens, C. D., & Consalus, K. P. (2006). Modeling drivers' visual attention allocation while interacting with in-vehicle technologies. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Applied, 12(2), 67–78. https://doi.org/10.1037/1076-898X.12.2.67
seminal_2_synopsis: Two simulator experiments showed that the SEEV (Salience, Effort, Expectancy, Value) computational model accounted for approximately 95% of variance in drivers' glance distribution across in-vehicle and road-ahead targets, validating an attention-modeling framework applicable to display and HMI design. Cited more than 560 times, the paper anchored subsequent computational approaches to predicting driver visual behavior and inattention risk.
expertise_overview: Driving and transportation: driver distraction, cognitive workload, attention allocation modeling, drowsiness and fatigue, and human-automation interaction in partially and fully automated vehicles. Validated the SEEV computational model, accounting for roughly 95 percent of variance in drivers' glance distribution across in-vehicle and road-ahead targets, anchoring predictive approaches to driver visual behavior and inattention risk. Meta-analysis establishing that hands-free phone conversation substantially impairs reaction time while hands-free versus handheld status matters little, shaping distracted-driving policy. Drowsiness countermeasures: voluntary breaks transiently reduce lane departures with benefits decaying within minutes while caffeine provides the most sustained effect; identifies who declines restorative breaks. How drivers form mental models of ADAS, how branding and training shape Level 2 expectations, and how driver monitoring systems detect disengagement, spanning calibration and behavioral safety.
notes: Identity is unambiguous: Horrey is explicitly listed as an Associate Editor of Human Factors journal and is a prominent transportation human factors researcher at the AAA Foundation for Traffic Safety. Citation counts sourced from Google Scholar (accessed June 2026). For the third recent paper, the Biondi et al. (2024) preface is an editorial/preface rather than a full empirical article; the next strongest peer-reviewed candidate would be Pai et al. (2023) in Accident Analysis & Prevention (Vol. 190, 107130) on ACC exposure and mental models, or Singer et al. (2022/published 2024 in Human Factors, Vol. 66(5), pp. 1531-1544) on automation branding and training — both have Horrey as a co-author. The Biondi et al. preface is included because it appeared in print in 2024 in the flagship journal Human Factors and reflects his editorial leadership role.

name: Israeliski, Edmond W.
recent_1: Israelski, E.W., & Muto, W.H. (2022). Risk Management: How to Assess and Control Risk Related to Human Error in Products and Systems (Users' Guides to Human Factors and Ergonomics Methods). Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Press, Washington, DC. ISBN: 9780945289036.
recent_1_synopsis: A practical methods guide to applying FMEA, fault tree analysis, and related risk tools specifically to 'use errors' — predictable human errors attributable to inadequate design — across consumer, medical, industrial, and transportation products and systems. As an HFES Press Users' Guide, it fills a gap between general risk standards and HF engineering practice by centering human performance characteristics in risk analyses.
recent_2: Israelski, E.W. (2021). Human Factors Considerations in the User Interface Design of Bridge Ventilator Devices. In A.H. Hakimi, T.E. Milner, G.R. Rajan, & B.J-F. Wong (Eds.), Mechanical Ventilation Amid the COVID-19 Pandemic: A Guide for Physicians and Engineers (Chapter 19). Springer, Cham, Switzerland.
recent_2_synopsis: Describes how human factors engineering — as a user-centered, iterative design process required by FDA and international regulators — should be applied to the user interfaces of bridge/emergency ventilators developed rapidly during the COVID-19 pandemic. The chapter grounds FDA HFE guidance in the specific context of devices designed under crisis conditions for diverse and potentially inexperienced operators.
recent_3: Israelski, E.W., & Muto, W.H. (2012). Human Factors Risk Management in Medical Devices. In P. Carayon (Ed.), Handbook of Human Factors and Ergonomics in Healthcare and Patient Safety (2nd ed.). Lawrence Erlbaum / Taylor & Francis. [Originally published 2006, updated 2nd ed. 2012.]
recent_3_synopsis: An updated reference chapter providing a comprehensive framework for integrating HF risk management — including use-error-focused FMEA and fault tree analysis — into the medical device design and regulatory cycle. Its placement in the field's premier healthcare HF handbook has made it a standard reference for both practitioners and researchers entering medical device HF.
seminal_1: Israelski, E.W., & Muto, W.H. (2004). Human Factors Risk Management as a Way to Improve Medical Device Safety: A Case Study of the Therac-25 Radiation Therapy System. Joint Commission Journal on Quality and Safety, 30(12), 689–695.
seminal_1_synopsis: Demonstrated through the notorious Therac-25 radiation overdose accidents that a systematic use-error risk analysis (FMEA/FTA) could have predicted and prevented catastrophic patient harm — establishing a compelling, widely-cited argument for mandatory HF risk management in medical device design. Cited in patient safety, medical device regulation, and HF engineering literature across multiple disciplines, it helped lay the groundwork for what became AAMI HE75 and IEC 62366 usability engineering standards.
seminal_2: Israelski, E.W., & Muto, W.H. (2003). Use-Error Focused Risk Analysis for Medical Devices: A Case Study of the Therac-25 Radiation Therapy System. Proceedings of the 47th Annual Meeting of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society. [Also reprinted as a chapter in: Gosbee, J.W., & Gosbee, L.L. (Eds.) (2005). Using Human Factors to Improve Patient Safety. Joint Commission Resources, Oakbrook Terrace, IL.]
seminal_2_synopsis: The conference paper that introduced the 'use-error focused' risk analysis methodology to the HFES community, reframing device failures not as operator mistakes but as foreseeable design-induced errors amenable to systematic analysis. Its subsequent reprinting in the Gosbee patient safety handbook extended its reach to clinical quality improvement audiences and cemented Israelski's framing of use error as a design — not a user — problem.
expertise_overview: Healthcare and medical device human factors engineering: use-error risk analysis and designing predictable human performance failures out of medical devices and pharmaceutical combination products. FMEA, fault tree analysis, and related risk tools applied to use errors across consumer, medical, industrial, and transportation systems, demonstrating through the Therac-25 radiation overdose accidents that systematic use-error analysis could have prevented catastrophic patient harm. Introduced the use-error-focused risk analysis methodology, reframing device failures as foreseeable design problems rather than operator mistakes. Medical device usability standards work; handbook chapters on basic human abilities, anthropometry and biomechanics, and testing and evaluation, spanning displays and controls, physical ergonomics, and usability methodology. Pharmaceutical human factors, drug labeling eye-tracking studies, crisis-era bridge ventilator user interface design, telecommunications HCI.
notes: Identity is confidently established: Israelski's own website (israelski.com) provides a full CV, and multiple independent sources (Northwestern University design faculty profile, eMedEvents speaker profile, ResearchGate, HFES) corroborate his role as an editorial board member of Human Factors journal. The 2022 Risk Management book is the most recent peer-reviewed/formally-published work; no journal articles dated 2023–2026 were found, consistent with his retired-consultant status. The 2004 Joint Commission Journal article and its 2003 HFES proceedings antecedent are treated together as seminal because they form a single intellectual contribution; the 2003 paper is also reprinted in the Gosbee patient safety handbook (2005). Citation counts were not numerically retrievable (ResearchGate blocked), but the Therac-25 papers are widely cited in medical device HF, patient safety, and usability engineering literature across multiple venues. The 2006/2012 Carayon handbook chapter is included as a recent publication because the 2012 second edition update is the version most currently in circulation; however, strictly it is a chapter update, not a fully new publication.

name: Jorgensen, Michael J.
recent_1: Rungere, D., Dondi, A., Bellacov, R., Omoifo-Irefo, M., Taheri, M., Ahire, A., et al. (incl. Jorgensen, M.) (2026). A Comprehensive Evaluation of Job Rotation: Biomechanical Risk, Body Discomfort, and Psychosocial Demands. Human Factors.
recent_1_synopsis: This study provides a multi-dimensional assessment of job rotation by simultaneously examining biomechanical loading, perceived bodily discomfort, and psychosocial demands, going beyond prior work that addressed only single dimensions of the intervention's effectiveness.
recent_2: Manning, C., & Jorgensen, M. (2025). Warehousing and Distribution Injuries in Kansas: Costs and Trends from the State Workers' Compensation Database, 2014–2023. Safety Science, 191, 106932.
recent_2_synopsis: An epidemiological analysis of workplace injury patterns and associated workers' compensation costs in Kansas warehousing and distribution, identifying injury trends over a decade to inform occupational health and safety priorities in a high-risk sector.
recent_3: Jorgensen, M., Martinez, A., & Hakansson, N. A. (2024). Comparison of multi-task ergonomic assessment methods for risk of upper extremity and low back musculoskeletal disorders. Applied Ergonomics, 119, 104313.
recent_3_synopsis: Compared four ergonomic assessment tools (RSI, DUET, RNLE, LiFFT) in multi-task manufacturing settings, finding that RSI and DUET correlated strongly (rs = 0.933) but showed only fair categorical agreement, with implications for which tool practitioners select in complex job environments.
seminal_1: Marras, W. S., Jorgensen, M. J., Granata, K. P., & Wiand, B. (2001). Female and male trunk geometry: size and prediction of the spine loading trunk muscles derived from MRI. Clinical Biomechanics, 16(1), 38–46.
seminal_1_synopsis: Using MRI, this study developed gender-specific databases of trunk muscle cross-sectional areas across thoracic and lumbar levels and derived prediction equations from external anthropometric measures. With 367 citations, it became a foundational reference for building sex-differentiated biomechanical spine models used widely in occupational ergonomics research.
seminal_2: Jorgensen, M., Davis, K., Kotowski, S., Aedla, P., & Dunning, K. (2005). Characteristics of job rotation in the Midwest US manufacturing sector. Ergonomics, 48(15), 1721–1733.
seminal_2_synopsis: This large-scale survey documented how job rotation was actually practiced across Midwest manufacturing plants — what drives task selection, how frequently workers rotate, and perceived benefits and limitations. Cited over 267 times, it became the empirical benchmark for evidence-based job rotation design aimed at reducing musculoskeletal disorder risk.
expertise_overview: Occupational biomechanics, ergonomic risk assessment, and musculoskeletal disorder prevention: spine loading mechanics, low back injury prevention, and whole-body and segmental vibration. Developed gender-specific databases of trunk muscle cross-sectional areas across thoracic and lumbar levels using MRI and derived prediction equations from external anthropometric measures, a foundational basis for sex-differentiated biomechanical spine models. Administrative and engineering interventions including job rotation scheduling and passive shoulder exoskeletons in high-risk manufacturing such as aviation and warehousing; documented how job rotation is actually practiced across plants to guide evidence-based design. Multi-dimensional job rotation assessment integrating biomechanical loading, perceived discomfort, and psychosocial demands. Compares ergonomic assessment tools, RSI, DUET, RNLE, and LiFFT, in multi-task settings; analyzes workers' compensation injury epidemiology, spanning physical ergonomics, anthropometry, and occupational health measurement.
notes: Identification is confident: Michael J. Jorgensen is unambiguously a Professor at Wichita State University in Industrial, Systems, and Manufacturing Engineering, with a Google Scholar profile (user=SEhYdZIAAAAJ) confirming his identity and publication record. He has presented at the HFES 2025 Annual Meeting (Aspire 2025) and has a 2026 paper appearing in Human Factors itself, directly corroborating his journal affiliation as Editorial Board member. The 2026 Human Factors paper (Rungere et al.) is listed in his Google Scholar profile but full author list and volume/page details were not yet available at time of research; the citation is presented without volume/page numbers accordingly. The 2025 Safety Science paper citation is confirmed via Google Scholar but DOI was not independently verified.

name: Jurewicz, Katherina A.
recent_1: Dowers, K. H., & Jurewicz, K. A. (2026). Quantifying surface contamination in a primary care clinic using 3D gestural human-computer interaction. Journal of Infection Prevention. https://doi.org/10.1177/17571774251394887
recent_1_synopsis: A between-subjects experimental study demonstrating that patients using 3D gestural HCI spread contamination to significantly fewer surfaces than those using traditional touchscreen interfaces in a simulated primary care clinic. The work quantifies an infection-prevention benefit of contactless gestural interaction while also documenting a tradeoff: the gestural group required significantly longer process times.
recent_2: Kyle, A. R., Rouser, B., Paul, R. C., & Jurewicz, K. A. (2025). Hierarchical Bayesian Modeling for Physiological Data in Small-N Aviation Human Factors Research. Aerospace, 12(11), 1004. https://doi.org/10.3390/aerospace12111004
recent_2_synopsis: Proposes a hierarchical Bayesian modeling framework for analyzing multimodal physiological data (EEG, ECG, respiration) from small pilot samples, addressing the statistical challenges common in aviation human factors studies. The approach enables more robust inference about individual pilot workload and automation responses when traditional frequentist methods are underpowered.
recent_3: Uba, J., Mata, M., & Jurewicz, K. A. (2025). Investigating Automation Level and Automation Reliability in Hybrid Automation Systems. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting, 69(1). https://doi.org/10.1177/10711813251395047
recent_3_synopsis: Examines how varying levels of automation and reliability affect operator performance, mental workload, and situation awareness in hybrid automation systems where humans and automated agents share control. Results inform design guidelines for automation systems in which the reliability and degree of automation are not fixed.
seminal_1: Jurewicz, K. A., Neyens, D. M., Catchpole, K., & Reeves, S. T. (2018). Developing a 3D gestural interface for anesthesia-related human-computer interaction tasks using both experts and novices. Human Factors, 60(7), 992–1007. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720818780544
seminal_1_synopsis: Established a systematic human factors methodology for designing and evaluating 3D gestural interfaces in the high-stakes anesthesia context, comparing performance across expert and novice users. This paper anchors Jurewicz's research program and has become the foundational citation (~21+ citations) in her lab's line of work on contactless gestural HCI for clinical environments.
seminal_2: Neyens, D. M., Bayramzadeh, S., Catchpole, K., Joseph, A., Taaffe, K., Jurewicz, K., Khoshkenar, A., & San, D. (2019). Using a systems approach to evaluate a circulating nurse's work patterns and workflow disruptions. Applied Ergonomics, 78, 293–300. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apergo.2018.07.001
seminal_2_synopsis: Applied a sociotechnical systems lens to naturalistic observation of circulating nurses in the operating room, quantifying how physical OR layout influences work patterns and flow disruptions. With ~45 citations it is the most-cited work in Jurewicz's publication record and has influenced healthcare facility design guidelines linking OR spatial configuration to team workflow and safety.
expertise_overview: Human-computer interaction, healthcare human factors, and human-automation interaction. 3D gestural and embodied HCI: interface design, usability-accuracy tradeoffs, workload effects of prolonged exposure, and infection prevention, showing contactless gestural interaction spreads contamination to fewer surfaces than touchscreens while incurring longer process times, and establishing systematic methodology for gestural interfaces in the high-stakes anesthesia context across expert and novice users. Physiological measurement, EEG, ECG, and respiration, with hierarchical Bayesian methods to quantify pilot mental workload and support adaptive automation from small samples. How levels of automation and reliability affect performance, workload, and situation awareness in hybrid shared-control systems. Sociotechnical and resilience engineering relating operating room layout to nurse workflow, flow disruptions, and team safety.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. Jurewicz is an Assistant Professor at Oklahoma State University (School of Industrial Engineering and Management) with a Google Scholar profile (user=IWJrZdcAAAAJ) and an OSU faculty page. Her role as a Past/Provisional Reviewer (PR) for Human Factors is consistent with her 2018 publication in that journal and her active publication of HFES annual meeting proceedings. Citation counts are from her Google Scholar profile as of mid-2026 (162 total citations, h-index 6). The 2026 Journal of Infection Prevention paper (Dowers & Jurewicz) and the 2026 Aerospace Medicine and Human Performance paper (Rouser, Rock, Paul, & Jurewicz on EEG and spatial disorientation) are the two most recent confirmed peer-reviewed journal articles; the latter could not be fully verified beyond the Scholar listing and was excluded from the recent-3 list in favor of papers with confirmed DOIs. The HFES 2025 proceedings paper on hybrid automation systems (Uba et al.) was included as a peer-reviewed conference paper. The 2019 Applied Ergonomics paper is listed under Neyens et al. because Jurewicz appears as a non-first author, but it is the most-cited work associated with her record.

name: Kim, Ji-Eun
recent_1: Prendez, D., Li, J., Higgins, E. A., & Kim, J.-E. (2026). Toward sustainable interventions for enhancing vigilance: A scoping review. Applied Ergonomics, 131, 104628.
recent_1_synopsis: This scoping review systematically maps the evidence base for interventions targeting occupational vigilance decrement, identifying what works sustainably across high-stakes domains including healthcare, transportation, and air traffic control. It is Ji-Eun Kim's most recent peer-reviewed journal article and directly underpins her NSF CAREER program on mitigating vigilance decrement in clinical environments.
recent_2: Li, J., Huang, S., & Kim, J.-E. (2025). Predicting multitasking performance: An EEG- and eye movement-based dynamic Bayesian network. International Journal of Human–Computer Interaction, 1–11. https://doi.org/10.1080/10447318.2025.2480882
recent_2_synopsis: This paper develops a real-time predictive model of multitasking performance using concurrent EEG and eye-tracking signals fed into a dynamic Bayesian network, enabling prospective detection of performance decrements before errors occur. The work advances neurophysiological human performance modeling for safety-critical settings such as air traffic control and emergency response.
recent_3: Jónsdóttir, A. A., Kang, Z., Sun, T., Mandal, S., & Kim, J.-E. (2023). The effects of language barriers and time constraints on online learning performance: An eye-tracking study. Human Factors, 65(5), 779–791. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208211010949
recent_3_synopsis: Using eye-tracking, this study quantified how English-as-a-second-language status and time pressure jointly degrade self-directed online learning performance, identifying gaze patterns that distinguish EFL from ESL learners under load. It is Kim's highest-profile publication in the flagship Human Factors journal and is among her most-cited recent articles.
seminal_1: Kim, J.-E., Nembhard, D. A., & Kim, J. H. (2016). The effects of group size and task complexity on deadline reactivity. International Journal of Industrial Ergonomics, 56, 106–114.
seminal_1_synopsis: This early empirical study established how teams of different sizes respond to imminent deadlines as a function of task complexity, contributing foundational findings on time-pressure dynamics and group work pacing. With roughly 26 citations it is among Kim's most-cited works and anchored the individual-differences and temporal motivation thread that defines much of her subsequent research program.
seminal_2: Kim, J.-E., Kessler, L., McCauley, Z., Niiyama, I., & Boyle, L. N. (2020). Human factors considerations in designing a personalized wearable dialysis device: An interview study. Applied Ergonomics, 85, 103003.
seminal_2_synopsis: Through structured interviews with patients and caregivers, this paper identified the human factors requirements — usability, personalization, monitoring, and mobility — that must be met for a wearable artificial kidney to succeed clinically. With approximately 25 citations it seeded a sustained healthcare-HF research stream and is frequently co-cited by the nephrology engineering community studying wearable dialysis technology.
expertise_overview: Vigilance and sustained attention; wearable neurophysiological sensors (EEG, electrodermal activity, eye-tracking, heart rate) feeding dynamic Bayesian network models that predict and mitigate vigilance decrement in healthcare, transportation, and air traffic control. Real-time models detecting cognitive workload and multitasking performance decrements from concurrent EEG and eye-tracking signals before errors occur. Time-pressure reactivity, procrastination, and temporal motivation as individual-difference constructs shaping operator and team performance under deadlines, including task complexity and group size effects. Human-centered design of medical devices (wearable artificial kidney/dialysis usability requirements); language status and time pressure in self-directed online learning; interventions for occupational vigilance decrement and personalized, sensor-driven mitigation.
notes: Identification is confident: Ji-Eun Kim (jikim@uw.edu) is an Associate Professor in the Department of Industrial and Systems Engineering at the University of Washington, Seattle, with a PhD from Penn State and an MS in Experimental Psychology from Korea University. She is Faculty Advisor for the UW HFES Chapter and has published in Human Factors, Applied Ergonomics, and the HFES Annual Meeting Proceedings — all squarely within the Human Factors journal reviewer pool. No other Ji-Eun Kim in HF/ergonomics/HCI was found. Publication details were verified against her official HAS Lab website (sites.google.com/uw.edu/has/publications), her personal CV site (sites.google.com/view/jieunkim/cv), PubMed, and SAGE Journals. Citation counts for seminal works are approximate (drawn from Google Scholar as of June 2026) and the 2025 IJHCI paper page range (1–11) reflects an online-first publication without final volume/issue numbers yet assigned.

name: Kirlik, Alexander
recent_1: Lee, J., Rodriguez, S. S., Natarrajan, R., Chen, J., Deep, H., & Kirlik, A. (2021). What's This? A Voice and Touch Multimodal Approach for Ambiguity Resolution in Voice Assistants. Proceedings of the 2021 ACM International Conference on Multimodal Interaction (ICMI '21), Montréal, QC, Canada, pp. 512–520. https://doi.org/10.1145/3462244.3479902
recent_1_synopsis: This paper proposes a multimodal voice-plus-touch interaction technique to resolve the inherent ambiguity in voice assistant queries, allowing users to point at physical objects to disambiguate spoken commands. The approach demonstrates improved task efficiency and user satisfaction compared to voice-only alternatives in an empirical study.
recent_2: Park, J. S., Barber, R., Kirlik, A., & Karahalios, K. (2019). A Slow Algorithm Improves Users' Assessments of the Algorithm's Accuracy. Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction, 3(CSCW), Article 102. https://doi.org/10.1145/3359204
recent_2_synopsis: This study shows that deliberately slowing an algorithm's apparent response time improves users' calibration of their trust in it: participants tracked high-quality algorithms more faithfully and were more skeptical of low-quality ones when responses were delayed. The finding challenges standard UX orthodoxy that speed is always beneficial, with direct implications for human-AI teaming and decision support design.
recent_3: Lin, J. H., Kirlik, A., & Xu, X. (2018). New technologies in human factors and ergonomics research and practice. Applied Ergonomics, 66, 179–181. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apergo.2017.08.012
recent_3_synopsis: This editorial introduction to a special issue maps the landscape of emerging technologies — including VR, wearables, and computational modeling — entering human factors and ergonomics research and practice, and articulates the methodological opportunities and challenges they create for the field.
seminal_1: Kirlik, A. (1993). Modeling strategic behavior in human-automation interaction: Why an 'aid' can (and should) go unused. Human Factors, 35(2), 221–242. https://doi.org/10.1177/001872089303500203
seminal_1_synopsis: Using sensitivity analysis and process modeling, Kirlik demonstrated that operators rationally choose not to use automation aids when the cognitive cost of monitoring and managing the aid exceeds its benefit — a finding that reframed the 'disuse of automation' problem from operator error to system design failure. This paper (281+ citations) became foundational to the human-automation interaction literature and continues to anchor debates on complacency, over-trust, and the design of decision aids.
seminal_2: Kirlik, A., Miller, R. A., & Jagacinski, R. J. (1993). Supervisory control in a dynamic and uncertain environment: A process model of skilled human-environment interaction. IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics, 23(4), 929–952.
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper presented a closed-loop process model of how skilled operators manage multitask supervisory control under environmental uncertainty, integrating control theory with cognitive psychology to characterize expert-novice differences. With 139+ citations it established an ecological, control-theoretic framework for understanding human supervisory behavior that influenced subsequent work on situation awareness, adaptive automation, and operator performance modeling.
expertise_overview: Cognitive engineering and ecological approaches to human factors: human-automation interaction, trust calibration, supervisory control, adaptive behavior, decision support, and cognitive modeling of operator performance in aviation, emergency management, and sociotechnical systems. Brunswikian lens-model approaches to judgment and decision making under uncertainty connecting ecological psychology to human factors methodology. Showed via sensitivity analysis and process modeling that operators rationally disuse automation when monitoring costs exceed benefits, reframing disuse from operator error to system design failure and anchoring complacency/over-trust debates. Closed-loop, control-theoretic process model of multitask supervisory control under uncertainty characterizing expert-novice differences. Human-AI teaming, algorithm aversion and adoption, trust calibration through deliberate response delay, and multimodal voice-plus-touch disambiguation of voice-assistant commands.
notes: Alexander Kirlik died on October 8, 2025, at age 64, so he is a deceased member of the reviewer pool. His most recent confirmed first- or co-authored peer-reviewed publication is from 2021 (ICMI); no 2023–2026 journal articles by Kirlik as an author (rather than as a dissertation advisor) were identified in searches. The 2021 ICMI paper and the 2019 CSCW paper are confirmed via ACM DL and Illinois Experts. The 2018 Applied Ergonomics item is an editorial introduction rather than a full research article but is peer-reviewed and the most recent solo/senior work found. The Google Scholar profile cited for citation counts is user=ZS-2t_AAAAAJ. The 1993 Human Factors paper's DOI is confirmed via PubMed (PMID 8349287). The IEEE SMC 1993 paper volume/page details (vol. 23, pp. 929–952) are confirmed via Semantic Scholar and IEEE Xplore record 247880.

name: Laux, Lila F.
recent_1: Laux, L. F. (2019). Human factors in accident analysis. In R. W. McLay & R. N. Anderson (Eds.), Engineering Standards for Forensic Application (Chapter 24). Elsevier.
recent_1_synopsis: This book chapter provides a practitioner-oriented framework for applying human factors principles to forensic accident analysis, covering cognitive, perceptual, and behavioral science dimensions alongside ergonomic considerations. It serves as a reference for expert-witness and litigation contexts where human error and system design are at issue.
recent_2: Wickens, C. D., Hutchins, S. D., Laux, L., & Sebok, A. (2015). The impact of sleep disruption on complex cognitive tasks: A meta-analysis. Human Factors, 57(6), 930–946. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720815571935
recent_2_synopsis: This meta-analysis synthesized empirical research across three categories of sleep disruption—total deprivation, sleep restriction, and circadian disruption—to quantify their effects on complex cognitive task performance. A key finding was that complex cognitive tasks may be less severely impaired by sleep disruption than simple tasks, with direct implications for safety-critical professions such as aviation, healthcare, and transportation.
recent_3: Sebok, A., Laux, L., & Wickens, C. (2015). Supporting human-automation interaction in the rail industry by applying lessons from aviation. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society 59th Annual Meeting.
recent_3_synopsis: This paper transferred knowledge from aviation human-automation interaction research—particularly lessons around flight management system mode complexity and mode transitions—to the design of Positive Train Control (PTC) automation in locomotive cabs. It revisited Bainbridge's 'Ironies of Automation' and argued that aviation experience offers actionable guidance for mitigating operator error with emerging rail automation.
seminal_1: Wickens, C. D., Hutchins, S. D., Laux, L., & Sebok, A. (2015). The impact of sleep disruption on complex cognitive tasks: A meta-analysis. Human Factors, 57(6), 930–946. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720815571935
seminal_1_synopsis: This is the most widely cited peer-reviewed work confirmed for Laux; it established a quantitative evidence base distinguishing how different forms of sleep disruption affect complex versus simple cognitive task performance, and is heavily referenced in safety-critical operations research. Its publication in the flagship Human Factors journal amplified its influence across aviation, rail, healthcare, and military human factors communities.
seminal_2: Muller, M. J., Wharton, C., McIver, W. J., Jr., & Laux, L. (1997). Toward an HCI research and practice agenda based on human needs and social responsibility. Proceedings of the ACM CHI 97 Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (pp. 155–161). ACM.
seminal_2_synopsis: This CHI paper made an early case for reorienting human-computer interaction research around broader human needs and social responsibility rather than narrow usability metrics, situating HCI within a framework of societal impact. It has been cited in subsequent work on social justice in HCI and the ethics of user-centered design, representing Laux's contribution to expanding the scope of human factors beyond task performance.
expertise_overview: Warning and risk communication: product safety labels, hazard perception, severity messaging; displays, controls, and human-system communication. Human-automation interaction in transportation, transferring aviation lessons on flight-management mode complexity, mode transitions, and automation complacency to rail Positive Train Control design and revisiting the ironies of automation. Meta-analysis synthesizing total sleep deprivation, sleep restriction, and circadian disruption to quantify effects on complex versus simple cognitive task performance, finding complex tasks may be less severely impaired, with implications for fatigue, workload, and sustained attention in aviation, healthcare, and military operations. Forensic human factors frameworks for accident and human-error analysis; HCI accessibility for people with disabilities; aging-related driving differences; reorienting HCI toward broader human needs and social responsibility.
notes: Identification is confident: Laux is explicitly listed by SAGE Publications as an author affiliated with "Human Factors Consulting, USA" and is confirmed as an Editorial Board member of Human Factors journal. Her ResearchGate profile (46 publications, ~980 citations) and expert-witness profiler records confirm her identity as a human factors PhD from Rice University. The most recent publication confirmed is the 2019 book chapter; no peer-reviewed journal articles from 2020–2026 were surfaced through web search, which is consistent with her shift toward consulting and expert-witness practice. The sleep disruption meta-analysis (Wickens et al., 2015) is listed as both a recent and seminal work given it is the most highly cited confirmed peer-reviewed paper and falls within the decade preceding the knowledge cutoff. Warning-label conference papers from 1989–1991 (with Laughery et al.) are early and foundational contributions but were published only in HFES Annual Meeting Proceedings, not peer-reviewed journals, so they are not listed as seminal; the CHI 1997 paper is used instead as the second seminal work. Citation counts are not precisely confirmed; Semantic Scholar and ResearchGate pages returned 403 errors during retrieval.

name: Lavender, Steven A.
recent_1: Lavender, S.A., Sommerich, C.M., & Kachlan, A. (2025). A field test of the EMS LiftKit: Validating its usability, usefulness, and desirability. Applied Ergonomics, 127, 104519.
recent_1_synopsis: Sixteen LiftKit sets were deployed across five EMS organizations over a 12-month real-world trial; survey and interview data confirmed that the seven patient-handling tools received strong usability and usefulness ratings, particularly for use in confined spaces, establishing ecological validity for the device beyond the laboratory.
recent_2: Lavender, S.A., Sommerich, C.M., & Kachlan, A. (2025). Identifying ergonomics practices currently used by grocery distribution centers. Applied Ergonomics, 125, 104440.
recent_2_synopsis: Through interviews with 97 managers at 30 U.S. grocery distribution centers, the study catalogued 44 distinct ergonomic practices organized across six categories (facility setup, warehouse management systems, order selector tools, full-case picking, organizational support, and piece-pick hazard reduction), creating an evidence base that practitioners can benchmark against to reduce musculoskeletal disorder rates.
recent_3: Lavender, S.A., Charbonnet, J., & Sommerich, C.M. (2023). Biomechanical assessment of alternative hand trucks for transporting heavy loads up and down stairs. Applied Ergonomics, 110, 104010.
recent_3_synopsis: EMG data from nine experienced delivery workers moving a 52.3 kg washing machine on stairs showed that a powered two-speed hand truck significantly reduced erector spinae, trapezius, and biceps muscle activation compared to conventional and multi-wheeled designs, providing biomechanical evidence to guide occupational equipment selection.
seminal_1: Marras, W.S., Lavender, S.A., Leurgans, S.E., Rajulu, S.L., Allread, W.G., Fathallah, F.A., & Ferguson, S.A. (1993). The role of dynamic three-dimensional trunk motion in occupationally-related low back disorders: The effects of workplace factors, trunk position, and trunk motion characteristics on risk of injury. Spine, 18(5), 617–628.
seminal_1_synopsis: Analysis of more than 400 industrial lifting jobs across 48 industries identified five biomechanical risk factors — lifting frequency, load moment, trunk lateral velocity, trunk twisting velocity, and trunk sagittal angle — whose combination predicted low back disorder risk more than three times better than existing lifting guidelines at the time; this landmark epidemiologic-biomechanics study became a foundational reference for occupational risk assessment and the development of dynamic spinal loading models.
seminal_2: Lavender, S.A., Mirka, G.A., Schoenmarklin, R.W., Sommerich, C.M., Sudhakar, L.R., & Marras, W.S. (1989). The effects of preview and task symmetry on trunk muscle response to sudden loading. Human Factors, 31(1), 101–115.
seminal_2_synopsis: Using EMG from eight trunk muscles while subjects caught falling weights under four levels of advance warning (0–400 ms) and symmetric vs. asymmetric postures, the study demonstrated linear relationships between preview time and peak/mean muscle activation, and showed that asymmetric posture substantially altered preparatory muscle recruitment; this paper established foundational principles about how anticipation and body orientation govern spinal injury risk during unexpected loading events.
expertise_overview: Occupational ergonomics and biomechanics: musculoskeletal loading during manual material handling, prospectively validated risk models for work-related low back disorders, and ergonomic interventions across EMS providers, grocery distribution workers, healthcare workers, and firefighters. Analyzed over 400 industrial lifting jobs to identify five biomechanical risk factors—lifting frequency, load moment, trunk lateral velocity, trunk twisting velocity, and trunk sagittal angle—whose combination predicted low back disorder risk far better than existing guidelines, a foundational reference for dynamic spinal loading models. EMG evidence on how advance warning time and symmetric versus asymmetric posture govern preparatory muscle recruitment and spinal injury risk during unexpected loading. EMG and field evaluation of powered hand trucks and patient-handling tools, catalogued distribution-center ergonomic practices, and organizational barriers and facilitators to intervention adoption.
notes: Identity is highly confident: Steven A. Lavender, PhD, CPE is unambiguously the OSU occupational biomechanics/ergonomics researcher with extensive HFES involvement, including publications in Human Factors dating to 1989 and fellow status in HFES. The 1993 Spine paper is a multi-author work with Marras as lead; Lavender is a key co-author and the study is routinely attributed to the Marras/Lavender OSU group.
