name: Bitan, Yuval
recent_1: Ben Yaakov, Y., Bitan, Y., & Meyer, J. (2026). Information shapes decisions: The access and use of decision support. Computers in Human Behavior Reports, 21, 100949. DOI: 10.1016/j.chbr.2026.100949
recent_1_synopsis: This study examines how people access and use automated decision-support information, showing that the way information is presented and made available shapes the decisions operators ultimately make. It contributes to understanding human reliance on decision aids and the design of effective support systems. The work reflects Bitan's continuing collaboration with Joachim Meyer on trust in and use of automation.
recent_2: Moshe, A., Meyer, J., Jaffe, E., & Bitan, Y. (2026). Between Compliance and Complexity: Dispatchers' Use of an Automatic Emergency Vehicle Assignment System. International Journal of Human-Computer Interaction, 1-13. DOI: 10.1080/10447318.2025.2607554
recent_2_synopsis: This paper investigates how emergency medical dispatchers interact with an automated ambulance-assignment system, analyzing when dispatchers comply with or override the automation amid operational complexity. It illuminates the tension between algorithmic recommendations and human situational judgment in safety-critical dispatching. The findings inform the design of automation that supports rather than constrains expert decision making.
recent_3: Braverman, A., Frenkel, A., Schwarzfuchs, D., Jaffe, E., & Bitan, Y. (2026). Verbal and visual information exchange in EMS-to-ED patient handovers: An observational and attitudinal study. International Emergency Nursing, 84, 101735. DOI: 10.1016/j.ienj.2025.101735
recent_3_synopsis: Through observation and attitudinal survey, this study characterizes how information is transferred between emergency medical services and emergency department staff during patient handovers, combining verbal and visual channels. It identifies gaps and risks in handover communication that can threaten patient safety. The work applies human factors methods to a high-stakes communication transition point in acute care.
seminal_1: Bitan, Y., Meyer, J., Shinar, D., & Zmora, E. (2004). Nurses' reactions to alarms in a neonatal intensive care unit. Cognition, Technology & Work, 6(4), 239-246. DOI: 10.1007/s10111-004-0162-2
seminal_1_synopsis: This is Bitan's most-cited paper (approximately 142 citations per Google Scholar, June 2026), establishing empirically how NICU nurses respond to clinical alarms and how alarm characteristics shape their reactions. It is foundational to the alarm-fatigue and clinical-alarm-management literature, demonstrating that nurses learn the meaning and reliability of alarms and adjust their responsiveness accordingly. The study helped frame human factors approaches to medical alarm design and patient monitoring.
seminal_2: Meyer, J., & Bitan, Y. (2002). Why Better Operators Receive Worse Warnings. Human Factors, 44(3), 343-353. DOI: 10.1518/0018720024497754
seminal_2_synopsis: This Human Factors paper (approximately 85 citations per Google Scholar, June 2026) shows the counterintuitive result that more skilled or attentive operators may be served by less useful warning systems because warnings are calibrated to average performance. It is foundational to the theory of warning and alerting system design, informing how alarms and cues should account for operator capability. The work anchors Bitan's long-running research program on human interaction with warnings and automation.
expertise_overview: Yuval Bitan is a human factors and systems-analysis researcher focused on patient safety and the interface between clinicians and their work environment. His core topics include medical alarm and warning design, medication safety (including container/vial and label design and color-coding), and human reliance on decision-support and automation. He works extensively in emergency and disaster contexts, studying incident-commander decision making, mass-casualty incident simulation, EMS dispatch automation, and clinical handover communication. Methodologically he favors simulation-based study, observational field research, card-sorting and cognitive task analysis, and quantitative modeling of communication and decision processes. His domains span the NICU and ICU, emergency departments, and prehospital/EMS systems, and he applies machine-learning-based clinical decision support and trust calibration. He is a senior lecturer in Health Policy and Management at Ben-Gurion University and founding director of SimReC, the research center for simulation in healthcare.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. The healthcare human factors researcher Yuval Bitan (PhD Industrial Engineering & Management 2003, Ben-Gurion University of the Negev; former research associate at University of Chicago Cognitive Technologies Lab, HumanEra/University Health Network and University of Toronto; founding director of SimReC) is the author of all four hf_papers and the selected recent/seminal works, confirmed via his personal site (yuvalbitan.com), Google Scholar (scholar.google.com/citations?user=R89cdtkAAAAJ), Ben-Gurion University profile/sciprofiles, and his frequent co-author Joachim Meyer. All six cited DOIs were verified to resolve via the Crossref API (api.crossref.org) on 2026-06-17 with matching titles. No unverifiable citations. name: Bowden, Vanessa K.
recent_1: Greenwell-Barnden, J. N., Visser, T. A. W., Loft, S., Whitney, S. J., & Bowden, V. K. (2026). How Multi-Tasking Ability Impacts Performance, Workload, Situation Awareness, Stress and Trust with Simulated Imperfect Automation. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 68(8), 1056-1077. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261431968
recent_1_synopsis: Using a simulated environment with imperfect automation, this study examined how individual differences in multi-tasking ability relate to performance, workload, situation awareness, stress, and trust. It links operator aptitude to a broad range of human-automation teaming outcomes, with implications for selection and for designing automation support tailored to differing multi-tasking capacity.
recent_2: Gegoff, I., Tatasciore, M., Bowden, V. K., & Loft, S. (2025). The benefits of automation transparency are dependent on human attention control capacity. Applied Ergonomics, 129, 104619. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2025.104619
recent_2_synopsis: This experiment showed that the benefits of automation transparency depend on an operator's attention control capacity (ACC): higher-ACC participants used automated advice more accurately when given more transparency, while lower-ACC participants did not benefit. It demonstrates that transparency is not universally helpful and should be matched to individual cognitive capacity.
recent_3: Bowden, V. K., Gegoff, I., Kilpatrick, P. J., & Loft, S. (2025). The Impact of Lower Degree Automation Reliability on Higher Degree Automation Failure Detection in Simulated Air Traffic Control. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 67(11), 1121-1135. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251335536
recent_3_synopsis: In a simulated air traffic control task with multiple degrees of automation, this study found that the reliability of lower-degree automation shapes operators' ability to detect failures in higher-degree automation. It highlights cross-dependencies between automation layers that affect failure detection in safety-critical supervisory control.
seminal_1: Sanderson, J. A., Bowden, V., Swire-Thompson, B., Lewandowsky, S., & Ecker, U. K. H. (2023). Listening to misinformation while driving: Cognitive load and the effectiveness of (repeated) corrections. Journal of Applied Research in Memory and Cognition, 12(3), 325-334. DOI: 10.1037/mac0000057
seminal_1_synopsis: This study tested whether corrections of misinformation remain effective when listeners are under cognitive load (driving in a simulator), finding that repeated corrections outperformed single corrections and that load reduced correction effectiveness. With roughly 48 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), it is among Bowden's most-cited works and is influential in the misinformation-correction and applied-cognition literatures for showing how concurrent task demands constrain belief updating.
seminal_2: Strickland, L., Boag, R. J., Heathcote, A., Bowden, V., & Loft, S. (2023). Automated decision aids: When are they advisors and when do they take control of human decision making? Journal of Experimental Psychology: Applied, 29(4), 849-868. DOI: 10.1037/xap0000463
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper applied a computational evidence-accumulation model to determine when an automated decision aid functions as an advisor versus when it effectively takes control of the operator's decision, across varying aid reliability in an air traffic control conflict-detection task. With roughly 32 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), it is foundational for formal, model-based accounts of how humans integrate automated advice and how reliability shapes reliance.
expertise_overview: Bowden studies human cognition and attention in interaction with technology, with central themes of human-automation teaming, automation transparency, and appropriate reliance on automated advice and decision aids. A substantial line of work concerns mental workload, sustained attention/vigilance, situation awareness, and how concurrent task demands and individual differences (e.g., attention control capacity, multi-tasking ability) shape performance. She applies these questions to safety-critical domains such as air traffic control, submarine track management, and transportation/driving, including the effects of cognitive load on processing information (e.g., correcting misinformation while driving). Methodologically she combines controlled experiments, high-fidelity simulation, computational/cognitive modeling of decision making (evidence accumulation), and the measurement of workload and situation awareness. Recent work emphasizes when and for whom automation transparency improves the accuracy of automation use.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Vanessa K. Bowden, University of Western Australia (School of Psychological Science), Senior Lecturer; frequent co-author with Shayne Loft, consistent with the pre-verified Human Factors papers. No unverifiable citations were included.

name: Brecknell, Birgit
recent_1: Pascale, M. T., Sanderson, P., Liu, D., Mohamed, I., Brecknell, B., & Loeb, R. G. (2019). The Impact of Head-Worn Displays on Strategic Alarm Management and Situation Awareness. Human Factors, 61(4), 537-563. DOI: 10.1177/0018720818814969
recent_1_synopsis: Investigated whether head-worn displays (HWDs) help mobile clinicians make better alarm-management decisions and achieve better situation awareness than auditory alarms alone. A preclinical laboratory study showed HWDs supported superior situation awareness and more appropriate alarm responses while participants were mobile and multitasking. This is Brecknell's most recent published paper under her own name (no 2023-2026 papers were located; see notes).
recent_2: Zestic, J., Brecknell, B., Liley, H. G., & Sanderson, P. (2019). A Novel Auditory Display for Neonatal Resuscitation: Laboratory Studies Simulating Pulse Oximetry in the First 10 Minutes After Birth. Human Factors, 61(1), 119-138. DOI: 10.1177/0018720818793769
recent_2_synopsis: Tested whether enhanced sonifications of pulse oximetry could improve clinicians' ability to judge a simulated neonate's oxygen saturation during the first 10 minutes after birth, when target SpO2 ranges change minute-by-minute. Laboratory studies showed that adding auditory enhancements demarcating target ranges improved range-identification accuracy. Demonstrates application of auditory display design to the high-stakes neonatal resuscitation context.
recent_3: Li, S. Y. W., Tse, M.-K., Brecknell, B., & Sanderson, P. M. (2019). Spearcon Sequences for Monitoring Multiple Patients: Laboratory Investigation Comparing Two Auditory Display Designs. Human Factors, 61(2), 288-304. DOI: 10.1177/0018720818797502
recent_3_synopsis: Compared two auditory display designs using spearcon (time-compressed speech) sequences for monitoring the vital signs of multiple simulated patients simultaneously. Examined how sequence structure affects clinicians' ability to track oxygen saturation and heart rate across patients. Extends the spearcon patient-monitoring research line toward multi-patient supervisory contexts.
seminal_1: Li, S. Y. W., Tang, T.-L., Hickling, A., Yau, S., Brecknell, B., & Sanderson, P. M. (2017). Spearcons for Patient Monitoring: Laboratory Investigation Comparing Earcons and Spearcons. Human Factors, 59(5), 765-781. DOI: 10.1177/0018720817697536
seminal_1_synopsis: Compared single-tone earcons against spearcons (time-compressed spoken phrases) for conveying two commonly monitored vital signs, oxygen saturation and heart rate. Found participants were more accurate identifying SpO2 and HR with spearcons than with abstract earcons, establishing spearcons as a strong candidate auditory display for patient monitoring. Foundational to the UQ group's spearcon research program (approx. 20-25 citations; seed citation hint 23), repeatedly cited in subsequent multiple-patient and head-worn display monitoring studies.
seminal_2: Hickling, A. (Brecknell, B.), Loeb, R. G., & Sanderson, P. (2016). Using a Sequence of Earcons to Monitor Multiple Simulated Patients. Human Factors, 59(2), 248-262. DOI: 10.1177/0018720816670986
seminal_2_synopsis: Tested whether a serial sequence of earcons could allow clinicians to monitor the vital signs of several simulated patients at once, an early step in extending single-patient auditory monitoring to multi-patient supervision. Established the methodological paradigm (laboratory simulation of concurrent patient monitoring via abstract auditory cues) that the group's later spearcon and head-worn display work built upon. Approximately 12 citations (seed citation hint 12); foundational for the multiple-patient auditory monitoring line.
expertise_overview: Brecknell's research centers on auditory display design for clinical patient monitoring, with particular focus on earcons (abstract sounds) and spearcons (time-compressed speech) for communicating vital signs such as oxygen saturation and heart rate. A recurring theme is supporting clinicians in monitoring multiple patients simultaneously and while mobile, including head-worn display interfaces for strategic alarm management and situation awareness. She also works on enhanced pulse-oximetry sonification for specialized high-stakes settings such as neonatal resuscitation, where target ranges shift over time. Methodologically she relies on controlled laboratory and preclinical simulation studies measuring identification accuracy, alarm-management decisions, and situation awareness. Her work sits at the intersection of cognitive engineering, auditory perception, human-computer interaction, and healthcare/anesthesia patient safety, within the University of Queensland Cognitive Engineering Research Group tradition.

name: Callaghan, Jack P.
recent_1: Pinto, B. L., Beach, T. A. C., & Callaghan, J. P. (2025). Does the Directive to Avoid Low Back Flexion Hinder Physical Performance? Examining Isometric Strength in Postures Adopted During Light Mass Lifting. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251404836
recent_1_synopsis: Examines whether the common ergonomic and clinical directive to avoid lumbar flexion during lifting actually compromises strength output. Using isometric strength testing in postures adopted during light-mass lifting, the work informs the ongoing debate over flexion-avoidance instruction in occupational settings. Reflects Callaghan's continued translational interest in lifting mechanics and low back injury prevention.
recent_2: Davidson, J. M., & Callaghan, J. P. (2025). Do lumbar spine kinematics contribute to individual low back pain development in habitual sitting? Ergonomics, 69(5), 756-769. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2025.2481608
recent_2_synopsis: Tracks lumbar spine kinematics across a week of habitual office sitting in non-pain developers versus pain developers (classified by visual-analogue scale). Investigates whether individual movement patterns predict transient low back pain development during prolonged sitting. Extends his standing/sitting-induced low back pain research program into real-world field measurement.
recent_3: Callaghan, J. P., et al. (2024). Lateral Pelvis and Lumbar Motion in Seated and Standing Office Work and Their Association With Transient Low Back Pain. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208241249423
recent_3_synopsis: Measures lateral pelvis and lumbar motion during seated and standing office work and relates these to transient low back pain. Builds on the sit-stand paradigm literature by isolating frontal-plane motion as a potential contributor to symptom onset. Directly relevant to the design of office workstations and sit-stand interventions.
seminal_1: Callaghan, J. P., & McGill, S. M. (2001). Intervertebral disc herniation: studies on a porcine model exposed to highly repetitive flexion/extension motion with compressive force. Clinical Biomechanics, 16(1), 28-37. DOI: 10.1016/S0268-0033(00)00063-2
seminal_1_synopsis: Foundational in-vitro demonstration that intervertebral disc herniation in non-degenerated porcine motion segments is driven primarily by highly repetitive flexion/extension motion rather than by applied joint compression, with higher compressive force increasing injury frequency and severity. Approx. 700+ Google Scholar citations (estimate, June 2026). Widely cited as primary mechanistic evidence linking cumulative flexion exposure to disc injury, underpinning much subsequent occupational spine-loading and lifting-guideline research.
seminal_2: Callaghan, J. P., & McGill, S. M. (2001). Low back joint loading and kinematics during standing and unsupported sitting. Ergonomics, 44(3), 280-294. DOI: 10.1080/00140130118276
seminal_2_synopsis: Used a detailed 104-muscle anatomical model with EMG-driven spine posture to show that prolonged unsupported sitting produces higher lumbar compressive loads than standing, challenging the assumption that sitting is a low-demand posture. Approx. 700+ Google Scholar citations (estimate, June 2026). A foundational reference in office ergonomics and the sit-stand literature, frequently cited in workstation design and sedentary-work health research.
expertise_overview: Callaghan's research centers on the etiology and prevention of low back and spine injuries arising from cumulative and repetitive occupational loading. He combines in-vitro tissue mechanics (porcine and human functional spinal units examining flexion/extension, compression, and disc herniation pathways) with in-vivo human studies of spine kinematics, joint loading, and muscle activation. A major theme is sitting versus standing biomechanics and the sit-stand paradigm, including transient/standing-induced low back pain, prolonged office work, and workstation and automotive seat design. He also studies lifting mechanics and flexion-avoidance directives, posture measurement methods, and ergonomic exposure assessment. His work bridges fundamental biomechanics with applied ergonomics and injury-prevention guidelines. Recurring application domains include office work, driving/seating, and occupational lifting.
notes: Identity confirmed with HIGH confidence: Jack P. Callaghan, Professor of Kinesiology & Health Sciences, University of Waterloo; Canada Research Chair in Spine Biomechanics and Injury Prevention; Director, CRE-MSD; Canadian Certified Professional Ergonomist; cross-appointed to Mechanical & Mechatronics Engineering. Sources: University of Waterloo faculty profile (uwaterloo.ca/kinesiology-health-sciences/people-profiles/jack-callaghan), Google Scholar (scholar.google.ca/citations?user=2BgSJ-AAAAAJ, ~16,447 total citations), ResearchGate, Canada Research Chairs profile (chairs-chaires.gc.ca). All five cited DOIs verified via Crossref API (HTTP 200, matching titles/authors) on 2026-06-17: recent_1 10.1177/00187208251404836 and recent_3 10.1177/00187208241249423 are pre-verified hf_papers; recent_2 10.1080/00140139.2025.2481608 (Ergonomics, Davidson & Callaghan), seminal_1 10.1016/S0268-0033(00)00063-2 (Clinical Biomechanics, Callaghan & McGill), seminal_2 10.1080/00140130118276 (Ergonomics, Callaghan & McGill) all confirmed. Citation counts for seminal papers are APPROXIMATE estimates (Google Scholar, June 2026); exact per-paper counts were not directly scraped from the profile. No unverifiable DOIs were cited.

name: Clegg, Benjamin A.
recent_1: Gutzwiller, R. S., Bhat, R., Guarino, S., Clegg, B. A., Lynn, S. K., Kwan, V., Hypolite, J., Sieffert, M., Locasto, M., Kelle, D., Slocum, M., Wu, C., Harrison, S., Revelle, M., & Latiff, S. (2026). Exploiting Loss Aversion Disrupts Skilled Attackers in a Realistic Cyber Range. Computational Brain & Behavior. DOI: 10.1007/s42113-026-00297-5
recent_1_synopsis: Drawing on prospect theory, the study tested whether framing defensive cues to exploit attackers' loss aversion could degrade the performance of skilled adversaries operating in a realistic cyber range. Results showed that manipulating perceived losses disrupted and slowed even experienced attackers, demonstrating an 'oppositional human factors' approach that turns cognitive biases against intruders. It matters because it reframes cyber defense as a problem of shaping adversary cognition rather than only hardening systems.
recent_2: Patton, C. E., Clegg, B. A., Davis, B. C., & Blanchard, N. (2025). (Some) Benefits in Operator Decisions to Use AI After Experiencing Optimal Outcomes. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 68(3), 368-380. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251387933
recent_2_synopsis: This experiment examined how operators' prior experience with outcomes shapes their willingness to rely on an AI decision aid, finding that experiencing optimal outcomes produced some, but limited, improvement in subsequent choices to use the AI. The work refines models of automation reliance by showing experience-driven calibration is partial rather than complete. It informs the design of AI aids where appropriate (rather than over- or under-) reliance is the goal.
recent_3: Poole, C. A., Warden, A. C., Wickens, C. D., Raikwar, A., Clegg, B. A., Buckman, M., & Ortega, F. R. (2025). Information Access Costs With an Augmented Reality Head-Mounted Display. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 68(2), 149-161. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251377311
recent_3_synopsis: The study quantified the cognitive and physical 'information access costs' incurred when operators retrieve information presented on an augmented reality head-mounted display versus other display formats. It showed how the effort of accessing AR-presented information shapes attention allocation and performance. The findings guide AR interface design by characterizing when head-mounted presentation helps versus hinders information seeking.
seminal_1: Clegg, B. A., DiGirolamo, G. J., & Keele, S. W. (1998). Sequence learning. Trends in Cognitive Sciences, 2(8), 275-281. DOI: 10.1016/S1364-6613(98)01202-9
seminal_1_synopsis: This influential review synthesized research on how people learn serial order in perceptual-motor and cognitive tasks, distinguishing implicit from explicit sequence learning and clarifying methods such as the serial reaction time task. With approximately 356 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is Clegg's most-cited work and a foundational reference in the implicit learning and skill acquisition literature. It is foundational because it framed the conceptual and methodological landscape that subsequent sequence-learning research built upon.
seminal_2: Wickens, C. D., Clegg, B. A., Vieane, A. Z., & Sebok, A. L. (2015). Complacency and Automation Bias in the Use of Imperfect Automation. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 57(5), 728-739. DOI: 10.1177/0018720815581940
seminal_2_synopsis: This study experimentally separated automation complacency (failure to detect missing automation advice) from automation bias (over-acceptance of incorrect advice), examining operator behavior at the first failure after repeated reliable automation. With approximately 274 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is a heavily cited touchstone in human-automation interaction research. It is foundational because it provided clean experimental evidence and definitions distinguishing two failure modes central to designing trustworthy decision-aiding automation.
expertise_overview: Clegg studies human performance and applied cognitive psychology, with central interests in skill acquisition, training, and implicit/sequence learning, including how knowledge acquired without intention transfers to performance contexts. A major strand of his work addresses human-automation and human-AI interaction, examining complacency, automation bias, trust, and operators' decisions about when to rely on automated or AI decision aids. He also investigates attention, situation awareness, workload, and spatial trajectory prediction and judgment under uncertainty. His applied work spans augmented and virtual reality and head-mounted displays (information access costs, eccentricity effects, clutter), cybersecurity defense via oppositional human factors, and simulation- and game-based training. Methodologically he relies on controlled behavioral experiments, serial reaction time and decision tasks, and increasingly physiological measures such as fNIRS for workload assessment. His application domains include aviation and maritime collision avoidance, cyber operations, and adaptive training systems.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Benjamin A. Clegg, Professor of Cognitive Psychology, Colorado State University. Confirmed via his CSU faculty/Google Sites page (sites.google.com/rams.colostate.edu/benclegg), Google Scholar profile (user 58z66zsAAAAJ), and consistency between the pre-verified Human Factors papers and his profile. name: Cooper, Joel M.
recent_1: Crabtree, K. W., McDonnell, A. S., Cooper, J. M., & Strayer, D. L. (2024). Multitasking Induced Contextual Blindness. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 67(3), 225-245. DOI: 10.1177/00187208241274040
recent_1_synopsis: This study examined how concurrent secondary tasks degrade drivers' encoding and awareness of the surrounding visual scene, producing a form of inattentional or 'contextual' blindness even when the eyes remain on the road. The work extends the lab's program on cognitive distraction by showing that multitasking impairs situational understanding beyond simple gaze metrics, with implications for distraction policy and in-vehicle interface design.
recent_2: Lohani, M., Cooper, J. M., McDonnell, A. S., Erickson, G. G., Simmons, T. G., Carriero, A. E., Crabtree, K. W., & Strayer, D. L. (2024). Reliable but multi-dimensional cognitive demand in operating partially automated vehicles: implications for real-world automation research. Cognitive Research: Principles and Implications, 9(1), 60. DOI: 10.1186/s41235-024-00591-5
recent_2_synopsis: Using an on-road study of 71 drivers across four sessions, this paper assessed the test-retest reliability of five physiological and behavioral measures of cognitive demand (heart rate and its variability, EEG alpha power, and Detection Response Task reaction time and hit rate) during partially automated driving. EEG alpha power showed excellent reliability while cardiac and DRT measures ranged from good to moderate, supporting a multi-dimensional, psychometrically grounded approach to measuring workload in real-world automation research.
recent_3: Sanbonmatsu, D. M., Crabtree, K. W., McDonnell, A. S., Cooper, J. M., & Strayer, D. L. (2024). Automated driving experiences, attention, and intentions following extensive on-road usage of a level 2 automation vehicle. Journal of Safety Research, 90, 199-207. DOI: 10.1016/j.jsr.2024.05.002
recent_3_synopsis: This on-road longitudinal study tracked how prolonged use of Level 2 vehicle automation changed drivers' subjective experience, attention, and adoption intentions over time. Automation made driving more enjoyable and less stressful, but inattention increased with continued use, and the favorability of the automated experience strongly predicted intentions to use and purchase such systems, highlighting a safety-relevant tradeoff between comfort and engagement.
seminal_1: Drews, F. A., Yazdani, H., Godfrey, C. N., Cooper, J. M., & Strayer, D. L. (2009). Text Messaging During Simulated Driving. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 51(5), 762-770. DOI: 10.1177/0018720809353319
seminal_1_synopsis: Using a high-fidelity simulator, this study showed that text messaging while driving slowed braking responses and degraded both forward and lateral vehicle control, with roughly a sixfold increase in crashes relative to driving alone. With approximately 600 citations (Google Scholar), it is one of Cooper's most influential works and an early, widely cited empirical demonstration of the dangers of texting that helped shape distracted-driving research and policy.
seminal_2: Strayer, D. L., Turrill, J., Cooper, J. M., Coleman, J. R., Medeiros-Ward, N., & Biondi, F. (2015). Assessing Cognitive Distraction in the Automobile. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 57(8), 1300-1324. DOI: 10.1177/0018720815575149
seminal_2_synopsis: Across multiple studies integrating primary-task, secondary-task, subjective, and physiological measures, this paper developed a systematic cognitive-distraction scale rating common in-vehicle tasks. Cited roughly 420 times (Google Scholar), it is foundational for establishing a standardized, multi-measure methodology for quantifying mental workload behind the wheel and demonstrating that 'hands-free' does not mean 'risk-free.'
expertise_overview: Cooper's research centers on driver distraction, cognitive workload, and attention in the context of driving and increasingly partial vehicle automation. He specializes in measuring mental demand through converging methods, combining vehicle and behavioral performance, the Detection Response Task, subjective ratings, and physiological indices such as heart rate variability and EEG alpha power. A recurring theme is the cognitive cost of multitasking and in-vehicle technologies (voice assistants, infotainment systems, and Level 2 automation), including phenomena like inattentional/contextual blindness and the persistence of distraction after a task ends. His work spans high-fidelity simulator studies and on-road experiments, with strong attention to the reliability and validity of workload measurement instruments. He also brings an applied, instrumentation-oriented perspective, developing portable cognitive and physiological measurement tools for studying driver behavior.
notes: Confidently identified as the University of Utah / Applied Cognition Lab (David Strayer) driving-distraction researcher; also affiliated with Red Scientific Inc. and previously Precision Driving Research. Identity confirmed via Google Scholar (user=LfO53RQAAAAJ), the University of Utah Applied Cognition Lab research-team page, and ResearchGate, all matching the pre-verified Human Factors hf_papers. All cited DOIs were verified to resolve via Crossref on 2026-06-17 with matching titles (hf_papers DOIs pre-verified; recent_2, recent_3, seminal_1, and seminal_2 DOIs independently verified). Citation counts are approximate, from Google Scholar as of mid-2025 (Text Messaging ~600; Assessing Cognitive Distraction ~420). No fabricated DOIs; no unverifiable citations included.

name: Craig, Curtis M.
recent_1: Sievert, K., Craig, C. M., Achtemeier, J., & Morris, N. L. (2025). Examining Biological Motion as a Potential Factor in E-Scooter Conspicuity and Safety. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251344366
recent_1_synopsis: This study investigated whether biological motion cues (the visible articulated movement of a rider's limbs) influence how readily drivers detect and recognize e-scooter riders, a population whose gliding posture may suppress the motion signals pedestrians normally provide. The work matters because it links a basic perceptual mechanism to the conspicuity gap underlying the rising toll of e-scooter crashes, offering implications for rider visibility countermeasures.
recent_2: Morris, N. L., Schwieters, K. R., Tian, D., & Craig, C. M. (2024). Evaluation of driver navigational errors and acceptance of a simulated J-turn intersection. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 198, 107490. DOI: 10.1016/j.aap.2024.107490
recent_2_synopsis: Using a driving simulator, this paper measured how often drivers made navigational errors at an unfamiliar J-turn (restricted crossing U-turn) intersection design and how acceptable they found it. The findings inform the human-factors trade-offs of deploying alternative intersection geometries that reduce severe-crash conflict points but may confuse drivers.
recent_3: Adams, S. C., Craig, C. M., Klein, M. I., Achtemeier, J., & Morris, N. L. (2023). Task-Relevant Smartphone Messages Within Work Zones: A Driving Simulation Study. Human Factors, 65(8), 1734-1751. DOI: 10.1177/00187208231167641
recent_3_synopsis: This simulator study examined how task-relevant smartphone messages delivered to drivers approaching and traversing roadway work zones affected driving performance and attention. It speaks to the design of in-vehicle and mobile information systems that aim to support rather than distract drivers in safety-critical, high-workload settings.
seminal_1: Craig, C. M., Morris, N. L., Van Houten, R., & Mayou, D. (2019). Pedestrian Safety and Driver Yielding Near Public Transit Stops. Transportation Research Record, 2673(1), 514-523. DOI: 10.1177/0361198118822313
seminal_1_synopsis: This field study quantified driver yielding behavior toward pedestrians crossing near public transit stops and evaluated factors affecting compliance. With roughly 74 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2026) it is Craig's most-cited work and is foundational to applied pedestrian-safety research, connecting transit infrastructure to measurable driver-yielding outcomes.
seminal_2: Craig, C., Klein, M. I., Griswold, J., Gaitonde, K., McGill, T., & Halldorsson, A. (2012). Using Cognitive Task Analysis to Identify Critical Decisions in the Laparoscopic Environment. Human Factors, 54(6), 1025-1039. DOI: 10.1177/0018720812448393
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper applied cognitive task analysis to surgical laparoscopy to surface the critical decisions and knowledge that distinguish expert performance. With approximately 58 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2026) it is an influential early demonstration of CTA methods in the operating room and remains a reference point for human-factors work on surgical expertise and use error in healthcare.
expertise_overview: Craig studies human cognition in safety-critical real-world domains, with attention and mental workload as recurring threads. A large share of his work concerns transportation and roadway safety: pedestrian and e-scooter conspicuity, driver yielding behavior, distraction and in-vehicle messaging, work zones, alternative intersection designs, automated-vehicle trust, and drowsy driving. He also works on attention restoration and the cognitive effects of natural versus built environments, including vigilance and sustained-attention paradigms. A separate line applies cognitive task analysis and human-error frameworks to healthcare settings such as laparoscopic surgery. Methodologically he relies on driving simulation, naturalistic and field observation, behavioral experiments, and survey research, often combining objective performance measures with workload and acceptance assessments.
notes: Identified with high confidence. Anchored by the five pre-verified Human Factors papers and confirmed via Google Scholar (user=jqDe3MEAAAAJ; ~843 total citations, h-index 17, i10 20), the University of Minnesota HumanFIRST Laboratory staff page (roadcoach.umn.edu/staff/craig), his UMN 2-page CV, and Experts@Minnesota. He is a research associate at UMN's HumanFIRST Lab (PhD in Experimental Psychology/Human Factors, Texas Tech, 2016), Certified Human Factors Professional. Citation counts are from Google Scholar as of mid-2026. New DOIs verified via Crossref: J-turn AAP 2024 (10.1016/j.aap.2024.107490), transit-yielding TRR 2019 (10.1177/0361198118822313), and laparoscopic CTA HF 2012 (10.1177/0018720812448393); the three Human Factors DOIs are pre-verified. Author order and page numbers for recent_1 (E-Scooter, advance online at time of writing) were taken from Crossref/Scholar; recent_1 had no print volume/issue assigned yet. No citation in this profile is unverifiable.

name: DeLucia, Patricia R.
recent_1: Huisman, T., DeLucia, P. R., & Oberfeld, D. (2026). The effect of vehicle sound power on auditory time-to-collision estimation. Acta Acustica, 10, Article 23. DOI: 10.1051/aacus/2026019
recent_1_synopsis: This study tested how a vehicle's acoustic sound power affects auditory judgments of time-to-collision, finding that for the same physical time-to-collision louder sources are perceived as arriving sooner than quieter ones. The result has direct safety implications for pedestrians and for the design of quiet (electric) vehicles, which may lead to riskier street-crossing decisions.
recent_2: DeLucia, P. R., Oberfeld, D., Kearney, J. K., Cloutier, R., Jilla, A. M., Zhou, J., Corona, R., Cormier, J., Taylor, S., Wykoff, C. C., & Baurès, R. (2025). Visual, auditory, and audiovisual time-to-collision estimation among participants with age-related macular degeneration compared to a normal-vision group: The TTC-AMD study. PLOS One, 20(12), e0337549. DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0337549
recent_2_synopsis: Using a virtual-reality roadway viewed from a pedestrian's perspective, this study compared visual, auditory, and audiovisual time-to-collision estimation between people with age-related macular degeneration and a normal-vision control group. It found that people with central vision loss judged vehicle arrival nearly as accurately as normally sighted observers, informing mobility safety and multisensory perception for low-vision pedestrians.
recent_3: Greenlee, E. T., DeLucia, P. R., & Newton, D. C. (2022). Driver Vigilance Decrement is More Severe During Automated Driving than Manual Driving. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society. DOI: 10.1177/00187208221103922
recent_3_synopsis: This driving-simulation study showed that the vigilance decrement-declining hazard detection over time-is more severe during automated driving than during manual driving, where the driver is more actively engaged. It underscores a core safety risk of partial vehicle automation: sustained passive monitoring degrades drivers' ability to detect hazards.
seminal_1: Greenlee, E. T., DeLucia, P. R., & Newton, D. C. (2018). Driver Vigilance in Automated Vehicles: Hazard Detection Failures Are a Matter of Time. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 60(4), 465-476. DOI: 10.1177/0018720818761711
seminal_1_synopsis: This study demonstrated that drivers monitoring an automated vehicle fail to detect hazards as a function of time on task, establishing the vigilance decrement as a fundamental limit on supervisory monitoring of automation. With roughly 143 citations (Human Factors metadata / Google Scholar, June 2026), it is among DeLucia's most-cited works and is widely referenced in the automated-driving safety literature for showing that hazard-detection failures grow with monitoring duration.
seminal_2: DeLucia, P. R. (1991). Pictorial and motion-based information for depth perception. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, 17(3), 738-748. DOI: 10.1037/0096-1523.17.3.738
seminal_2_synopsis: This classic perception paper provided early evidence for the 'size-arrival effect'-that a large, far approaching object is judged to arrive sooner than a small, near object-showing that time-to-contact perception is not based solely on the optical invariant tau but on multiple cues and heuristics. With several hundred citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), it is foundational to theories of collision and time-to-contact perception and to applied work on driver and pedestrian misjudgments of approaching vehicles.
expertise_overview: DeLucia studies visual and multisensory perception of motion, depth, and collision-especially time-to-collision (time-to-contact) judgments and the size-arrival effect, in which object size biases estimates of when an approaching object will arrive. A central thread is how perception draws on multiple sources of information and heuristics rather than a single optical invariant, and how this plays out in normal, impaired, and low-vision observers (e.g., age-related macular degeneration). She applies this work to human factors in transportation safety (driver and pedestrian hazard perception, automated-vehicle vigilance), as well as health care and surgical/medical task performance. Her methods span controlled psychophysical experiments, driving and collision-avoidance simulations, virtual reality, and meta-analysis. Recent work emphasizes audiovisual cue integration in time-to-collision estimation and the safety consequences of quiet electric vehicles for pedestrians.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Patricia R. DeLucia, Rice University (Department of Psychological Sciences; formerly Texas Tech), former Editor-in-Chief of Human Factors. Confirmed via Google Scholar profile (user=lLdYZJsAAAAJ; ~3,600 citations, h-index 33), Rice University faculty page (patricia-delucia.scholar.rice.edu), and Rice News (Dec 2025), all consistent with the pre-verified Human Factors papers. All cited DOIs verified to resolve via Crossref with matching titles, venues, and DeLucia in the author list: recent_1 (Huisman, DeLucia, Oberfeld), recent_2 (DeLucia et al., PLOS One), recent_3 / seminal_1 (Greenlee, DeLucia, Newton), seminal_2 (DeLucia, sole author, JEP:HPP 1991). Citation counts from Google Scholar / Human Factors metadata, retrieved 2026-06-17 (2018 driver-vigilance paper ~143; per-paper counts for the 1991 paper are approximate). No unverifiable citations were included.

name: Demir, Mustafa
recent_1: Jei, H. G., Demir, M., & Sasangohar, F. (2026). Eyes on the mission: Mixed methods assessment of eye-tracker-enabled interactive decision support in a simulated unmanned aerial vehicle system. Applied Ergonomics, 136, 104780. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104780
recent_1_synopsis: This mixed-methods study evaluated an eye-tracker-enabled interactive decision-support aid for operators of a simulated unmanned aerial vehicle system, examining how gaze-driven cueing affects mission performance and operator experience. It demonstrates how physiological sensing can be embedded into adaptive decision aids for complex supervisory-control tasks.
recent_2: Demir, M., Miratsky, J., Mishra, P., Nguyen, J., Chan, C. K., & Singharoy, A. (2025). Exploring Artificial Intelligence Tutor Teammate Adaptability to Harness Discovery Curiosity and Promote Learning in the Context of Interactive Molecular Dynamics. Cognitive Computation, 17(5). DOI: 10.1007/s12559-025-10498-y
recent_2_synopsis: This work positioned a generative-AI tutor as an adaptive teammate that detects and harnesses learners' discovery curiosity during interactive molecular dynamics tasks to promote learning. It extends human-autonomy teaming concepts into AI-assisted education, treating the AI not as a tool but as an adaptive collaborator.
recent_3: Grimm, D. A. P., Gorman, J. C., Cooke, N. J., Demir, M., & McNeese, N. J. (2023). Dynamical Measurement of Team Resilience. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making, 17(4), 351-382. DOI: 10.1177/15553434231199729
recent_3_synopsis: This paper developed dynamical, time-series-based measures of team resilience—how teams reorganize and recover after perturbations—using data from human-autonomy teaming experiments. It advances quantitative, systems-level methods for capturing the temporal coordination signatures that distinguish resilient from brittle teams.
seminal_1: McNeese, N. J., Demir, M., Cooke, N. J., & Myers, C. (2018). Teaming With a Synthetic Teammate: Insights into Human-Autonomy Teaming. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 60(2), 262-273. DOI: 10.1177/0018720817743223
seminal_1_synopsis: This influential study placed a synthetic (computational) teammate into a three-agent remotely-piloted-aircraft team and analyzed how human-autonomy teams coordinate, communicate, and perform relative to all-human teams. With roughly 510 citations (Google Scholar, 2026-06-17) it is Demir's most-cited work and a foundational empirical demonstration of human-autonomy teaming as a distinct phenomenon from individual human-automation interaction.
seminal_2: Demir, M., McNeese, N. J., & Cooke, N. J. (2017). Team situation awareness within the context of human-autonomy teaming. Cognitive Systems Research, 46, 3-12. DOI: 10.1016/j.cogsys.2016.11.003
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper reconceptualized team situation awareness as a dynamic, system-level property emerging from interactions among human and autonomous agents rather than a static sum of individual awareness. With roughly 217 citations (Google Scholar, 2026-06-17) it is a foundational theoretical contribution framing situation awareness through coordination dynamics in human-autonomy teams.
expertise_overview: Demir studies human-autonomy teaming (HAT)—how people and autonomous or AI agents coordinate, communicate, build trust, and maintain shared situation awareness as integrated teams. A central methodological thread is treating teams as complex dynamical systems and applying nonlinear time-series and coordination-dynamics techniques (e.g., recurrence and entropy measures) to quantify team coordination, resilience, and trust calibration over time. His application domains include remotely piloted aircraft and unmanned aerial vehicle operations, simulated command-and-control tasks, and increasingly generative-AI teammates in education and decision support. He examines how training, technological failures, and synthetic-teammate behavior shape communication strategies and team effectiveness. More recent work extends into human-centered AI, physiological and eye-tracking-based adaptive support, and AI tutors as adaptive teammates, bridging team cognition with human-centered data science.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Mustafa Demir (Google Scholar user=X41iFxEAAAAJ; ~3,466 total citations, h-index 31), formerly Arizona State University (Human Systems Engineering / CHART, long collaboration with Nancy J. Cooke) and now affiliated with Texas A&M University, evidenced by recurring co-author Farzan Sasangohar on the 2026 papers. Disambiguation anchored by the pre-verified Human Factors hf_papers and recurring co-authors Nancy J. Cooke, Nathan J. McNeese, Jamie C. Gorman, Aaron Likens, and David Grimm across HAT-themed work. Citation counts from Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17 (seminal_1 ~510; seminal_2 ~217). All cited DOIs verified via Crossref (HTTP 200, matching titles, Demir in author list): recent_1 (10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104780), recent_2 (10.1007/s12559-025-10498-y), recent_3 (10.1177/15553434231199729), seminal_1 (10.1177/0018720817743223), seminal_2 (10.1016/j.cogsys.2016.11.003). No unverifiable citations are included in the output.

name: Donmez, Birsen
recent_1: Wang, J., Li, W., Wang, Z., Ayas, S., Donmez, B., He, D., & Wu, K. (2026). DrowsyDG-Phys: Generalizable driver drowsiness estimation in conditional automated vehicles using physiological signals. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 228, 108407. DOI: 10.1016/j.aap.2026.108407
recent_1_synopsis: This paper develops a machine-learning approach that estimates driver drowsiness from physiological signals while generalizing across drivers and conditions in conditionally automated vehicles. It addresses the practical problem that drowsiness detection models often fail to transfer to new individuals, advancing robust driver-state monitoring for automated driving.
recent_2: DeGuzman, C. A., Demir, B., Ayas, S., & Donmez, B. (2026). A review of in-vehicle touchscreen safety and usability. Transportation Research Interdisciplinary Perspectives, 36, 101873. DOI: 10.1016/j.trip.2026.101873
recent_2_synopsis: A rapid review synthesizing 73 studies on the safety and usability of in-vehicle touchscreens, finding that touchscreens generally perform worse than physical or voice controls and that no consolidated design guidelines exist. It frames an evidence base intended to inform future interface design standards for distraction-resistant in-vehicle systems.
recent_3: Ayas, S., He, D., & Donmez, B. (2026). Driver state classification: Identifying high cognitive load and drowsiness through driver performance and physiology. IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems, 27(1), 1105-1115. DOI: 10.1109/tits.2025.3633499
recent_3_synopsis: This study builds classifiers that distinguish high cognitive load from drowsiness by combining driving-performance measures with physiological signals. Disentangling these two impairing states is important because they call for different interventions, and the work informs the design of adaptive driver monitoring systems.
seminal_1: Donmez, B., Boyle, L. N., & Lee, J. D. (2007). Safety implications of providing real-time feedback to distracted drivers. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 39(3), 581-590. DOI: 10.1016/j.aap.2006.10.003
seminal_1_synopsis: This driving-simulator study demonstrated that real-time feedback keyed to drivers' off-road glances can reduce engagement in in-vehicle distractions and improve attention to the road across age groups. With roughly 320 citations (Google Scholar), it is her most-cited work and a foundational demonstration that behavioral feedback can mitigate distraction in real time.
seminal_2: Donmez, B., Boyle, L. N., & Lee, J. D. (2006). The impact of distraction mitigation strategies on driving performance. Human Factors, 48(4), 785-804. DOI: 10.1518/001872006779166415
seminal_2_synopsis: Published in Human Factors, this paper evaluated how different distraction-mitigation strategies affect driving performance and how drivers accept them, distinguishing advising from locking-out approaches. With roughly 175 citations (Google Scholar), it is foundational for the design of adaptive, driver-acceptable distraction countermeasures.
expertise_overview: Donmez studies driver distraction, attention, and human-automation interaction, with a focus on how feedback and monitoring can guide safer driver behavior. Her work spans the detection and classification of impairing states such as visual-manual and cognitive distraction, drowsiness, and high workload, often combining driving-performance metrics with physiological signals and machine learning. She investigates anticipatory driving, takeover and disengagement in partially and conditionally automated vehicles, and the design of in-vehicle interfaces (including touchscreens and feedback displays). Methodologically she relies on driving simulators, naturalistic and on-road studies, eye-tracking, statistical modeling of crash and behavioral data, and questionnaire-based individual-differences research. Application domains extend beyond driving to interruptions in intensive care, supervisory control of unmanned vehicles, and decision support for transportation safety. A recurring theme is understanding individual differences in susceptibility to distraction and using that understanding to design effective, acceptable countermeasures.
notes: Identified with high confidence via Google Scholar (user=mmsNZdoAAAAJ), University of Toronto MIE/HFAST/UTTRI faculty pages, and ResearchGate; all eight anchor hf_papers in Human Factors match this profile (University of Toronto, NSERC Canada Research Chair in Human Factors and Transportation). Citation counts from Google Scholar as of June 2026 (total ~4,600; h-index 39). All cited DOIs verified via Crossref (HTTP 200, matching titles) on 2026-06-17: recent papers 10.1016/j.aap.2026.108407, 10.1016/j.trip.2026.101873, 10.1109/tits.2025.3633499 (TITS article carries a 2026 issue date in Crossref though listed as 2025 online); seminal papers 10.1016/j.aap.2006.10.003 and 10.1518/001872006779166415. No fabricated DOIs; no unverifiable citations.

name: Dorneich, Michael C.
recent_1: Sanaei, M., Gilbert, S. B., Perron, A. J., Dorneich, M. C., & Kelly, J. W. (2024). An examination of scene complexity's role in cybersickness. Ergonomics, 68(10), 1635-1646. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2024.2427862
recent_1_synopsis: A between-subjects VR experiment (44 participants playing a Pendulum Chair game) tested whether scene complexity, manipulated via optic flow and scene familiarity, drives cybersickness. Contrary to expectation, cybersickness did not differ significantly between simple and complex scenes, suggesting that optic flow and familiarity interact with other factors and are unreliable as standalone predictors. The work informs standardized assessment of cybersickness in immersive systems.
recent_2: Tan, A., Dorneich, M. C., & Cotos, E. (2025). Developing and evaluating the fidelity of virtual reality-artificial intelligence (VR-AI) environment for situated learning. Frontiers in Virtual Reality, 6. DOI: 10.3389/frvir.2025.1587768
recent_2_synopsis: Describes the design and evaluation of an immersive VR environment populated by AI-driven agents that simulates academic conference poster sessions for practicing research communication, with real-time feedback to learners. The authors operationalize and assess fidelity across four dimensions: physical, functional, psychological, and social. The paper connects adaptive/intelligent agent design to situated, experiential learning environments.
recent_3: Dorneich, M. C., et al. (2026). Adaptive Decision-Making Under Pressure: Effects of Control Mode-Cognitive Resource Alignment on Performance Within the Contextual Control Model. Human Factors, 68(5), 599-616. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261418892
recent_3_synopsis: Applies the Contextual Control Model (COCOM) to examine how alignment between an operator's control mode and available cognitive resources affects decision-making performance under pressure. The study extends cognitive-engineering theory on adaptive control to high-tempo, stressful task settings. Published in Human Factors (2026); cited once as of June 2026.
seminal_1: Mehta, R. K., et al. & Dorneich, M. C. (2019). Shoulder Muscular Fatigue From Static Posture Concurrently Reduces Cognitive Attentional Resources. Human Factors, 62(4), 589-602. DOI: 10.1177/0018720819852509
seminal_1_synopsis: Demonstrates that localized shoulder muscular fatigue from sustained static posture concurrently depletes cognitive attentional resources, establishing a measurable physical-to-cognitive coupling relevant to ergonomics and neuroergonomics. With roughly 24 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), it is among Dorneich's most-cited recent Human Factors journal papers and is foundational to his line of work linking physical workload, fatigue, and cognitive capacity in operational settings.
seminal_2: Smith, A. & Dorneich, M. C., et al. (2024). Effects of a Passive Back-Support Exosuit on Postural Control and Cognitive Performance During a Fatigue-Inducing Posture Maintenance Task. Human Factors, 66(11), 2451-2467. DOI: 10.1177/00187208231221890
seminal_2_synopsis: Evaluates how a passive back-support exosuit affects both postural control and concurrent cognitive performance during fatiguing posture maintenance, showing that wearable assistive devices can shift the physical-cognitive resource trade-off. With roughly 17 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), it is one of his most-cited recent journal papers and exemplifies his integration of exoskeleton/wearable ergonomics with cognitive-resource measurement.
expertise_overview: Dorneich researches joint human-machine systems that keep people effective in complex, high-stress environments such as aviation, military, robotic, and space operations. Core interests include adaptive automation and adaptive interfaces that tailor assistance to a user's real-time cognitive state, augmented cognition, and neuroergonomics (cognitive state estimation, EEG-based monitoring, and physical-cognitive resource coupling). His methods span controlled human-subjects experiments, cognitive state assessment, model-based and cognitive-engineering frameworks (e.g., the Contextual Control Model), and design of decision-support and interactive learning systems. Recent work emphasizes virtual and augmented reality, AI-driven agents for situated learning, cybersickness, and wearable/exosuit ergonomics and their effects on cognition. Application domains include aerospace, construction, team tutoring, building control, and data-driven sustainability. He measures both performance and cognitive/physiological cost to understand the dual benefits and risks of adaptive systems.
notes: Identification confidence: HIGH. Confirmed as Michael C. Dorneich, Morrill Professor, Dept. of Industrial and Manufacturing Systems Engineering, Iowa State University; Ph.D. Industrial Engineering, Univ. of Illinois (1999); prior Honeywell Laboratories (1999-2012); Associate Editor, IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems. Sources: ISU faculty/CV pages (imse.iastate.edu/dorneich, engineering.iastate.edu/people/profile/dorneich), Google Scholar (user OIrwoyAAAAAJ), ResearchGate, LinkedIn. name: Finomore, Victor S.
recent_1: Rezai, A. R., Thompson-Lake, D. G. Y., D'Haese, P.-F., Meyer, N., Ranjan, M., Farmer, D., Finomore, V. S., Hodder, S. L., Carpenter, J. S., & Mahoney, J. J. (2025). Focused ultrasound neuromodulation: Exploring a novel treatment for severe opioid use disorder. Biological Psychiatry, 98(1), 56–64. DOI: 10.1016/j.biopsych.2025.01.001
recent_1_synopsis: This open-label safety-and-feasibility study delivered low-intensity transcranial focused ultrasound to the bilateral nucleus accumbens in patients with severe opioid (and co-occurring) use disorders, tracking craving, substance use, and neurological safety over a 90-day follow-up. It positions noninvasive neuromodulation as a candidate adjunct to behavioral treatment for addiction, with Finomore contributing the human-performance and neuro-monitoring expertise.
recent_2: Mahoney, J. J., Thompson-Lake, D. G. Y., Ranjan, M., Marton, J. L., Carpenter, J. S., Finomore, V. S., Hodder, S. L., & Rezai, A. R. (2025). A case report of focused ultrasound neuromodulation of the bilateral nucleus accumbens for methamphetamine use disorder. Experimental and Clinical Psychopharmacology. DOI: 10.1037/pha0000793
recent_2_synopsis: A first-in-kind case report applying focused-ultrasound neuromodulation of the bilateral nucleus accumbens to methamphetamine use disorder, a condition with no FDA-approved pharmacotherapy. It demonstrates the precision targeting of reward neurocircuitry as a translational neuroergonomic intervention.
recent_3: Greenlee, E. T., Hess, L. J., Simpson, B. D., & Finomore, V. S. (2024). Vigilance to spatialized auditory displays: Initial assessment of performance and workload. Human Factors, 66(12), 2627–2640. DOI: 10.1177/00187208221139744
recent_3_synopsis: Using a spatialized (3-D) auditory display, this vigilance study measured detection performance and subjective workload to assess whether spatial audio aids sustained-attention monitoring tasks. It extends classic vigilance-decrement research into the auditory and communications-display domain central to Finomore's military human-factors work.
seminal_1: Finomore, V. S., Matthews, G., Shaw, T. H., & Warm, J. S. (2009). Predicting vigilance: A fresh look at an old problem. Ergonomics, 52(7), 791–808. DOI: 10.1080/00140130802641627
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper revisited the long-standing problem of forecasting who will sustain attention under monotonous monitoring conditions, integrating cognitive, individual-difference, and workload predictors of vigilance performance and stress. With roughly 160 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2026), it is one of Finomore's most influential works and a touchstone for the resource-theory account of vigilance.
seminal_2: Sawyer, B. D., Finomore, V. S., Calvo, A. A., & Hancock, P. A. (2014). Google Glass: A driver distraction cause or cure? Human Factors, 56(7), 1307–1321. DOI: 10.1177/0018720814555723
seminal_2_synopsis: An early controlled driving-simulator evaluation of texting via a head-mounted wearable (Google Glass) versus a smartphone, showing the wearable mitigated—but did not eliminate—distraction-related performance costs. With roughly 130 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2026), it is a foundational empirical reference for wearable-display distraction and remains his most-cited Human Factors article.
expertise_overview: Finomore studies vigilance and sustained attention, with particular emphasis on the workload, stress, and cognitive-resource costs of prolonged monitoring tasks. A core thread is the design and evaluation of auditory, spatial-audio, multimodal, and communications displays to support detection performance in demanding operational environments. His neuroergonomics work uses physiological and neuroimaging measures (e.g., cerebral blood-flow velocity, transcranial Doppler, EEG) to index cognitive state and operator effectiveness. He also examines human-machine teaming and trust in automation, including studies of automated driving and parking. More recently his research has expanded into applied clinical neuroscience, applying transcranial focused-ultrasound neuromodulation to substance use disorders and brain-barrier modulation.

name: Fischer, Steven L.
recent_1: Davidson, J. B., Larson, D. J., Li, J. X., & Fischer, S. L. (2025). Validating force-estimating insoles for calculating centre of pressure and vertical ground reaction forces during occupational tasks. Ergonomics, 1-11. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2025.2536545
recent_1_synopsis: This study assessed whether commercially available force-estimating insoles can serve as a mobile, field-deployable substitute for in-ground force plates when measuring vertical ground reaction force and centre of pressure during occupational tasks. The authors quantified the accuracy and limitations of the insole estimates against laboratory force-plate references, providing practical guidance on where wearable load-sensing instrumentation is and is not adequate for ergonomic exposure assessment outside the lab.
recent_2: Armstrong, D. P., & Fischer, S. L. (2025). Evaluating the prospective benefit of considering movement variability in ergonomic risk assessment. Theoretical Issues in Ergonomics Science, 26(4), 544-563. DOI: 10.1080/1463922X.2025.2477148
recent_2_synopsis: This paper examined whether incorporating trial-to-trial movement variability, rather than relying on a single representative posture or motion sample, improves the prospective predictive value of ergonomic risk assessments. It argues that variability carries information about injury risk that conventional single-snapshot assessment methods discard, with implications for how repetitive occupational tasks should be sampled and scored.
recent_3: Davidson, J. B., & Fischer, S. L. (2024). Predicting preferred motorcycle riding postures to support human factors/ergonomic trade-off analyses within a multi-objective optimisation-based digital human model. Ergonomics, 68(3), 359-373. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2024.2329694
recent_3_synopsis: This work developed a multi-objective optimisation-based digital human model to predict the postures riders prefer when interacting with new motorcycle geometry, enabling early-stage human factors/ergonomic trade-off analyses before physical prototypes exist. By balancing competing comfort and biomechanical objectives, the model supports designers in evaluating how vehicle-package decisions shape predicted occupant posture and accommodation.
seminal_1: McFarland, T., & Fischer, S. (2019). Considerations for Industrial Use: A Systematic Review of the Impact of Active and Passive Upper Limb Exoskeletons on Physical Exposures. IISE Transactions on Occupational Ergonomics and Human Factors, 7(3-4), 322-347. DOI: 10.1080/24725838.2019.1684399
seminal_1_synopsis: This systematic review synthesized the evidence on how active and passive upper-limb industrial exoskeletons alter workers' physical exposures, reporting substantial reductions in shoulder muscle activity during overhead and sustained-arm tasks while flagging potential exposure shifts to other body regions. With roughly 170 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2025), it is Fischer's most-cited work and is widely treated as a foundational reference for evaluating occupational exoskeletons before workplace deployment.
seminal_2: Chan, V. C. H., Ross, G. B., Clouthier, A. L., Fischer, S. L., & Graham, R. B. (2022). The role of machine learning in the primary prevention of work-related musculoskeletal disorders: A scoping review. Applied Ergonomics, 98, 103574. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2021.103574
seminal_2_synopsis: This scoping review mapped how machine learning has been applied to the primary prevention of work-related musculoskeletal disorders, screening thousands of records to identify roughly 130 relevant studies and categorizing their use for intervention development and risk-factor identification. With approximately 145 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2025), it is foundational in framing the emerging intersection of data-driven methods and occupational injury prevention.
expertise_overview: Fischer's research centers on occupational biomechanics and ergonomics, with a primary focus on understanding and enhancing physical performance and injury prevention in physically demanding work. Core topics include manual materials handling and lifting movement strategy, physical employment (pre/post-hire) testing for demanding occupations such as paramedics and firefighters, and the biomechanical evaluation of assistive technologies including upper-limb exoskeletons. Methodologically, he combines motion capture and electromyography with force measurement, digital human modeling, and multi-objective optimization to predict postures and quantify musculoskeletal load. A recurring theme is movement variability and individual factors (e.g., arm and leg strength) that shape how workers perform occupational tasks. His work increasingly incorporates machine learning and wearable sensing (e.g., force-estimating insoles) to extend rigorous exposure assessment from the laboratory into the field.

name: Fischhoff, Baruch
recent_1: Mohan, D., Chang, C.-C. H., Fischhoff, B., Elmer, J., Rak, K. J., Barnes, J. L., Peitzman, A. B., Bendesky, B., Carr, C., Chapman, A. C., Forsythe, R. M., Guyette, F. X., Hynes, A. recent_1_synopsis: This randomized clinical trial tested whether a serious video game could improve trauma surgeons' adherence to triage guidelines at non-trauma centers. The game-based deliberate practice intervention significantly increased guideline-consistent decision-making, demonstrating that behavioral decision tools can close the gap between clinical knowledge and real-world performance.
recent_2: Krishnamurti, T., White, G., Dewitt, B., Mosley, E., & Fischhoff, B. (2025). Public Understanding of Risk and Benefit of Mifepristone. JAMA Network Open, 8(2), e2460236. DOI: 10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2024.60236
recent_2_synopsis: This randomized trial examined how well the public understands the risks and benefits of mifepristone, a medication at the center of reproductive health policy debates, and tested communication interventions. The study applies Fischhoff's risk communication framework to a high-stakes, politically salient health context, showing how evidence-based communication design can improve public understanding.
recent_3: Yang, Y., Wong-Parodi, G., & Fischhoff, B. (2025). Visual displays for communicating multiple uncertain forecasts. Decision, 12(3), 268-285. DOI: 10.1037/dec0000260
recent_3_synopsis: This study examined how different visual display formats affect people's comprehension and use of probabilistic forecasts when multiple uncertain predictions must be communicated simultaneously. It connects foundational work on calibration and risk communication to applied challenges in climate and weather forecasting, informing best practices for public-facing uncertainty communication.
seminal_1: Fischhoff, B. (1975). Hindsight is not equal to foresight: The effect of outcome knowledge on judgment under uncertainty. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, 1(3), 288-299. DOI: 10.1037/0096-1523.1.3.288
seminal_1_synopsis: This landmark paper introduced the concept of hindsight bias — the tendency for people who know an outcome to believe they would have predicted it — through a series of controlled experiments showing that outcome knowledge systematically distorts retrospective probability judgments. With approximately 4,200 citations (Google Scholar), it founded an entire research field and has profound implications for how we evaluate expert forecasters, legal fact-finders, and policy analysts. The paper remains one of the most-cited works in judgment and decision-making research.
seminal_2: Fischhoff, B., Slovic, P., Lichtenstein, S., Read, S., & Combs, B. (1978). How safe is safe enough? A psychometric study of attitudes towards technological risks and benefits. Policy Sciences, 9(2), 127-152. DOI: 10.1007/BF00143739
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper established the psychometric paradigm for studying risk perception, using factor-analytic methods to show that public risk judgments are structured by dimensions such as 'dread' and 'unknown risk' rather than by objective probability-times-magnitude calculations. With approximately 4,800 citations (Google Scholar), it is Fischhoff's most-cited work and provided the empirical foundation for decades of research on risk communication, technology acceptance, and regulatory policy. It fundamentally reframed risk as a social and psychological construct, not merely a technical quantity.
expertise_overview: Baruch Fischhoff studies how people perceive, communicate, and act on risk and uncertainty, spanning basic judgment and decision-making research and applied policy domains. His foundational contributions include the psychometric study of risk perception, hindsight bias, calibration of subjective probability, and the elicitation of values and preferences for use in policy analysis. He works extensively on risk communication — designing and evaluating messages that convey probabilistic and uncertain information to lay audiences about health, safety, technology, environment, and security. Applied domains include adolescent risk-taking, pandemic preparedness, cybersecurity (phishing and social bot detection), climate and energy decision-making, and medical decision support. Methodologically, his work combines laboratory experiments, randomized trials, and survey methods with normative decision theory. A recurring theme is supporting people — individuals and societies — in making better decisions under uncertainty when the stakes are high and information is incomplete.

name: Garcia, Maria-Gabriela
recent_1: Garcia, M.-G., Espoz, M., Albuja, C., Yañez, R., Arauz, P. G., & Martin, B. J. (2026). Biomechanical and physiological effects of a passive upper-body exoskeleton during stair ascent and descent. PLOS One, 21(2), e0343438. DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0343438
recent_1_synopsis: This study extended the team's exoskeleton research to a more challenging locomotor task by evaluating how a passive upper-body (shoulder-support) exoskeleton alters joint kinematics, lower-limb muscle activity, and physiological load while ascending and descending stairs. The work probes whether load-carrying assistive devices designed for level walking still provide net benefit, or instead introduce gait disturbances, on inclines and steps. Findings inform safe deployment of passive exoskeletons for occupational material handling in multi-level environments.
recent_2: Garcia, M.-G., Yañez, R., Espoz, M., Albuja, C., Arauz, P. G., & Martin, B. J. (2025). Biomechanical and physiological effects of an upper-body exoskeleton during simulated load-carrying on an inclined surface. PLOS One, 20(6), e0325230. DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0325230
recent_2_synopsis: Garcia and colleagues measured whole-body kinematics, leg muscle activation, and physiological responses while participants carried loads on an inclined walkway with and without a passive upper-body exoskeleton. The study addresses how slope interacts with assistive-device support, a condition common in industrial and logistics settings. Results characterize the trade-offs between reduced upper-body effort and possible redistribution of demand to the lower limbs.
recent_3: Garcia, M.-G., Arauz, P. G., Alvarez, D., Encalada, J., Vega, D., Baldo, P., & Martin, B. J. (2024). It establishes the baseline (level-surface) reference for the team's subsequent inclined-surface and stair studies. The work contributes evidence on whether passive assistive devices reduce perceived exertion without compromising movement quality during occupational load carrying.
seminal_1: Garcia, M.-G., Läubli, T., & Martin, B. J. (2018). Muscular and Vascular Issues Induced by Prolonged Standing With Different Work–Rest Cycles With Active or Passive Breaks. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 60(6), 806-821. DOI: 10.1177/0018720818769261
seminal_1_synopsis: Approximately 27 citations (Crossref, as of 2026-06-17). This study compared muscular fatigue and lower-leg vascular/edema outcomes under different work-rest cycles using active versus passive breaks during prolonged standing. It is foundational because it provides direct experimental evidence on how break scheduling and break type modulate the physiological cost of standing work, directly informing ergonomic guidelines for standing-intensive occupations.
seminal_2: Garcia, M.-G., Wall, R., Steinhilber, B., Läubli, T., & Martin, B. J. (2016). Long-Lasting Changes in Muscle Twitch Force During Simulated Work While Standing or Walking. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 58(8), 1117-1127. DOI: 10.1177/0018720816669444
seminal_2_synopsis: Approximately 25 citations (Crossref, as of 2026-06-17). This work demonstrated that prolonged simulated standing work induces objectively measurable, long-lasting reductions in muscle twitch force (an electrically evoked, effort-independent fatigue marker), and contrasted standing with walking. It is influential for introducing a sensitive, objective fatigue measure to occupational standing research and for showing that fatigue effects persist well beyond the work period, with implications for recovery and injury risk.
expertise_overview: Maria-Gabriela Garcia's research centers on occupational ergonomics and human biomechanics, with a long-standing focus on the physiological consequences of prolonged standing and walking work, including muscle fatigue, lower-limb venous pooling, and edema. She specializes in objective fatigue assessment using techniques such as electrically evoked muscle twitch force, surface electromyography, and motion capture. A major recent thrust evaluates wearable interventions for occupational strain, including compression stockings and, prominently, passive upper-body exoskeletons assessed across level, inclined, and stair conditions. She also studies ergonomic and well-being factors of home-office work (notably during COVID-19) and applies biomechanical analysis to sports populations such as weightlifters and footballers. Her work bridges laboratory physiology and applied workplace design, aiming to reduce musculoskeletal and vascular risk for standing- and handling-intensive jobs.
