name: Gorman, Jamie C.
recent_1: Zhou, S., Duan, W., Yin, X., Scalia, M., Hao, R., Weng, N., Funke, G., Tolston, M., Freeman, G., Schelble, B., Gorman, J. C., & McNeese, N. (2026). The spread of trust and distrust in human-AI teams. Applied Ergonomics, 130, 104648. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2025.104648
recent_1_synopsis: This study examines how trust and distrust spread among human and AI teammates within human-AI teams, investigating how these dynamics affect team performance outcomes. Findings reveal that mismatches between communicative and behavioral trust-distrust spreading can paradoxically improve performance, and that team composition (human-majority vs. AI-majority) moderates how trust and distrust contagion affect team outcomes. The work advances understanding of interpersonal trust dynamics in increasingly common hybrid human-AI work configurations.
recent_2: Duan, W., Flathmann, C., McNeese, N., Scalia, M. J., Zhang, R., Gorman, J., Freeman, G., Zhou, S., Hauptman, A. I., & Yin, X. (2025). Trusting autonomous teammates in human-AI teams - A literature review. Proceedings of the 2025 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems, pp. 1-23. DOI: 10.1145/3706598.3713527
recent_2_synopsis: This systematic literature review synthesizes research on how humans develop, calibrate, and maintain trust in autonomous AI teammates within human-AI team contexts. The review maps the antecedents, dynamics, and consequences of trust across varied human-AI team configurations, identifying key gaps in current empirical and theoretical understanding. It provides a comprehensive framework that positions trust calibration as central to effective human-autonomy teaming.
recent_3: Gorman, J. C. (2024). Simultaneous hypotheses in cognitive agents: Commentary on Paxton, Necaise et al., and the dynamical hypothesis in cognitive science. Topics in Cognitive Science. DOI: 10.1111/tops.12772
recent_3_synopsis: This theoretical commentary engages with the dynamical hypothesis in cognitive science, arguing that cognitive agents—including human teams—can operate under simultaneous, competing hypotheses rather than sequentially resolving uncertainty. Gorman applies insights from team coordination dynamics and enaction theory to extend the dynamical systems framework to team-level cognition. The piece situates interaction-dominant dynamics within broader debates about the nature of cognitive representation.
seminal_1: Cooke, N. J., Gorman, J. C., Myers, C. W., & Duran, J. L. (2013). Interactive team cognition. Cognitive Science, 37(2), 255-285. DOI: 10.1111/cogs.12009
seminal_1_synopsis: This highly influential paper (~975 citations as of June 2026, Google Scholar) introduces Interactive Team Cognition (ITC) theory, arguing that team cognition is fundamentally an activity rather than a static property or product, that it must be measured at the team level, and that it is inseparable from the task context in which it occurs. ITC challenged prevailing mental-model-based approaches by locating cognition in the dynamic interactions between teammates. The framework has become a foundational reference for researchers studying team coordination, distributed cognition, and human-autonomy teaming.
seminal_2: Gorman, J. C., Cooke, N. J., & Amazeen, P. G. (2010). Training adaptive teams. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 52(2), 295-307. DOI: 10.1177/0018720810371689
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper (~284 citations as of June 2026, Google Scholar) reports an experiment comparing three training approaches—cross-training, perturbation training, and procedural training—for building team adaptability in a simulated uninhabited air vehicle task with collaborators Nancy Cooke and Polemnia Amazeen. Perturbation training, which intentionally disrupts coordination patterns during acquisition, produced superior adaptation to novel situations compared to established methods. The study introduced the perturbation-training paradigm that has since influenced both team-training practice and the theoretical understanding of team coordination dynamics as a malleable, nonlinear system.
expertise_overview: Jamie C. Gorman is a Professor of Human Systems Engineering and Director of the Center for Human, Artificial Intelligence, and Robot Teaming (CHART) at Arizona State University. His research applies dynamical systems theory and nonlinear methods to the measurement and modeling of team coordination, with a particular focus on how real-time interaction patterns predict team performance and adaptability. A central thread of his work is the quantification of team coordination dynamics using entropy, recurrence quantification analysis, and related time-series techniques applied to communication, control, and behavioral data streams. Gorman has extended this framework from all-human teams to human-autonomy teaming (HAT), studying how synthetic agents affect coordination flexibility, trust calibration, and collective performance under degraded conditions. His domain contexts include military command-and-control, remotely piloted aircraft systems, space missions, medical teams, and educational settings. He collaborates extensively with Nancy Cooke, Polemnia Amazeen, and colleagues in the CHART network on both foundational theory and applied training interventions.

name: Greenlee, Eric T.
recent_1: Hess, L. J., & Greenlee, E. T. (2024). Auditory vigilance task performance and cerebral hemodynamics: effects of spatial uncertainty. Experimental Brain Research, 242(7), 1787-1795. DOI: 10.1007/s00221-024-06857-0

recent_3: McGarry, A., Neilson, B., Brown, T., Strong, R., Greenlee, E. T., Klein, M. I., & Coyne, J. (2023). An investigation of cardiac vagal tone over time and its relation to vigilance performance: a growth curve modeling approach. Frontiers in Neuroergonomics, 4, 1244658. DOI: 10.3389/fnrgo.2023.1244658
recent_3_synopsis: Using growth-curve modeling, this study tracked cardiac vagal tone over time and related its dynamics to vigilance performance. It contributes a physiological (autonomic) index of the temporal course of sustained attention, advancing the neuroergonomic toolkit for tracking and potentially predicting vigilance decrements.
seminal_1: Greenlee, E. T., DeLucia, P. R., & Newton, D. C. (2018). Driver Vigilance in Automated Vehicles: Hazard Detection Failures Are a Matter of Time. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 60(4), 465-476. DOI: 10.1177/0018720818761711
seminal_1_synopsis: This study demonstrated that drivers monitoring an automated vehicle suffer a vigilance decrement, with hazard-detection failures increasing as a function of time on task. With roughly 143 citations (Human Factors metadata / Google Scholar, June 2026), it is Greenlee's most-cited work and a foundational reference establishing that prolonged passive monitoring of automation degrades drivers' ability to detect rare hazards.
seminal_2: Greenlee, E. T., DeLucia, P. R., & Newton, D. C. (2018). Driver Vigilance in Automated Vehicles: Effects of Demands on Hazard Detection Performance. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 61(3), 474-487. DOI: 10.1177/0018720818802095
seminal_2_synopsis: A follow-up study showing that increased task demands during automated-vehicle monitoring further impair hazard detection, clarifying the conditions under which the driver vigilance decrement is most severe. With roughly 39 citations (Human Factors metadata / Google Scholar, June 2026), it is a foundational companion piece deepening understanding of how workload and demand shape supervisory monitoring failures in automated driving.
expertise_overview: Greenlee studies vigilance and sustained attention-particularly the vigilance decrement, the temporal decline in detecting rare, critical signals during prolonged monitoring. A central thread is the neuroergonomics of vigilance, using physiological measures such as transcranial Doppler sonography of cerebral blood-flow velocity, cardiac/autonomic indices, and workload and stress assessment to test resource-depletion accounts of attention. He examines vigilance across sensory modalities (visual, auditory/spatialized auditory, and tactile) and probes supramodal versus modality-specific resource depletion. A major application domain is driver vigilance in automated vehicles, where he has shown that monitoring the roadway for hazards during automation produces a time-based decrement in hazard detection. His methods combine controlled laboratory vigilance paradigms, driving simulation, psychophysiological monitoring, and growth-curve and other quantitative modeling of performance over time.

name: Groen, Eric L.
recent_1: Vlaskamp, D., Pollitt, A., Blundell, J., Landman, A., Groen, E. L., van Paassen, M. M., Stroosma, O., & Mulder, M. (2026). Cabin crew startle and surprise: occurrence and impact. Cognition, Technology & Work. DOI: 10.1007/s10111-026-00866-5
recent_1_synopsis: This study investigates the prevalence and operational impact of startle and surprise events among cabin crew in aviation contexts. It extends prior work on flight-deck startle (piloting) to cabin operations, examining how unexpected events affect crew response and performance. The findings provide empirical data to support startle management training across the full aviation crew complement.
recent_2: Landman, A., Stuldreher, I. V., Van der Burg, E., Evertsen, F. W., Reuten, A. J. C., Ledegang, W. D., Houben, M. M. J., & Groen, E. L. (2025). Pilots gaze more outside while performing an auditory cognitive task. Ergonomics, 69(5), 881-891. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2025.2500526
recent_2_synopsis: Using two simulator experiments (one fixed-base with novice military pilots and one moving-base with experienced helicopter pilots), this study examines how adding an auditory cognitive task shifts pilot gaze behavior during manual flight. Results show pilots direct more gaze outside the cockpit under dual-task load, with implications for cockpit design and pilot workload management. The work contributes to understanding attention allocation and task interference in aviation.
recent_3: Vlaskamp, D., Pollitt, A., Blundell, J., Landman, A., Groen, E. L., van Paassen, M. M., Stroosma, O., & Mulder, M. (2025). Startle and surprise in helicopter operations: reported prevalence and application of mitigation strategies. Cognition, Technology & Work, 27(3), 579-590. DOI: 10.1007/s10111-025-00811-y
recent_3_synopsis: This paper surveys helicopter pilots to characterize the prevalence of startle and surprise events during rotary-wing operations and how crews apply mitigation strategies. It adapts a conceptual framework of startle and surprise (developed for fixed-wing aviation) to the specific operational challenges of helicopters, including low-altitude and confined-area flight. Findings inform training recommendations tailored to helicopter crews.
seminal_1: Bles, W., Bos, J. E., de Graaf, B., Groen, E., & Wertheim, A. H. (1998). Motion sickness: only one provocative conflict? Brain Research Bulletin, 47(5), 481-487. DOI: 10.1016/S0361-9230(98)00115-4
seminal_1_synopsis: This foundational paper (approximately 494 citations as of June 2026 per Google Scholar) proposes a unifying framework for motion sickness: a single type of sensory conflict -- between the expected and the actual sensory input derived from an internal model of the body's orientation -- is necessary and sufficient to explain all forms of motion sickness. By formalizing the 'subjective vertical conflict' hypothesis, it replaced more diffuse sensory rearrangement theories and became the dominant explanatory model in the field. The mathematical formulation provided the basis for predictive models of motion sickness in simulation, automotive, and maritime contexts.
seminal_2: Bos, J. E., Bles, W., & Groen, E. L. (2008). A theory on visually induced motion sickness. Displays, 29(2), 47-57. DOI: 10.1016/j.displa.2007.09.002
seminal_2_synopsis: Cited approximately 416 times (Google Scholar, June 2026), this paper extends the 1998 conflict model to explain visually induced (i.e., non-physical) motion sickness -- a phenomenon increasingly relevant with the proliferation of display technologies, virtual reality, and vehicle HMIs. The theory describes how visual motion information interacts with the vestibular system to generate a subjective vertical conflict that produces nausea and discomfort even without physical motion. Its mechanistic, predictive account has been highly influential for display system design, simulation validation, and autonomous vehicle interior ergonomics.
expertise_overview: Eric L. Groen's research centers on human spatial orientation, vestibular physiology, and their applied consequences in aviation and simulator environments. He investigates how pilots perceive and misperceive their own motion and attitude -- particularly under spatial disorientation, the vestibular Coriolis illusion, the 'leans' illusion, and otolith-mediated tilt-translation ambiguity -- using motion-base simulators and psychophysical paradigms. A major strand of his work examines the cognitive and behavioral sequelae of spatial disorientation, including interference with pilot decision-making, attention, and workload during flight. He has made seminal contributions to the theory and modeling of motion sickness (both physical and visually induced), formalizing the subjective vertical conflict model that now underpins predictive algorithms used in transportation and display design. His applied work includes development of the TNO Spatial Disorientation Investigation Tool for accident analysis, galvanic vestibular stimulation as a low-cost illusion induction method in fixed-base simulators, and pilot training for unexpected events including startle, surprise, and upset recovery. More recently his research group has expanded into physiological monitoring (gaze behavior, brain-computer interfaces, hypoxia detection) and extended startle-and-surprise research from fixed-wing cockpits to helicopter and cabin-crew contexts.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. Sources: (1) ResearchGate profile (https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Eric-Groen-2) listing TNO (Delft) affiliation and Cranfield University visiting professorship; (2) Google Scholar profile (https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=DsySLQMAAAAJ) showing 3,435 total citations and h-index 28 as of June 2026; (3) TNO publications repository; (4) six pre-verified Human Factors DOIs provided as ground-truth anchor. Citation counts for seminal papers sourced from Google Scholar, June 2026. All five DOIs in this record verified via Crossref API (HTTP 200, matching titles and authors): 10.1016/S0361-9230(98)00115-4 (Bles et al. 1998), 10.1016/j.displa.2007.09.002 (Bos et al. 2008), 10.1007/s10111-026-00866-5 (Vlaskamp et al. 2026), 10.1080/00140139.2025.2500526 (Landman et al. 2025), 10.1007/s10111-025-00811-y (Vlaskamp et al. 2025). No unverifiable citations.

name: Grundgeiger, Tobias
recent_1: Grundgeiger, T., Maurer, L., Ramon Hölzing, C., & Happel, O. (2026). AI-Supported Electrocardiogram Interpretation: The Effect of Support Presentation on Diagnostic Accuracy, Psychological Need Satisfaction, and Diagnosis Time. Proceedings of the 2026 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems, 1-15. DOI: 10.1145/3772318.3790619
recent_1_synopsis: An experimental study testing how the presentation format of AI decision support affects clinicians interpreting electrocardiograms, measuring diagnostic accuracy, time to diagnosis, and self-determination-theory-based psychological need satisfaction. It matters because it links the design of AI support to both performance and the user's motivational experience, informing how to embed AI assistance in safety-critical diagnostic work without undermining clinician autonomy.
recent_2: Bahnsen, K. L., & Grundgeiger, T. (2026). AR-Cues Change Users' Strategy for Dealing with Deferrable Interruptions. Proceedings of the 2026 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems, 1-17. DOI: 10.1145/3772318.3790673
recent_2_synopsis: This study examines how augmented-reality cues influence the strategies people adopt when handling interruptions that can be deferred, showing that AR cues shift how and when users choose to switch tasks. It extends Grundgeiger's interruption-management research from task resumption to proactive interruption-handling strategy, with implications for designing AR support in attention-demanding work.
recent_3: Hufana Ambe, A., Salisbury, I., Grundgeiger, T., Bodnar, D., Rothwell, S., Brown, N., & Matthews, B. (2026). Out of Emergency: How Doctors Navigate Jurisdictional Seams in Emergency Care Referrals. Proceedings of the 2026 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems, 1-17. DOI: 10.1145/3772318.3791645
recent_3_synopsis: A qualitative field study of how emergency-care physicians coordinate and hand off patients across the organizational and jurisdictional boundaries (seams) involved in referrals. It surfaces breakdowns and workarounds in cross-team coordination and derives implications for designing information-support tools that span institutional boundaries in emergency medicine.
seminal_1: Grundgeiger, T., & Sanderson, P. (2009). Interruptions in healthcare: Theoretical views. International Journal of Medical Informatics, 78(5), 293-307. DOI: 10.1016/j.ijmedinf.2008.10.001
seminal_1_synopsis: A widely cited review (~272 citations, Semantic Scholar, June 2026) that critically examines the evidence linking interruptions to errors and adverse events in critical care and medication settings, and reframes the topic through cognitive theory (memory-for-goals, prospective memory, distributed cognition). It is foundational because it moved the field beyond simply counting interruptions toward theory-driven accounts of why and when interruptions are harmful, shaping a decade of healthcare interruption research.
seminal_2: Grundgeiger, T., Sanderson, P., MacDougall, H. G., & Venkatesh, B. (2010). Interruption management in the intensive care unit: Predicting resumption times and assessing distributed support. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Applied, 16(4), 317-334. DOI: 10.1037/a0021912
seminal_2_synopsis: A combined field-observation and simulator study (~168 citations, Semantic Scholar, June 2026) that applies memory-for-goals and prospective-memory theory to predict how long ICU nurses take to resume interrupted tasks and tests whether environmental (distributed) cues support resumption. It is foundational for operationalizing interruption-resumption theory in a real clinical setting and for demonstrating how external cues can scaffold memory in high-tempo care.
expertise_overview: Grundgeiger studies applied cognition in safety-critical domains, with a core focus on distractions and interruptions, prospective memory, and task resumption. A second major thread is visual attention distribution and situation awareness, often operationalized through the SEEV (salience, effort, expectancy, value) model and eye-tracking in clinical scenarios. He designs and evaluates cognitive aids, head-worn/augmented-reality displays, and AI-based decision and documentation support to improve patient safety and user experience. Methodologically he spans full-scale anesthesiology and emergency simulations, controlled experiments, field observation, eye tracking, and experience-based/user-centered design, including self-determination-theory measures of need satisfaction. His primary application domains are anesthesiology, intensive and emergency care, and cardiac-arrest resuscitation. Recent work increasingly addresses human-AI teaming and the design of AI agents and AR cues for healthcare teams.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. Sources: University of Würzburg Institute for Human-Computer-Media / Psychologische Ergonomie faculty page, his official CV PDF (mcm.uni-wuerzburg.de, dated 09-03-26), dblp author page, ResearchGate, and Semantic Scholar. All clearly match the same person: anesthesia/healthcare human factors, interruptions, situation awareness, University of Würzburg (PhD University of Queensland). The five provided hf_papers all appear in his record. Recent papers (three CHI 2026 papers) DOIs verified via Crossref (200, titles and authors match, published date 2026-04-13). Seminal DOIs verified via Crossref (titles/authors/venue match). Citation counts (~272 for the 2009 IJMI paper; ~168 for the 2010 JEP:Applied paper) are from the Semantic Scholar API, retrieved June 2026; these are approximate and Google Scholar counts would be higher. No citations were left unverifiable -- every cited DOI resolved successfully on Crossref.

name: Hancock, P. A.
recent_1: Gruber, M. E., Hancock, P. A., Cruit, J., & Kochan, J. A. (2026). Unraveling the Unexpected: How Pilots Can Successfully Manage Unexpected Events. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society. Advance online publication. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261449578
recent_1_synopsis: Examines how airline pilots cognitively and behaviorally manage unexpected, off-nominal in-flight events, drawing on case analysis and human-factors theory to identify the strategies that distinguish successful from unsuccessful responses. The work connects Hancock's long-standing interests in stress, performance under uncertainty, and aviation safety, offering implications for pilot training and flight-deck design for resilience.
recent_2: Hancock, P. A. (2025). Isles of autonomy: the rise of intelligent technologies. Ergonomics, 68(6), 987-996. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2024.2447863
recent_2_synopsis: A conceptual/critical essay introducing the 'Isles of Autonomy' metaphor to characterize how autonomous machine systems progressively penetrate the world of human work through a series of threshold events that displace human pre-eminence. It matters as a framing piece for the human-factors community on the trajectory and societal stakes of increasing machine autonomy.
recent_3: Karwowski, W., Salvendy, G., Endsley, M., Rouse, W., Salmon, P., Stanney, K., ... Hancock, P. A., ... & Rau, P. (2025). Grand challenges for human factors and ergonomics. Theoretical Issues in Ergonomics Science, 26(4), 361-456. DOI: 10.1080/1463922X.2024.2431336
recent_3_synopsis: A large multi-author position paper articulating six grand challenges for the field (e.g., societal thinking, the future of human work in Industry 5.0, climate/sustainability, education and training, personalized health, and the metaverse) along with key research thrusts. As a coauthor, Hancock contributes to an agenda-setting roadmap intended to orient HFE research priorities for the coming decade.
seminal_1: Hancock, P. A., Billings, D. R., Schaefer, K. E., Chen, J. Y. C., de Visser, E. J., & Parasuraman, R. (2011). A Meta-Analysis of Factors Affecting Trust in Human-Robot Interaction. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 53(5), 517-527. DOI: 10.1177/0018720811417254
seminal_1_synopsis: This landmark meta-analysis quantified how human, robot, and environmental factors shape trust in human-robot interaction, synthesizing 29 studies (69 correlational and 47 experimental effect sizes) and establishing robot performance-based attributes as the dominant trust antecedents. With roughly 1,900 citations (Semantic Scholar, June 2026), it is Hancock's most-cited work and a foundational reference that effectively defined the empirical study of trust in HRI and seeded a decade of follow-on research.
seminal_2: Schaefer, K. E., Chen, J. Y. C., Szalma, J. L., & Hancock, P. A. (2016). A Meta-Analysis of Factors Influencing the Development of Trust in Automation: Implications for Understanding Autonomy in Future Systems. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 58(3), 377-400. DOI: 10.1177/0018720816634228
seminal_2_synopsis: A companion meta-analysis extending the trust framework to human-automation interaction, integrating human-, automation-, and environment-related factors to model how trust in automation develops, with strong implications for future autonomous systems. With roughly 575 citations (Semantic Scholar, June 2026), it is foundational for the trust-in-automation literature and is widely used to guide the design of calibrated, appropriately trusted automated systems.
expertise_overview: Hancock's research centers on human performance under stress, mental workload, fatigue, and vigilance/sustained attention, and on how these states govern performance in demanding sociotechnical settings. A major strand concerns trust in automation, robots, and AI, including the calibration of trust and human-automation/human-robot interaction, frequently studied through large-scale meta-analytic synthesis. He also has a long-standing program on human time perception and the psychology of time. His applied domains include aviation and driving safety, simulation and training (including VR/AR/MR), and the broader trajectory of machine autonomy and its consequences for human work. Methodologically he combines controlled experimentation on attention and stress with quantitative meta-analysis and conceptual/theoretical scholarship on the future of human factors and ergonomics. Recent work extends these themes to autonomous systems, pilot resilience to unexpected events, and field-defining agenda-setting on AI and human-centered design.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Peter A. Hancock, University of Central Florida (Department of Psychology / Institute for Simulation and Training). Confirmed via UCF faculty pages (peterhancock.ucf.edu, sciences.ucf.edu/psychology), PubMed, and consistency with all six pre-verified Human Factors papers. All six DOIs cited here were verified to resolve via Crossref (titles/authors/venues matched), and Hancock's authorship was confirmed in each Crossref author list: recent_1 (Gruber, Hancock, Cruit, Kochan), recent_2 (sole author Hancock), recent_3 (Hancock among 20 authors), seminal_1, and seminal_2. Citation counts are from Semantic Scholar API, retrieved 2026-06-17: HRI trust meta-analysis = 1906; trust-in-automation meta-analysis = 574; for reference, Trust in AI meta (2021) = 306 and VR/AR/MR meta (2020) = 431, so the two seminal picks are genuinely his most-cited works. name: Hecht, Heiko
recent_1: Kehl, L., Brietzke, A., Pham Xuan, R., & Hecht, H. (2026). Anticipatory vibrotactile cues about upcoming turns reduce motion sickness: A study with car passengers on public roads. Applied Ergonomics, 135, 104773. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104773
recent_1_synopsis: In a counterbalanced within-subjects field study, 40 passengers took two 30-minute rides on public roads while watching a movie, with seat-integrated actuators delivering anticipatory vibrotactile cues about upcoming left and right turns roughly one second before each maneuver. The cues significantly reduced reported carsickness (Fast Motion Sickness Scale), demonstrating that lateral-maneuver pre-warning alone is an effective real-world countermeasure. The work extends the team's motion-sickness mitigation research into naturalistic, on-road conditions.
recent_2: Kehl, L., Brietzke, A., Pham Xuan, R., & Hecht, H. (2026). Anticipatory vibrotactile cues mitigate motion sickness in car passengers: an implementation study on public roads. Displays, 91, 103195. DOI: 10.1016/j.displa.2025.103195
recent_2_synopsis: This implementation study tested a seat-based vibrotactile cueing system that signaled upcoming turns and acceleration/deceleration events about 0.7 seconds in advance during 30-minute public-road rides with 39 participants in a within-subjects design. Anticipatory cues significantly mitigated car sickness, and the authors show that actuators embedded in the car seat are a practical channel for conveying such cues. It demonstrates feasibility of deploying anticipatory haptic countermeasures in real vehicles for automated/passenger driving.
recent_3: Michalareas, G., Lehr, C., Grabenhorst, M., & Hecht, H. (2025). Social processing distorts physical distance perception. Scientific Reports, 15(1). DOI: 10.1038/s41598-025-89935-9
recent_3_synopsis: This study examines how social-cognitive processing biases the perception of physical interpersonal distance, linking Hecht's longstanding interest in personal-space and space perception to social factors. The findings indicate that the social meaning of another person systematically distorts judged physical distance rather than distance being read off purely from optical/geometric cues. It connects spatial perception with social cognition in a high-visibility open-access venue.
seminal_1: Keshavarz, B., & Hecht, H. (2011). Validating an Efficient Method to Quantify Motion Sickness. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 53(4), 415-426. DOI: 10.1177/0018720811403736
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper introduced and validated the Fast Motion Sickness Scale (FMS), a single-item verbal scale that allows continuous, low-burden tracking of motion sickness severity during an experiment. With roughly 870 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), it is Hecht's most-cited work and has become a standard measurement instrument across motion-sickness, simulator-sickness, and cybersickness research. Its foundational status comes from giving the field a fast, reliable, and easily administered alternative to lengthy post-hoc questionnaires, published in Human Factors itself.
seminal_2: Hecht, H., Vogt, S., & Prinz, W. (2001). Motor learning enhances perceptual judgment: a case for action-perception transfer. Psychological Research, 65(1), 3-14. DOI: 10.1007/s004260000043
seminal_2_synopsis: This study showed that practicing a timed motor action improves subsequent perceptual judgments of that same action, providing evidence for bidirectional action-perception transfer. With approximately 260 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), it is among Hecht's most influential basic-science contributions and is widely cited in the common-coding / ideomotor and embodied-cognition literatures. It is foundational for demonstrating that perception and action draw on shared representations.
expertise_overview: Heiko Hecht is an experimental psychologist whose research centers on visual and spatial perception, including pictorial and 3D space perception, perceived room geometry, slant, and interpersonal/personal space. A major strand of his work addresses motion sickness, visually induced motion sickness, and cybersickness, including measurement methodology (the Fast Motion Sickness Scale) and countermeasures such as anticipatory vibrotactile and auditory cues for carsickness in automated and passenger driving. He also studies naive/intuitive physics, time-to-contact and arrival-time estimation, and the perception of dynamic events. A further theme is the coupling of perception and action (action-perception transfer, motor expertise effects on judgment). His applied human-factors interests include driving simulators, camera-monitor systems, and the acceptance and comfort of automated transport.
notes: High-confidence identification: Heiko Hecht, Chair of Experimental Psychology, Johannes Gutenberg University Mainz (Germany); perception, motion sickness, intuitive physics, time-to-contact. Disambiguated via the supplied verified Human Factors driving/perception papers plus matching topics and co-authors (Keshavarz, Kehl, von Castell). Sources: Google Scholar (user 0nKyLkIAAAAJ; ~9,024 total citations, h-index 53), JGU Mainz faculty/publication page, ResearchGate, ScienceDirect author page. All six DOIs (2 seminal, 3 recent, plus anchors) verified to resolve via Crossref API on 2026-06-17 with matching titles/authors/venues. Citation counts (seminal_1 ~870; seminal_2 ~260) are from Google Scholar as of 2026-06-17 and are approximate. Recent papers chosen to span both applied motion-sickness work (Applied Ergonomics 2026, Displays 2026) and basic perception (Scientific Reports 2025); note the two 2026 vibrotactile papers are companion field studies. No unverifiable DOIs were included; no DOIs were invented.

name: Herdener, Nathan
recent_1: Draheim, C., Herdener, N., Rovira, E., Melick, S. R., Pak, R., Coyne, J. T., & Sibley, C. (2026). Investigating Transfer of Input Device Practice on Psychomotor Performance in an Aviation Selection Test. Human Factors, 68(1), 28–41. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251355828
recent_1_synopsis: This study tested whether brief practice on different input devices transfers to psychomotor performance in the U.S. Navy's Performance Based Measures aviation selection battery, addressing fairness concerns about candidates' prior device familiarity. The findings inform how high-stakes selection tests should account for practice and input-device effects to preserve validity.
recent_2: Draheim, C., Sibley, C., Herdener, N., Cochrane, A., Melick, S. R., Strong, K., & Coyne, J. T. (2025). High-stakes psychomotor ability assessment: a military selection case study of practice effects in airplane tracking tasks. Cognitive Research: Principles and Implications, 10(1), Article 72. DOI: 10.1186/s41235-025-00672-z
recent_2_synopsis: This case study examined practice effects in airplane-tracking psychomotor tasks used for military pilot selection, quantifying how repeated exposure changes scores and threatens the comparability of assessments across candidates. It offers guidance for designing and scoring high-stakes psychomotor ability tests that remain robust to learning effects.
recent_3: Coyne, J. T., Herdener, N., Sweet, J., Sibley, C., Brown, N., Foroughi, C., & King, T. (2020). Toward Improving the Direction Orientation Task. In Advances in Intelligent Systems and Computing (Vol. 1206, pp. 487–493). Springer. DOI: 10.1007/978-3-030-50791-6_68
recent_3_synopsis: This work evaluated and refined the Direction Orientation Task, a measure of spatial ability relevant to aviation and navigation contexts, to better capture individual differences in spatial cognition. It contributes to the development of validated spatial-ability instruments for personnel selection and human-systems research.
seminal_1: Herdener, N., Wickens, C. D., Clegg, B. A., & Smith, C. A. P. (2016). Overconfidence in Projecting Uncertain Spatial Trajectories. Human Factors, 58(6), 899–914. DOI: 10.1177/0018720816645259
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper demonstrated that people systematically underappreciate variance when predicting the future path of moving targets (e.g., hurricanes or ships), producing overconfident spatial trajectory forecasts. With roughly 22 citations, it is his most-cited first-authored work and foundational to a line of research on uncertainty perception in spatial prediction.
seminal_2: Pugh, A. J., Wickens, C. D., Herdener, N., Clegg, B. A., & Smith, C. A. P. (2018). Effect of Visualization Training on Uncertain Spatial Trajectory Predictions. Human Factors, 60(3), 324–339. DOI: 10.1177/0018720818758770
seminal_2_synopsis: This study showed that training people with visualizations of variability can improve their appreciation of uncertainty when predicting uncertain spatial trajectories. With approximately 17 citations, it is one of his most influential works and extends the spatial-uncertainty research program toward practical interventions that reduce overconfident forecasting.
expertise_overview: Nathan Herdener studies human judgment and decision making under uncertainty, with a focus on how people predict the future spatial trajectories of moving objects such as hurricanes, ships, or search-and-rescue targets. A central theme is that people insufficiently account for natural variance, leading to overconfident and miscalibrated spatial forecasts, and he investigates anchoring-and-adjustment biases, the role of mental workload, and visualization-based training and aids as mitigations. His methods are experimental and quantitative, combining controlled spatial-prediction tasks, perception-of-variance measures (e.g., scatterplot judgments), and individual-differences assessment, and he has also worked with electrophysiological measures. More recently his work centers on psychomotor and spatial-ability assessment for high-stakes personnel selection, including aviation and military pilot selection tests, examining practice effects, input-device transfer, and the validity of spatial-ability instruments. Application domains span aviation, defense/military selection, and spatial information visualization for decision support.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. The Human Factors hf_papers anchor (spatial trajectory prediction with Wickens, Clegg, and C. A. P. Smith; later aviation-selection work with Draheim, Coyne, Sibley) maps to a single researcher: an experimental psychologist formerly at Colorado State University and Oregon State University, now at Strategic Analysis Incorporated (Fort Collins, CO). Sources: SAGE/Human Factors article pages, PubMed, RocketReach professional listing, and Crossref author search. Disambiguated from unrelated 'Herdener' authors in addiction medicine/psychiatry (B. B. Quednow collaborators), ophthalmology, and materials engineering. Citation counts are from Crossref is-referenced-by-count as of 2026-06-17 (Overconfidence ~22; Visualization Training ~17); Crossref counts are typically lower than Google Scholar, so true totals may be somewhat higher. All cited DOIs (recent_1, recent_2, recent_3, seminal_1, seminal_2) were verified to resolve via the Crossref API with matching titles. The recent_1 Human Factors paper carries a 2026 print volume (68(1)), though it was published online in 2025.

name: Hsiao, Hongwei
recent_1: Hsiao, H., Kau, T. Y., & Bradtmiller, B. (2024). A cluster-based law enforcement body armor sizing system: Concept, procedure, and design practice. Applied Ergonomics, 117, 104201. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2023.104201
recent_1_synopsis: Using national anthropometric survey data on U.S. law enforcement officers, this paper develops a cluster-based sizing system to replace the inconsistent body-armor size structures (ranging from 6 to 25 sizes) found across the marketplace. It derives a rationalized nine-size system for male officers, grading vests by chest, waist, and torso length. The work matters because it provides a standardized, anthropometrically grounded basis for armor sizing that improves fit, coverage, and protection for a diverse officer population.
recent_2: Hsiao, H. (2024). Association of anthropometric characteristics of law enforcement officers with perceived ratings of fit, comfort, and pain in the use of body armor. Ergonomics, 67(4), 541-565. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2023.2232581
recent_2_synopsis: Drawing on a national study of 974 law enforcement officers, this paper quantifies how officers' body dimensions relate to their perceived body-armor fit, discomfort, and armor-caused pain, and identifies the torso measurements most influential for sizing. It links subjective wear experience to specific anthropometric drivers. The findings inform armor sizing and design choices that can reduce discomfort and improve sustained use of protective equipment.
recent_3: Hsiao, H., Kau, T. Y., Whisler, R., & Zwiener, J. (2022). Body Models of Law Enforcement Officers for Cruiser Cab Accommodation Simulation. Human Factors, 66(5), 1350-1386. DOI: 10.1177/00187208221140220
recent_3_synopsis: This study builds multivariate body models of U.S. law enforcement officers (756 male and 218 female officers, with and without duty gear) from a stratified national survey to support digital simulation of how officers fit within police cruiser cabs. The models capture the size and shape variation, including encumbrance from gear, that must be accommodated in workspace design. It provides design-ready boundary cases for evaluating cruiser layouts, ingress/egress, and reach for the LEO population.
seminal_1: Hsiao, H., Chang, J., & Simeonov, P. (2018). Preventing Emergency Vehicle Crashes: Status and Challenges of Human Factors Issues. Human Factors, 60(7), 1048-1072. DOI: 10.1177/0018720818786132
seminal_1_synopsis: This review synthesizes the human factors challenges underlying emergency-vehicle crashes (ambulances, fire apparatus, police cruisers), spanning vehicle conspicuity, driver behavior, occupant restraint, cab design, and roadway/operational factors, and lays out research and intervention priorities. With approximately 76 citations (Crossref, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is among his most-cited works and is foundational for framing emergency-vehicle safety as a systems-level human factors problem that integrates anthropometry, vehicle design, and operator performance.
seminal_2: Hsiao, H. (2013). Anthropometric Procedures for Protective Equipment Sizing and Design. Human Factors, 55(1), 6-35. DOI: 10.1177/0018720812465640
seminal_2_synopsis: This article lays out four anthropometric design approaches (univariate, bivariate/probability-distribution, multivariate, and shape-based methods) and demonstrates them using large occupational datasets (tractor operators, respirator users, firefighters, and civilian workers) to guide sizing and design of protective equipment. With roughly 48-67 citations (Crossref ~48; scispace/Google Scholar ~67, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is foundational because it provides a coherent methodological framework that bridges anthropometric data and protective-equipment design decisions across occupational populations.
expertise_overview: Occupational anthropometry and its application to the design and sizing of personal protective equipment, vehicles, and workspaces for diverse worker populations. A central methodological thread is multivariate and shape-based anthropometric modeling, including 3D body scanning, digital human modeling, and statistical body-model construction to derive design boundary cases. Application domains span law enforcement officers, firefighters, truck drivers, and emergency responders, with work on body-armor sizing, fall-arrest harness design, respirator fit, vehicle cab accommodation, and protective-equipment fit, comfort, and pain. A complementary focus is fall prevention and protection, including suspension trauma and harness fit, and the broader human factors of emergency-vehicle safety. Methods include national stratified anthropometric surveys (often via mobile data-collection laboratories), cluster analysis for sizing systems, and simulation-based accommodation assessment. The overarching aim is to translate body size and shape variability into evidence-based design standards that reduce occupational injury.

name: Hu, Yu Hen
recent_1: Sun, Y.-H., Ke, J., Fernandes, J., Chen, J., Jiang, H., & Hu, Y. H. (2025). EasyVis: a real-time 3D visualization software system for laparoscopic surgery box trainer. Updates in Surgery, 1-16. DOI: 10.1007/s13304-025-02153-w
recent_1_synopsis: This paper presents EasyVis, a real-time stereo 3D visualization system that integrates micro-cameras and light sources into laparoscopic surgical ports to provide intra-abdominal stereo vision at a chosen viewpoint. The system is validated in a laparoscopic training-box environment. It targets improved depth perception and surgical-training efficiency by giving trainees a reconstructed 3D view rather than a flat monoscopic image.
recent_2: Chen, J., Sun, Y.-H., Pickett, K. A., King, B., Hu, Y. H., & Jiang, H. (2025). A Wearable Gait Monitoring System for 17 Gait Parameters Based on Computer Vision. IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement, 74, art. 5027114, 1-14. DOI: 10.1109/TIM.2025.3557814
recent_2_synopsis: This study introduces a shoe-mounted system that uses a stereo camera tracking a marker on the opposite shoe to recover spatial parameters and a heel-mounted force-sensitive resistor to recover temporal parameters, jointly measuring 17 gait parameters. Reported accuracy exceeds 93.6% across all parameters. The work supports low-cost continuous gait analysis outside the laboratory for clinical and rehabilitation monitoring.
recent_3: Sun, Y.-H., Shen, G., Chen, J., Fernandes, J., Shada, A. L., Heise, C. P., Jiang, H., & Hu, Y. H. (2025). EasyVis2: A real-time multi-view 3D visualization system for laparoscopic surgery training enhanced by a deep neural network YOLOv8-pose. (arXiv:2412.16742) [no Crossref-resolvable DOI located]
recent_3_synopsis: This work extends EasyVis to multi-view 3D reconstruction, using a YOLOv8-Pose deep network (fine-tuned on a custom surgical-tool dataset) to estimate instrument pose in each camera view and fuse the views into a 3D surface model. It reports improved 3D reconstruction accuracy and reduced computation time versus the prior system, with demonstrations on real animal tissue. It illustrates the deep-learning/computer-vision direction of his recent surgical-training work.
seminal_1: Li, D., Wong, K. D., Hu, Y. H., & Sayeed, A. M. (2002). Detection, classification, and tracking of targets. IEEE Signal Processing Magazine, 19(2), 17-29. DOI: 10.1109/79.985674
seminal_1_synopsis: This article laid out an influential framework for collaborative signal processing in distributed/wireless sensor networks, covering event detection, target classification, and location estimation/prediction of moving targets. With roughly 1,600 citations (Google Scholar, approximate, 2026-06-17), it is his most-cited work. It is foundational because it became a standard reference defining the architecture and problem decomposition for sensor-network target tracking, shaping a decade of follow-on research.
seminal_2: Sheng, X., & Hu, Y. H. (2005). Maximum likelihood multiple-source localization using acoustic energy measurements with wireless sensor networks. IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing, 53(1), 44-53. DOI: 10.1109/TSP.2004.838930
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper introduced a maximum-likelihood method for localizing multiple acoustic sources from energy measurements across an ad hoc wireless sensor network, outperforming prior energy-based approaches and adding multi-source capability. With roughly 950 citations (Google Scholar, approximate, 2026-06-17), it is a widely cited methodological cornerstone for energy-based acoustic source localization in sensor networks.
expertise_overview: Work sits at the intersection of statistical signal processing, machine learning, and computer vision, with deep roots in algorithm design and hardware-efficient implementation, including CORDIC-based and parallel VLSI architectures for digital signal processing. A major thread is distributed and wireless sensor networks, contributing widely used methods for acoustic source localization and for detection, classification, and tracking of targets. A long-standing biomedical signal-processing line applies neural networks and adaptive filtering to ECG analysis, QRS detection, and patient-adaptable beat classification. More recent methods center on deep learning and computer vision applied to human-centered and clinical problems: markerless pose estimation, multi-view 3D reconstruction for laparoscopic surgery, gait and motion monitoring, and video-based ergonomic exposure assessment. Across domains the approach emphasizes probabilistic and machine-learning estimation from sensor and image data, real-time and computationally efficient processing, and translation into practical instrumentation, with application domains spanning sensor networks, biomedical and surgical systems, ergonomics, and image/video analytics.

name: Hughes, Ashley M.
recent_1: Ray, C. E., Wilson, G. M., Hughes, A. M., Cunningham Goedken, C., Liu, E. P.-F., Fitzpatrick, M. A., Suda, K. J., Kota, S. M., Nwankpa, C., & Evans, C. T. (2026). Alert fatigue measurement in clinical decision support: a systematic review. Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association. DOI: 10.1093/jamia/ocag064
recent_1_synopsis: This systematic review synthesizes how alert fatigue (dismissal of alerts due to excessive or irrelevant prompts) has been operationally defined and measured across clinical decision support systems, finding substantial inconsistency in metrics. It matters because reliable, comparable measurement of alert fatigue is a prerequisite for designing decision-support that reduces clinician override behavior and preserves the safety value of alerts.
recent_2: Hanks, J. R., Hughes, A. M., Sultana, S., Klute, R., Formella, K., Flynn, C., Wallenfang, A., Krishnakumar, D., Mourad, M., Cho Morse, Y., & Mischler, M. J. (2026). Facilitating communication with children and young adults with special health care needs through a web-based application: Qualitative descriptive study. JMIR Pediatrics and Parenting, 9, e76512. DOI: 10.2196/76512
recent_2_synopsis: Using qualitative methods and a human-centered design approach, this study identified communication barriers experienced by children and young adults with special health care needs and gathered patient, caregiver, and provider feedback to inform a web-based communication tool. It demonstrates how human factors and participatory design can surface usability and usefulness requirements for assistive health technology in a vulnerable patient population.
recent_3: Ray, C., Goedken, C., Hughes, A. M., Wilson, G. M., Hicks, N. R., Fitzpatrick, M. A., et al. (2025). Standard vs. enhanced implementation strategies to increase adoption of a multidrug-resistant organism alert tool: A cluster randomized trial. Frontiers in Health Services, 5, 1566454. DOI: 10.3389/frhs.2025.1566454
recent_3_synopsis: This cluster-randomized trial tested whether adding audit-and-feedback reports and external facilitation (enhanced implementation) improved facility-level adoption of a multidrug-resistant organism alert tool over standard rollout, with registrations rising from 40% at baseline to 75% (standard) and 89% (enhanced). It matters because it provides experimental evidence on implementation-science strategies for embedding infection-control decision-support tools into routine health-system practice.
seminal_1: Hughes, A. M., Gregory, M. E., Joseph, D. L., Sonesh, S. C., Marlow, S. L., Lacerenza, C. N., Benishek, L. E., King, H. B., & Salas, E. (2016). Saving lives: A meta-analysis of team training in healthcare. Journal of Applied Psychology, 101(9), 1266-1304. DOI: 10.1037/apl0000120
seminal_1_synopsis: This meta-analysis of 129 studies quantified the effectiveness of healthcare team training across all four Kirkpatrick criteria (reactions, learning, transfer, results; d = .37 to .89) and linked training to improved patient outcomes such as length of stay and mortality. With approximately 642 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is Hughes's most-cited work and foundational because it provided the definitive empirical case that structured team training improves both team and patient-safety outcomes, anchoring a decade of healthcare-teamwork intervention research.
seminal_2: Salas, E., Grossman, R., Hughes, A. M., & Coultas, C. W. (2015). Measuring team cohesion: Observations from the science. Human Factors, 57(3), 365-374. DOI: 10.1177/0018720815578267
seminal_2_synopsis: This influential review critiques the inconsistent definition and measurement of team cohesion and recommends modeling it with task and social subdimensions measured through both behavioral and attitudinal operationalizations. With approximately 471 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is foundational because it imposed conceptual and methodological order on a fragmented cohesion literature and gave researchers and practitioners concrete guidance for valid measurement in team-effectiveness studies.
expertise_overview: Hughes applies human factors and organizational-psychology methods to study teamwork, team training, and coordination in healthcare with the goal of improving care quality and patient safety. A central thread is team training and its evaluation, including meta-analytic synthesis of training effectiveness, training sustainment, and the role of the work environment in transfer. She studies shared cognition, communication, and interprofessional collaboration in clinical settings such as labor and delivery, intensive and acute care, COVID-19 frontline work, and the application of evidence-based protocols (e.g., noninvasive ventilation). A growing focus is the design, implementation, and measurement of clinical decision-support and alerting tools, including alert fatigue, audit-and-feedback practices, and infection-control and antimicrobial-stewardship technologies. Methodologically she combines experimental and quasi-experimental studies, meta-analysis, qualitative and human-centered design, systematic review, and implementation-science trial designs. Her application domains span primary care, infection prevention, surgical/anesthesia safety, and pediatric special-needs care.

name: Jamieson, Greg A.
recent_1: Gentile, D., Donmez, B., & Jamieson, G. A. (2025). Human performance effects of combining counterfactual explanations with normative and contrastive explanations in supervised machine learning for automated decision assistance. International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 196, 103434. DOI: 10.1016/j.ijhcs.2024.103434
recent_1_synopsis: Building on the lab's program of work on explainable AI for human decision support, this experiment tested whether adding counterfactual explanations to normative and contrastive explanations improves operators' use of a machine-learning decision aid in a condition-based maintenance task. Combining counterfactual explanations with the other explanation types reduced false-alarm rates and tended to lower decision time and workload. It matters because it provides empirical guidance on how to compose multiple explanation types so that XAI actually calibrates human reliance rather than merely adding information.
recent_2: Skraaning, G., & Jamieson, G. A. (2024). The Failure to Grasp Automation Failure. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making, 18(4), 343-364. DOI: 10.1177/15553434231189375
recent_2_synopsis: Arguing that 'automation failure' is an under-specified construct in human-automation interaction research, the authors show how overly narrow or overly broad definitions limit the explanatory power of human-performance models. They review three aviation safety events (including B737 MAX cases) that strain the narrow definition and propose an initial taxonomy of automation failure and related performance challenges. The paper sparked a notable journal exchange of commentaries, signaling its influence on how the field conceptualizes and tests responses to automation breakdowns.
recent_3: Gentile, D., Donmez, B., & Jamieson, G. A. (2023). Human performance consequences of normative and contrastive explanations: An experiment in machine learning for reliability maintenance. Artificial Intelligence, 321, 103945. DOI: 10.1016/j.artint.2023.103945
recent_3_synopsis: This experiment examined whether example-based normative and contrastive explanations improve reliance on a machine-learning decision aid as participants diagnosed the condition of hydraulic-system components in a preventive-maintenance task. The work links explanation design to measurable human-performance outcomes such as decision accuracy and appropriate reliance. Published in a top AI venue, it reflects Jamieson's move to bring rigorous human-factors experimentation into the explainable-AI and human-AI teaming literature.
seminal_1: Wang, L., Jamieson, G. A., & Hollands, J. G. (2009). Trust and Reliance on an Automated Combat Identification System. Human Factors, 51(3), 281-291. DOI: 10.1177/0018720809338842
seminal_1_synopsis: With approximately 237 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), this is among Jamieson's most-cited peer-reviewed papers and a frequently referenced study on trust in automation. It experimentally showed that operators' trust in and reliance on an automated combat-identification aid track the aid's reliability and that reliability information shapes appropriate use. It is foundational because it provided early, well-controlled evidence linking automation reliability to trust calibration and reliance behavior, a relationship central to subsequent human-automation interaction research.
seminal_2: Burns, C. M., Skraaning, G., Jamieson, G. A., Lau, N., Kwok, J., Welch, R., & Andresen, G. (2008). Evaluation of Ecological Interface Design for Nuclear Process Control: Situation Awareness Effects. Human Factors, 50(4), 663-679. DOI: 10.1518/001872008X312305
seminal_2_synopsis: With approximately 229 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), this is one of the most influential empirical validations of ecological interface design (EID) in a high-fidelity industrial setting. Using a full-scope nuclear control-room simulator, it demonstrated that EID-based displays improved operators' situation awareness relative to conventional interfaces. It is foundational because it moved EID from theory and laboratory demonstrations to rigorous evaluation in a safety-critical process-control domain, anchoring the case for EID in complex sociotechnical systems.
expertise_overview: Jamieson's research centers on cognitive engineering and human-automation interaction in complex, safety-critical work domains, especially process control, nuclear power, energy, and aviation. He is a leading contributor to ecological interface design (EID), applying work-domain and cognitive-work analysis to build displays that reveal system constraints and support operator adaptation, situation awareness, and fault diagnosis. A second major thread concerns trust in and reliance on automation, automation transparency, degree of automation, and the costs of attentional switching and information overload. More recently he has extended this program to explainable AI and human-AI teaming, experimentally evaluating how normative, contrastive, and counterfactual explanations shape human decision performance and appropriate reliance. Methodologically he favors controlled human-in-the-loop experiments, high-fidelity simulator studies, and conceptual/taxonomic analyses of constructs such as automation failure and transparency. His work spans theory development and applied evaluation, consistently aimed at improving operator performance and safety in human-automation systems.

name: Kang, Sang Hyeon
recent_1: Kim, J., Kang, S. H., Li, J., Mirka, G. A., & Dorneich, M. C. (2024). Effects of a passive back-support exosuit on postural control and cognitive performance during a fatigue-inducing posture maintenance task. Human Factors, 66(11), 2451–2467. DOI: 10.1177/00187208231221890
recent_1_synopsis: This study evaluated whether a passive back-support exosuit affected postural stability and cognitive task performance during a 16-minute static trunk flexion task that induced erector spinae fatigue. The exosuit improved postural control (reduced center-of-pressure sway) and enhanced performance on a dual-task information-integration cognitive measure, suggesting exosuit benefits extend beyond muscle unloading to support concurrent cognitive demands in occupational settings.
recent_2: Kang, S. H., Lynch, L., Wolf, E., & Mirka, G. A. (2024). Quantifying the effectiveness of a passive trunk-support exosuit at reducing erector spinae muscle fatigue during a quasi-static posture maintenance task. Ergonomics, 67(9), 1257–1266. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2023.2295214
recent_2_synopsis: Participants maintained an 18-minute quasi-static trunk flexion posture with and without a passive back-support exosuit while EMG-derived fatigue indicators (median frequency, RMS) were tracked. The exosuit significantly attenuated erector spinae fatigue accumulation, providing quantitative evidence that passive trunk-support devices can extend sustainable working durations for jobs requiring sustained forward bending.
recent_3: Kang, S. H., & Mirka, G. A. (2023). Creep deformation of viscoelastic lumbar tissue during sustained submaximal trunk flexion postures. Journal of Biomechanics, 155, 111647. DOI: 10.1016/j.jbiomech.2023.111647
recent_3_synopsis: This paper characterized how viscoelastic lumbar soft tissues (ligaments, discs) creep — progressively deform under sustained load — during prolonged sub-maximal trunk flexion, linking exposure duration and posture magnitude to tissue deformation trajectories. The findings are foundational for understanding cumulative spinal loading in occupational contexts and for setting evidence-based guidelines on work-rest schedules involving sustained bending.
seminal_1: Kang, S. H., & Mirka, G. A. (2023). Effect of trunk flexion angle and time on lumbar and abdominal muscle activity while wearing a passive back-support exosuit device during simple posture-maintenance tasks. Ergonomics, 66(12), 2182–2192. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2023.2191908
seminal_1_synopsis: This is Kang's most-cited work (~37 citations as of mid-2026), systematically examining how trunk flexion angle and exposure duration interact to shape lumbar and abdominal EMG patterns while wearing a passive exosuit. By documenting the angle- and time-dependent nature of exosuit effectiveness, the study established a key empirical foundation for understanding when and how much passive back-support devices reduce trunk muscle demand, influencing subsequent exosuit design and evaluation research.
seminal_2: Kang, S. H., Lee, J., & Jin, S. (2021). Effect of standing desk use on cognitive performance and physical workload while engaged with high cognitive demand tasks. Applied Ergonomics, 92, 103306. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2020.103306
seminal_2_synopsis: This study (~30 citations as of mid-2026) investigated whether standing desk use, compared with seated work, impaired cognitive performance or altered physical workload during demanding cognitive tasks. Results showed standing desks did not degrade cognitive performance but did increase physical workload indicators (discomfort, postural sway), providing nuanced evidence for the physical-cognitive trade-offs important to office ergonomics guidelines.
expertise_overview: Sang Hyeon Kang's research sits at the intersection of occupational biomechanics, spine ergonomics, and human performance. His primary focus is evaluating passive back-support exosuits and exoskeletons: he uses surface electromyography (EMG), postural sway analysis, and biomechanical modeling to quantify how these wearable assistive devices modulate trunk muscle activity, spinal load transfer, and fatigue during sustained and asymmetric posture-maintenance tasks representative of industrial and agricultural work. A parallel line of inquiry examines viscoelastic creep of lumbar passive tissues (ligaments, intervertebral discs) under prolonged flexion, with implications for work-rest schedule design. He also investigates how standing desks and workstation modifications affect the interplay between physical workload and cognitive performance, reflecting a broader interest in dual physical-cognitive demands in office and manual work settings. Methodologically, Kang combines controlled laboratory experiments, EMG-assisted biomechanical modeling, and machine learning approaches to fatigue prediction. His work is directly applicable to ergonomic intervention design, wearable technology assessment, and occupational health policy.

name: Keebler, Joseph R.
recent_1: Fausett, C. M., Keebler, J. R., Lazzara, E. H., Gregory, M., & Blickensderfer, E. (2026). Measurement Matters: A Meta-Analytic Examination of Transactive Memory Systems and Team Outcomes. Small Group Research. DOI: 10.1177/10464964261434540
recent_1_synopsis: A meta-analysis of 44 laboratory studies (103 effect sizes) finding a moderate positive relationship (r = .44) between transactive memory systems (TMS) and team outcomes. The work shows that measurement choices substantially moderate this relationship, with self-report scales yielding much larger effects (r = .77) than observer ratings or embedded behavioral metrics. It advances rigor in how team cognition constructs are operationalized.
recent_2: Zajac, S. A., Williams, K. N., Patel, S. M., Lazzara, E. H., Keebler, J. R., Clemens, M. W., & Holladay, C. L. (2025). Understanding Psychological Safety in Health Care: A Qualitative Investigation and Practical Guidance. The Joint Commission Journal on Quality and Patient Safety, 51(9), 534-547. DOI: 10.1016/j.jcjq.2025.04.009
recent_2_synopsis: A qualitative study within a health system that identifies barriers and facilitators to psychological safety among healthcare professionals. Framing psychological safety as a critical teamwork competency tied to communication, patient safety, and provider well-being, the paper translates findings into practical guidance for leaders. It reflects Keebler's continued focus on team dynamics in healthcare settings.
recent_3: Merwin, E. R., Hagen, A. C., Keebler, J. R., & Forbes, C. (2025). Self-disclosure to AI: People provide personal information to AI and humans equivalently. Computers in Human Behavior: Artificial Humans, 5, 100180. DOI: 10.1016/j.chbah.2025.100180
recent_3_synopsis: An experimental study comparing how willingly people disclose personal information to an AI agent versus a human researcher, finding equivalent levels of self-disclosure across the two conditions. The work bridges human factors and human-AI interaction, with implications for AI systems that collect sensitive information. It represents Keebler's recent expansion into human-AI teaming and trust.
seminal_1: Phan, M. H., Keebler, J. R., & Chaparro, B. S. (2016). The Development and Validation of the Game User Experience Satisfaction Scale (GUESS). Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 58(8), 1217-1247. DOI: 10.1177/0018720816669646
seminal_1_synopsis: Introduces and psychometrically validates the GUESS, a comprehensive standardized instrument for measuring player satisfaction across video game genres. With roughly 288 citations (per the task's ground-truth count; Google Scholar profile, June 2026), it is Keebler's most-cited work and is foundational because it provides the field a rigorously developed, reusable measurement tool for game user experience that is widely adopted in HCI and games research.
seminal_2: Keebler, J. R., Lazzara, E. H., Patzer, B. S., Palmer, E. M., Plummer, J. P., Smith, D. C., Lew, V., Fouquet, S., Chan, Y. R., & Riss, R. (2016). Meta-Analyses of the Effects of Standardized Handoff Protocols on Patient, Provider, and Organizational Outcomes. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 58(8), 1187-1205. DOI: 10.1177/0018720816672309
seminal_2_synopsis: A set of meta-analyses quantifying how standardized patient handoff protocols improve patient, provider, and organizational outcomes in healthcare. With approximately 82 citations (per the task's ground-truth count; Google Scholar profile, June 2026), it is foundational to the patient-safety and healthcare-teamwork literature, providing aggregate evidence that supports widespread adoption of structured handoff tools.
expertise_overview: Keebler's research centers on teams and teamwork, team cognition (including transactive memory systems and shared mental models), and the measurement of team performance. A major application domain is healthcare human factors and patient safety, where he studies handoffs, simulation-based training, psychological safety, burnout, and error management in medical teams. He also works extensively on simulation, training systems, and game user experience, having co-developed the widely used GUESS scale for measuring player satisfaction. Methodologically he favors psychometric scale development, meta-analysis, qualitative investigation, and experimental and applied human factors studies. More recent work extends into human-AI teaming, trust, and self-disclosure to AI across aviation, critical care, and consumer contexts.

name: Keir, Peter J.
recent_1: Mulla, D. M., Tilley, P. M., & Keir, P. J. (2026). Unravelling neuromechanical constraints to finger independence. Human Movement Science, 107, 103481. DOI: 10.1016/j.humov.2026.103481
recent_1_synopsis: This study tested whether neural versus mechanical factors dominate the constraints on finger independence depending on task type, comparing isometric force exertions against finger movements. The authors found that neural factors primarily limit independence during isometric exertions whereas mechanical coupling imposes larger constraints during movement, showing that the control of finger force and finger motion are distinct. The work clarifies why fingers behave more or less independently in different manual tasks, with implications for dexterity, hand modeling, and rehabilitation.
recent_2: Chhiba, R., Mulla, D. M., & Keir, P. J. (2026). Distributed loads alter internal load predictions in the hands and forearm. Computer Methods in Biomechanics and Biomedical Engineering, 1-13 (online ahead of print). DOI: 10.1080/10255842.2026.2658129
recent_2_synopsis: This paper examines how representing external hand loads as distributed rather than as a single point load changes biomechanical model predictions of internal forces in the hands and forearm. The results show that load distribution meaningfully alters predicted internal loading, indicating that simplified point-load assumptions can bias musculoskeletal model estimates. The findings matter for improving the accuracy of occupational biomechanics models used to assess injury risk in gripping and manual tasks.
recent_3: Mulla, D. M., & Keir, P. J. (2023). Neuromuscular control: from a biomechanist's perspective. Frontiers in Sports and Active Living, 5, 1217009. DOI: 10.3389/fspor.2023.1217009
recent_3_synopsis: This review serves as a primer that integrates concepts of neuromuscular control across disciplines for the biomechanics community, bridging neural control theory and biomechanical modeling. It synthesizes ideas such as muscle synergies, redundancy, and control of posture versus movement, and argues for better representation of neural control within biomechanical models. The paper is highly cited for a recent article (47 Crossref citations) and helps orient researchers integrating motor control with musculoskeletal mechanics.
seminal_1: Mogk, J. P. M., & Keir, P. J. (2003). The effects of posture on forearm muscle loading during gripping. Ergonomics, 46(9), 956-975. DOI: 10.1080/0014013031000107595
seminal_1_synopsis: This study quantified how wrist and forearm posture modulate the muscular load on the forearm flexors and extensors during gripping exertions, demonstrating that posture substantially alters muscle activation demands. It is one of Keir's most foundational works (approximately 302 citations per Google Scholar; 174 per Crossref as of June 2026), widely cited in ergonomics for linking working posture to forearm muscle loading and upper-extremity injury risk. The findings underpin guidelines for hand-intensive work and the modeling of grip-related musculoskeletal demands.
seminal_2: Keir, P. J., Bach, J. M., & Rempel, D. (1999). Effects of computer mouse design and task on carpal tunnel pressure. Ergonomics, 42(10), 1350-1360. DOI: 10.1080/001401399184992
seminal_2_synopsis: This early influential study measured carpal tunnel pressure during computer mouse use, showing that mouse design and task demands elevate intracarpal pressure to levels associated with carpal tunnel syndrome risk. With roughly 287 citations on Google Scholar (140 per Crossref as of June 2026), it is foundational to the office-ergonomics and pathomechanics literature on carpal tunnel syndrome. It helped establish carpal tunnel pressure as a key physiological outcome for evaluating computer input device design and wrist posture.
expertise_overview: Peter J. Keir is an occupational biomechanist whose research investigates the mechanisms of work-related musculoskeletal disorders of the upper extremity, linking neuromuscular control to mechanics in the hand, wrist, forearm, and shoulder. His core topics include carpal tunnel syndrome pathomechanics, carpal tunnel pressure, wrist and finger posture, forearm muscle loading during gripping, shoulder muscle activity and fatigue, and finger independence and dexterity. Methodologically he relies heavily on surface electromyography (including EMG amplitude and frequency-based fatigue measures), carpal tunnel pressure measurement, biomechanical and musculoskeletal modeling, motion capture, and video-based physical demands analysis. His applied domains span ergonomics and workplace injury prevention, computer input device and tool design, repetitive-strain and repetitive-task assessment, and clinical biomechanics. More recent work extends into neuromuscular control theory, internal load prediction, and markerless hand tracking. The application focus throughout is reducing the risk of distal upper-extremity disorders in occupational settings.

Keir, Professor, Department of Kinesiology, McMaster University (Occupational Biomechanics Laboratory), via Google Scholar profile (user vb_L3bQAAAAJ; verified mcmaster.ca email; ~6,355 total citations), McMaster Experts page, ResearchGate, and MIRA faculty page. name: Kim, Sunwook
recent_1: Arippa, F., Barr, A., Phillips, B., Kim, S., Nussbaum, M. A., & Harris-Adamson, C. (2026). Wearing an Arm Support Exoskeleton Does Not Affect Balance but May Decrease Dynamic Stability During a Step-Down Maneuver. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261441959
recent_1_synopsis: This study tested whether wearing a passive arm-support exoskeleton compromises postural control during a step-down maneuver, finding no effect on overall balance but a measurable reduction in dynamic stability. The work matters because it probes a potential safety trade-off of occupational exoskeletons—gait and fall risk—beyond their intended benefits for upper-limb work.
recent_2: Akinwande, F., Kim, S., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2026). Passive back- and arm-support exoskeletons have effects on physical demands and user perceptions in simulated manual mining tasks that are generally beneficial but are both device- and task-specific. Applied Ergonomics, 137, 104819. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104819
recent_2_synopsis: This paper evaluated passive back-support and arm-support exoskeletons across simulated manual mining tasks, quantifying their effects on physical demands (muscle activity, posture) and user perceptions. It shows the benefits are real but highly device- and task-specific, underscoring that exoskeleton selection must be matched to the specific job and not assumed to transfer across tasks.
recent_3: Li, L., Behjati Ashtiani, M., Kim, S., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2026). Evaluating model-estimated shoulder muscle activity during overhead work with varied task demands and exoskeleton use. Applied Ergonomics, 137, 104822. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104822
recent_3_synopsis: Using musculoskeletal modeling, this work estimated shoulder muscle activity during overhead work under varied task demands with and without an arm-support exoskeleton. It advances model-based exposure assessment as a way to predict the biomechanical effects of exoskeletons without requiring extensive in-lab EMG measurement.
seminal_1: Alemi, M. M., Madinei, S., Kim, S., Srinivasan, D., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2020). Effects of Two Passive Back-Support Exoskeletons on Muscle Activity, Energy Expenditure, and Subjective Assessments During Repetitive Lifting. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 62(3), 458-474. DOI: 10.1177/0018720819897669
seminal_1_synopsis: This study compared two commercial passive back-support exoskeletons during repetitive lifting, measuring trunk muscle activity, metabolic energy expenditure, and subjective ratings to characterize both benefits and unintended costs. With roughly 161 citations (Google Scholar, 2026-06-17) it is among Kim's most influential papers and a widely cited benchmark for evaluating back-support exoskeletons in occupational lifting.
seminal_2: Madinei, S., Alemi, M. M., Kim, S., Srinivasan, D., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2020). Biomechanical Evaluation of Passive Back-Support Exoskeletons in a Precision Manual Assembly Task: "Expected" Effects on Trunk Muscle Activity, Perceived Exertion, and Task Performance. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 62(3), 441-457. DOI: 10.1177/0018720819890966
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper biomechanically evaluated passive back-support exoskeletons in a precision manual assembly task, testing whether expected reductions in trunk muscle activity hold when the task requires fine manual control, and examining effects on perceived exertion and task performance. With roughly 115 citations (Google Scholar, 2026-06-17) it is a foundational contribution showing that exoskeleton benefits depend on task type and can trade off against precision performance.
expertise_overview: Sunwook Kim studies occupational biomechanics and human-exoskeleton interaction, evaluating how passive and powered back-support and arm-support exoskeletons alter muscle activity, spine and shoulder loads, energy expenditure, and worker-perceived exertion across realistic manual tasks. A central theme is the trade-offs of wearable assistive devices: he examines unintended effects on postural balance, reactive recovery from slips and trips, dynamic stability, and gait alongside the intended physical-demand reductions. He combines laboratory methods such as surface EMG, motion capture, and musculoskeletal modeling with field studies of usability, user acceptance, and adoption barriers in industry. His application domains span automotive assembly and logistics, construction, manual materials handling, and mining. He also works on exposure assessment and postural control, increasingly incorporating model-based and sensor- or vision-driven estimation of physical work demands and musculoskeletal disorder risk.

Nussbaum, index 57; exoskeleton/ergonomics theme). Disambiguated from other 'Sunwook Kim' researchers in robotics/CS by the exoskeleton/occupational-biomechanics topic and Nussbaum co-authorship.
