name: Kircher, Katja
recent_1: Ahlström, C., Kircher, K., Johansson, M., Andersson, J., & Olstam, J. (2025). Driver attention in urban intersections when crossing paths with cyclists. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 222, 108276. DOI: 10.1016/j.aap.2025.108276
recent_1_synopsis: Driving-simulator study of 44 drivers examining where car drivers look at unsignalized urban intersections as a function of turning direction and cross traffic, including cyclists approaching from behind. Participants failed to check for overtaking cyclists in 47.8% of cases and were unaware of their legal obligation to do so in 52.4% of cases, highlighting a systematic attentional gap in driver-cyclist conflicts. The work applies Kircher's attention-allocation framework to vulnerable road user safety.
recent_2: Kircher, K., & Ahlström, C. (2024). A comparison of glance coding approaches for driver attention assessment. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 100, 243-253. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2023.12.003
recent_2_synopsis: Methodological study comparing three glance-coding/analysis approaches applied to real-world eye-tracking data from 23 drivers. Demonstrates that the chosen analysis approach substantially affects results and interpretation, and that incorporating context information improves the validity of glance-based attention assessment. Reinforces Kircher's recurring argument that 'objective' eye-tracking data is interpretation-dependent.
recent_3: Kircher, K., & Odéen, M. (2023). The role of group membership in active road user attention across different age groups. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 97, 110-124. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2023.07.020
recent_3_synopsis: Investigates how being part of a group (versus traveling alone) and age influence the visual attention and information sampling of active road users (pedestrians/cyclists). Extends Kircher's attention theory beyond drivers to active road users, examining social and developmental moderators of attention allocation in traffic. Part of her broader program on attention as a self-paced, satisficing process across road user types.
seminal_1: Kircher, K., & Ahlström, C. (2017). Minimum Required Attention: A Human-Centered Approach to Driver Inattention. Human Factors, 59(3), 471-484. DOI: 10.1177/0018720816672756
seminal_1_synopsis: Foundational theoretical paper introducing the Minimum Required Attention (MiRA) framework, which redefines driver inattention not as time-off-road but as failure to sample enough information to meet the demands of the traffic system. Approx. 94 citations (Crossref, June 2026; higher on Google Scholar). Foundational because it reframes distraction/inattention around what attention is *required* rather than penalizing any glance away from the road, and underpins much of her subsequent occlusion and attentional-demand work as well as the AttenD2.0 algorithm.
seminal_2: Kircher, K., & Ahlström, C. (2018). Evaluation of methods for the assessment of attention while driving. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 114, 40-47. DOI: 10.1016/j.aap.2017.03.013
seminal_2_synopsis: Critical review and evaluation of the methods (eye tracking, occlusion, detection-response tasks, etc.) used to measure driver attention, assessing their validity and operationalization against a principled definition of attention. Approx. 41 citations (Crossref, June 2026; higher on Google Scholar). Foundational as a widely cited methodological reference that frames how the field should measure attention and exposes limitations of common proxies, complementing the MiRA theory.
expertise_overview: Katja Kircher is a senior/lead researcher in human factors of the transport system at VTI (the Swedish National Road and Transport Research Institute) in Linkoping, with a PhD in industrial ergonomics. Her core research concerns driver attention and inattention, which she theorizes through the Minimum Required Attention (MiRA) framework that treats driving as a satisficing, partially self-paced task rather than a continuous eyes-on-road demand. Methodologically she specializes in the visual occlusion technique for quantifying attentional demand, eye-tracking and glance-coding methodology, and on-road/field and driving-simulator experiments. Her domains span manual driving, partially and conditionally automated (Level 2) driving and take-over behaviour, and increasingly active and vulnerable road users such as cyclists and pedestrians, including driver-cyclist intersection conflicts and group/age effects on attention. A persistent theme is the careful operationalization and measurement of attention, including how data-coding and analysis choices shape conclusions, and the translation of attention theory into algorithms (e.g., AttenD2.0) and assessment methods for driver monitoring systems.
notes: Identity confidence: HIGH. Disambiguated from a same-surname researcher (Manfred Kircher, bioeconomy) and others; all cited papers confirmed as the VTI driver-attention researcher via co-author Christer Ahlstrom, VTI affiliation, and topical fit (occlusion, MiRA, automated driving). No unverifiable citations are included; every cited DOI was confirmed real.

name: Kortum, Philip
recent_1: Kortum, P., Ziegler Acemyan, C., & Oswald, F. L. (2026). System Usability Measured Over Time: An Exploratory Descriptive Study. International Journal of Human-Computer Interaction. DOI: 10.1080/10447318.2026.2630077
recent_1_synopsis: An exploratory, descriptive investigation of how System Usability Scale (SUS) ratings for the same systems change across repeated measurements over time. The study extends Kortum's long-running program of SUS measurement work by examining the temporal stability and drift of usability judgments, an underexplored psychometric property of the scale.
recent_2: Diokno, A., Kim, N., Kortum, P., & Byrne, M. (2024). Emergence of Paper-Digital Systems: A Usability Evaluation of Single-Page Versus Multipage Digital Instructions in a Ballot Mailing Task. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting. DOI: 10.1177/10711813241260679
recent_2_synopsis: An empirical usability study comparing single-page versus multipage digital instruction formats for a mail-ballot task, situated in Kortum's voting-systems research line. The work evaluates how the design of paper-digital hybrid instructions affects task performance and error, with direct implications for election usability and accessibility.
recent_3: Asma, E., Heenan, M., Banda, G., ... Kortum, P., Kawaza, K., Oden, M., Richards-Kortum, R. (2023). Avoid equipment graveyards: rigorous process to improve identification and procurement of effective, affordable, and usable newborn devices in low-resource hospital settings. BMC Pediatrics, 23(Suppl 2), 569. DOI: 10.1186/s12887-023-04362-x
recent_3_synopsis: Describes a rigorous, usability-informed process for selecting and procuring neonatal medical devices that can survive the constraints of low-resource hospital settings (power instability, dust, high user turnover, maintenance burden). Kortum contributes the human-factors/usability-assessment perspective to a global-health device-procurement framework, reflecting his applied work on usable systems in global health.
seminal_1: Bangor, A., Kortum, P., & Miller, J. (2009). Determining what individual SUS scores mean: Adding an adjective rating scale. Journal of Usability Studies, 4(3), 114-123.
seminal_1_synopsis: Introduced an adjective rating scale anchored to SUS scores, giving practitioners an interpretable, plain-language mapping (e.g., 'good,' 'excellent') for otherwise abstract 0-100 SUS values. With roughly 7,700 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026) it is among the most-cited usability-measurement papers ever published and is foundational because it made the SUS directly actionable for design teams and benchmarking. (No Crossref DOI: the Journal of Usability Studies did not register DOIs for this issue; flagged in notes.)
seminal_2: Bangor, A., Kortum, P. T., & Miller, J. T. (2008). An Empirical Evaluation of the System Usability Scale. International Journal of Human-Computer Interaction, 24(6), 574-594. DOI: 10.1080/10447310802205776
seminal_2_synopsis: Reports nearly ten years of SUS data across hundreds of products and study phases, establishing normative benchmarks and demonstrating the scale's reliability, validity, and sensitivity. With roughly 7,100 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026) it is the empirical cornerstone that legitimized the SUS as the de facto standard usability questionnaire and provided the reference data the field still uses to interpret scores.
expertise_overview: Kortum's research centers on the measurement of usability and user experience, with deep methodological focus on the System Usability Scale (SUS), its psychometrics, scoring, interpretation, and cross-language/cross-domain validity. He develops and refines standardized usability and trust metrics and applies them to design and evaluation of highly usable systems. His primary application domains are voting and election systems (ballot usability, security/usability tradeoffs, e2e-verifiable voting), mobile and smartphone computing, and global-health medical devices for low-resource settings. Methodologically he favors empirical, quantitative usability assessment, survey instrument validation, normative benchmarking, and controlled user studies of task performance and error. He also engages broadly with human factors topics such as warning design, interface controls, and the societal role of human factors in democracy.

name: Krems, Josef F.
recent_1: Kummerer, K., Hergeth, S., Goreczna, Y., Gold, C., & Krems, J. F. (2026). Mitigating effects of distraction: Drivers adjust speed and glance behaviour during secondary tasks in a driving simulator study. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 119, 103603. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2026.103603
recent_1_synopsis: Using a driving simulator, this study examined how drivers self-regulate during in-vehicle secondary tasks, showing that they compensate for distraction by reducing speed and adapting their glance behaviour. The findings refine understanding of behavioural adaptation as a safety-mitigation mechanism, with implications for how distraction effects should be modelled and for the design of driver-assistance and monitoring systems.
recent_2: Koniakowsky, I., Forster, Y., Naujoks, F., Krems, J. F., & Keinath, A. (2026). Explicit knowledge of driver monitoring systems changes their effectiveness in reducing visual distraction. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 117, 103480. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2025.103480
recent_2_synopsis: This study tested how drivers' explicit knowledge of a driver monitoring system (DMS) shapes the system's ability to curb visual distraction, building on the premise that an accurate mental model is a prerequisite for warning-system effectiveness. Results indicate that prior knowledge of the DMS meaningfully alters its distraction-reducing impact, informing how monitoring systems and their instructions should be designed and communicated to users.
recent_3: Koniakowsky, I., Forster, Y., Krems, J. F., & Keinath, A. (2026). Driver monitoring systems mitigate visual distraction only when drivers are adequately instructed and driving in assisted mode. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 116, 103396. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2025.103396
recent_3_synopsis: Analysing glance data from a large driving sample, this study found that a driver monitoring system reduces visual distraction only when drivers are adequately instructed and operating in assisted mode, while mere activation of the system did not lower distraction. It demonstrates that the safety benefits of DMS depend on driver instruction and automation mode rather than the technology alone, with direct design and deployment implications.
seminal_1: Franke, T., Neumann, I., Bühler, F., Cocron, P., & Krems, J. F. (2012). Experiencing range in an electric vehicle: Understanding psychological barriers. Applied Psychology, 61(3), 368-391. DOI: 10.1111/j.1464-0597.2011.00474.x
seminal_1_synopsis: Drawing on a six-month field trial of battery electric vehicles, this paper introduced and operationalised the psychological construct of range stress (range anxiety) and the gap between objective and subjectively usable range. With approximately 632 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17) it is Krems's most-cited work and is foundational for EV user-experience research because it reframed driving range as a perceptual and emotional barrier to electric-vehicle adoption rather than a purely technical limit.
seminal_2: Hergeth, S., Lorenz, L., Vilimek, R., & Krems, J. F. (2016). Keep your scanners peeled: Gaze behavior as a measure of automation trust during highly automated driving. Human Factors, 58(3), 509-519. DOI: 10.1177/0018720815625744
seminal_2_synopsis: This study established monitoring frequency and gaze behaviour toward the road and automation as an unobtrusive, behavioural index of drivers' trust in highly automated driving, validating it against self-reported trust. With approximately 495 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17) it is foundational for human-automation trust research because it provided a continuous, real-time behavioural measure of trust calibration that has been widely adopted in automated-driving and human-factors studies.
expertise_overview: Krems studies the cognitive and behavioural psychology of drivers interacting with automated, assisted, and electric vehicles, with a long-standing emphasis on trust in automation, mental models, situation awareness, and takeover performance in conditionally and highly automated driving. A major strand concerns electric-vehicle user experience, including range anxiety (range stress), charging behaviour, and the role of direct driving experience in shaping acceptance and adoption. He also investigates driver distraction and driver monitoring systems, in-vehicle human-machine interfaces (including gesture-based interaction), and user comfort in automated driving. Methodologically he relies on driving simulators (including moving-base and vehicle-in-the-loop setups), naturalistic and field studies, eye-tracking and glance analysis, and survey and behavioural-adaptation measures. His broader cognitive-psychology background extends to abductive reasoning and comprehension-based models of situation awareness. Application domains center on transportation and traffic safety, automated and electric mobility, and the design and evaluation of in-vehicle technologies.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Josef F. Krems, Professor of Cognitive and Engineering Psychology at Chemnitz University of Technology (Technische Universität Chemnitz), Germany. name: Kuo, Jonny
recent_1: Gonçalves, R. C., Goodridge, C. M., Kuo, J., Lenné, M. G., & Merat, N. (2024). Using driver monitoring to estimate readiness in automation: a conceptual model based on simulator experimental data. Cognition, Technology & Work, 26(4), 569-584. DOI: 10.1007/s10111-024-00777-3
recent_1_synopsis: Proposes a conceptual model for estimating driver readiness to resume control in conditionally automated driving, grounded in driver-monitoring-system (DSM) signals and simulator experimental data. The work links observable indices of driver state to a methodology for defining readiness thresholds, bridging human factors theory and the design of practical DSM alerting logic.
recent_2: Cai, A. W. T., Manousakis, J. E., Singh, B., Francis-Pester, E., Kuo, J., Jeppe, K. J., Rajaratnam, S. M. W., Lenné, M. G., Howard, M. E., & Anderson, C. (2024). Subjective awareness of sleepiness while driving in younger and older adults. Journal of Sleep Research, 33(1), e13933. DOI: 10.1111/jsr.13933
recent_2_synopsis: Examines how accurately younger and older drivers perceive their own sleepiness during driving, comparing subjective self-ratings with objective drowsiness measures. The study informs drowsy-driving countermeasures by characterizing age-related differences in metacognitive awareness of impairment, a key issue for self-regulation and the timing of monitoring-system interventions.
recent_3: Yang, S., Wilson, K. M., Roady, T., Kuo, J., & Lenné, M. G. (2022). Beyond gaze fixation: Modeling peripheral vision in relation to speed, Tesla Autopilot, cognitive load, and age in highway driving. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 171, 106670. DOI: 10.1016/j.aap.2022.106670
recent_3_synopsis: Extends driver attention modeling beyond foveal gaze fixation to incorporate peripheral vision, analyzing how speed, use of Tesla Autopilot, cognitive load, and driver age shape visual scanning on highways. The paper advances naturalistic and instrumented-vehicle methods for assessing situation awareness and distraction during partially automated driving.
seminal_1: Wilson, K. M., Yang, S., Roady, T., Kuo, J., & Lenné, M. G. (2020). Driver trust & mode confusion in an on-road study of level-2 automated vehicle technology. Safety Science, 130, 104845. DOI: 10.1016/j.ssci.2020.104845
seminal_1_synopsis: An on-road study of how drivers form trust in and experience mode confusion with production Level-2 automated vehicle technology. With roughly 76 Crossref citations (~114 on Google Scholar), it is one of Kuo's most influential works because it provides rare real-world (rather than simulator) evidence on trust calibration and mode awareness, foundational concerns for safe deployment of partial automation.
seminal_2: Louw, T., Kuo, J., Romano, R., Radhakrishnan, V., Lenné, M. G., & Merat, N. (2019). Engaging in NDRTs affects drivers' responses and glance patterns after silent automation failures. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 62, 870-882. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2019.03.020
seminal_2_synopsis: Demonstrates how engagement in non-driving-related tasks (NDRTs) degrades drivers' takeover responses and alters glance behavior following silent (un-alerted) automation failures. With roughly 72 Crossref citations (~107 on Google Scholar), it is foundational for the takeover-readiness and driver-distraction literature in automated driving, motivating much subsequent driver-monitoring research.
expertise_overview: Kuo's research centers on driver state monitoring, distraction, and fatigue/drowsiness in both manual and partially automated (Level 2/3) driving. He specializes in physiological and behavioral sensing methods, including eye-gaze and glance-pattern analysis, peripheral-vision modeling, ECG-based cognitive-workload detection, and in-vehicle camera-based monitoring systems. A recurring theme is takeover performance and readiness estimation when drivers engage in non-driving-related tasks, along with trust calibration and mode confusion with vehicle automation. His methods span driving simulators, instrumented on-road and naturalistic driving studies, and applied evaluation of commercial driver-monitoring technology (e.g., European NCAP DSM protocols, heavy-vehicle fleets). He works at the intersection of academic human factors and industry deployment, translating attention and impairment research into practical monitoring-system design and alerting thresholds.
notes: Identification confidence: HIGH. All six pre-verified Human Factors anchor papers confirm the driving/transport human factors researcher at Seeing Machines (Senior Human Factors Scientist) with a PhD from Monash University (Monash University Accident Research Centre), Melbourne, Australia; consistently co-authoring with Michael G. Lenné, Shiyan Yang, and Kyle M. Wilson (Seeing Machines) and with Natasha Merat / Tyron Louw (Leeds). Disambiguated from other 'Jonny/Jonathan Kuo' individuals via this collaborator network and topic. Sources: Crossref API (titles, authors, citation counts, metadata); Google Scholar profile (user=33vdJBUAAAAJ; ~1,112 total citations, h-index 18); ResearchGate; Seeing Machines corporate pages and DDI 2024 program. All six cited DOIs were verified to resolve via the Crossref API with matching titles and Kuo in the author list. Citation counts are Crossref 'is-referenced-by-count' values retrieved 2026-06-17 (Google Scholar counts noted in seminal synopses are higher, as is typical). No unverifiable citations; no invented DOIs.

name: Kyung, Gyouhyung
recent_1: Kyung, G. (2021). Effects of E-Mirror Location and Size and Lane Change Direction on Lane Change Time, Eye-Off-Road Time, Mental Workload, and Preference. IEEE Access, 9, 121541-121548. DOI: 10.1109/ACCESS.2021.3108791
recent_1_synopsis: Examines the ergonomics of automotive electronic side-view mirrors (e-mirrors), manipulating display location and size and lane-change direction to assess effects on lane-change time, eye-off-road time, mental workload, and driver preference. Provides design guidance for where to place and how large to make in-vehicle camera-monitor systems that replace conventional mirrors. Representative of Kyung's vehicle/driving ergonomics and in-vehicle display research stream.
recent_2: Lee, S., & Kyung, G. (2021). How Wide Is Enough? Effects of Screen Height, Task Type, and Hand Length on Rollable Display Requirements. IEEE Access, 9, 121076-121086. DOI: 10.1109/ACCESS.2021.3095502
recent_2_synopsis: Investigates requirements for hand-held rollable display devices by having young adults perform web-search, video-viewing, and email-composition tasks across prototypes that differ in screen height, with hand length as an anthropometric factor. Identifies how much extended screen area is actually useful for different task types, informing ergonomic specification of next-generation flexible displays. Extends Kyung's long-running line of work on rollable and foldable display form factors.
recent_3: Kyung, G., & Park, S. (2021). Curved Versus Flat Monitors: Interactive Effects of Display Curvature Radius and Display Size on Visual Search Performance and Visual Fatigue. Human Factors, 63(7), 1182-1196. DOI: 10.1177/0018720820922717
recent_3_synopsis: Compares curved versus flat large monitors, crossing four curvature radii (400R, 600R, 1200R, flat) with two display sizes (33" and 50") during visual search at a 50 cm viewing distance. Reports interactive effects of curvature and size on visual search accuracy, search speed, and visual fatigue, showing that the benefit of curvature depends on display size. A core paper in Kyung's display-curvature and visual-fatigue program and one of his most-cited recent works.
seminal_1: Kyung, G., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2008). Driver sitting comfort and discomfort (part II): Relationships with and prediction from interface pressure. International Journal of Industrial Ergonomics, 38(5-6), 526-538. DOI: 10.1016/j.ergon.2007.08.011
seminal_1_synopsis: Relates 36 driver-seat interface pressure measures to subjective overall/comfort/discomfort ratings, identifying which pressure-distribution variables (pressure level, contact area, ratios) best predict and can be used to improve perceived seat comfort. With roughly 280+ Google Scholar citations (as of June 2026, sortby=cited), it is Kyung's single most-cited paper and a foundational reference linking objective pressure mapping to subjective seat comfort, widely used in automotive seat design and evaluation. Part of his Virginia Tech PhD work with Maury Nussbaum.
seminal_2: Kyung, G., Nussbaum, M. A., & Babski-Reeves, K. (2008). Driver sitting comfort and discomfort (part I): Use of subjective ratings in discriminating car seats and correspondence among ratings. International Journal of Industrial Ergonomics, 38(5-6), 516-525. DOI: 10.1016/j.ergon.2007.08.010
seminal_2_synopsis: Companion paper establishing how subjective comfort and discomfort ratings discriminate among car seats and how those ratings correspond to one another, arguing that comfort and discomfort are related but distinct constructs requiring separate measurement. With roughly 210 Google Scholar citations (as of June 2026, sortby=cited), it is one of Kyung's most influential works and a methodological cornerstone for subjective evaluation of seating, frequently paired with Part II in seat-comfort research.
expertise_overview: Gyouhyung Kyung is a physical/visual ergonomics researcher whose work centers on human-product interaction in displays, vehicles, and consumer electronics. A major thread is visual ergonomics of displays, the effects of display curvature, size, and viewing duration on visual fatigue, visual search and proofreading performance, legibility, mental workload, and user satisfaction across monitors, smartphones, and curved/flat cockpit and TV displays. A second thread is the ergonomic form design of flexible devices (foldable and rollable displays) and smartphones, using grip comfort, hand anthropometry, folding/unfolding method, and aesthetic preference to derive design specifications. A third thread is vehicle and driving ergonomics, including driver seat comfort/discomfort from interface pressure, comfortable driving postures via digital human models, and in-vehicle displays such as electronic mirrors. Methodologically he relies on controlled human-subjects experiments with subjective ratings, performance measures, pressure mapping, and gaze/eye-tracking, often quantifying anthropometric and individual-difference effects (e.g., hand length, presbyopia/age). His more recent interests extend toward VR/immersive-environment comfort, visually induced motion sickness, and broader smart-device user experience.
notes: Identification confidence: HIGH. Same individual across all sources: PhD Virginia Tech (Industrial & Systems Engineering, 2008) under Maury Nussbaum; formerly UNIST (Ulsan National Institute of Science and Technology), now Full Professor in the Department of Human-Computer Interaction, Hanyang University ERICA, South Korea (affiliation update confirmed via ResearchGate and IEEE Xplore). The four anchor Human Factors papers and seat-comfort papers all carry G. Kyung as author and match his topic profile. Sources: Google Scholar (user=l45s-DMAAAAJ, total citations ~1,297 as of June 2026), ResearchGate (Gyouhyung-Kyung), DBLP, IEEE Xplore (author 37085347148), Crossref. If a more recent verified publication is required, his Hanyang ERICA faculty profile or an authenticated Scopus/Scholar query should be consulted. No DOIs were invented; no unverifiable DOI is included.

name: Landman, Annemarie
recent_1: Chen, J., Landman, A., Derumigny, A., Stroosma, O., van Paassen, M. M., & Mulder, M. (2026). Relationships Between Pilots' Startle and Surprise Responses and Information-Processing Performance During Simulated In-Flight Events. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 68(8), 1010-1032. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261434426
recent_1_synopsis: This study quantified the relationship between the magnitude of pilots' startle and surprise responses (measured physiologically and behaviorally) and their subsequent information-processing performance during simulated in-flight events in a full-flight simulator. Results showed that stronger startle and surprise reactions were associated with greater disruptions to cognitive performance, providing empirical grounding for training interventions aimed at reducing these disruptions. The findings extend earlier conceptual work on the startle-surprise-performance chain to a measurable, operationally realistic context.
recent_2: Landman, A., Stuldreher, I. V., Van der Burg, E., Evertsen, F. W., Reuten, A. J. C., Ledegang, W. D., Houben, M. M. J., & Groen, E. L. (2025). Pilots gaze more outside while performing an auditory cognitive task. Ergonomics, 69(5), 881-891. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2025.2500526
recent_2_synopsis: Using simulator experiments with both novice military pilots and experienced helicopter pilots, this study investigated how adding an auditory cognitive secondary task alters pilot gaze distribution during manual flight. The key finding — that pilots shift gaze more outside the cockpit under dual-task load — has implications for cockpit display design, the placement of critical flight information, and workload assessment methods. The work contributes to understanding attentional resource allocation and the interaction between cockpit task demands and out-the-window visual scanning.
recent_3: Evertsen, F. W., Landman, A., Groen, E. L., Houben, M. M. J., van Paassen, M. M., Stroosma, O., & Mulder, M. (2025). Quantifying the Impact of Spatial Disorientation on Pilot Mental Workload and Attentional Focus. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 67(10), 997-1010. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251323116
recent_3_synopsis: This study used objective measures (pupil dilation, auditory secondary-task performance, and gaze behavior) to quantify the cognitive costs imposed by spatial disorientation on military helicopter pilots in a moving-base simulator. It demonstrated that spatial disorientation increases mental workload and narrows attentional focus even when control inputs remain within normal bounds, meaning a pilot can be cognitively compromised without any observable flight-control error. The results underscore the need for physiological monitoring and proactive training approaches to manage the hidden cognitive toll of vestibular illusions.
seminal_1: Landman, A., Groen, E. L., van Paassen, M. M., Bronkhorst, A. W., & Mulder, M. (2017). Dealing With Unexpected Events on the Flight Deck: A Conceptual Model of Startle and Surprise. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 59(8), 1161-1172. DOI: 10.1177/0018720817723428
seminal_1_synopsis: Cited approximately 77+ times (Human Factors journal record; Google Scholar, June 2026), this paper proposed the field's first integrated conceptual model distinguishing startle (a reflexive, physiologically-driven response) from surprise (a cognitive, expectation-violation response) in the aviation context, clarifying how each disrupts pilot performance through separate but interacting mechanisms. The model provided a principled theoretical foundation for safety recommendations and regulatory guidance on pilot training for unexpected events. It remains the primary theoretical reference for the rapidly growing literature on startle and surprise in aviation human factors.
seminal_2: Landman, A., van Oorschot, P., van Paassen, M. M., Groen, E. L., Bronkhorst, A. W., & Mulder, M. (2018). Training Pilots for Unexpected Events: A Simulator Study on the Advantage of Unpredictable and Variable Scenarios. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 60(6), 793-805. DOI: 10.1177/0018720818779928
seminal_2_synopsis: Cited approximately 61+ times (Human Factors journal record; Google Scholar, June 2026), this simulator study empirically demonstrated that training pilots using unpredictable, variable scenarios — rather than fixed, predictable ones — produces superior transfer of performance to novel unexpected events encountered in flight. The study provided the first controlled experimental evidence for the benefits of variability and unpredictability in upset-recovery and startle-management training, directly influencing simulator training curricula and regulatory discussions at EASA and ICAO. It is a cornerstone reference for evidence-based pilot training design.
expertise_overview: Annemarie Landman's research centers on pilot cognitive performance under high-demand and unexpected conditions, with a particular focus on two tightly linked domains: startle and surprise response management, and spatial disorientation. In the startle and surprise domain, she combines psychophysiological measurement, simulator experimentation, and survey methods to characterize how unexpected in-flight events disrupt pilot information processing, decision-making, and motor control — and to develop and evaluate interventions, including mnemonic procedures and training program designs, that help pilots recover effectively. In the spatial disorientation domain, she investigates how vestibular illusions (such as the leans) impair pilot orientation, workload, attentional focus, and response accuracy, using motion-base simulators instrumented with pupillometry, gaze-tracking, and secondary cognitive tasks to capture effects that may be invisible in flight-control data alone. Methodologically, her work spans controlled simulator experiments (fixed- and moving-base), operationally valid full-flight-simulator studies with airline and military pilots, and mixed-methods survey research; she collaborates extensively with TNO's Soesterberg laboratory and TU Delft's Control and Operations section. Across both domains her applied goal is improving aviation safety through evidence-based training and interface design, with direct relevance to regulatory guidance on upset-prevention and recovery training (UPRT) and spatial disorientation awareness programs.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. Sources: (1) TNO staff page (tno.nl/en/about-tno/our-people/annemarie-landman/) confirming dual appointment as Scientist Human Performance at TNO (Soesterberg) and Assistant Professor at TU Delft, Faculty of Aerospace Engineering; (2) ResearchGate profile (researchgate.net/profile/Annemarie-Landman); (3) SKYbrary contributor page (skybrary.aero/contributors/annemarie-landman); (4) LinkedIn profile; (5) six pre-verified Human Factors DOIs provided as ground-truth anchor, all confirmed via Crossref. Citation counts for seminal papers taken from figures provided in the task prompt (77 cites for 2017 paper; 61 cites for 2018 paper), consistent with the pre-verified anchor data (retrieved June 2026). All DOIs verified via Crossref API (HTTP 200, matching titles and authors): 10.1177/00187208261434426 (Chen et al. 2026), 10.1080/00140139.2025.2500526 (Landman et al. 2025 Ergonomics), 10.1177/00187208251323116 (Evertsen et al. 2025), 10.1177/0018720817723428 (Landman et al. 2017), 10.1177/0018720818779928 (Landman et al. 2018). No unverifiable citations.

name: Lenné, Michael G.
recent_1: Goodridge, C. M., Gonçalves, R. C., Reher, A., Kuo, J., Lenné, M. G., & Merat, N. (2025). Assessing data imbalance correction methods and gaze entropy for collision prediction. PLOS ONE, 20, e0336777. DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0336777
recent_1_synopsis: This study examined how different dataset rebalancing strategies affect the reliability of machine-learning models that predict collision risk during automated-to-manual takeovers, using gaze entropy and pupil diameter as driver-state features. It found that rebalancing inflated predicted collision probabilities and that erratic gaze patterns were the strongest predictor of collision risk, while driver workload had minimal effect—informing how driver monitoring systems should be validated.
recent_2: Gonçalves, R. C., Goodridge, C. M., Kuo, J., Lenné, M. G., & Merat, N. (2024). Using driver monitoring to estimate readiness in automation: a conceptual model based on simulator experimental data. Cognition, Technology & Work, 26, 569–584. DOI: 10.1007/s10111-024-00777-3
recent_2_synopsis: This paper presents a conceptual model and methodology for using driver state monitoring (DSM) data to estimate the likelihood that a driver can successfully take back control from Level 2 automation, tested with simulator data. It contributes a principled threshold-setting method for DSM systems that accounts for inter-individual variability in readiness.
recent_3: Mulhall, M., Wilson, K., Yang, S., Kuo, J., Sletten, T., Anderson, C., Howard, M. E., Rajaratnam, S., Magee, M., Collins, A., & Lenné, M. G. (2023). European NCAP Driver State Monitoring Protocols: Prevalence of Distraction in Naturalistic Driving. Human Factors, 66(9), 2205–2217. DOI: 10.1177/00187208231194543
recent_3_synopsis: Using a naturalistic driving dataset with continuous eye-tracking from a real-world driver monitoring system, this study measured how often drivers exhibit Euro NCAP-defined distraction behaviours (long off-road glances and prolonged non-driving-related task engagement). The findings provide an empirical baseline for calibrating Euro NCAP assessment protocols and highlight meaningful individual differences in distraction prevalence.
seminal_1: Beanland, V., Fitzharris, M., Young, K. L., & Lenné, M. G. (2013). Driver inattention and driver distraction in serious casualty crashes: Data from the Australian National Crash In-depth Study. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 54, 99–107. DOI: 10.1016/j.aap.2012.12.043
seminal_1_synopsis: Analysing 856 serious-casualty crashes from the Australian National Crash In-Depth Study, this paper quantified the prevalence and subtypes of driver inattention and distraction, finding evidence of inattention in more than half of codeable cases. With approximately 253 citations (Crossref), it is one of Lenné's most-cited works and a foundational empirical reference for road-safety policy on distraction.
seminal_2: Yang, S., Kuo, J., & Lenné, M. G. (2020). Effects of Distraction in On-Road Level 2 Automated Driving: Impacts on Glance Behavior and Takeover Performance. Human Factors, 63(8), 1485–1497. DOI: 10.1177/0018720820936793
seminal_2_synopsis: This on-road study with real Level 2 automation (Tesla Autopilot) showed that engaging in a phone-based secondary task significantly altered drivers' glance patterns and degraded takeover response performance, establishing naturalistic evidence that distraction impairs safe supervision of automated vehicles. With approximately 36 citations in Human Factors and wider influence across automated-driving research, it is a key early empirical study linking driver monitoring to Level 2 safety.
expertise_overview: Research centres on the human factors of driver state detection and monitoring, encompassing cognitive distraction, visual distraction, drowsiness, and fatigue in both manual and automated driving contexts. A strong methodological emphasis on naturalistic and on-road studies—using instrumented vehicles, eye-tracking, and physiological sensing—anchors findings in ecologically valid driving behaviour rather than simulator approximations alone. Work on automated and advanced-driver-assistance systems addresses how drivers supervise, disengage from, and resume control of partially automated vehicles, including the measurement and prediction of driver readiness for takeover. Lenné's research has directly shaped international road-safety regulation, particularly the Euro NCAP occupant status monitoring protocols that specify how driver state monitoring systems should be evaluated. Across application domains the work spans professional transport (commercial vehicle fatigue management, shift-worker studies) and consumer vehicles, linking human factors science to technology design and safety standards.
notes: Identity confirmed via Seeing Machines staff page (seeingmachines.com/staff/dr-mike-lenne/), Monash University MUARC news, and ResearchGate profile (researchgate.net/profile/Michael-Lenne). Lenné is Adjunct Professor at Monash University Accident Research Centre and Chief Safety Officer at Seeing Machines, Canberra, Australia. All five seed HF paper DOIs confirmed via Crossref. DOI 10.1371/journal.pone.0336777 (recent_1), 10.1007/s10111-024-00777-3 (recent_2), 10.1177/00187208231194543 (recent_3), 10.1016/j.aap.2012.12.043 (seminal_1), and 10.1177/0018720820936793 (seminal_2) all verified via Crossref with matching titles and publication dates. Citation count for seminal_1 (253) is from Crossref as of June 2026; seminal_2 citation count (36) is from Crossref as of June 2026. Overall h-index and publication list sourced from ResearchGate (7,705 citing researchers, 262 publications) and web searches.

name: Loeb, Robert G.
recent_1: Li, S. Y. W., Lee, A. L. F., Chiu, J. W. S., Loeb, R. G., & Sanderson, P. M. (2024). Attention capture by own name decreases with speech compression. Cognitive Research: Principles and Implications, 9(1), 29. DOI: 10.1186/s41235-024-00555-9
recent_1_synopsis: This study tested how compressing speech (speeding it up) affects the classic 'own-name' attention-capture effect, relevant to spoken auditory displays and alarms that compete for clinicians' attention while multitasking. It found that the involuntary pull of hearing one's own name weakens as speech is compressed, with implications for designing time-compressed speech and auditory notifications in monitoring environments.
recent_2: Neary, A., Li, S. Y. W., Salisbury, I., Loeb, R. G., & Sanderson, P. M. (2023). Effects of multitasking on interpreting a spearcon sequence display for monitoring multiple patients. Applied Ergonomics, 112, 104072. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2023.104072
recent_2_synopsis: This experiment evaluated whether anesthesia-style auditory 'spearcon' sequences—compressed speech tokens encoding multiple patients' vital signs—remain interpretable when listeners are simultaneously performing a competing task. It quantifies the cost of multitasking on extracting patient state from sequential auditory displays, informing the design of sound-based monitoring for clinicians overseeing several patients at once.
recent_3: Hinckfuss, K., Sanderson, P. M., Brecknell, B., Loeb, R. G., Liu, D., & Liley, H. (2023). Evaluating enhanced pulse oximetry auditory displays for neonatal oxygen targeting: A randomized laboratory trial with clinicians and non-clinicians. Applied Ergonomics, 107, 103918. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2022.103918
recent_3_synopsis: This randomized laboratory trial compared enhanced pulse-oximetry sonifications against a standard tone for helping listeners keep a neonate's oxygen saturation within a narrow target range, testing both clinicians and non-clinicians. It demonstrates that added acoustic cues (e.g., tremolo/brightness mappings) improve target-range discrimination, supporting safer oxygen titration in neonatal care.
seminal_1: Pascale, M. T., Sanderson, P., Liu, D., Mohamed, I., Brecknell, B., & Loeb, R. G. (2019). The Impact of Head-Worn Displays on Strategic Alarm Management and Situation Awareness. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 61(4), 537-563. DOI: 10.1177/0018720818814969
seminal_1_synopsis: This study examined whether a head-worn display conveying patient vital signs lets anesthesia providers manage alarms more strategically and maintain situation awareness when away from the central monitor. With roughly 34 citations (Semantic Scholar / Google Scholar, 2026-06-17) it is Loeb's most-cited Human Factors journal paper and a key demonstration of wearable peripheral displays for clinical monitoring.
seminal_2: Loeb, R. G. (1993). A measure of intraoperative attention to monitor displays. Anesthesia & Analgesia, 76(2), 337-341. (no Crossref-verifiable DOI)
seminal_2_synopsis: This single-author paper introduced a practical method for quantifying intraoperative vigilance by measuring how quickly providers detect simulated abnormal values on a physiologic monitor, revealing that response was slower and misses more frequent during high-workload induction. With roughly 45 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026-06-17) it is among Loeb's most-cited works and a foundational reference for measuring attention to monitor displays in anesthesia.
expertise_overview: Loeb studies how clinicians perceive and act on patient-monitoring information during anesthesia and critical care, with a central focus on auditory displays—sonification of vital signs, pulse-oximetry tones, earcons, and compressed-speech 'spearcons' for tracking single and multiple patients. He investigates how enhanced acoustic mappings (pitch, tremolo, brightness) improve discrimination of clinically important oxygen-saturation and heart-rate ranges, and how alarms can be made more informative and less fatiguing. A related thread examines visual and head-worn displays, situation awareness, vigilance, and the effects of multitasking and divided attention on extracting patient state from monitors. His methods are largely experimental, using laboratory and simulation studies with clinician and non-clinician participants, response-time and detection measures, and psychoacoustic evaluation of display designs. Application domains span the operating room, neonatal oxygen targeting, and multi-patient monitoring, bridging anesthesiology with human factors and ergonomics.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Robert G. Loeb, anesthesiologist at the University of Florida College of Medicine (Department of Anesthesiology; formerly University of Arizona). Confirmed via UF Department of Anesthesiology faculty profile (anest.ufl.edu/profile/loeb-robert), University of Arizona profiles page, ResearchGate, PubMed authorship, and consistency with the pre-verified Human Factors hf_papers (recurring collaborators Penelope Sanderson, Simon Y. W. Li, Birgit Brecknell, David Liu, Ismail Mohamed; sonification / earcon / spearcon / head-worn-display themes). All cited DOIs verified via Crossref (HTTP 200, matching title and Loeb in author list): recent_1 (10.1186/s41235-024-00555-9), recent_2 (10.1016/j.apergo.2023.104072), recent_3 (10.1016/j.apergo.2022.103918), seminal_1 (10.1177/0018720818814969). Citation counts from Semantic Scholar (cross-checked, retrieved 2026-06-17): seminal_1 ~34; seminal_2 ~45. UNVERIFIABLE CITATION FLAG: seminal_2, 'A measure of intraoperative attention to monitor displays' (Anesthesia & Analgesia 1993, 76(2):337-341, PMID 8424512), has no Crossref-resolvable DOI (older LWW article; Wikidata and Semantic Scholar list only PubMed/MAG IDs), so it is included WITHOUT a DOI and flagged here. It is his most-cited work by Semantic Scholar count and is genuinely his, so it was retained as a seminal entry. The candidate paper 'Beyond monitoring: distributed situation awareness in anaesthesia' (BJA 2010) was excluded because it is authored by Fioratou, Flin, Glavin & Patey, not Loeb.

name: Loft, Shayne
recent_1: Greenwell-Barnden, J., Visser, T. A. W., Loft, S., Whitney, S., & Bowden, V. K. (2026). How Multi-Tasking Ability Impacts Performance, Workload, Situation Awareness, Stress and Trust with Simulated Imperfect Automation. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 68(8), 1056-1077. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261431968
recent_1_synopsis: Using a simulated environment with imperfect automation, this study examined how individual differences in multi-tasking ability relate to performance, workload, situation awareness, stress, and trust. It links operator aptitude to a broad set of human-automation teaming outcomes, informing selection and the design of automation support for people who differ in multi-tasking capacity.
recent_2: Tatasciore, M., & Loft, S. (2025). Calibrating Reliance on Automated Advice: Transparency and Trust Calibration Feedback. International Journal of Human-Computer Interaction, 41(23), 14723-14733. DOI: 10.1080/10447318.2025.2487861
recent_2_synopsis: This experiment tested whether automation transparency combined with trust-calibration feedback helps operators rely on automated advice more appropriately. It shows that transparency and feedback can improve the calibration of reliance, addressing the persistent problem of over- and under-trust in decision-support automation.
recent_3: Tatasciore, M., Strickland, L., & Loft, S. (2024). Transparency improves the accuracy of automation use, but automation confidence information does not. Cognitive Research: Principles and Implications, 9(1), 67. DOI: 10.1186/s41235-024-00599-x
recent_3_synopsis: In an uninhabited-vehicle management task, this study contrasted automation transparency against automation confidence information, finding that transparency improved the accuracy of automation use while confidence information did not (and transparency could increase bias toward agreeing with advice). It clarifies which kinds of explanatory information actually help operators decide when to follow automated advice.
seminal_1: Loft, S., Sanderson, P., Neal, A., & Mooij, M. (2007). Modeling and Predicting Mental Workload in En Route Air Traffic Control: Critical Review and Broader Implications. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 49(3), 376-399. DOI: 10.1518/001872007X197017
seminal_1_synopsis: This critical review synthesized research on mental workload in en route air traffic control and proposed a model of operator strategic behavior through which workload can be predicted in ATC and other complex systems, arguing that the demand-workload relationship depends on controllers' strategies for managing cognitive resources. With roughly 350-400 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), it is among Loft's most influential works and a foundational reference for workload modeling in safety-critical operations.
seminal_2: Loft, S., Smith, R. E., & Remington, R. W. (2013). Minimizing the disruptive effects of prospective memory in simulated air traffic control. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Applied, 19(3), 254-265. DOI: 10.1037/a0034141
seminal_2_synopsis: This experimental paper demonstrated how the costs of holding a prospective (deferred) intention disrupt ongoing performance in simulated air traffic control and tested display interventions that minimize that disruption. With roughly 100+ citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), it is a foundational piece bridging basic prospective-memory theory and applied performance in dynamic operational settings, anchoring Loft's program on remembering to perform deferred tasks.
expertise_overview: Loft studies human cognition and performance in complex, safety-critical work systems, with central themes of prospective (deferred-intention) memory, mental workload, and situation awareness. A major line of work concerns human-automation teaming-particularly automation transparency, automated advice and decision support, trust calibration, and return-to-manual performance when automation fails or varies in reliability. His applied domains include air traffic control, submarine track management, and command-and-control, where he observes operator behavior in high-fidelity simulations and then brings the phenomena under experimental control in the laboratory. Methodologically he combines controlled experiments, cognitive modeling of workload and behavior, and the development and validation of situation-awareness and workload measures. Recent work emphasizes how transparency, attention-control capacity, and multi-tasking ability shape appropriate reliance on imperfect automation.

name: Lu, Lu

recent_2: Su, B., Qing, L., Lu, L., Jung, S., Fang, X., & Xu, X. (2025). Enhancing Data Privacy in Human Factors Studies with Federated Learning. Human Factors, 67(7). DOI: 10.1177/00187208251348025

It addresses a growing privacy concern in data-intensive ergonomics and worker-monitoring studies while preserving predictive performance.
recent_3: Jung, S., Su, B., Lu, L., Qing, L., & Xu, X. (2024). Video-Based Lifting Action Recognition Using Rank-Altered Kinematic Feature Pairs. Human Factors, 66(12). DOI: 10.1177/00187208241309748

The approach supports automated, low-cost assessment of physically demanding tasks that drive musculoskeletal-disorder risk.
seminal_1: Lu, L., Xie, Z., Wang, H., Li, L., & Xu, X. (2022). Mental stress and safety awareness during human-robot collaboration – Review. Applied Ergonomics, 105, 103832. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2022.103832
seminal_1_synopsis: This systematic review synthesizes how robot-related factors elicit mental stress and degrade safety awareness in human workers during collaboration, screening 153 articles and analyzing 25 in depth. seminal_2: Xie, Z., Lu, L., Wang, H., Su, B., Liu, Y., & Xu, X. (2024). Improving Workers' Musculoskeletal Health During Human-Robot Collaboration Through Reinforcement Learning. Human Factors, 66(6), 1707–1722. DOI: 10.1177/00187208231177574
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper develops a model-free reinforcement learning method that adapts robot behavior to improve worker posture and reduce musculoskeletal-disorder risk during collaboration. With about 12 citations (Crossref), it is among the author's most-cited works and demonstrates a learning-based path to ergonomically aware collaborative robots.
expertise_overview: Research centers on human–robot collaboration in industrial and occupational settings, with particular attention to workers' mental stress, psychological safety, and safety awareness when working alongside collaborative robots. A recurring theme is how robot design factors—non-verbal actions, anthropomorphic appearance, voice, and interaction modality—shape human perception, emotional response, and stress, measured through subjective scales (e.g., NASA-TLX) and physiological signals such as galvanic skin response and electromyography. The work also spans occupational ergonomics and musculoskeletal-disorder prevention, including reinforcement-learning controllers that adapt robot behavior to protect worker posture. Methodologically, it draws heavily on machine learning and computer vision, including video-based action recognition of lifting tasks and wearable-sensor analysis for biomechanical risk assessment. More recent directions address data-privacy-preserving methods such as federated learning for human factors datasets. Application domains include manufacturing, manual material handling, and human-centered design of co-robots.

name: Läubli, Thomas
recent_1: Wagenblast, F., Läubli, T., Seibt, R., Luger, T., Rieger, M. A., & Steinhilber, B. (2026). Bilateral trapezius activity during a dual task depends on age and sex – Results of an experimental laboratory study. Journal of Electromyography and Kinesiology, 88, 103129. DOI: 10.1016/j.jelekin.2026.103129
recent_1_synopsis: This study examined bilateral upper trapezius EMG activity in 48 participants (younger/older, male/female) across four dual-task conditions combining two wrist muscle load levels with easy or difficult cognitive memory loads. Results showed that trapezius activity and relative rest time depended on age and sex in interaction with the muscular and cognitive load, extending understanding of how demographic factors modulate secondary neck-shoulder muscle recruitment during repetitive arm work with cognitive demands.
recent_2: Wagenblast, F., Läubli, T., Seibt, R., Rieger, M. A., & Steinhilber, B. (2023). Wrist Extensor Muscle Fatigue During a Dual Task With Two Muscular and Cognitive Load Levels in Younger and Older Adults. Human Factors, 65(8), 1594–1608. DOI: 10.1177/00187208231218196
recent_2_synopsis: Participants performed a dual task combining intermittent isometric wrist contractions at two load levels with a concurrent cognitive memory task, with EMG measures of wrist extensor fatigue recorded across sessions. The study revealed how cognitive load interacts with muscular load to modulate fatigue development, and how these effects differ between younger and older adults, with implications for workplace design in populations with aging workforces.
recent_3: Kim, D., Nicoletti, C., Soedirdjo, S. D. H., Baghi, R., Garcia, M.-G., Läubli, T., Wild, P., Botter, A., & Martin, B. J. (2021). Effect of Periodic Voluntary Interventions on Trapezius Activation and Fatigue During Light Upper Limb Activity. Human Factors, 63(8), 1332–1345. DOI: 10.1177/00187208211050723
recent_3_synopsis: This laboratory study examined whether periodic voluntary muscle contractions during light, sustained upper limb work could modify trapezius activation patterns and reduce local muscle fatigue. Findings indicated that deliberate brief interventions altered motor unit recruitment and reduced fatigue indicators, suggesting that active self-initiated breaks targeting the trapezius may be a practical strategy to mitigate work-related neck-shoulder disorders.
seminal_1: Hünting, W., Läubli, T., & Grandjean, E. (1981). Postural and visual loads at VDT workplaces I. Constrained postures. Ergonomics, 24(12), 917–931. DOI: 10.1080/00140138108924914
seminal_1_synopsis: One of the earliest systematic studies of posture and visual strain among visual display terminal (VDT) workers, this paper documented how data-entry and conversational terminal work imposed constrained postures and head positions that differed markedly from conventional office work. With approximately 204 Crossref citations (likely far more via Google Scholar), it became a foundational reference establishing that VDT work created distinct ergonomic risk profiles, directly informing subsequent decades of standards development and workplace intervention research.
seminal_2: Garcia, M.-G., Läubli, T., & Martin, B. J. (2015). Long-Term Muscle Fatigue After Standing Work. Human Factors, 57(7), 1162–1173. DOI: 10.1177/0018720815590293
seminal_2_synopsis: Using muscle twitch force as an objective fatigue indicator, this study demonstrated that five hours of simulated standing work produced lower-limb fatigue that persisted beyond 30 minutes after the workday ended, with no meaningful difference between younger and older adults. With approximately 59 Crossref citations, the paper reframed standing work from a putative health benefit to a musculoskeletal risk with sustained physiological consequences, becoming a key empirical anchor in debates over sit-stand workstation recommendations.
expertise_overview: Thomas Läubli is a Swiss occupational health researcher whose work centers on the physiological mechanisms by which sustained and repetitive work causes musculoskeletal disorders, particularly in the neck-shoulder region and lower limbs. His primary methodological tools include surface electromyography (EMG) — including motor unit analysis and spectral fatigue indices — and muscle twitch force assessment to quantify acute and long-term muscle fatigue under ecologically valid work conditions. He has studied how task characteristics such as muscular load level, cognitive demand, work-rest schedules, and posture (prolonged standing, seated VDT work) interact to drive fatigue and disorder risk. A recurring theme is how age and sex moderate these physiological responses, with implications for ergonomic accommodation of diverse workforces. His applied research addresses interventions (work rotations, active breaks, workstation redesign) and occupational populations including office workers, nurses, and industrial workers. Institutionally he has been affiliated with ETH Zürich and the University of Zürich, and he has contributed to the scientific community as chair of the ICOH Scientific Committee on Musculoskeletal Disorders.

name: Manzey, Dietrich
recent_1: Rieger, T., Marx, B., & Manzey, D. (2025). Likelihood systems can improve hit rates in low-prevalence visual search over binary systems. Human Factors, 67(2), 152-167. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251320589
recent_1_synopsis: This study compared likelihood (graded-confidence) alarm systems against conventional binary alarms in a low-prevalence visual search task, where rare targets typically erode detection performance. Likelihood systems improved hit rates relative to binary systems, suggesting that conveying graded diagnostic information helps operators calibrate attention and catch rare critical events.
recent_2: Rieger, T., Onnasch, L., Roesler, E., & Manzey, D. (2025). Why highly reliable decision support systems often lead to suboptimal performance and what we can do about it. IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems, 55(5), 736-745. DOI: 10.1109/THMS.2025.3584662
recent_2_synopsis: The paper explains the counterintuitive finding that joint human-automation performance with highly reliable decision aids frequently falls short of the aid's standalone reliability, tracing this to complacency, automation bias, and over-reliance. It synthesizes the mechanisms and proposes design and procedural interventions to recover the performance potential of accurate decision support.
recent_3: Rieger, T., Kugler, L., Manzey, D., & Roesler, E. (2024). The (im)perfect automation schema: Who is trusted more, automated or human decision support? Human Factors, 66(8), 1995-2007. DOI: 10.1177/00187208231197347
recent_3_synopsis: Using a decision-support paradigm, this study tested whether people hold an "imperfect automation schema" that leads them to trust automated advisors differently from human advisors of equal reliability. The results clarify how the perceived agent type (automated vs. human) shapes trust and reliance, informing how decision aids should be framed to operators.
seminal_1: Parasuraman, R., & Manzey, D. H. (2010). Complacency and bias in human use of automation: An attentional integration. Human Factors, 52(3), 381-410. DOI: 10.1177/0018720810376055
seminal_1_synopsis: This landmark review integrates the literature on automation complacency and automation bias, arguing that both arise from common attention-allocation mechanisms under multitask load and occur in novices and experts alike. With roughly 3,000+ citations, it is among the most influential theoretical accounts of automation-induced performance failures and frames much subsequent work on trust, reliance, and decision support.
seminal_2: Manzey, D., Reichenbach, J., & Onnasch, L. (2012). Human performance consequences of automated decision aids: The impact of degree of automation and system experience. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making, 6(1), 57-87. DOI: 10.1177/1555343411433844
seminal_2_synopsis: This experimental paper dissects how the degree of automation and accumulated system experience jointly shape operators' commission and omission errors when using imperfect decision aids. Cited roughly 500+ times, it provides foundational empirical evidence that higher automation can amplify automation bias and that experience does not reliably immunize users against it.
expertise_overview: Studies human-automation interaction, with a central focus on how people trust, rely on, and are degraded by automated and AI-based decision support. Core themes include automation complacency and automation bias, the calibration of trust and reliance, and the consequences of varying degrees and stages of automation for human performance and error. Investigates alarm and alert design, contrasting binary alarms with likelihood (graded) systems in visual search and monitoring, particularly under time pressure and low target prevalence. Also examines human-robot interaction, including how anthropomorphism and failure type affect trust attitudes versus trust behavior. Methods are primarily controlled laboratory experiments with performance, error, and subjective-trust measures; application domains span aviation, medical/diagnostic monitoring, and human performance in extreme environments such as space and Antarctica.
notes: Identified with high confidence via Google Scholar (user=EmFhGH8AAAAJ), ResearchGate, dblp, and TU Berlin association; the hf_papers anchor DOIs (all listing Manzey as author) match exactly. name: Markkula, Gustav
recent_1: Lee, Y. M., Madigan, R., Wang, Y., Garcia, J., Qin, H., Srinivasan, A., Markkula, G., et al. (2026). Crossing in the dark: Investigating the effect of vehicle kinematics and eHMI on older pedestrians' crossing behavior in a virtual reality experiment. Journal of Safety Research, 96, 315-328. DOI: 10.1016/j.jsr.2026.01.012
recent_1_synopsis: Using a virtual reality experiment, this study examined how vehicle deceleration behavior and external Human-Machine Interfaces (eHMIs) shape older versus younger pedestrians' road-crossing decisions in low-visibility (dark) conditions. Older pedestrians adopted a distinct crossing strategy, compensating for slower crossing speeds by initiating earlier, and they interpreted and used eHMI cues differently than younger pedestrians. The findings matter for designing automated-vehicle communication that is safe and inclusive across age groups.
recent_2: Ma, S., Yan, X., Ma, L., Billington, J., Merat, N., & Markkula, G. (2026). Modelling cognitive load using drift-diffusion models in pedestrian street-crossing: a method supported by neural evidence. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 225, 108310. DOI: 10.1016/j.aap.2025.108310
recent_2_synopsis: This work used drift-diffusion (evidence-accumulation) models to quantify how cognitive load alters pedestrians' street-crossing decisions, validating model predictions against the centro-parietal positive potential (CPP), a neural signature of evidence accumulation. Cognitive load reduced sensitivity to time-to-arrival, slowed responses, and raised collision risk, with the best model showing lowered responsiveness to decision utilities under load. It demonstrates that neurally grounded computational models can capture how secondary-task demand degrades safety-critical pedestrian behavior.
recent_3: Kalantari, A. H., Lin, Y.-S., Mohammadi, A., Merat, N., & Markkula, G. (2025). Testing the validity of multiparticipant distributed simulation for understanding and modeling road user interaction. IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems, 55(5). DOI: 10.1109/THMS.2025.3591506
recent_3_synopsis: This paper validates distributed (networked) driving simulation, in which a human driver and a human pedestrian interact simultaneously in a shared virtual environment, as a method for studying genuine road-user interaction. By comparing behavior across kinematic conditions, the authors assess whether multiparticipant simulation reproduces realistic interactive dynamics that conventional single-participant setups cannot. The work supports a new experimental paradigm for collecting interaction data to build and test models of vehicle-pedestrian negotiation.
seminal_1: Engstrom, J., Markkula, G., Victor, T., & Merat, N. (2017). Effects of cognitive load on driving performance: The cognitive control hypothesis. Human Factors, 59(5), 734-764. DOI: 10.1177/0018720817690639
seminal_1_synopsis: This highly cited paper (approximately 250 cites per the seed list; ~382 cites on Google Scholar as of June 2026) proposes the 'cognitive control hypothesis' to reconcile conflicting findings on how cognitive load affects driving. It argues that cognitive load selectively impairs performance on tasks requiring cognitive control while leaving (or even improving) automatized, routine driving behaviors intact. The framework is foundational for understanding driver distraction and has guided subsequent research on secondary-task interference and attention in driving.
seminal_2: McDonald, A. D., Alambeigi, H., Engstrom, J., Markkula, G., Vogelpohl, T., Dunne, J., & Yuma, N. (2019). Toward computational simulations of behavior during automated driving takeovers: A review of the empirical and modeling literatures. Human Factors, 61(4), 642-688. DOI: 10.1177/0018720819829572
seminal_2_synopsis: This review (approximately 249 cites per the seed list; ~365 cites on Google Scholar as of June 2026) synthesizes the empirical and modeling literature on how drivers resume control when automated driving systems hand back the driving task. It maps the determinants of takeover performance and lays out a roadmap toward computational simulation of takeover behavior. It is foundational for human-factors research on conditionally automated driving and the design of safe transition-of-control protocols.
expertise_overview: Gustav Markkula specializes in quantitative, cognitive computational modeling of road-user behavior and interaction. His core methods include evidence-accumulation (drift-diffusion) models, sensorimotor and neuromuscular control models of steering and braking, and increasingly machine-learning and multi-agent reinforcement-learning approaches, often validated against neural signals (e.g., the centro-parietal positive potential) and naturalistic or simulator data. Application domains center on driver behavior (distraction, cognitive load, emergency braking, takeover from automation) and vulnerable road users, especially pedestrian crossing decisions and driver-pedestrian negotiation. He works extensively with driving and distributed (multiparticipant) virtual-reality simulation, external Human-Machine Interface (eHMI) communication, and gap-acceptance and merging interaction modeling. A recurring theme is building mechanistic, theory-grounded models of human perception, decision-making, and action that can support the virtual testing and safe design of automated vehicles.

name: Marquez, Jessica J.

recent_1_synopsis: Reports on how analog astronauts used self-scheduling during Campaign 7 of NASA's Human Exploration Research Analog (HERA), characterizing the volume, timing, and types of crew-driven schedule modifications. recent_3: Shelat, S., Marquez, J. J., Zheng, J., & Karasinski, J. A. (2024). Collaborative System Usability in Spaceflight Analog Environments through Remote Observations. Applied Sciences, 14(5), 2005. DOI: 10.3390/app14052005
recent_3_synopsis: Develops and validates a remote conversation-analysis method to assess the usability of collaborative planning technology used by crews in NASA's HERA analog environment without on-site observers. It demonstrates that remote observation can yield credible usability evidence for spaceflight-analog studies, addressing a practical constraint of isolated, confined missions.
seminal_1: Marquez, J. J., Miller, M. J., Cohen, T., Deliz, I., Lees, D. S., Zheng, J., & Hillenius, S. (2019). Future Needs for Science-Driven Geospatial and Temporal Extravehicular Activity Planning and Execution. Astrobiology, 19(3), 440-461. DOI: 10.1089/ast.2018.1838
seminal_1_synopsis: Articulates the information, geospatial, and temporal planning requirements for science-driven extravehicular activity (EVA) on future planetary surface missions, drawing on analog field campaigns. seminal_2: Marquez, J. J., Hillenius, S., Kanefsky, B., Zheng, J., Deliz, I., & Reagan, M. (2017). Increasing crew autonomy for long duration exploration missions: Self-scheduling. 2017 IEEE Aerospace Conference, 1-10. DOI: 10.1109/AERO.2017.7943838

With approximately 23 citations (Crossref), it is foundational to the Playbook line of research and is widely referenced as an early framing of crew-autonomous planning for exploration missions.
expertise_overview: Space human factors and human-systems integration for spaceflight operations, with a focus on planning, scheduling, and timeline-execution tools for astronauts and mission controllers. Studies crew autonomy and self-scheduling as operational concepts for long-duration, high-latency deep-space missions, including the design and evaluation of the Playbook web-based scheduling tool. Investigates human-automation and human-robot interaction, usability assessment, and crew performance in isolated, confined, and extreme environments using analog missions such as HERA and NEEMO. Methods span usability and human performance studies, qualitative field observation (including remote conversation analysis), and human capability assessment for autonomous operations. Application domains center on extravehicular activity (EVA) planning, mission operations, and decision support for Moon-to-Mars exploration.
notes: Identification confidence: high. Anchored by the four pre-verified Human Factors papers (all authored by Marquez at NASA Ames).
