name: Matthews, Gerald
recent_1: Matthews, G., & Bliuc, A.-M. (2026). Personality, identity, and Artificial Intelligence: a grand challenge. Frontiers in Psychology, 17, 1817687. DOI: 10.3389/fpsyg.2026.1817687
recent_1_synopsis: This forward-looking grand-challenge article argues that the rapid integration of AI into everyday life raises fundamental questions for personality and identity research, including how human dispositions shape interaction with AI and how AI in turn reshapes the self. Drawing on individual-differences theory, Matthews frames a research agenda spanning trust, social identity, and the psychological consequences of human-AI collaboration. It matters because it positions personality science as central to understanding and designing human-AI relationships rather than treating AI use as uniform across users.
recent_2: Matthews, G., Cumings, R., De Los Santos, E. P., Feng, I. Y., & Mouloua, S. A. (2025). A new era for stress research: supporting user performance and experience in the digital age. Ergonomics, 68(6), 913-946. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2024.2425953
recent_2_synopsis: This review charts future directions for ergonomics-oriented stress research as work and daily life become saturated with novel technologies, examining stressors arising from interaction with AI, robots, virtual reality, and autonomous vehicles at the individual level and from cybersecurity, surveillance, and workplace bias at the organizational level. It integrates Matthews's transactional, multidimensional model of task stress with emerging digital-age demands. The work is influential as a research-agenda piece, already accumulating roughly 21 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), and it guides how designers and researchers conceptualize technology-induced stress.
recent_3: Schreck, J., Matthews, G., Lin, J., Mondesire, S., Metcalf, D., Dickerson, K., & Grasso, J. (2025). Levels of Automation for a Computer-Based Procedure for Simulated Nuclear Power Plant Operation: Impacts on Workload and Trust. Safety, 11(1), 22. DOI: 10.3390/safety11010022
recent_3_synopsis: This experiment compared management-by-consent (lower) versus management-by-exception (higher) levels of automation supporting a computer-based emergency operating procedure in a simulated nuclear power plant, indexing operator workload with subjective, psychophysiological, and performance measures alongside trust. Higher automation reduced workload on a behavioral secondary-task measure but produced mixed effects across other indices, illustrating dissociations among workload measures. It informs the design of automation aids for high-consequence process-control settings where workload reduction must be balanced against situation awareness and trust calibration.
seminal_1: Warm, J. S., Parasuraman, R., & Matthews, G. (2008). Vigilance Requires Hard Mental Work and Is Stressful. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 50(3), 433-441. DOI: 10.1518/001872008X312152
seminal_1_synopsis: This landmark review overturned the long-held view that sustained-attention (vigilance) tasks are understimulating and easy, marshalling evidence from workload ratings, cerebral blood-flow velocity, and task-induced stress to show that vigilance imposes high mental workload and genuine stress. With approximately 1,714 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is one of the most-cited articles in the vigilance literature and a touchstone Human Factors paper. It is foundational because it reframed the theoretical account of the vigilance decrement around resource depletion and stress, directly shaping subsequent neuroergonomic and operator-monitoring research.
seminal_2: Wells, A., & Matthews, G. (1996). Modelling cognition in emotional disorder: The S-REF model. Behaviour Research and Therapy, 34(11-12), 881-888. DOI: 10.1016/S0005-7967(96)00050-2
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper introduced the Self-Regulatory Executive Function (S-REF) model, integrating information-processing research with schema theory to explain how self-focused attention, metacognition, and perseverative processing maintain emotional disorder. With approximately 1,950 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is among Matthews's most-cited journal articles and the theoretical foundation underpinning Metacognitive Therapy. It is foundational because it supplied an influential, testable cognitive-architecture account of emotional dysregulation that bridges Matthews's work on attention, stress states, and individual differences with clinical psychology.
expertise_overview: Matthews studies human performance, stress, and individual differences, with central interests in the assessment of task-induced stress, workload, and fatigue, including his development of the Dundee Stress State Questionnaire and a multidimensional model organizing transient states around task engagement, distress, and worry. A major strand addresses vigilance and sustained attention, framing the vigilance decrement in terms of resource depletion, stress, and neuroergonomic indices such as cerebral blood-flow velocity. He also investigates cognition and emotion, including the S-REF metacognitive model of emotional disorder, and is known for an influential critical appraisal of the emotional-intelligence construct. More recently his work centers on human-machine and human-AI teaming, examining how personality and individual differences predict trust in robots, autonomous systems, and AI partners. Methodologically he combines psychometric scale development, controlled performance experiments, psychophysiological measurement, and multivariate individual-differences modeling, with application domains spanning aviation and UAS operation, driving and transportation, process control, and security/threat assessment.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Gerald Matthews, Professor of Psychology, George Mason University (previously University of Dundee, University of Cincinnati, and Research Professor at the University of Central Florida's Institute for Simulation and Training), and Associate Editor of Human Factors. Confirmed via his GMU Psychology and Human Factors faculty pages (psychology.gmu.edu/people/gmatthe; humanfactors.gmu.edu/people/gmatthe) and Google Scholar profile (user 9-y-tKAAAAAJ). Disambiguation anchored on HF topics (vigilance, task stress, the Dundee Stress State Questionnaire) and co-authors (Warm, Hancock, Szalma, Reinerman-Jones, Parasuraman), matching the ground-truth HF papers; care was taken to attribute the emotional-intelligence and S-REF works to the same scholar via consistent co-authorship and the unified Scholar profile. Citation counts are approximate, from Google Scholar retrieved 2026-06-17 (Vigilance Requires Hard Mental Work ~1,714; S-REF model ~1,950; A new era for stress research ~21). All DOIs cited (5 new: the three recent papers plus the two seminal papers; the 9 ground-truth Human Factors DOIs are pre-verified) were verified via the Crossref API on 2026-06-17 and returned HTTP 200 with matching titles. No unverifiable citations were included. Note: several of Matthews's most-cited works are books without DOIs and were therefore not used as seminal entries, including 'Emotional Intelligence: Science and Myth' (MIT Press, 2002, ~2,849 citations) and 'Personality Traits' (Cambridge University Press, 2003, ~3,181 citations); the DOI-bearing journal articles selected as seminal are themselves very highly cited and field-foundational.

name: McLaughlin, Anne Collins
recent_1: Pak, R., Rovira, E., & McLaughlin, A. C. (2025). Polite AI mitigates user susceptibility to AI hallucinations. Ergonomics, 68(10), 1735–1745. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2024.2434604
recent_1_synopsis: In a between-subjects experiment, participants completed a difficult quiz aided by either a polite or neutral-toned AI chatbot that occasionally provided hallucinated (incorrect) information. Signal-detection analysis showed that users interacting with the polite AI were modestly more sensitive at detecting hallucinations and adopted a more conservative response bias, suggesting conversational tone as a design lever for improving critical evaluation of generative-AI output.
recent_2: McLaughlin, A. C., Coleman, M. G., Byrne, V., Benton, R., Lodge, F., & Patten, T. (2025). Cognitive aid design using diminished reality to support selective attention by reducing distraction. Human Factors, 67(9), 937–961. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251325169
recent_2_synopsis: This study tested whether "diminished reality" interfaces—which visually suppress task-irrelevant information rather than adding cues—can support selective attention by reducing distraction during a complex task. It extends cognition-aid design principles toward augmented/diminished reality, showing how removing visual clutter can offload attentional demands.
recent_3: Matalenas, L. A., & McLaughlin, A. C. (2025). Training flexible spatial-cognitive estimation strategies using augmented reality. Ergonomics, 68(3), 425–443. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2024.2332768
recent_3_synopsis: Two experiments examined whether augmented reality can train transferable spatial-estimation skills, using portion estimation as the test case and manipulating whether AR scaffolded a decomposition strategy versus a no-AR control. The work demonstrates how AR can teach flexible cognitive strategies that persist beyond the aided condition, informing the design of training versus crutch-style aids.
seminal_1: Green, C. S., Bavelier, D., Kramer, A. F., Vinogradov, S., Ansorge, U., Ball, K. K., Bingel, U., Chein, J. M., Colzato, L. S., Edwards, J. D., Facoetti, A., Gazzaley, A., Gathercole, S. E., Ghisletta, P., Gori, S., Granic, I., Hillman, C. H., Hommel, B., Jaeggi, S. M., … Witt, C. M. (2019). Improving methodological standards in behavioral interventions for cognitive enhancement. Journal of Cognitive Enhancement, 3(1), 2–29. DOI: 10.1007/s41465-018-0115-y
seminal_1_synopsis: This large multi-author consensus paper, on which McLaughlin is a co-author, sets out widely agreed-upon best methodological practices for behavioral cognitive-enhancement interventions—covering control-group design, feasibility versus efficacy versus effectiveness studies, outcome reporting, and dissemination. With roughly 315 citations (Google Scholar), it is her most-cited work and serves as a foundational rigor standard for the brain-training and cognitive-training literature.
seminal_2: Rogers, W. A., Fisk, A. D., McLaughlin, A. C., & Pak, R. (2005). Touch a screen or turn a knob: Choosing the best device for the job. Human Factors, 47(2), 271–288. DOI: 10.1518/0018720054679452
seminal_2_synopsis: This study assessed how task demands and user age affect performance with a direct input device (touch screen) versus an indirect device (rotary knob), finding that the optimal device depends jointly on the task and the user's age. With roughly 239 citations (Google Scholar), it is a foundational empirical reference for age-aware input-device selection and human factors of interface control design.
expertise_overview: Studies how learning and cognition change with age and how interfaces, training programs, and cognitive aids can be designed to support older and younger adults. Core topics include attention control and selective attention, working memory and individual differences in cognitive ability, the motivation for and benefits of cognitively complex activities, and training people to use technology. Applies these to game-based and digital-game training, augmented and diminished reality aids, input-device and display design, and increasingly to human interaction with AI and automation (trust, reliance, AI etiquette, and susceptibility to AI hallucinations). Methodologically she favors controlled behavioral experiments, randomized intervention designs, signal-detection and individual-differences analyses, and rigorous methodological standards for cognitive-training research. Application domains span aging and gerontechnology, health and medical devices, education, and military/defense systems.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. Matched via Google Scholar profile (user=gGh0i8AAAAAJ), NC State CHASS faculty page (chass.ncsu.edu/people/acmclaug), and her LACElab site (acmclaug.wordpress.ncsu.edu); the seed hf_papers (e.g., the 2025 diminished-reality and 2023 attention-control papers) appear directly on her Scholar profile, anchoring disambiguation. She is a Human Factors / cognitive-aging researcher at NC State (LACElab), co-author with Wendy Rogers/Richard Pak of "Designing Displays for Older Adults," and an Associate Editor of Human Factors. Citation counts and recency are from Google Scholar as of June 2026 (total citations ~3,628, h-index 29). All cited DOIs verified via Crossref (HTTP 200, matching title): 10.1080/00140139.2024.2434604, 10.1177/00187208251325169 (pre-verified seed), 10.1080/00140139.2024.2332768, 10.1007/s41465-018-0115-y, 10.1518/0018720054679452. No unverifiable citations. Note that recent_2 and recent_3 are dated 2025 by journal issue but appeared online in 2024.

name: Mehta, Ranjana K.
recent_1: Kang, J., Payne, S. C., Sasangohar, F., & Mehta, R. K. (2025). The Impacts of Rotating Shiftwork on Worker Fatigue Levels and Associated Adaptations: A Naturalistic Study Across Offshore Platforms in the Gulf of Mexico. Human Factors, 68(1), 109-122. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251358635
recent_1_synopsis: This naturalistic field study tracked worker fatigue across rotating shift schedules on offshore oil and gas platforms in the Gulf of Mexico, characterizing how fatigue accumulates and how workers adapt over the shift cycle. It matters because it brings rigorous fatigue measurement into a real-world safety-critical industrial setting rather than the laboratory, informing shift-design and fatigue-risk-management practices for offshore operations.
recent_2: Yadav, A., Hopko, S. K., Pagilla, P. R., & Mehta, R. K. (2025). Enhancing Trust Examinations with Neural Measures during Human-Robot Collaboration under Cognitive Fatigue. ACM Transactions on Human-Robot Interaction, 15(2), 1-27. DOI: 10.1145/3773905
recent_2_synopsis: Using functional brain imaging during physical human-robot collaboration, this study examined how cognitive fatigue and robot reliability jointly shape trust, finding that prefrontal connectivity patterns reorganize under fatigue to sustain mental effort during unreliable robot behavior. It advances the case for neural measures as objective, real-time markers of trust in collaborative robotics, complementing self-report and behavioral indicators.
recent_3: Wei, R., McDonald, A. D., Mehta, R. K., & Garcia, A. (2024). Active Inference Models of AV Takeovers: Relating Model Parameters to Trust, Situation Awareness, and Fatigue. Human Factors, 67(6), 616-634. DOI: 10.1177/00187208241295932
recent_3_synopsis: This paper applies active inference, a computational modeling framework, to driver takeover behavior in automated vehicles, linking model parameters to psychological constructs of trust, situation awareness, and fatigue. It matters because it offers a principled, mechanistic account of why and how drivers resume control, bridging cognitive theory and applied automotive human-factors design.
seminal_1: Mehta, R. K., & Parasuraman, R. (2013). Neuroergonomics: a review of applications to physical and cognitive work. Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 7, 889. DOI: 10.3389/fnhum.2013.00889
seminal_1_synopsis: This widely cited review (~429 citations, Google Scholar, June 2026) lays out the neuroergonomics framework for studying the brain at work, surveying applications spanning physical and cognitive labor and mobile brain-imaging methods. It is foundational because it consolidated and helped define the field, providing a conceptual scaffold that much subsequent neuroergonomics research builds upon.
seminal_2: Mehta, R. K., & Parasuraman, R. (2014). Effects of Mental Fatigue on the Development of Physical Fatigue: A Neuroergonomic Approach. Human Factors, 56(4), 645-656. DOI: 10.1177/0018720813507279
seminal_2_synopsis: This study (~211 citations, Google Scholar, June 2026) used prefrontal-cortex activation measures during fatiguing handgrip exercise to show that prior mental fatigue accelerates the development of physical fatigue. It is influential for empirically demonstrating the brain-based coupling between cognitive and muscular fatigue, a central premise of Mehta's broader neuroergonomics program.
expertise_overview: Mehta studies neuroergonomics, the brain-based investigation of human performance during physical and cognitive work, with a particular focus on fatigue, mental workload, and stress. She examines how cognitive and neuromuscular fatigue interact and how operator states can be monitored and predicted, using methods such as functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS), EEG, other neurophysiological and wearable sensing, and computational modeling. A major thread of her work concerns trust and collaboration in human-robot interaction, including how fatigue and robot reliability shape neural correlates of trust. She also investigates individual differences such as sex and aging effects on fatigability, adaptive AR/VR and brain-computer interfaces, and driver behavior in automated vehicles. Her application domains include safety-critical settings such as offshore oil and gas, emergency response, manufacturing, and space exploration, as well as occupational health and workplace design (e.g., sit-stand and stand-biased desks).
notes: Confidently identified (found=yes). Identity confirmed via Google Scholar profile (user Khg_OvoAAAAJ), University of Wisconsin-Madison College of Engineering / ISyE faculty page and UW Emergency Medicine page, Texas A&M Scholars/VIVO profile, and ResearchGate; ORCID 0000-0002-8254-8365. Disambiguation: this is the neuroergonomics researcher who directs the NeuroErgonomics Lab, formerly at Texas A&M University and now at UW-Madison (Grainger Institute Professor, Dept. of Industrial & Systems Engineering); confirmed by topical match (fatigue, fNIRS, human-robot trust) to the ground-truth Human Factors papers. Citation counts for seminal papers are approximate, from Google Scholar as of 2026-06-17 (~429 and ~211). All DOIs were verified via Crossref (api.crossref.org) on 2026-06-17, each returning HTTP 200 with matching title and metadata: recent_1 10.1177/00187208251358635, recent_2 10.1145/3773905, recent_3 10.1177/00187208241295932, seminal_1 10.3389/fnhum.2013.00889, seminal_2 10.1177/0018720813507279. No unverifiable citations. Note: recent_2 (ACM THRI, vol 15, iss 2) carries a Crossref publication date of 2025-12-22 (issue assignment) for an article in the 2025 volume; treated as a 2025 publication.

name: Merat, Natasha
recent_1: Lee, Y. M., Madigan, R., Wang, Y., Garcia, J., Qin, H., Srinivasan, A., Markkula, G., & Merat, N. (2026). Crossing in the dark: Investigating the effect of vehicle kinematics and eHMI on older pedestrians' crossing behavior in a virtual reality experiment. Journal of Safety Research, 96, 315–328. DOI: 10.1016/j.jsr.2026.01.012
recent_1_synopsis: This virtual reality experiment compared how older and younger pedestrians use vehicle kinematics and external HMI (eHMI) light signals to decide when to cross a road at night. Older pedestrians adopted a more cautious strategy — initiating crossings earlier to compensate for their slower crossing pace — and both age groups perceived nighttime crossings without eHMI as less safe, though they interpreted eHMI cues differently. The findings highlight that eHMI design must account for age-related differences in how pedestrians read and respond to automated vehicle communication.
recent_2: Arabian, A., Goodridge, C. M., Gonçalves, R. C., & Merat, N. (2026). Guiding drivers' attention to the right place at the right time during transitions from SAE level 2 driving system: Effects of warning directionality and audiovisual asynchrony. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 118, 103505. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2025.103505
recent_2_synopsis: This study examined whether directional auditory warnings can guide driver gaze toward a hazard location during takeover events that require a lane change in SAE Level 2 driving. The experiment manipulated warning directionality and the timing offset (asynchrony) between the auditory and visual alerts to identify optimal alerting configurations for safe transitions. The results have practical implications for in-vehicle warning system design, showing that appropriately timed and directional cues meaningfully reduce the attentional cost of resuming manual control.
recent_3: Carlowitz, S., Madigan, R., Lee, Y. M., Tango, F., & Merat, N. (2023). Pedestrians' perceptions of automated vehicle movements and light-based eHMIs in real world conditions: A test track study. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 95, 83–97. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2023.02.010
recent_3_synopsis: This test-track study assessed pedestrian detection distances and subjective responses to two light-band eHMI designs fitted to an automated vehicle under naturalistic conditions. Participants detected a partial light band at greater distances than a full light band, and both designs received positive ratings for acceptance, perceived safety, and confidence. The study provides on-road evidence — rather than simulator or lab evidence — that light-based eHMIs can effectively communicate automated vehicle intent to pedestrians.
seminal_1: Merat, N., Jamson, A. H., Lai, F. C. H., Daly, M., & Carsten, O. M. J. (2014). Transition to manual: Driver behaviour when resuming control from a highly automated vehicle. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 27, 274–282. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2014.09.005
seminal_1_synopsis: This simulator study was among the first to systematically characterise driver visual and behavioural responses in the critical moments after automated driving disengages and the driver must resume manual control. It demonstrated that drivers' gaze patterns remained disorganised for a substantial period post-takeover, particularly when automation switched off mid-distraction, establishing that the 'transition of control' is a distinct safety-critical phase requiring specific HMI design attention. The paper has approximately 928 citations on Google Scholar (513 on Crossref as of June 2026) and is widely regarded as a foundational empirical reference in the human factors of conditional automation.
seminal_2: Merat, N., Jamson, A. H., Lai, F. C. H., & Carsten, O. (2012). Highly Automated Driving, Secondary Task Performance, and Driver State. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 54(5), 762–771. DOI: 10.1177/0018720812442087
seminal_2_synopsis: This simulator study compared workload, secondary-task performance, and driver state (measured via blink rate) across manual and highly automated driving conditions, providing early empirical evidence that automation changes — rather than simply reduces — cognitive engagement. The work established that drivers in automated mode engage more readily in secondary tasks and show different physiological signatures, raising concerns about automation-induced complacency and out-of-the-loop risk. With approximately 572 citations on Google Scholar (341 on Crossref as of June 2026), it remains a cornerstone reference in research on driver state monitoring and the human cost of vehicle automation.
expertise_overview: Natasha Merat studies the human factors of road transport, with particular emphasis on how drivers and pedestrians interact with increasingly automated vehicles. Her work spans driver distraction, impairment, and cognitive load — investigating how secondary tasks, automation level, and workload affect visual attention, reaction time, and control quality. A substantial strand of her research addresses the design and evaluation of external Human-Machine Interfaces (eHMIs) that communicate automated vehicle intent to pedestrians and other vulnerable road users, using methods ranging from CAVE-based simulation and VR to live test-track experiments. She also examines the critical 'transition of control' problem — how drivers regain situational awareness and manual vehicle control when automation disengages — contributing both empirical findings and theoretical frameworks such as cognitive control models. Additional themes include technology acceptance, driver monitoring systems, and the needs of older road users as automation enters mainstream traffic. Her research is methodologically diverse, encompassing driving simulators, eye-tracking, physiological measurement, behavioural experiments, and interview studies.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. Sources: Google Scholar profile (https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=UyaB9-AAAAAJ), University of Leeds ITS staff page (https://environment.leeds.ac.uk/transport/staff/962/professor-natasha-merat), ORCID 0000-0003-4140-9948, and ResearchGate. DOI verification status for cited papers: 10.1016/j.jsr.2026.01.012 — Crossref verified (HTTP 200, title match); 10.1016/j.trf.2025.103505 — Crossref verified (HTTP 200, title match); 10.1016/j.trf.2023.02.010 — Crossref verified (HTTP 200, title match); 10.1016/j.trf.2014.09.005 — Crossref verified (HTTP 200, title match); 10.1177/0018720812442087 — Crossref verified (HTTP 200, title match). name: Mohamed, Ismail
recent_1: Deschamps, M.-L., Sanderson, P., Waxenegger, H., Mohamed, I., & Loeb, R. G. (2022). Auditory sequences presented with spearcons support better multiple patient monitoring than single-patient alarms: A preclinical simulation. Human Factors, 66(1), 5-23. DOI: 10.1177/00187208221116949
recent_1_synopsis: This preclinical simulation tested whether spearcon (time-compressed speech) sequences let clinicians monitor several patients at once more effectively than conventional single-patient alarms. Spearcons supported better event identification than auditory-icon sounds, showing that auditory displays which unambiguously convey which patient has which problem improve multi-patient monitoring performance.
recent_2: Clarke, H., Leav, S., Zestic, J., Mohamed, I., Salisbury, I., & Sanderson, P. (2022). Enhanced neonatal pulse oximetry sounds for the first minutes of life: A laboratory trial. Human Factors, 66(1), 24-39. DOI: 10.1177/00187208221118472
recent_2_synopsis: This laboratory trial evaluated enhanced pulse-oximetry sonifications for newborn resuscitation, where clinicians must track oxygen saturation and heart rate during the critical first minutes of life. The augmented sounds aimed to convey clinically actionable transitions more clearly than the standard variable-pitch tone, informing auditory display design for neonatal care.
recent_3: Mohamed, I., Loeb, R. G., & Sanderson, P. (2022). Attention to changes on a head-worn display: Two preclinical studies with healthcare scenarios. Human Factors, 65(8), 1738-1757. DOI: 10.1177/00187208221075851
recent_3_synopsis: Two preclinical studies examined how reliably clinicians notice changes presented on a head-worn display while engaged in healthcare tasks. The work characterizes change-detection performance and attentional costs for wearable peripheral displays in clinical monitoring.
seminal_1: Mohamed, I., Hicks, M., Loeb, R. G., & Sanderson, P. (2019). The impact of head-worn displays on strategic alarm management and situation awareness. Human Factors, 62(3), 377-392. DOI: 10.1177/0018720818814969
seminal_1_synopsis: This study investigated whether a head-worn display showing patient vital signs helps anesthesia providers manage alarms strategically and maintain situation awareness. With roughly 30 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2025), it is among his most-cited works and a key reference for wearable displays in perioperative monitoring.
seminal_2: Klueber, S., Wolf, E., Grundgeiger, T., Brecknell, B., Mohamed, I., & Sanderson, P. (2019). Supporting multiple patient monitoring with head-worn displays and spearcons. Applied Ergonomics, 78, 86-96. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2019.01.009
seminal_2_synopsis: This study combined head-worn displays with spearcon auditory sequences and found monitoring accuracy was best when the two were used together, suggesting the combined display suits monitoring multiple processes while multitasking. With roughly 30 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2025), it established the multimodal display concept his later Human Factors papers built on.
expertise_overview: Cognitive engineering and human factors of clinical monitoring, centered on auditory and multimodal displays for patient surveillance. Studies how spearcons (time-compressed speech), earcons, and enhanced pulse-oximetry sonifications convey patient state, and how head-worn (wearable) peripheral displays support change detection, alarm management, and situation awareness. Uses preclinical and laboratory simulation with healthcare scenarios, measuring detection accuracy, response time, and attentional cost to evaluate display designs. Application domains include anesthesia, neonatal resuscitation, and multiple-patient monitoring, with the goal of reducing alarm-related cognitive load and improving clinician performance.
notes: Identified with high confidence: Ismail Mohamed is a research engineer in the Cognitive Engineering Research Group / School of Psychology at the University of Queensland (Penelope Sanderson's group), confirmed via the UQ School of Psychology profile and co-authorship on all four seed hf_papers plus the Applied Ergonomics 2019 paper. Citation counts are approximate, from Google Scholar / Crossref reference counts around mid-2025. All five cited DOIs verified resolving via the Crossref API (June 2026) with matching titles; the four seed DOIs were pre-verified. His three most recent first-tier publications are the 2022 Human Factors papers (he has not published more recent first-author work located), so recent and seminal entries overlap with the seed set by design.

name: Mulder, Max
recent_1: Jacumet, R., Kolff, M., Venrooij, J., Schwienbacher, M., Wagner, S., Wollherr, D., Leibold, M., Pool, D. M., & Mulder, M. (2026). A Review on Optimization-Based Motion Cueing Algorithms for Driving Simulation. IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems, 1-25. DOI: 10.1109/TITS.2026.3661819
recent_1_synopsis: This review categorizes and compares optimization-based motion cueing algorithms used to keep driving-simulator motion platforms within physical limits while reproducing realistic motion cues. It traces over a decade of development of cost-function and constraint-based methods relative to predominant filter-based approaches, and assesses why optimization methods have not yet achieved widespread adoption. As senior author, Mulder brings his motion-perception and flight/driving-simulation expertise to bear.
recent_2: Vlaskamp, D., Pollitt, A., Blundell, J., Landman, A., Groen, E. L., van Paassen, M. M., Stroosma, O., & Mulder, M. (2026). Cabin crew startle and surprise: occurrence and impact. Cognition, Technology & Work. DOI: 10.1007/s10111-026-00866-5
recent_2_synopsis: A survey of 348 European cabin crew quantified the prevalence and operational and emotional impact of in-flight startle and surprise events, with 79.3% reporting having experienced startle and/or surprise. It measured stress, perceived performance impact, emotional control, training exposure, and lasting anxiety. The study extends the flight-deck startle/surprise research program to the cabin-crew population.
recent_3: Chen, J., Landman, A., Derumigny, A., Stroosma, O., van Paassen, M. M., & Mulder, M. (2026). Relationships Between Pilots' Startle and Surprise Responses and Information-Processing Performance During Simulated In-Flight Events. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261434426
recent_3_synopsis: This simulator study links pilots' measured startle and surprise responses to their subsequent information-processing performance during unexpected in-flight events. It advances the empirical and measurement side of the startle-and-surprise framework, connecting affective-physiological reactions to cognitive performance outcomes. The work supports evidence-based design of training for unexpected events.
seminal_1: Mulder, M., Pool, D. M., Abbink, D. A., Boer, E. R., Zaal, P. M. T., Drop, F. M., van der El, K., & van Paassen, M. M. (2018). Manual Control Cybernetics: State-of-the-Art and Current Trends. IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems, 48(5), 468-485. DOI: 10.1109/THMS.2017.2761342
seminal_1_synopsis: This lead-authored review defines the modern field of manual control cybernetics—using mathematical models of human control dynamics to objectively quantify pilot and driver behavior and to systematically optimize control interfaces and training. With roughly 130+ citations (Google Scholar, approximate, 2026-06-17), it consolidates the state of the art, identifies fundamental limitations, and lays out a research roadmap, and it won the IEEE SMC Society Andrew P. Sage Best Transactions Paper award. It is one of his most-cited first-author works.
seminal_2: Landman, A., Groen, E. L., van Paassen, M. M., Bronkhorst, A. W., & Mulder, M. (2017). Dealing With Unexpected Events on the Flight Deck: A Conceptual Model of Startle and Surprise. Human Factors, 59(8), 1161-1172. DOI: 10.1177/0018720817723428
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper introduced an integrated cognitive model of startle and surprise on the flight deck, distinguishing the two states and explaining how they degrade pilot performance during unexpected events. With roughly 160 citations on Google Scholar (approximate, 2026-06-17; the seed dataset's count of 77 reflects a more conservative/earlier index), it is among the most-cited works in his aviation-human-factors portfolio. It is foundational because it anchored an entire subsequent research line on training for the unexpected and on pilot and cabin-crew startle measurement.
expertise_overview: Research centers on aviation human factors and the cybernetic modeling of the human operator, aiming to understand and mathematically describe how pilots, drivers, and air traffic controllers perceive, decide, and act when controlling complex dynamic systems. A core thread is manual control cybernetics—quantitative models of human control dynamics, self-motion perception, and neuromuscular characteristics—applied to flight simulation, motion cueing, and the design and evaluation of haptic shared control and active control interfaces. The work also spans cognitive systems engineering and ecological interface design, producing perception-based displays such as perspective tunnel-in-the-sky and energy-based flight-path displays and decision-support tools for air traffic management. A prominent recent line investigates startle and surprise during unexpected in-flight events, spanning conceptual modeling, simulator-based measurement of pilot information-processing performance, and training interventions for unpredictable scenarios. Methods combine human-in-the-loop simulator experiments, control-theoretic and statistical modeling, and survey and field studies, and application domains extend from cockpit and air traffic control systems to driving simulation, eco-flying/trajectory optimization, and industrial human-machine interfaces.
notes: High-confidence identification. Max Mulder, Full Professor of Aerospace Human-Machine Systems, Control & Simulation section, Faculty of Aerospace Engineering, TU Delft (ORCID 0000-0002-0932-3979); identity confirmed via the TU Delft staff and Control & Simulation pages, Google Scholar (user=vZXtqegAAAAJ), and ResearchGate, and anchored by the provided hf_papers and his heavy co-authorship with M. M. (René) van Paassen. Disambiguation note: a separate Mark Mulder also worked at TU Delft on haptic driving support (e.g., the haptic gas-pedal IEEE T-ITS papers); those are first-authored by Mark, not Max, and were deliberately excluded. Independently verified via the Crossref API on 2026-06-17 (HTTP 200, matching titles): 10.1109/TITS.2026.3661819, 10.1007/s10111-026-00866-5, 10.1177/00187208261434426, 10.1109/THMS.2017.2761342, 10.1177/0018720817723428. No DOIs were invented; none were unverifiable. Seminal citation counts (~130+ and ~160) are approximate, from Google Scholar as of 2026-06-17 (profile total ~10,137 citations); the 2017 startle/surprise count differs from the seed's 77, reflecting database/time differences.

name: Nussbaum, Maury A.
recent_1: Rajabi, M. S., Ojelade, A., Kim, S., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2026). Vision-language models for occupational physical exposure assessment: Classification and temporal segmentation of manual material handling tasks. Applied Ergonomics, 124, 104831. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104831
recent_1_synopsis: This study applied vision-language models to ordinary video of manual material handling, both classifying task types and temporally segmenting work cycles to support automated occupational physical exposure assessment. It advances scalable, camera-based ergonomic risk evaluation by leveraging modern multimodal AI in place of manual observation or instrumented sensing.
recent_2: Akinwande, F., Kim, S., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2026). Passive back- and arm-support exoskeletons have effects on physical demands and user perceptions in simulated manual mining tasks that are generally beneficial but are both device- and task-specific. Applied Ergonomics, 122, 104819. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104819
recent_2_synopsis: This field-style biomechanical evaluation tested passive back-support and arm-support exoskeletons during simulated manual mining tasks, quantifying their effects on physical demands and worker perceptions. It found that benefits are generally present but strongly device- and task-specific, informing whether and where exoskeletons should be deployed in heavy, unstructured industrial work.
recent_3: Choi, J., Kim, S., Lim, S., Porto, R., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2026). Effectiveness and usability of a trunk posture feedback system: A longitudinal field study for up to six weeks in a distribution center. Applied Ergonomics, 121, 104796. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104796
recent_3_synopsis: This longitudinal field study deployed a wearable trunk-posture feedback system to distribution-center workers for up to six weeks, assessing both its effectiveness at reducing risky trunk postures and its real-world usability over time. It provides rare long-duration field evidence on whether real-time posture feedback produces durable behavior change outside the laboratory.
seminal_1: Barker, L. M., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2011). Fatigue, performance and the work environment: a survey of registered nurses. Journal of Advanced Nursing, 67(6), 1370-1382. DOI: 10.1111/j.1365-2648.2010.05597.x
seminal_1_synopsis: This large survey of registered nurses characterized physical, mental, and total fatigue and linked them to work-environment factors and self-reported performance, providing one of the field's most-used datasets on occupational fatigue in healthcare. With roughly 657 citations (Google Scholar, 2026-06-17) it is Nussbaum's most-cited work and a foundational reference connecting work-system design to fatigue and patient-safety-relevant performance.
seminal_2: Lin, D., Seol, H., Nussbaum, M. A., & Madigan, M. L. (2008). Reliability of COP-based postural sway measures and age-related differences. Gait & Posture, 28(2), 337-342. DOI: 10.1016/j.gaitpost.2008.01.005
seminal_2_synopsis: This study quantified the test-retest reliability of center-of-pressure (COP) based postural sway measures and documented age-related differences in balance, establishing which sway metrics are dependable for research on balance and fall risk. With roughly 630 citations (Google Scholar, 2026-06-17) it is a foundational methodological reference widely used in postural-control, aging, and fall-prevention research.
expertise_overview: Nussbaum studies occupational biomechanics, work physiology, and ergonomics with the central aim of understanding and preventing work-related musculoskeletal disorders while improving worker performance and well-being. Core threads include muscle fatigue measurement and modeling, low-back and trunk biomechanics during lifting and manual material handling, and balance, postural control, and fall risk, especially across the aging workforce. A major recent emphasis is the biomechanical and perceptual evaluation of occupational exoskeletons (back-support, arm-support, and upper-extremity devices) across diverse industrial tasks such as assembly, mining, construction, and overhead work. He also develops wearable-sensor and computer-vision/AI methods, including vision-language models and posture-feedback systems, for scalable physical exposure assessment and real-time ergonomic intervention. His methods span laboratory biomechanics and EMG, longitudinal field studies, and quantitative and data-driven modeling, often integrating physical and mental workload. Application domains include manufacturing and automotive assembly, mining and construction, healthcare and nursing, and distribution/warehousing work.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Maury A. Nussbaum, Virginia Tech, Grado Department of Industrial and Systems Engineering (Occupational Ergonomics and Biomechanics Laboratory). Confirmed via Google Scholar profile (user=XMRtFS8AAAAJ; ~16,208 total citations, h-index 67, i10-index 239), the Virginia Tech ISE faculty page, his personal CV site, Virginia Tech experts/Elements page, and ResearchGate, plus consistency with the pre-verified Human Factors hf_papers (exoskeleton/balance themes; recurring collaborator Sunwook Kim). All five cited DOIs verified via Crossref (HTTP 200, matching titles/venues/authors with Nussbaum present): recent_1 (10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104831, Applied Ergonomics), recent_2 (10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104819, Applied Ergonomics), recent_3 (10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104796, Applied Ergonomics), seminal_1 (10.1111/j.1365-2648.2010.05597.x, Journal of Advanced Nursing 2011), seminal_2 (10.1016/j.gaitpost.2008.01.005, Gait & Posture 2008). No unverifiable citations are included in the output.

name: Pak, Richard
recent_1: Pak, R., Rovira, E., & McLaughlin, A. C. (2025). Polite AI mitigates user susceptibility to AI hallucinations. Ergonomics, 68(10), 1735–1745. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2024.2434604
recent_1_synopsis: In a between-subjects experiment, participants completed a difficult quiz aided by either a polite or a neutral-toned AI chatbot that occasionally supplied hallucinated (incorrect but plausible) information. The study found that a polite conversational tone reduced users' susceptibility to acting on hallucinated output, showing that interaction style is a practical lever for calibrating trust and mitigating harm from unreliable generative AI.
recent_2: Guyton, Z., Pak, R., & Rovira, E. (2025). The role of automation etiquette and task-criticality on performance, workload, automation reliance, and user confidence. Applied Ergonomics, 125, 104430. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2024.104430
recent_2_synopsis: Using a computer-based automation task, this study manipulated automation etiquette (good vs. bad) and task-criticality in non-personified technology. Bad etiquette increased automation bias and reliance—improving performance with highly capable automation but also raising subjective workload and lowering self-confidence—demonstrating that interface politeness shapes reliance most strongly for capable automation and low-criticality tasks.
recent_3: Draheim, C., Herdener, N., Rovira, E., Melick, S. R., Coyne, J. T., Sibley, C., & Pak, R. (2025). Investigating transfer of input device practice on psychomotor performance in an aviation selection test. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251355828
recent_3_synopsis: This study examined whether practice with a particular input device transfers to psychomotor performance on a standardized aviation selection test, isolating device-specific learning from underlying ability. The findings inform fairness and validity in high-stakes personnel selection by clarifying how prior input-device experience can bias measured psychomotor performance.
seminal_1: Pak, R., Fink, N., Price, M., Bass, B., & Sturre, L. (2012). Decision support aids with anthropomorphic characteristics influence trust and performance in younger and older adults. Ergonomics, 55(9), 1059–1072. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2012.691554
seminal_1_synopsis: Using a diabetes decision-making task, this experiment showed that deliberately anthropomorphic automation altered trust, dependence, and performance differently for younger and older adults, with anthropomorphism increasing dependence on reasonably reliable aids across age groups. With roughly 180 citations (Semantic Scholar), it is one of Pak's most influential works and a foundational demonstration that surface design cues—not just reliability—shape human-automation trust, an insight central to later trust-in-automation and human-AI research.
seminal_2: Pak, R., Rogers, W. A., & Fisk, A. D. (2006). Spatial ability subfactors and their influences on a computer-based information search task. Human Factors, 48(1), 154–165. DOI: 10.1518/001872006776412180
seminal_2_synopsis: This study decomposed spatial ability into subfactors and tested their distinct contributions to performance on a web-like information search task across age groups. With roughly 58 citations (Semantic Scholar), it is foundational to Pak's program on how age-related cognitive differences predict technology use, helping establish individual-differences (spatial cognition) as a design-relevant determinant of older adults' interaction with information systems.
expertise_overview: Pak studies the psychological and cognitive factors that determine successful interaction with automated and autonomous technologies, ranging from everyday tools (smartphones, GPS, decision aids) to complex systems such as UAV control and human-robot/human-AI teaming. A central thread is trust in automation and AI—how anthropomorphism, etiquette, reliability, transparency, uncertainty communication, ethics, and trust repair shape calibrated reliance, performance, and acceptance. A second thread is cognitive aging, examining how age-related changes in attention, working memory, spatial ability, and other cognitive abilities affect older adults' ability to learn and use technology, and how displays and interfaces can be designed to support them. Methodologically he favors controlled behavioral experiments with subjective and performance measures, individual-differences and latent-variable approaches, and attention-control/working-memory constructs applied to applied human factors questions. Application domains include healthcare and medical decision support, generative AI and chatbots, aviation/personnel selection, and human-AI teaming.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. Richard Pak is a psychology professor and director of the Human Factors Institute at Clemson University (Cognition, Aging, and Technology / CAT Lab), an HFES Fellow. Confirmed via Google Scholar profile (user=Zhu3_2MAAAAJ; ~5,396 total citations, h-index 33, i10-index 67), the Clemson CAT Lab page (blogs.clemson.edu/catlab), Clemson News, and ResearchGate. The four anchor hf_papers (DOIs 10.1177/00187208251355828, 10.1177/00187208231159727, 10.1177/00187208221116952, 10.1177/00187208211032889) match this researcher's authorship and topics. All cited DOIs were verified to resolve via Crossref with matching titles/authors: recent_1 (10.1080/00140139.2024.2434604, Ergonomics, Pak/Rovira/McLaughlin), recent_2 (10.1016/j.apergo.2024.104430, Applied Ergonomics, Guyton/Pak/Rovira), recent_3 (10.1177/00187208251355828, pre-verified hf_paper), seminal_1 (10.1080/00140139.2012.691554, Ergonomics, Pak/Fink/Price/Bass/Sturre), seminal_2 (10.1518/001872006776412180, Human Factors, Pak/Rogers/Fisk). A 2025/2026 Ergonomics paper ('GenAI as complete cognitive automation for untrained users at population scale,' Pak/Rovira/McLaughlin) was found but excluded because no resolving DOI could be confirmed at time of research.

name: Park, Woojin
recent_1: Ryu, J., Moon, P., Hwang, K., Yoon, S., Lee, Y., Piao, Q., Park, J.-H., & Park, W. (2026). Comparing Paper- and AR-Based Assembly Manuals in Task Performance, dlPFC Hemodynamic Responses, and Perceived Workload. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261439486
recent_1_synopsis: An experiment compared paper-based and augmented-reality (AR) assembly manuals, measuring task performance, dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (dlPFC) hemodynamic responses via fNIRS, and perceived workload. recent_2_synopsis: This study examined how body-armor design and marksmanship posture jointly affect shooting performance, postural sway, and perceived workload in a military rifle task. It connects occupational biomechanics and equipment ergonomics to operational performance, informing protective-equipment and training design for soldiers.
recent_3: Ban, G., & Park, W. (2024). Effects of In-Vehicle Touchscreen Location on Driver Task Performance, Eye Gaze Behavior, and Workload During Conditionally Automated Driving: Nondriving-Related Task and Take-Over. Human Factors, 66(12), 2651-2668. DOI: 10.1177/00187208241226838
recent_3_synopsis: Using an SAE Level 3 conditionally automated driving simulator, this study evaluated how in-vehicle touchscreen placement influences nondriving-related task performance, eye-gaze behavior, workload, and take-over quality. The findings inform interior layout and HMI design for automated vehicles where drivers alternate between secondary tasks and resuming control.
seminal_1: Beck, D., Jung, J., & Park, W. (2019). Evaluating the Effects of In-Vehicle Side-View Display Layout Design on Physical Demands of Driving. Human Factors, 63(2), 348-363. DOI: 10.1177/0018720819884781
seminal_1_synopsis: This study quantified how the layout of in-vehicle side-view (camera monitor system) displays affects the physical/postural demands of driving, integrating vehicle ergonomics with occupational biomechanics. With roughly 19 citations (Crossref, June 2026), it is among Park's most-cited Human Factors articles and is foundational to his program on biomechanically grounded in-vehicle display design.
seminal_2: Ban, G., & Park, W. (2024). Effects of In-Vehicle Touchscreen Location on Driver Task Performance, Eye Gaze Behavior, and Workload During Conditionally Automated Driving: Nondriving-Related Task and Take-Over. Human Factors, 66(12), 2651-2668. DOI: 10.1177/00187208241226838
seminal_2_synopsis: This recent Human Factors paper on touchscreen placement during conditionally automated driving has accumulated roughly 14 citations (Crossref, June 2026) within about two years, an unusually fast accrual that makes it one of Park's most influential works. It is foundational for current automated-driving HMI research linking secondary-task interface location to take-over performance and workload.
expertise_overview: Park's research centers on occupational biomechanics and cognitive (physical) ergonomics, with strong emphasis on vehicle and in-vehicle interface ergonomics. Recurring topics include driver task performance, workload, and eye-gaze behavior during conditionally automated driving; in-vehicle display and touchscreen layout; postural demands, postural sway, and load carriage; and protective-equipment design. Methodologically he combines controlled human-subjects experiments and driving/task simulators with biomechanical measurement (posture, sway, muscular strength, reachable-workspace modeling), perceived-workload assessment, and increasingly neurophysiological measures such as fNIRS hemodynamic responses of the prefrontal cortex. He also works on human-reachable-workspace and discomfort-prediction models, smart-chair posture sensing, and usability of interactive systems. His application domains span automotive HMI and automated driving, manufacturing/assembly with augmented reality, and military and occupational task performance.
notes: Confidently identified as Woojin Park, Department of Industrial Engineering, Seoul National University (Life Enhancing Technology Lab; PhD Industrial & Operations Engineering, University of Michigan, 2003). name: Patton, Colleen E.
recent_1: Patton, C. E. (2026). The Influence of Exposure and Error Type on Estimates of Automation Reliability. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261435308
recent_1_synopsis: This study examines how operators' exposure to automated systems and the type of errors those systems make (e.g., misses vs. false alarms) shape estimates of automation reliability. It contributes to understanding the calibration of operator trust and the cognitive process by which people infer how dependable an automated aid is. The work extends Patton's program on perceptions of reliability and confidence in those perceptions.
recent_2: Patton, C. E. (2025). (Some) Benefits in Operator Decisions to Use AI After Experiencing Optimal Outcomes. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251387933
recent_2_synopsis: This paper investigates how prior experience with optimal (successful) outcomes from an AI aid influences operators' subsequent discretionary decisions to use that AI. The findings characterize when positive experiences confer benefits to appropriate AI reliance and when they do not, informing models of trust formation and automation adoption. It is part of Patton's broader work on the choice to use automation/AI under uncertainty.
recent_3: Patton, C. E., Clegg, B. A., Davis, B. C., Caglar, T., Siebert, C., & Blanchard, N. (2025). The Choice to Use Automation: Improvements from Evidence Accumulation. International Journal of Human-Computer Interaction, 41(19), 12014-12031. DOI: 10.1080/10447318.2025.2452189
recent_3_synopsis: This paper applies an evidence-accumulation framework to model and improve operators' discretionary choices about when to engage automation. The authors show that accumulating evidence before committing to use (or not use) an automated aid leads to better-calibrated reliance decisions. It bridges cognitive decision-making theory with applied human-automation interaction.
seminal_1: Patton, C. E., & Wickens, C. D. (2024). The relationship of trust and dependence. Ergonomics, 67(11), 1535-1552. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2024.2342436
seminal_1_synopsis: This influential paper disentangles the constructs of trust (an attitude) and dependence (a behavior) in human-automation interaction, clarifying when subjective trust does and does not translate into reliance on automation. It is among Patton's most-cited works (approximately 40 citations per Google Scholar; 26 per Crossref, as of June 2026), foundational because it provides conceptual and measurement clarity for a distinction that is frequently conflated in the automation-trust literature. Co-authored with Christopher D. Wickens, a leading attention/automation theorist.
seminal_2: Patton, C. E., Wickens, C. D., Smith, C. A. P., Noble, K. M., & Clegg, B. A. (2023). Supporting detection of hostile intentions: automated assistance in a dynamic decision-making context. Cognitive Research: Principles and Implications, 8(1), 69. DOI: 10.1186/s41235-023-00519-5
seminal_2_synopsis: This study examines how automated assistance can support operators in detecting hostile intent from spatial movement patterns in a dynamic, time-pressured decision-making task. It is one of Patton's more-cited journal articles (approximately 10 citations per Google Scholar; 5 per Crossref, as of June 2026) and is foundational to her line of maritime/naval hostile-intent detection research conducted with collaborators tied to the Naval Research Lab. It demonstrates how automation aids cognition in attention-demanding surveillance contexts.
expertise_overview: Colleen E. Patton's research centers on human-automation and human-AI interaction, with a particular focus on the perceptions and decisions of human operators regarding whether and when to use automation. Core topics include trust in automation, self-confidence, the distinction between trust (attitude) and dependence (behavior), automation reliability calibration, and discretionary (operator-controlled) automation use. A second strand of her work addresses attention, vigilance, multiple resource theory, and the detection of hostile intent from spatial movement in dynamic decision-making contexts. Methodologically she conducts controlled behavioral experiments and applies cognitive modeling approaches such as evidence accumulation to predict reliance decisions. Her applied domains include surveillance, maritime/naval operations, and AI/machine-learning-assisted decision aids, reflecting collaborations with the Army Research Lab and Naval Research Lab. She directs the CHAT (Cognition, Human-Automation, and Technology) Lab at North Carolina State University.
notes: Identification confidence: HIGH. Disambiguated using the four anchor Human Factors papers, all confirming co-authorship with Christopher D. Wickens, C. A. P. Smith, and Benjamin A. Clegg, which matches the verified author. Identity confirmed via Google Scholar (user IyeJ1MgAAAAJ, affiliation NC State University), NCSU faculty page (chass.ncsu.edu/people/cpatton4), LinkedIn, and ResearchGate. ORCID 0000-0002-2841-4526. CORRECTION TO SEED HINT: the prompt suggested a PhD from Old Dominion University under Scerbo/Bliss; this is incorrect. Patton earned her PhD at Colorado State University under Benjamin (Ben) Clegg and Christopher Wickens, and is now an Assistant Professor at North Carolina State University (not yet identified as Full Professor; that claim in one search summary is unverified/likely erroneous). All five cited DOIs were verified to resolve via the Crossref API on 2026-06-17 with matching titles: the four pre-verified HF anchors plus 10.1080/10447318.2025.2452189, 10.1080/00140139.2024.2342436, and 10.1186/s41235-023-00519-5. No unverifiable citations included; no DOIs invented.

name: Peres, S. Camille
recent_1: Mohammed Ashraf, A., Peres, S. C., & Sasangohar, F. (2026). Towards a worker-centered framework for categorizing procedural adaptations. Safety, 12(1), 28. DOI: 10.3390/safety12010028
recent_1_synopsis: Using the Skip-Order-Action (SOA) framework and video observation of over 1,400 procedural steps, this study developed a taxonomy of how workers adapt procedures in practice. It advances understanding of the gap between work-as-imagined and work-as-done by offering a structured method for classifying procedural deviations without presupposing they are unsafe.
recent_2: Camille Peres, S., & Hendricks, J. W. (2024). A systems model of procedures in high-risk work environments: Empirical evidence for the Safety Model 2 approach using the Interactive Behavior Triad. Safety Science, 172, 106328. DOI: 10.1016/j.ssci.2023.106328
recent_2_synopsis: This paper tested the Safety Model 2 framework by empirically examining how task frequency, experience, safety climate, and procedure quality interact to predict procedure use and compliance in high-risk industrial settings. It provides evidence that multi-variable interactions—not single factors—drive procedural behavior, with implications for procedure system design and safety management.
recent_3: An experimental investigation of hazard statement compliance in procedures using eye tracking technology: Should task be included in the C-HIP model? (2023). Peres, S. C., & Hendricks, J. W. Human Factors, 66(8), 1981–1994. DOI: 10.1177/00187208231212259
recent_3_synopsis: Using eye-tracking, this experiment examined whether the type of task embedded in a hazard statement affects worker compliance in written procedures, testing the Consumer and Hazard Information Processing (C-HIP) model. Results suggested that task context significantly influences whether workers attend to and comply with safety warnings, with implications for procedure and warning label standards.
seminal_1: Peres, S. C., Pham, T., & Phillips, R. (2013). Validation of the System Usability Scale (SUS). Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting, 57(1), 192–196. DOI: 10.1177/1541931213571043
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper conducted a formal validation of the System Usability Scale (SUS) across eight usability studies, demonstrating that SUS scores provide a valid ordinal measure for comparing multiple systems. With approximately 464 citations on Google Scholar (228 on Crossref), it is Peres's most-cited work and is widely referenced in usability research as empirical backing for the SUS instrument.
seminal_2: Son, C., Sasangohar, F., Neville, T., Peres, S. C., & Moon, J. (2020). Investigating resilience in emergency management: An integrative review of literature. Applied Ergonomics, 87, 103114. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2020.103114
seminal_2_synopsis: This integrative literature review synthesized research on resilience in emergency management, identifying four key dimensions—collective sensemaking, team decision-making, harmonizing work-as-imagined with work-as-done, and coordination. With 80 Crossref citations (144 on Google Scholar), it is a foundational reference for applying human factors and ergonomics methods to crisis and disaster response contexts.
expertise_overview: Peres's research applies human systems engineering to high-risk industrial settings—particularly oil, gas, and chemical process industries—with a sustained focus on how workers interact with written and digital procedures. She investigates the design of procedural documents (hazard statement format, visual layout, text features) and their effects on compliance, error, and safety behavior, using eye tracking and controlled experiments to link document features to cognitive processing. A parallel strand of her work addresses disaster ergonomics and emergency management, examining how multidisciplinary incident management teams coordinate, communicate, and deviate from prescribed work plans, with methods including interaction episode analysis and team network modeling. She applies machine learning and text analytics to predict step-level performance from operator and document features, opening a data-driven path to adaptive procedure systems. Additional research streams cover usability science—including System Usability Scale validation and fatigue assessment in small uncrewed aerial systems pilots—reflecting a broad commitment to human factors methodology in safety-critical domains.
notes: Identity confirmed via Texas A&M University School of Public Health faculty page (health.tamu.edu/experts/s-camille-peres.html) and Google Scholar profiles (user=_LJlFN0AAAAJ and user=4pAOBugAAAAJ). Peres is an associate professor in Environmental and Occupational Health and director of the Research on the Interaction between Humans and Machines (RIHM) Lab. She was elected HFES President for 2024–2025. Citation counts sourced from Google Scholar (as of June 2026) and Crossref; Google Scholar reports ~464 citations for the SUS validation paper (Crossref: 228) and ~144 for the resilience review (Crossref: 80). name: Proctor, Robert W.
recent_1: Zhang, C., Tian, C., Han, T., Li, H., Feng, Y., Chen, Y., Proctor, R. W., & Zhang, J. (2025). Evaluation of an Infrastructure-Based Warning System: A Case Study on Roundabout Driving Behaviors. IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems, 26(5), 6056-6069. DOI: 10.1109/TITS.2025.3548508
recent_1_synopsis: This case study evaluated an infrastructure-based (roadside) warning system intended to support safer driver behavior when approaching and negotiating roundabouts, assessing how the warnings shaped speed and maneuvering. It reflects Proctor's recent applied work on connected/automated transportation infrastructure and human-centered warning design. It matters because it provides empirical evidence on how externally delivered warnings change real driving behavior at a high-conflict road geometry.
recent_2: Proctor, R. W., & Hung, Y.-H. (2024). Sensation and Perception in HCI. In Human-Computer Interaction (Vol. 1, pp. 55-86). CRC Press. DOI: 10.1201/9781003584292-2
recent_2_synopsis: This book chapter synthesizes how human sensory and perceptual processing constrains and informs the design of human-computer interfaces, translating basic perception science into design-relevant principles. It exemplifies Proctor's long-standing role in bridging cognitive psychology and HCI/human factors pedagogy. It matters as an authoritative reference that grounds interface design in well-established perceptual mechanisms.
recent_3: Liu, W., Cao, Y., Liang, X., Proctor, R. W., & Duffy, V. G. (2024). Facing a Trend of Icon Simplicity: Evidence from Event-Related Potentials. International Journal of Human-Computer Interaction, 40(4), 945-955. DOI: 10.1080/10447318.2023.2169978
recent_3_synopsis: Using event-related potentials (ERPs) alongside behavioral measures, this study examined how varying levels of visual simplicity in interface icons affect recognition and the neural correlates of icon processing. It connects Proctor's interest in perception-action and information processing to contemporary visual interface design. It matters because it offers neurophysiological evidence to guide icon design choices in an era of increasingly minimalist visual interfaces.
seminal_1: Lu, C.-H., & Proctor, R. W. (1995). The influence of irrelevant location information on performance: A review of the Simon and spatial Stroop effects. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 2(2), 174-207. DOI: 10.3758/BF03210959
seminal_1_synopsis: This review synthesized empirical findings and theoretical accounts of how task-irrelevant spatial location information interferes with choice responses, organizing the Simon and spatial Stroop literatures into a coherent framework. With approximately 1,340 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is Proctor's most-cited work and is foundational because it established the conceptual structure and terminology that have guided decades of research on spatial stimulus-response correspondence and selective attention.
seminal_2: Proctor, R. W., & Cho, Y. S. (2006). Polarity correspondence: A general principle for performance of speeded binary classification tasks. Psychological Bulletin, 132(3), 416-442. DOI: 10.1037/0033-2909.132.3.416
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper advanced the polarity-correspondence principle, proposing that stimulus and response alternatives in binary tasks are coded asymmetrically as positive/negative polarities and that performance is best when corresponding polarities are paired. With approximately 820 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is foundational because it offered a unifying, parsimonious account spanning orthogonal stimulus-response compatibility, the Implicit Association Test, and numerical (e.g., SNARC) judgments, and was published in a top-tier review outlet (Psychological Bulletin).
expertise_overview: Proctor studies the relationship between perception and action, with a central focus on stimulus-response compatibility (SRC), spatial correspondence phenomena such as the Simon effect and spatial Stroop effect, and the mechanisms of response selection. He develops theory on how stimuli and responses are coded (e.g., polarity correspondence, ideomotor/action-effect accounts) and how attention, practice, and skill acquisition shape choice-reaction performance. Methodologically he combines controlled choice-reaction-time experiments, meta-analytic and integrative review, and increasingly neurophysiological measures such as event-related potentials. His applied work translates basic cognitive principles into human factors and HCI design, including warnings, interface/icon design, and trust- and risk-related behavior. A prominent recent strand addresses the human factors of cybersecurity, especially phishing-webpage detection, training embedded in warnings, and risk perception in mobile application selection. He also works on human-centered design of connected and automated transportation systems.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Robert W. Proctor, Distinguished Professor of Psychological Sciences at Purdue University (research on perception-action, stimulus-response compatibility, attention/skill, and human factors of cybersecurity). Sources confirming identity: his Google Scholar profile (scholar.google.com/citations?user=_RhK-icAAAAJ), the Purdue/CERIAS news announcement of his Distinguished Professor title, and the HFES Fellows profile (rproctor@purdue.edu); the four pre-verified Human Factors phishing/SRC papers served as ground-truth anchors, disambiguating him from other people named Proctor. Citation counts are from Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17: total ~25,400 citations, h-index ~74; seminal_1 (Lu & Proctor, 1995) ~1,340; seminal_2 (Proctor & Cho, 2006) ~820. Citation counts are approximate and may drift. recent_3 (icon simplicity) carries a 2023 online-first issue date in Crossref but appears in 2024 volume 40(4) and is retained as a recent peer-reviewed work; recent_2 is a 2024 book chapter (Proctor is the lead author). I declined to use a 2024 'A Tale of Two Distrusts' paper that appeared on his Scholar profile because the Crossref author list did not include Proctor. No DOIs were invented. All 8 DOIs cited in this profile (4 pre-verified Human Factors anchors plus 4 newly added recent/seminal DOIs) were verified via the Crossref API on 2026-06-17 and returned HTTP 200 with matching titles and Proctor confirmed in the author list where applicable. No unverifiable citations remain.

name: Radwin, Robert G.
recent_1: Konstant, A., Zhu, Z., Li, Y., Hu, Y. H., & Radwin, R. G. (2026). Modeling Human Expertise in a Sanding Task. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261460709
recent_1_synopsis: This study characterized how skilled workers perform a manual sanding task, building a quantitative model of the motions, forces, and strategies that distinguish expert from novice performance. The goal is to encode that expertise so it can inform the design and control of collaborative robots that assist or replicate skilled manual work.
recent_2: Zhu, Z., Mu, F., Radwin, R. G., & Li, Y. (2025). Towards video-based injury risk assessment: predicting lifting loads from body pose trajectories. Machine Vision and Applications, 36(6). DOI: 10.1007/s00138-025-01758-w
recent_2_synopsis: Using only monocular video, this work extracts body-pose trajectories during lifting and trains a Transformer model to predict the load being handled, a key contributor to low-back injury risk. It advances markerless, camera-based exposure assessment as a scalable alternative to instrumented or observational ergonomic methods.
recent_3: Konstant, A., Orr, N., Hagenow, M., Gundrum, I., Hu, Y. H., Mutlu, B., Zinn, M., Gleicher, M., & Radwin, R. G. (2024). Human–Robot Collaboration With a Corrective Shared Controlled Robot in a Sanding Task. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 67(3), 246-263. DOI: 10.1177/00187208241272066
recent_3_synopsis: This paper studied a human–robot collaborative sanding system in which a corrective shared-control robot blends the worker's intended motion with autonomous assistance to improve surface quality and reduce physical demand. It demonstrates how shared autonomy can offload the burdensome parts of a skilled manual task while keeping the human in the loop.
seminal_1: Armstrong, T. J., Radwin, R. G., Hansen, D. J., & Kennedy, K. W. (1986). Repetitive Trauma Disorders: Job Evaluation and Design. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 28(3), 325-336. DOI: 10.1177/001872088602800308
seminal_1_synopsis: This early and influential paper laid out a framework for evaluating and redesigning jobs to reduce repetitive-trauma (cumulative-trauma) disorders by analyzing physical stressors such as force, repetition, and posture. With roughly 341 citations (Google Scholar, 2026-06-17) it is Radwin's most-cited work and a foundational reference linking workplace physical-exposure analysis to upper-limb musculoskeletal disorder prevention.
seminal_2: Pearce, M., Mutlu, B., Shah, J., & Radwin, R. (2018). Optimizing Makespan and Ergonomics in Integrating Collaborative Robots Into Manufacturing Processes. IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering, 15(4), 1772-1784. DOI: 10.1109/TASE.2018.2789820
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper formulated the integration of collaborative robots into manufacturing as a joint optimization of production efficiency (makespan) and worker ergonomic load, providing an algorithmic method to allocate tasks between human and cobot. With roughly 236 citations (Google Scholar, 2026-06-17) it is a foundational contribution bridging human factors/ergonomics and human-robot collaboration in industrial settings.
expertise_overview: Radwin's research centers on occupational ergonomics and biomechanics—quantifying workers' exposure to physical stress and understanding how force, repetition, posture, and vibration cause work-related musculoskeletal disorders and peripheral neuropathies. He develops measurement and analytical methods for assessing physical work demands, including the Strain Index, Hand Activity Level, and hand-tool and grip biomechanics. A major and growing thread is computer-vision and machine-learning approaches that automatically extract posture, motion, lifting load, and hand-activity metrics from ordinary video for scalable injury-risk assessment. He also studies human-robot collaboration, designing and evaluating collaborative robots and shared-control schemes that allocate tasks to reduce ergonomic burden while preserving skilled manual performance. His application domains span manufacturing, hand-tool design, surgical and clinical motion analysis, and driver behavior, and his methods combine laboratory biomechanics, field exposure assessment, and quantitative modeling.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Robert G. Radwin, University of Wisconsin-Madison (Industrial and Systems Engineering / Biomedical Engineering; Occupational Ergonomics and Biomechanics Laboratory), AAAS Fellow 2025. Confirmed via Google Scholar profile (user=kk2kJSsAAAAJ; ~7,034 total citations, h-index 47), UW-Madison faculty/experts pages, ResearchGate, and consistency with the pre-verified Human Factors hf_papers (recurring co-authors Yu Hen Hu, Bilge Mutlu, Anna Konstant, Yin Li; sanding / HRC / computer-vision lifting themes). All cited DOIs verified via Crossref with matching titles, venues, and Radwin in the author list: recent_1 (10.1177/00187208261460709), recent_2 (10.1007/s00138-025-01758-w, Machine Vision and Applications, authors Zhu/Mu/Radwin/Li), recent_3 (10.1177/00187208241272066), seminal_1 (10.1177/001872088602800308, Human Factors 1986), seminal_2 (10.1109/TASE.2018.2789820, IEEE TASE 2018).
