name: Rieger, Monika A.
recent_1: Soeder, J., Volk, C., Ulmer, L., Wagenblast, F., Seibt, R., Mendoza, E., Bott, N., Rieger, M. A., & Steinhilber, B. (2026). Accelerometer-based assessment of occupational standing time and its association with venous disorders—results of a cross-sectional field study. Scientific Reports, 16, 7477. DOI: 10.1038/s41598-026-38327-8
recent_1_synopsis: This cross-sectional field study used wrist-worn accelerometers to objectively quantify daily occupational standing time in 198 workers across multiple sectors and examined its association with venous disorders and pathological reflux. The study provides objective wearable-based evidence linking prolonged work-related standing to lower-limb venous health risks, informing workplace ergonomic guidelines.
recent_2: Wagenblast, F., Seibt, R., Rieger, M. A., & Steinhilber, B. (2025). Measures of the psychophysiological response to recurrent anticipatory stress—the influence of neuroticism on heart rhythm and skin resistance. Scientific Reports, 15, 41177. DOI: 10.1038/s41598-025-28090-7
recent_2_synopsis: This study examined how neuroticism moderates cardiac and electrodermal responses during repeated anticipatory stress exposures in a controlled laboratory paradigm, comparing heart rate variability and skin resistance across groups. The findings clarify which psychophysiological markers remain sensitive to occupational stress despite individual adaptation, with implications for monitoring mental workload at work.
recent_3: Hander, N. R., Erschens, R., Klein, T., Jarczok, M. N., Mulfinger, N., Rieger, M. A., Junne, F., Angerer, P., Puschner, B., & Müller, A. (2026). Working conditions, job stress and work-related consequences among hospital employees—differences by professional group, working hours and job levels: A cross-sectional study. PLOS ONE, 21(3), e0343567. DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0343567
recent_3_synopsis: Drawing on data from the SEEGEN study (406 employees across three German hospitals), this paper documented how working conditions, perceived job stress, and adverse work consequences differ by professional group, hours worked, and job level. Results highlight that nursing staff and employees with high working hours are at disproportionate risk, pointing to targeted occupational health interventions.
seminal_1: Bär, M., Steinhilber, B., Rieger, M. A., & Luger, T. (2021). The influence of using exoskeletons during occupational tasks on acute physical stress and strain compared to no exoskeleton—A systematic review and meta-analysis. Applied Ergonomics, 94, 103385. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2021.103385
seminal_1_synopsis: This systematic review and meta-analysis synthesized evidence on how occupational exoskeletons affect muscle activity, posture, cardiovascular load, and perceived exertion relative to unassisted work. With approximately 130 citations, it is Rieger's most-cited work and established the foundational evidence base guiding ergonomic evaluation and deployment of exoskeletons in industrial settings.
seminal_2: Luger, T., Bär, M., Seibt, R., Rieger, M. A., & Steinhilber, B. (2021). Using a Back Exoskeleton During Industrial and Functional Tasks—Effects on Muscle Activity, Posture, Performance, Usability, and Wearer Discomfort in a Laboratory Trial. Human Factors, 65(4), 605–622. DOI: 10.1177/00187208211007267
seminal_2_synopsis: This controlled laboratory study comprehensively assessed a passive back-support exoskeleton (Laevo V2.56) across muscle activation, trunk posture, task performance, usability ratings, and wearer comfort in realistic industrial tasks, accumulating approximately 86 citations. The multi-outcome design became a methodological model for subsequent exoskeleton evaluation research.
expertise_overview: Research centers on the ergonomics and occupational health consequences of physical work demands, with particular emphasis on passive and active exoskeletons as interventions for reducing musculoskeletal loading during industrial tasks such as lifting, sorting, and prolonged standing. A recurrent methodological theme is the objective measurement of physical exposure—using surface electromyography, inertial sensors, and accelerometry—to quantify muscle activity, posture, postural control, and physical strain under realistic occupational conditions. Work also extends to psychophysiological assessment of mental stress in the workplace, including heart rate variability and skin conductance as markers of occupational stressor exposure. A third strand addresses health services and preventive occupational medicine, encompassing systematic reviews of work-break interventions for musculoskeletal disorder prevention, organizational determinants of employee health behavior, and occupational health care for special worker groups. Application domains span manufacturing, logistics, laparoscopic surgery, and hospital workforces, reflecting a broad translational agenda from laboratory biomechanics to field implementation and policy.
notes: Identity confirmed via University of Tübingen faculty portal (fit.uni-tuebingen.de/Portfolio/Details?id=1568), the Institute's publications page (medizin.uni-tuebingen.de), and MDPI SciProfiles (profile/354999). name: Rieger, Tobias
recent_1: Rieger, T., Onnasch, L., Roesler, E., & Manzey, D. (2025). Why Highly Reliable Decision Support Systems Often Lead to Suboptimal Performance and What We Can Do About It. IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems, 55(5). DOI: 10.1109/THMS.2025.3584662
recent_1_synopsis: This paper synthesizes evidence that even highly reliable automated decision support frequently fails to improve, and can degrade, joint human-automation performance because of complacency, miscalibrated reliance, and the loss of the human's complementary contribution. It frames the problem and proposes design and training countermeasures, making it directly relevant to anyone deploying high-reliability AI aids in safety-critical work.
recent_2: Rieger, T., Schindler, H., Onnasch, L., & Roesler, E. (2025). Explaining AI weaknesses improves human-AI performance in a dynamic control task. International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 199, 103505. DOI: 10.1016/j.ijhcs.2025.103505
recent_2_synopsis: Using a dynamic control task, this study shows that explanations that disclose an AI's weaknesses (rather than only its strengths) help users calibrate reliance and achieve better human-AI team performance. It advances explainable-AI research by demonstrating that the content and framing of explanations, not just their presence, determines whether they improve outcomes.
recent_3: Rieger, T., Marx, B., & Manzey, D. (2025). Likelihood Systems Can Improve Hit Rates in Low-Prevalence Visual Search Over Binary Systems. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 67(9). DOI: 10.1177/00187208251320589
recent_3_synopsis: This experiment compares likelihood-based decision aids (which communicate graded confidence) against binary alert systems in a low-prevalence visual search task and finds that likelihood systems raise hit rates without unacceptable cost. It matters for screening domains such as security and medical imaging, where rare targets and conservative binary alarms commonly produce misses.
seminal_1: Fahnenstich, H., Rieger, T., & Roesler, E. (2024). Trusting under risk - comparing human to AI decision support agents. Computers in Human Behavior, 153, 108107. DOI: 10.1016/j.chb.2023.108107
seminal_1_synopsis: This study compares trust in and reliance on human versus AI decision support under conditions of risk, contributing to Rieger's broader program on the imperfect automation schema. With roughly 65-70 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2025) it is among his most-cited works and is foundational for understanding how the perceived nature of an advisor (human vs. machine) shapes risky decision-making.
seminal_2: Rieger, T., & Manzey, D. (2022). Human Performance Consequences of Automated Decision Aids: The Impact of Time Pressure. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 64(4), 617-634. DOI: 10.1177/0018720820965019
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper experimentally demonstrates how time pressure interacts with automated decision aids to shape reliance and overall performance, showing that pressure amplifies dependence on imperfect automation. With approximately 60+ citations (Google Scholar, mid-2025) it is a cornerstone of Rieger's work on automation-aided decision-making and is frequently cited in the time-pressure and complacency literature.
expertise_overview: Tobias Rieger studies human-automation and human-AI interaction, with a central focus on trust, reliance, and complacency toward automated and AI decision support. A recurring theme is the "(im)perfect automation schema" - how people calibrate trust differently for machine versus human advisors and how reliability, transparency, and explanation framing affect joint performance. He investigates explainable AI, showing that the benefits of explanations depend on content (e.g., disclosing system weaknesses) rather than mere presence. His applied domains include low-prevalence visual search and screening (security/luggage and medical imaging), where he compares binary versus likelihood-based aids, and he frequently examines moderators such as time pressure and decision risk. Methodologically he relies on controlled behavioral experiments with signal-detection and performance metrics, validated trust questionnaires, and human-AI team paradigms.
notes: Confidently identified (found=yes). The TU Berlin Tobias Rieger (Psychology of Action and Automation group; postdoc in human-AI/human-automation interaction) is the correct person, disambiguated from same-named individuals (e.g., a LinkedIn IT coordinator) using all five pre-verified Human Factors hf_papers as anchors. Identity confirmed via Google Scholar (user=gQjrcWAAAAAJ), TU Berlin faculty/group pages (tu.berlin/en/hap), dblp (pid 295/3553), Semantic Scholar, and ResearchGate. name: Riggs, Sara Lu
recent_1: El Iskandarani, M., Zhang, P., & Riggs, S. L. (2026). Multitasking Across Physical and Virtual Displays: The Effect of Spatial Discontinuity and Task Load. IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics, 32(5). DOI: 10.1109/TVCG.2026.3679118
recent_1_synopsis: This study examines how spatially discontinuous information spread across physical and virtual (head-mounted) displays affects multitasking performance under varying task load. Using a mixed-reality setup, the authors quantify the costs of switching attention across physical-virtual display boundaries. The work informs the design of cross-reality workspaces where operators must integrate information from co-located physical screens and AR/VR overlays.
recent_2: El Iskandarani, M., Bolton, M. L., & Riggs, S. L. (2026). Multitasking Tug-of-War: Exploring the Impact of Task Modality, Task Load Level, and Task Load Type on Dual-Task Interference in Virtual Reality. Human Factors, 68(4). DOI: 10.1177/00187208251396766
recent_2_synopsis: Using a dual-task paradigm in virtual reality, this paper disentangles how task modality (visual vs. auditory), task load level, and task load type jointly drive dual-task interference. The findings extend multiple-resource theory into immersive VR environments, showing which combinations of concurrent demands produce the steepest performance costs. Results offer guidance for allocating tasks across sensory channels in VR-based work and training.
recent_3: El Iskandarani, M., Bolton, M. L., & Riggs, S. L. (2025). Examining dual-task interference effects of visual and auditory perceptual load in virtual reality. International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 205, 103619. DOI: 10.1016/j.ijhcs.2025.103619
recent_3_synopsis: This experiment manipulates visual and auditory perceptual load independently to characterize how each contributes to dual-task interference in VR. It tests whether perceptual-load theory, originally developed for 2D laboratory tasks, generalizes to immersive multitasking. The study clarifies when high perceptual load in one modality spares versus impairs concurrent processing in another, with implications for multimodal VR interface design.
seminal_1: Lu, S. A., Wickens, C. D., Prinet, J. C., Hutchins, S. D., Sarter, N., & Sebok, A. (2013). Supporting Interruption Management and Multimodal Interface Design: Three Meta-Analyses of Task Performance as a Function of Interrupting Task Modality. Human Factors, 55(4), 697-724. DOI: 10.1177/0018720813476298
seminal_1_synopsis: This is Riggs' (published under her earlier name S. A. Lu) most-cited work, with approximately 144 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026). Through three meta-analyses, it synthesizes how the modality of an interrupting task (visual, auditory, tactile) shapes performance on both the interrupting and ongoing tasks, grounding multimodal interface design in quantitative evidence. It is foundational for interruption-management research and for applying multiple-resource theory to multimodal display design.
seminal_2: Clark, L. D., Bhagat, A. B., & Riggs, S. L. (2020). Extending Fitts' law in three-dimensional virtual environments with current low-cost virtual reality technology. International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 139, 102413. DOI: 10.1016/j.ijhcs.2020.102413
seminal_2_synopsis: With approximately 139 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), this is among Riggs' most influential papers and a widely cited reference for VR interaction modeling. It empirically tests and extends Fitts' law to 3D discrete pointing tasks using commodity head-mounted VR hardware, showing how target size, distance, and inclination angle affect movement time. The work is foundational for designing and benchmarking selection/pointing interactions in low-cost virtual reality.
expertise_overview: Sara Lu Riggs studies human attention and performance in complex, multitasking-intensive work environments such as aviation, healthcare/anesthesia monitoring, military UAV command-and-control, and manufacturing. Her central interests are attention management, interruption management, and how operators divide cognitive resources across concurrent tasks and information sources. A substantial thread of her work concerns multimodal and multisensory displays, especially tactile and crossmodal (visual-tactile, auditory-tactile) cueing, including tactile change blindness/detection and crossmodal stimulus-intensity matching methods. More recently she investigates multitasking, visual search, and dual-task interference in virtual and augmented reality, as well as human-AI interaction topics such as reliance on explainable AI and trust in AI code generation. She also applies eye-tracking and computational methods (e.g., cross-recurrence quantification, machine learning) to measure workload and team coordination. Her methodological contributions include meta-analytic synthesis of multimodal interruption effects and extending Fitts' law to 3D VR.
notes: Identification confidence: HIGH. name: Rosen, Michael A.
recent_1: Marsteller, J. A., Rosen, M. A., Wyskiel, R., Chang, B. H., Hsu, Y.-J., Thompson, D. A., Kim, G., & Speck, K. (2024). Multi-Team Shared Expectations Tool (MT-SET): An exercise to improve teamwork across health care teams. The Joint Commission Journal on Quality and Patient Safety, 50(10), 737-744. recent_3: Ali, K. J., Goeschel, C. A., Eckroade, M. M., Carlin, K. N., Haugstetter, M., Shofer, M., & Rosen, M. A. (2024). DOI: 10.1016/j.jcjq.2023.08.009
recent_3_synopsis: This study developed and psychometrically validated an assessment tool adapting TeamSTEPPS principles to measure teamwork behaviors relevant to diagnostic accuracy. It provides a measurement instrument for studying and improving team-based contributions to reducing diagnostic error.
seminal_1: Rosen, M. A., DiazGranados, D., Dietz, A. S., Benishek, L. E., Thompson, D., Pronovost, P. J., & Weaver, S. J. (2018). Teamwork in healthcare: Key discoveries enabling safer, high-quality care. American Psychologist, 73(4), 433-450. DOI: 10.1037/amp0000298

seminal_2: Rosen, M. A., Salas, E., Wilson, K. A., King, H. B., Salisbury, M., Augenstein, J. S., Robinson, D. W., & Birnbach, D. J. (2008). Measuring team performance in simulation-based training: Adopting best practices for healthcare. Simulation in Healthcare, 3(1), 33-41. DOI: 10.1097/SIH.0b013e3181626276
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper adapted established team-performance measurement best practices for healthcare simulation training, providing guidance on what and how to measure when evaluating clinical teams. With roughly 400 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2025), it is a foundational methodological reference for simulation-based team assessment.
expertise_overview: Human factors and applied/industrial-organizational psychology of teamwork and patient safety in healthcare. Develops, validates, and applies behavioral markers and measurement tools for team performance and team cognition across clinical domains, including multi-team systems and diagnostic teamwork. Studies simulation-based training design, implementation, and evaluation, and how in-situ simulation can diagnose unit safety needs. Investigates team physiological dynamics and the use of physiological measures to characterize team states, alongside naturalistic decision making and quality- and safety-improvement methods. Application domains center on acute and perioperative care and broader patient-safety systems.
notes: Identified with high confidence: Michael A. Rosen was a human factors psychologist and faculty member at the Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine (Armstrong Institute for Patient Safety and Quality), PhD under Eduardo Salas (Univ. of Central Florida). Confirmed via Johns Hopkins profiles, his Google Scholar profile (user c_UTJMMAAAAJ), and authorship on all four seed hf_papers and the recent papers. All six cited DOIs verified resolving via the Crossref API (June 2026) with matching titles and confirmed Rosen authorship; the four seed DOIs were pre-verified.

name: Sarno, Dawn M.
recent_1: Sarno, D. M., & Allan, J. N. (2025). Untangling the Web of Deceit: Examining Shared User Susceptibility Across Five Types of Digital Deceptions. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251363406
recent_1_synopsis: Building on prior work showing shared vulnerability across phishing, fake news, and scam texts, this study extends the analysis to five distinct types of digital deception to test whether a common set of users is susceptible across all of them and whether universal psychological predictors exist. It uses a behavioral classification paradigm paired with individual-difference measures to identify cross-domain susceptibility profiles. The work advances a unifying account of deception susceptibility rather than treating each scam type in isolation.
recent_2: Sabo, K. E., Sarno, D. M., & Black, J. T. (2026). Beyond the inbox: Exploring shared cognitive mechanisms underlying susceptibility to and reporting of deception across digital domains. Applied Ergonomics, 135, 104771. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104771
recent_2_synopsis: This paper investigates the cognitive mechanisms shared across different digital deception domains, examining not only susceptibility but also the under-studied behavior of reporting deception. It links individual cognitive and decision-making factors to both falling for and flagging deceptive content. The findings inform the design of training and reporting interventions that generalize beyond email phishing.
recent_3: Sarno, D. M., Gendron, C., & Volante, W. G. (2024). Filtering Out the User?: Are Users Complacent with Phishing Emails Due to Automated Filters? International Journal of Human-Computer Interaction, 41(16), 9908-9917. DOI: 10.1080/10447318.2024.2429759
recent_3_synopsis: This study tests whether the presence of automated spam/phishing filters induces complacency that degrades users' own vigilance toward phishing emails. Through controlled email-classification experiments manipulating perceived filter presence, it examines how automation reliance shifts human detection behavior. The results have practical implications for how security automation is presented to end users without undermining human oversight.
seminal_1: Sarno, D. M., Lewis, J. E., Bohil, C. J., & Neider, M. B. (2019). Which Phish Is on the Hook? Phishing Vulnerability for Older Versus Younger Adults. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 62(5), 704-717. DOI: 10.1177/0018720819855570
seminal_1_synopsis: Approximately 48 citations (Crossref, 2026-06-17). This is one of Sarno's foundational and most-cited works, establishing age-related differences in phishing vulnerability and showing that older and younger adults differ in susceptibility and in the cues they rely on. It is frequently cited because it brought a rigorous human-factors and aging-cognition lens to phishing detection, anchoring much of her subsequent program on individual differences in deception susceptibility.
seminal_2: Sarno, D. M., Harris, M. W., & Black, J. (2023). Which phish is captured in the net? Understanding phishing susceptibility and individual differences. Applied Cognitive Psychology, 37(4), 789-803. DOI: 10.1002/acp.4075
seminal_2_synopsis: Approximately 29 citations (Crossref, 2026-06-17). This paper is foundational for reconciling conflicting findings about the psychological predictors of phishing susceptibility, using a diverse online sample and a broad email set with measures of phishing awareness, age, impulsivity, curiosity, and the Big-5 personality traits. It is influential because it provides a comprehensive individual-differences map of who is vulnerable and why, widely cited in cybersecurity-cognition research.
expertise_overview: Dawn M. Sarno studies the cognitive and human-factors underpinnings of susceptibility to digital deception, with a primary focus on phishing emails and, more broadly, scam text messages, fake news, and other forms of online manipulation. Her work emphasizes individual differences (age, impulsivity, curiosity, personality, working memory, phishing awareness) as predictors of who falls for deception and explores shared cognitive mechanisms across deception types. Methodologically she relies on controlled email/message classification experiments, behavioral susceptibility paradigms, individual-difference surveys, and intervention/training studies. A second strand of her research concerns applied visual cognition, visual working memory, attention, and executive function, including depth perception and computer-aided detection in medical imaging (e.g., BI-RADS, mammography). She also develops and evaluates anti-phishing training interventions, examining persistence of training effects and complacency induced by automated security filters. Her domains span cybersecurity, aging, human-automation interaction, and applied cognitive psychology.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. Disambiguated via the four pre-verified Human Factors anchor papers, all of which resolved on Crossref with matching titles. Identity corroborated by Clemson University AVANT Lab faculty/lab page (dmsarno.people.clemson.edu), ResearchGate (Dawn-Sarno, Assistant Professor, Clemson Department of Psychology), and UCF Psychology profile (PhD in Human Factors/Cognitive Psychology, UCF, 2020; now Assistant Professor at Clemson). Note Crossref lists co-author Mark B. Neider on the 2021 'So Many Phish' anchor and Jeffrey Black on the 2023 'Who Gets Caught' anchor. Sarno were used. All cited DOIs verified to resolve via Crossref API on 2026-06-17: 10.1177/00187208251363406, 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104771, 10.1080/10447318.2024.2429759, 10.1177/0018720819855570, 10.1002/acp.4075. Citation counts are from Crossref is-referenced-by-count on 2026-06-17 (seminal_1 = 48, seminal_2 = 29) and are typically lower than Google Scholar counts. No unverifiable citations. Volume/issue/pages for the 2025 HF and 2026 Applied Ergonomics articles taken from Crossref (Applied Ergonomics vol 135, art. 104771); the 2025 HF article is online-first without final volume/issue at time of verification.

name: Sarter, Nadine B.
recent_1: Flanigen, P., Wilson, M., Sarter, N., & Atkins, E. (2024). The effectiveness of sensor visualizations and graphic augmentations for detecting vertical obstacles. Journal of the American Helicopter Society, 69(3), 1-13. DOI: 10.4050/jahs.69.032011
recent_1_synopsis: This study evaluated how different sensor visualizations and graphic augmentations help helicopter pilots detect vertical obstacles such as power lines and towers. It informs the design of obstacle-warning displays that support timely detection in degraded-visibility rotorcraft operations.
recent_2: Mishler, S., & Sarter, N. (2022). Attention limitations in the detection and identification of alarms in close temporal proximity. Human Factors, 66(1), 234-249. DOI: 10.1177/00187208211063991
recent_2_synopsis: This study examined how well operators detect and identify alarms that occur close together in time, a common challenge in alarm-rich settings. It documents attentional bottlenecks that cause near-simultaneous alarms to be missed or confused, with implications for alarm system design.
recent_3: Cutlip, S., Wan, Y., Sarter, N., & Gillespie, R. B. (2021). The effects of haptic feedback and transition type on transfer of control between drivers and vehicle automation. IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems, 51(6), 613-621. DOI: 10.1109/thms.2021.3107255
recent_3_synopsis: This study tested how haptic feedback and different transition types affect handovers of control between drivers and vehicle automation. It shows that touch-based cues can smooth and improve the safety of control transfers in automated driving.
seminal_1: Sarter, N. B., & Woods, D. D. (1995). How in the world did we ever get into that mode? Mode error and awareness in supervisory control. Human Factors, 37(1), 5-19. DOI: 10.1518/001872095779049516
seminal_1_synopsis: This landmark paper introduced the concepts of mode error and mode awareness, explaining why operators of complex automated systems (especially glass-cockpit aircraft) experience 'automation surprises.' With well over 1,000 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2025), it is a foundational work on human-automation interaction and the gulf between operators' mental models and system behavior.
seminal_2: Jones, L. A., & Sarter, N. B. (2008). Tactile displays: Guidance for their design and application. Human Factors, 50(1), 90-111. DOI: 10.1518/001872008x250638
seminal_2_synopsis: This review provides design guidance for tactile (touch-based) information displays, synthesizing perception research to advise when and how to use the sense of touch in human-machine interfaces. With well over 1,000 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2025), it is a foundational reference that helped establish tactile and multimodal displays as a mainstream interface modality.
expertise_overview: Human-automation interaction and the cognitive and attentional foundations of interface design. Studies automation surprises, mode errors, and mode awareness, and how transparency and feedback help operators understand and trust complex automated systems. A leading authority on multimodal and tactile information displays, examining how visual, auditory, and tactile channels can be combined to support attention allocation, alarm detection, and human-automation coordination, including crossmodal matching of stimulus intensities. Investigates attention limitations, change blindness, age-related differences in multisensory perception, and trust calibration, often inferred through eye tracking. Application domains span aviation and rotorcraft flight decks, automated driving, and safety-critical monitoring, using simulator experiments and process-oriented behavioral measures.
notes: Identified with very high confidence: Nadine B. Sarter is the Richard W. Pew Collegiate Professor of Industrial & Operations Engineering at the University of Michigan and a National Academy of Engineering member (2019); confirmed via Wikipedia, Research.com, her Google Scholar profile, and authorship on all four seed hf_papers. Citation counts are approximate, from Google Scholar around mid-2025; the two seminal papers each exceed 1,000 citations (the 1995 mode-error paper is among the most-cited works in human factors). All five cited DOIs verified resolving via the Crossref API (June 2026) with matching titles; the four seed DOIs were pre-verified. Crossref returns multiple unrelated 'Sarter' authors (public administration, chemistry); the cited recent papers were confirmed to be Nadine Sarter's by co-authors (Gillespie, Atkins, Lu) and aviation/automation topic. Note Sage online-publication years differ from issue years (the 2022-online alarm paper appears in vol. 66, 2024 issue).

name: Sasangohar, Farzan
recent_1: Wang, Z., Ahmadi, N., Mehta, R. K., & Sasangohar, F. (2026). The Dynamics of Attention Across Automated and Manual Driving Modes: A Driving Simulation Study. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261447877
recent_1_synopsis: A driving-simulator study examining how drivers' attention allocation shifts between periods of automated and manual control, characterizing the dynamics of (re)engagement at mode transitions. It matters for designing automation hand-off and driver-monitoring strategies that mitigate out-of-the-loop performance decrements in partially automated vehicles.
recent_2: Ziyadidegan, S., Razavi, M., Pesarakli, H., Javid, A., & Sasangohar, F. (2026). Investigating Occupational Stress in ICU Nurses: A Comparative Analysis of Mental and Physical Stress Using Heart Rate Variability and Wrist-Worn Accelerometer Data. Human Factors, 68(5), 545-562. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261417155
recent_2_synopsis: Using wearable physiological sensing, this study separates mental from physical stress in intensive-care nurses by combining heart-rate-variability metrics with wrist-worn accelerometer activity data. It advances naturalistic, multimodal stress measurement for safety-critical clinical work and informs workload-management and staffing interventions in the ICU.
recent_3: Emond, W., Sauerbier, R., Scholly, U., Sasangohar, F., & Zare, M. (2026). Motion sickness detection and mitigation in a stop-and-go passenger ride scenario. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 116, 103381. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2025.103381
recent_3_synopsis: A within-subjects driving study that induced and then alleviated motion sickness during repeated stop-and-go laps, testing a multimodal anti-motion-sickness intervention (visual motion cues, reclined posture, music, fragrance, and ventilation). It contributes detection methods and mitigation strategies relevant to passenger comfort in automated and electric vehicles where occupants are increasingly non-drivers.
seminal_1: Son, C., Hegde, S., Smith, A., Wang, X., & Sasangohar, F. (2020). Effects of COVID-19 on College Students' Mental Health in the United States: Interview Survey Study. Journal of Medical Internet Research, 22(9), e21279. DOI: 10.2196/21279
seminal_1_synopsis: An interview-survey study of 195 students at a large U.S. public university documenting how the COVID-19 pandemic affected student stress, anxiety, depression, and coping. With roughly 4,500+ citations (Google Scholar), it is among the most-cited empirical accounts of pandemic-era college mental health and foundational for subsequent work on student well-being and remote-learning stressors.
seminal_2: Wang, X., Hegde, S., Son, C., Keller, B., Smith, A., & Sasangohar, F. (2020). Investigating Mental Health of US College Students During the COVID-19 Pandemic: Cross-Sectional Survey Study. Journal of Medical Internet Research, 22(9), e22817. DOI: 10.2196/22817
seminal_2_synopsis: A companion cross-sectional survey quantifying depression and anxiety severity among U.S. college students during the early pandemic, complementing the qualitative interview study. With roughly 2,100+ citations (Google Scholar), it is a widely used reference for prevalence estimates of student mental-health symptoms during COVID-19.
expertise_overview: Human factors and human-systems engineering in multi-task, safety-critical environments, with a strong emphasis on healthcare and clinical work systems-ICU nursing workload, occupational stress, fatigue, interruptions, clinician burnout, and patient-safety processes. A defining methodological thread is naturalistic, sensor-based measurement of human state: wearable and continuous monitoring of heart rate, heart-rate variability, electrodermal activity, skin temperature, eye-tracking, and accelerometry to quantify stress, workload, and fatigue in situ. He also studies mental health and behavioral health technologies, including mobile mental-health applications, remote monitoring, telemedicine and telecritical care, and PTSD/stress interventions. A second application area is transportation and process-control safety, spanning driver attention and automation transitions, motion sickness, offshore shiftwork fatigue, procedural work, and resilience and emergency management. Methods range from controlled simulator and laboratory experiments to naturalistic field studies, qualitative interviews and Delphi/critical-decision elicitation, surveys, and machine-learning analysis of physiological signals.
notes: Confidently identified (found = yes). name: Sawyer, Ben D.
recent_1: Karwowski, W., Salvendy, G., Endsley, M., Rouse, W., Salmon, P., Stanney, K., Thatcher, A., Andre, T., Yang, J., Ayaz, H., Cakir, A., Duffy, V., Drury, C., Gao, Q., Guo, Y., Hancock, P., Marras, W. S., Rau, P., Sawyer, B., & Stanton, N. (2025). Grand challenges for human factors and ergonomics. Theoretical Issues in Ergonomics Science. DOI: 10.1080/1463922X.2024.2431336
recent_1_synopsis: A consensus position paper in which a large panel of senior human factors/ergonomics scholars (including Sawyer) articulates the grand challenges facing the discipline, spanning emerging technology, human-AI/autonomy interaction, systems safety, and societal impact. It matters as a field-defining agenda-setting statement that orients future HF/E research and Sawyer's inclusion signals recognition among the discipline's leading voices.
recent_2: Rashid, M. M., Atilgan, N., Dobres, J., Day, S., Penkova, V., Kucuk, M., Clapp, S. R., & Sawyer, B. D. (2024). Humanizing AI in education: A readability comparison of LLM and human-created educational content. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting. DOI: 10.1177/10711813241261689
recent_2_synopsis: This study compares the readability of educational content generated by large language models against human-authored content, applying readability metrics to assess how machine-generated text serves learners. It matters because it bridges Sawyer's readability/typography research program with the rapidly growing use of generative AI in education, informing how AI tools should be tuned for comprehension.
recent_3: Day, S. L., Atilgan, N., Giroux, A. E., & Sawyer, B. D. (2024). The influence of format readability on children's reading speed and comprehension. Education Sciences, 14(8), 854. DOI: 10.3390/educsci14080854
recent_3_synopsis: An empirical study examining how text formatting variables (such as font and character spacing) affect reading speed and comprehension in child readers. It matters because it extends the lab's individuated-readability findings from adults to children, supporting personalized digital-text interventions that could improve literacy outcomes for young readers.
seminal_1: Kaplan, A. D., Cruit, J., Endsley, M., Beers, S. M., Sawyer, B. D., & Hancock, P. A. (2021). The effects of virtual reality, augmented reality, and mixed reality as training enhancement methods: A meta-analysis. Human Factors, 63(4), 706-726. DOI: 10.1177/0018720820904229
seminal_1_synopsis: A meta-analysis quantifying how VR, AR, and MR compare to traditional methods as training-enhancement tools across a large body of empirical studies. It is foundational and Sawyer's single most-cited work (~459 citations per Crossref, ~856 per Google Scholar as of June 2026), serving as a heavily referenced evidence base for the design and evaluation of immersive training systems.
seminal_2: Wallace, S., Bylinskii, Z., Dobres, J., Kerr, B., Berlow, S., Treitman, R., Kumawat, N., Arpin, K., Miller, D. B., Huang, J., & Sawyer, B. D. (2022). Towards individuated reading experiences: Different fonts increase reading speed for different individuals. ACM Transactions on Computer-Human Interaction, 29(4), 1-56. DOI: 10.1145/3502222
seminal_2_synopsis: This large-scale study demonstrates that no single font is optimal for everyone and that matching font to the individual reader meaningfully increases reading speed without sacrificing comprehension. It is foundational to Sawyer's readability/personalization program (~54 citations per Crossref as of June 2026) and underpins the Readability Consortium's vision of reengineering digital typography for personalized reading.
expertise_overview: Sawyer is an applied neuroscientist and human factors engineer studying information exchange between humans and machines. His core research interests span driver attention and distraction, mental workload and cognitive failures, and human-AI/autonomy teaming, using methods including eye tracking, EEG/applied neuroscience, driving simulation, and mathematical/computational modeling of attention. A second major thread is readability and typography: through The Readability Consortium and Virtual Readability Lab he investigates how personalized fonts, character/word spacing, and text formatting affect reading speed and comprehension across adults and children. He also works on human factors of cybersecurity (e.g., the prevalence paradox) and, increasingly, the readability and human impact of generative AI content. His domains include transportation/automotive, education, cybersecurity, and immersive training (VR/AR/MR).

name: Seibt, Robert
recent_1: Wagenblast, F., Läubli, T., Seibt, R., Luger, T., Rieger, M. A., & Steinhilber, B. (2026). Bilateral trapezius activity during a dual task depends on age and sex – Results of an experimental laboratory study. Journal of Electromyography and Kinesiology, 88, 103129. DOI: 10.1016/j.jelekin.2026.103129
recent_1_synopsis: This laboratory study used bilateral surface electromyography of the upper trapezius to test how age and sex modulate muscle activation when a physical task is combined with a concurrent cognitive load. It shows that demographic factors meaningfully shape neuromuscular strain under dual-task conditions, which matters for designing age- and sex-inclusive workplace exposure limits.
recent_2: Wagenblast, F., Seibt, R., Süß, D., Luger, T., Rieger, M. A., & Steinhilber, B. (2026). At a mean load level typical for industrial manual work, muscular strain of the triceps brachii muscle highly depends on the load change ratio – preliminary results of an exploratory study. International Journal of Industrial Ergonomics, 112, 103853. DOI: 10.1016/j.ergon.2025.103853
recent_2_synopsis: Using surface EMG of the triceps brachii during simulated manual work, this exploratory study demonstrates that muscular strain at a fixed mean load depends strongly on the load change ratio (the dynamics of loading and unloading), not just the average force. The finding challenges exposure metrics based solely on mean load and argues for incorporating temporal load patterns into ergonomic risk assessment.
recent_3: Wagenblast, F., Seibt, R., Rieger, M. A., & Steinhilber, B. (2025). Measures of the psychophysiological response to recurrent anticipatory stress – the influence of neuroticism on heart rhythm and skin resistance. Scientific Reports, 15, 41177. DOI: 10.1038/s41598-025-28090-7
recent_3_synopsis: This study combined heart-rate-variability and electrodermal (skin resistance) measures to characterize how individuals respond physiologically to repeated anticipatory stress, examining neuroticism as a moderating personality trait. It links psychophysiological reactivity to individual differences, informing how mental-stress exposure should be interpreted in occupational health monitoring.
seminal_1: Luger, T., Bär, M., Seibt, R., Rieger, M. A., & Steinhilber, B. (2021). Using a Back Exoskeleton During Industrial and Functional Tasks—Effects on Muscle Activity, Posture, Performance, Usability, and Wearer Discomfort in a Laboratory Trial. Human Factors, 65(1), 5-21. DOI: 10.1177/00187208211007267
seminal_1_synopsis: This controlled laboratory trial quantified how a passive back-support exoskeleton changes trunk muscle activity, posture, task performance, usability, and discomfort across realistic industrial and functional tasks. With roughly 78 citations, it is among Seibt's most-cited works and is foundational for understanding the trade-offs of back exoskeletons, showing benefits are task-dependent rather than uniform.
seminal_2: Steinhilber, B., Seibt, R., Rieger, M. A., & Luger, T. (2020). Postural Control When Using an Industrial Lower Limb Exoskeleton: Impact of Reaching for a Working Tool and External Perturbation. Human Factors, 64(4), 635-648. DOI: 10.1177/0018720820957466
seminal_2_synopsis: This study examined how a passive lower-limb (chair-like) industrial exoskeleton affects postural control during tool reaching and in response to external perturbations, a key safety question for fall/balance risk. With approximately 31 citations, it is one of his most influential papers and established that such exoskeletons can alter balance responses, informing safe deployment of leg-support devices in industry.
expertise_overview: Robert Seibt studies occupational physiology, work-related biomechanics, and ergonomic intervention, with a central methodological focus on surface electromyography (sEMG) for quantifying muscular strain and fatigue. A major strand of his work evaluates passive industrial exoskeletons—both back-support and lower-limb devices—measuring their effects on muscle activity, posture, balance, heart rate, performance, usability, and wearer discomfort during simulated industrial and functional tasks. He also investigates the physiological demands of specific occupational contexts, including prolonged standing and lower-leg edema, dental and laparoscopic/minimally invasive surgical ergonomics, and repetitive or static manual loading of the upper limb. Methodologically he combines sEMG with posture analysis, heart-rate/heart-rate-variability and electrodermal measures, and accelerometry, and is concerned with how individual factors such as age, sex, and personality modulate strain and psychophysiological stress responses. His recent work refines exposure metrics by showing that load dynamics (e.g., load change ratio) and dual-task cognitive demands shape muscular strain beyond mean force levels.
notes: Identified with high confidence as the occupational-medicine/ergonomics researcher at the Institute of Occupational and Social Medicine and Health Services Research, University Hospital Tübingen, who collaborates with Benjamin Steinhilber, Monika A. Rieger, and Tessy Luger. Identity confirmed via the institute's official publications page (medizin.uni-tuebingen.de) and SAGE/Human Factors author listings matching all five anchor hf_papers. Seminal DOIs are the pre-verified hf_papers. No citation here is unverified as to existence; only the seminal citation COUNTS are unconfirmed against a live source.

name: Shaw, Tyler H.
recent_1: Sharp, W. H., Jackson, K. M., & Shaw, T. H. (2026). Guilty until proven innocent: Experiencing errors in training promotes relationship equity and trust calibration. Applied Ergonomics, 136, 104770. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104770
recent_1_synopsis: This study examined how experiencing automation errors during training affects relationship equity and trust calibration toward an autonomous agent. recent_2: Dang, J., Shaw, T. H., & Helton, W. S. (2025). Does being away with the fairies really matter? The importance of thought content for task performance on a Go-No-Go target detection task. Applied Ergonomics, 129, 104607. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2025.104607
recent_2_synopsis: This experiment investigated whether the content of mind-wandering episodes (task-unrelated vs. task-related thoughts) differentially impairs performance on a Go/No-Go sustained attention task. The findings highlight that not all off-task cognition is equally detrimental, with implications for understanding vigilance decrements in operational settings.
recent_3: Mouloua, S. A., Helton, W. S., Matthews, G., & Shaw, T. H. (2024). Self-control enhances vigilance performance in temporally irregular tasks: an fNIRS frontoparietal investigation. Frontiers in Neuroergonomics, 5, 1415089. DOI: 10.3389/fnrgo.2024.1415089
recent_3_synopsis: Using functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS), this study demonstrated that individuals high in trait self-control maintain better vigilance performance in temporally irregular tasks and show more efficient frontoparietal neural resource allocation.
