seminal_1: Shaw, T. H., Warm, J. S., Finomore, V., Tripp, L., Matthews, G., Weiler, E., & Parasuraman, R. (2009). Effects of sensory modality on cerebral blood flow velocity during vigilance. Neuroscience Letters, 461(3), 207–211. DOI: 10.1016/j.neulet.2009.06.008
seminal_1_synopsis: This landmark study used Transcranial Doppler Sonography to show that cerebral blood flow velocity declines during vigilance tasks across both visual and auditory modalities, providing physiological evidence that sustained attention is neurologically taxing regardless of input channel. With approximately 92 citations, this paper established a key neuroergonomic marker of the vigilance decrement and has been foundational for subsequent work linking cerebral hemodynamics to operator performance.
seminal_2: Kohn, S. C., de Visser, E. J., Wiese, E., Lee, Y.-C., & Shaw, T. H. (2021). Measurement of trust in automation: A narrative review and reference guide. Frontiers in Psychology, 12, 604977. DOI: 10.3389/fpsyg.2021.604977
seminal_2_synopsis: This comprehensive narrative review catalogued and evaluated the self-report instruments, behavioral measures, and physiological indices used to assess human trust in automated systems, providing a consolidated methodological reference for the field. With approximately 254 citations (Crossref; ~445 on Google Scholar), it has become the standard citation for researchers selecting trust measurement tools in human–automation interaction studies.
expertise_overview: Research centers on the psychophysiology of vigilance and sustained attention, with particular focus on how cognitive resource depletion, individual differences in self-control, and task parameters (temporal regularity, sensory modality, signal probability) shape the vigilance decrement. Neuroimaging methods—Transcranial Doppler Sonography, Transcranial Cerebral Oximetry, and functional near-infrared spectroscopy—are used to index cerebral hemodynamic changes during prolonged monitoring tasks, linking observable performance decrements to underlying neural resource expenditure. A parallel research stream examines human–automation and human–autonomy teaming, investigating how trust is calibrated, repaired after violations, and managed when operators supervise multiple autonomous agents simultaneously. Work on mind-wandering and thought content explores how off-task cognition during Go/No-Go tasks affects commission and omission errors, connecting vigilance theory to everyday attentional failures. Application domains include aviation, unmanned systems supervision, human–robot interaction, and educational technology involving telepresence robots.
notes: Identity confirmed via George Mason University Department of Psychology faculty page (https://psychology.gmu.edu/people/tshaw4) and HFAC lab page (https://humanfactors.gmu.edu/people/tshaw4); also matches Google Scholar profile (user=aCY_eesAAAAJ). Shaw is Associate Professor in the Department of Psychology at GMU; Ph.D. 2008, University of Cincinnati. Citation counts: Crossref counts as of June 2026 (seminal_1: 92; seminal_2: 254); Google Scholar shows ~445 for seminal_2 and ~3,873 total citations. All DOIs verified via Crossref API (June 2026): 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104770 (recent_1) — verified; 10.1016/j.apergo.2025.104607 (recent_2) — verified; 10.3389/fnrgo.2024.1415089 (recent_3) — verified; 10.1016/j.neulet.2009.06.008 (seminal_1) — verified; 10.3389/fpsyg.2021.604977 (seminal_2) — verified. All five seed hf_papers DOIs also verified. No unverifiable citations.

name: Sheridan, Thomas B.
recent_1: Sheridan, T. B. (2020). A Distracted Scientist: The Life and Contributions of John Senders. Human Factors, 62(8), 1219-1228. DOI: 10.1177/0018720820941970
recent_1_synopsis: A tribute and intellectual biography of John Senders, recounting his life and his foundational contributions to attention sampling, visual workload, and driver distraction research. Beyond memorializing a colleague, it traces the lineage of key human factors ideas (e.g., the queueing/sampling models of attention) that remain central to studies of distraction and monitoring. It situates Senders's work within the broader development of the field.
recent_2: Sheridan, T. B. (2019). Extending Three Existing Models to Analysis of Trust in Automation: Signal Detection, Statistical Parameter Estimation, and Model-Based Control. Human Factors, 61(7), 1017-1023. DOI: 10.1177/0018720819829951
recent_2_synopsis: This paper recasts trust in automation through three established quantitative frameworks—signal detection theory, statistical parameter estimation, and model-based control—to give trust a more formal, analyzable structure. It argues that treating trust as a measurable decision variable allows designers to predict reliance and calibrate automation transparency. The work matters because it pushes the largely descriptive trust literature toward testable, model-driven analysis.
recent_3: Sheridan, T. B. (2017). The Intricacies of User Adjustments of Alerting Thresholds. Human Factors, 59(6), 901-910. DOI: 10.1177/0018720817698616
recent_3_synopsis: Sheridan analyzes how operators set and adjust alerting/alarm thresholds, exposing the trade-offs between false alarms and missed detections that govern user trust and response. Using a signal-detection lens, he shows why naive threshold choices can degrade joint human-automation performance. The paper provides design guidance for adaptive alerting systems where the operator, not just the engineer, tunes sensitivity.
seminal_1: Parasuraman, R., Sheridan, T. B., & Wickens, C. D. (2000). A model for types and levels of human interaction with automation. IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics - Part A: Systems and Humans, 30(3), 286-297. DOI: 10.1109/3468.844354
seminal_1_synopsis: This landmark paper proposes a framework distinguishing four broad functions that can be automated (information acquisition, information analysis, decision selection, and action implementation) and a continuum of levels of automation within each. Approximately 6,300 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), it is one of the most cited works in human factors and the standard vocabulary for describing where and how much to automate. It is foundational because it gave designers and researchers a shared, analyzable model for allocating functions between humans and machines.
seminal_2: Sheridan, T. B. (1992). Telerobotics, Automation, and Human Supervisory Control. Cambridge, MA: MIT Press. ISBN: 978-0-262-19316-7 (no Crossref DOI)
seminal_2_synopsis: This MIT Press monograph is the definitive synthesis of Sheridan's pioneering work on supervisory control and telerobotics, formalizing the idea that humans increasingly supervise rather than directly operate complex automated systems. Approximately 3,700 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), it defined the conceptual foundations—supervisory control loops, telepresence, and human-automation function allocation—that underpin modern research on remote operation, robotics, and automated vehicles. It is foundational as the canonical reference establishing supervisory control as a distinct field.
expertise_overview: Sheridan's research centers on human supervisory control: how human operators plan, monitor, intervene in, and trust automated and semi-automated systems rather than controlling them continuously. Core topics include levels and types of automation, function allocation between humans and machines, telerobotics and teleoperation, telepresence and virtual presence, and trust and reliance in automation. Methodologically he favors analytical and mathematical modeling—control theory, signal detection theory, estimation theory, queueing/attention-sampling models—to formalize otherwise descriptive human-machine concepts. Application domains span aviation and air traffic, nuclear and process control, undersea and space telerobotics, surface transportation and automated driving, and alerting/alarm system design. His work also engages alerting thresholds, operator situation awareness, and mode transitions in automation. Across these threads he emphasizes quantitative, testable models of joint human-automation performance.
notes: Identification confidence: HIGH. This is Thomas B. Sheridan, Professor Emeritus of Mechanical Engineering and Aeronautics/Astronautics at MIT, founding figure in supervisory control and telerobotics. Identity confirmed via Wikipedia, MIT/ILP faculty listing, ResearchGate (Thomas-Sheridan-5), Research.com profile, and Google Scholar (user skzsFMUAAAAJ), cross-checked against the four pre-verified Human Factors anchor papers (2016-2020), all of which list T. B. Sheridan of MIT. DOI verification (all via Crossref api.crossref.org, June 17 2026, HTTP 200 with matching titles): recent_1 10.1177/0018720820941970 (verified), recent_2 10.1177/0018720819829951 (verified), recent_3 10.1177/0018720817698616 (verified), seminal_1 10.1109/3468.844354 (verified; title and authors Parasuraman/Sheridan/Wickens confirmed). The three 'recent' papers are his most recent verifiable peer-reviewed journal articles; as an emeritus author his latest journal work is from 2020 (the John Senders tribute). Post-2020 output is chiefly book chapters (e.g., 'Human Supervisory Control of Automation,' Handbook of Human Factors and Ergonomics, 2021) and books ('Modeling Human-System Interaction,' Wiley, 2017). UNVERIFIED DOI: seminal_2, the 1992 MIT Press book 'Telerobotics, Automation, and Human Supervisory Control,' has NO Crossref DOI of its own (Crossref returns only book-review records, e.g., 10.1017/s0263574700016209 and 10.1016/0005-1098(94)90205-4, which are reviews, not the book); cited with ISBN 978-0-262-19316-7 instead. Citation counts (~6,300 for seminal_1; ~3,700 for the 1992 book) are from Google Scholar, accessed June 17 2026, and are approximate. An alternative high-impact seminal paper with a verified DOI is Sheridan's 'Musings on Telepresence and Virtual Presence,' Presence 1(1), 1992, DOI 10.1162/pres.1992.1.1.120 (verified, ~2,400 cites), should a fully DOI-backed second seminal be preferred over the book.

name: Smith, C. A. P.
recent_1: Patton, C. E., Wickens, C. D., Noble, K. M., Clegg, B. A., & Smith, C. A. P. (2021). Rendezvous Under Temporal Uncertainty. Human Factors, 65(4), 592-617. DOI: 10.1177/00187208211026112
recent_1_synopsis: Across three experiments, participants controlled a ship attempting to meet (rendezvous with) another moving vessel arriving from various trajectories under temporal uncertainty. The work characterizes the cognitive limitations and a planning bias that lead operators to only partially compensate for uncertainty in the arrival time, with implications for maritime navigation and interception tasks.
recent_2: Patton, C. E., Wickens, C. D., Smith, C. A. P., & Clegg, B. A. (2021). Detection of Hostile Intent by Spatial Movements. Human Factors, 65(2), 227-236. DOI: 10.1177/00187208211015022
recent_2_synopsis: This study examined how well people can infer hostile intent from the movement patterns of other vessels, manipulating three levels of variability in the paths of computer-controlled ships. Participants judged which entity was hostile, illuminating how movement regularity and predictability support threat detection in maritime and surveillance settings.
recent_3: Wickens, C. D., Williams, A., Clegg, B. A., & Smith, C. A. P. (2019). Nautical Collision Avoidance: The Cognitive Challenges of Balancing Safety, Efficiency, and Procedures. Human Factors, 62(8), 1304-1321. DOI: 10.1177/0018720819871409
recent_3_synopsis: The paper investigates the cognitive demands faced by ship operators when avoiding collisions, where they must trade off safety, efficiency, and adherence to the Rules of the Road (COLREGs). It analyzes how operators perceive closest-point-of-approach geometry and select maneuvers, informing display and training support for nautical collision avoidance.
seminal_1: Hopp, P. J., Smith, C. A. P., Clegg, B. A., & Heggestad, E. D. (2005). Interruption Management: The Use of Attention-Directing Tactile Cues. Human Factors, 47(1), 1-11. DOI: 10.1518/0018720053653884
seminal_1_synopsis: In a continuous aircraft-monitoring task with periodic interruptions, participants who received tactile cues signaling an interruption's arrival and location outperformed an uncued group on both tasks. With roughly 90 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), this is among Smith's most-cited works and is foundational to the interruption-management and tactile-cueing literatures by demonstrating that informative tactile cues can simultaneously alert and orient attention.
seminal_2: Herdener, N., Wickens, C. D., Clegg, B. A., & Smith, C. A. P. (2016). Overconfidence in Projecting Uncertain Spatial Trajectories. Human Factors, 58(6), 899-914. DOI: 10.1177/0018720816645259
seminal_2_synopsis: This study shows that people systematically underestimate the variability (and overestimate the precision) of an object's future position when predicting uncertain spatial trajectories, exhibiting overconfidence. With approximately 22-32 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), it anchored Smith's productive spatial-trajectory-uncertainty research line and is foundational to subsequent work on visualizing and training judgments of spatial uncertainty (e.g., hurricane forecast cones, anchoring-and-adjustment effects).
expertise_overview: C. A. P. Smith's research centers on human decision making and information systems under uncertainty and time pressure. A long-standing thread examines human prediction and estimation of uncertain spatial trajectories, including overconfidence, anchoring-and-adjustment biases, and visualization or training interventions to improve such judgments, applied to maritime navigation, collision avoidance, interception/rendezvous, hostile-intent detection, and hurricane-forecast uncertainty. A second major thread is interruption management and attention support, particularly the use of tactile and multimodal cues to alert and reorient operators in multitasking environments. He also works on decision support systems, group and team decision making under time pressure, naval/air-warfare threat assessment, and team cognition. His methods combine controlled human-subjects experiments with cognitive modeling of prediction, variability estimation, and decision quality.
notes: Identification confidence: high. The seed HF papers (spatial-trajectory / nautical / Wickens-Clegg cluster) map unambiguously to C.A.P. Smith of the Department of Computer Information Systems, Colorado State University (Fort Collins), confirmed via Google Scholar (user=so6ADn4AAAAJ) and ResearchGate (profile/Cap-Smith). He publishes consistently as 'C. A. P. Smith' / 'C.A.P. Smith'; his full given names were not definitively resolved on his faculty/Scholar/ORCID pages, so the canonical name retains initials. Sources: Google Scholar profile, ResearchGate, SAGE/Crossref records. All five DOIs cited (10.1177/00187208211026112, 10.1177/00187208211015022, 10.1177/0018720819871409, 10.1518/0018720053653884, 10.1177/0018720816645259) were verified via Crossref API on 2026-06-17 with matching titles and authors. Citation counts are approximate, from Google Scholar as of 2026-06-17; Scholar reported slightly higher per-paper counts than the pre-verified seed list (e.g., interruption-tactile-cues ~91; overconfidence ~32 vs. seed 22), so the seminal counts should be treated as approximate. Recent publication output is sparse after 2021 (no post-2021 papers found), so the three most recent verifiable papers (2019-2021) were used.

name: Stanton, Neville A.
recent_1: Shariati, A., Anvari, B., Kästle, J. L., Krol, J., Motaghedolhagh, K., Stanton, N. A., Reed, N., & Wurdemann, H. (2025). Analysing Variables Associated With Driver Reaction During the Transition From Automated to Manual Driving. IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems, 26(10), 14536-14567. DOI: 10.1109/tits.2025.3594202
recent_1_synopsis: A driving-simulator study examining how driver reaction during transitions from automated to manual control varies with operational and individual variables. The work continues Stanton's long line of takeover-of-control research, identifying factors that lengthen or shorten safe resumption of manual driving. Findings inform the design of transition warnings and handover protocols for highly automated vehicles.
recent_2: Hulme, A., Stanton, N. A., Walker, G. H., Waterson, P., & Salmon, P. M. (2024). Testing the reliability of accident analysis methods: a comparison of AcciMap, STAMP-CAST and AcciNet. Ergonomics, 67(5), 695-715. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2023.2240048
recent_2_synopsis: An empirical reliability comparison of three systemic accident analysis methods (AcciMap, STAMP-CAST, and the newer AcciNet) applied by independent analysts to the same incident. The study quantifies inter-analyst agreement to test whether these widely used sociotechnical methods produce consistent findings. It contributes practical guidance on method selection and the methodological rigor of safety-science accident analysis.
recent_3: Salmon, P. M., McLean, S., Carden, T., King, B. J., Thompson, J., Baber, C., Stanton, N. A., & Read, G. J. M. (2024). When tomorrow comes: A prospective risk assessment of a future artificial general intelligence-based uncrewed combat aerial vehicle system. Applied Ergonomics, 117, 104245. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2024.104245
recent_3_synopsis: A prospective, systems-level risk assessment of a hypothetical future artificial general intelligence (AGI)-controlled uncrewed combat aerial vehicle, using human factors risk-identification methods. It extends Stanton and colleagues' program on the safety and controllability risks of advanced AI in safety-critical and military sociotechnical systems. The paper argues that ergonomics methods can anticipate emergent hazards before such systems are deployed.
seminal_1: Eriksson, A., & Stanton, N. A. (2017). Takeover Time in Highly Automated Vehicles: Noncritical Transitions to and From Manual Control. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 59(4), 689-705. DOI: 10.1177/0018720816685832
seminal_1_synopsis: A seminal driving-simulator study measuring how long drivers take to resume manual control after non-critical takeover requests in highly automated vehicles, establishing distributions of takeover times that became a benchmark for automated-driving handover research. Approximately 575 citations (Crossref, June 2026; ~1,600+ on Google Scholar). It is foundational because it provided some of the first systematic empirical evidence on transition-of-control timing, directly shaping HMI design guidelines and regulatory thinking for SAE Level 3 automation.
seminal_2: Stanton, N. A., Stewart, R., Harris, D., Houghton, R. J., Baber, C., McMaster, R., Salmon, P., Hoyle, G., Walker, G., Young, M. S., Linsell, M., Dymott, R., & Green, D. (2006). Distributed situation awareness in dynamic systems: theoretical development and application of an ergonomics methodology. Ergonomics, 49(12-13), 1288-1311. DOI: 10.1080/00140130600612762
seminal_2_synopsis: A landmark theoretical and methodological paper that shifted situation awareness from an individual-cognition construct to a property distributed across human and non-human agents in a sociotechnical system, and introduced an ergonomics methodology to model it. Approximately 384 citations (Crossref, June 2026; well over 1,000 on Google Scholar). It is foundational because it launched the influential Distributed Situation Awareness (DSA) framework now widely applied across aviation, military command-and-control, energy, and transport domains.
expertise_overview: Stanton's research centers on modelling, predicting, analysing, and evaluating human performance in complex sociotechnical systems, and on designing the interaction between humans and technology. He is a leading developer and validator of human factors and ergonomics methods, including SHERPA (human error prediction), EAST (Event Analysis of Systemic Teamwork), cognitive work analysis, and the Distributed Situation Awareness framework. A major strand of his work addresses driving automation, vehicle interface design, takeover/transition of control, and road safety. He applies these methods across transport (automobiles, aircraft, ships, submarines), military command-and-control, healthcare, energy distribution, and control-room design. More recently his program has expanded to the human factors and safety risks of artificial intelligence and artificial general intelligence in safety-critical systems. His methodological toolkit spans simulator experiments, systems-analysis and accident-analysis methods, teamwork assessment, and sociotechnical systems theory.
notes: Identification confidence: very high. Confirmed as Neville A. Stanton, Emeritus Professor of Human Factors in Transport, University of Southampton (Transportation Research Group / hfesoton). Sources: Google Scholar profile (user=4miFHkcAAAAJ; ~51,800 total citations, h-index 113, accessed June 2026), University of Southampton faculty page, Wikipedia, ResearchGate, and Research.com. All four ground-truth Human Factors DOIs were independently re-verified via Crossref (200 + matching title/venue). Every recent and seminal DOI was verified via the Crossref API (api.crossref.org/works/<DOI>): IEEE TITS 2025 (10.1109/tits.2025.3594202), Ergonomics AcciMap 2024 (10.1080/00140139.2023.2240048), Applied Ergonomics AGI 2024 (10.1016/j.apergo.2024.104245), Takeover Time HF 2017 (10.1177/0018720816685832), and DSA Ergonomics 2006 (10.1080/00140130600612762). Stanton's co-authorship on all five was confirmed in the Crossref author lists. Citation counts cited above are Crossref is-referenced-by-count as of June 17 2026 (Takeover Time = 575; DSA = 384); Google Scholar totals are substantially higher (the Takeover Time and DSA papers each exceed 1,000+ on Scholar) but Scholar counts are not API-verifiable and are given only as approximate context. No DOIs were invented; no unverifiable DOIs were included. Note: an initially considered recent candidate ('Once a driver, always a driver', Applied Ergonomics 2024, 10.1016/j.apergo.2024.104366) was excluded because Crossref author metadata does not list Stanton.

name: Steinhilber, Benjamin
recent_1: Wagenblast, F., Läubli, T., Seibt, R., Luger, T., Rieger, M. A., & Steinhilber, B. (2026). Bilateral trapezius activity during a dual task depends on age and sex – Results of an experimental laboratory study. Journal of Electromyography and Kinesiology, 88, 103129. DOI: 10.1016/j.jelekin.2026.103129
recent_1_synopsis: This experimental laboratory study measured bilateral upper trapezius muscle activity (surface EMG) while participants performed a combined motor and cognitive dual task, showing that muscular load patterns differ systematically with both age and sex. The findings matter for ergonomic risk assessment because they indicate that demographic factors modulate neck/shoulder muscular strain under combined physical and mental demands, informing workplace design for an aging and diverse workforce.
recent_2: Luger, T., Bott, J., Seibt, R., Gabriel, J., Griese, M., Enghofer, S., Rieger, M. A., & Steinhilber, B. (2025). Influence of compression stockings with different compression classes on lower leg edema and perceived discomfort during a two-hour static standing exposure – a pilot study. Applied Ergonomics, 129, 104621. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2025.104621
recent_2_synopsis: This pilot study evaluated whether compression stockings of different compression classes reduce lower-leg edema and perceived discomfort during two hours of prolonged static standing, a common occupational exposure. By quantifying physiological (edema) and subjective (discomfort) responses, it provides early evidence on a low-cost intervention for workers in standing-intensive jobs such as retail and manufacturing.
recent_3: Schrøder Jakobsen, L., Samani, A., Desbrosses, K., de Zee, M., Steinhilber, B., & Madeleine, P. (2025). Effects of 24-weeks in-field use of a back-supporting exoskeleton on biomechanics, work intensity and musculoskeletal discomfort: A randomized controlled trial among logistic workers. Applied Ergonomics, 125, 104469. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2025.104469
recent_3_synopsis: This randomized controlled trial followed logistics workers over 24 weeks of real-world (in-field) use of a back-supporting exoskeleton, assessing changes in biomechanics, work intensity, and musculoskeletal discomfort. It is notable for moving beyond short laboratory trials to a long-duration field deployment, addressing the open question of whether occupational exoskeletons deliver sustained benefit under realistic working conditions.
seminal_1: Bär, M., Steinhilber, B., Rieger, M. A., & Luger, T. (2021). The influence of using exoskeletons during occupational tasks on acute physical stress and strain compared to no exoskeleton – A systematic review and meta-analysis. Applied Ergonomics, 94, 103385. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2021.103385
seminal_1_synopsis: This systematic review and meta-analysis (~200 citations per Google Scholar, June 2026) synthesized 63 studies (52 in quantitative meta-analysis) on back, upper-limb, and lower-limb occupational exoskeletons, finding consistent reductions in muscle activity, joint moments, and perceived strain in the supported body regions while flagging that net effects on worker health remain unclear. It is foundational as the most-cited quantitative consolidation of the occupational-exoskeleton evidence base, widely used to frame subsequent intervention research and practice guidance.
seminal_2: Luger, T., Bär, M., Seibt, R., Rieger, M. A., & Steinhilber, B. (2021). Using a Back Exoskeleton During Industrial and Functional Tasks—Effects on Muscle Activity, Posture, Performance, Usability, and Wearer Discomfort in a Laboratory Trial. Human Factors, 65(1), 5-21. DOI: 10.1177/00187208211007267
seminal_2_synopsis: This Human Factors laboratory trial (~78-121 citations; 78 from the verified seed list and ~121 per Google Scholar, June 2026) comprehensively evaluated a passive back exoskeleton across multiple industrial and functional tasks, measuring muscle activity, posture, task performance, usability, and wearer discomfort together rather than in isolation. It is influential because its multi-outcome design exposed the trade-offs of exoskeleton use (reduced back muscle load but potential discomfort and task-dependent effects), shaping how the field evaluates these devices.
expertise_overview: Steinhilber studies work physiology and ergonomics, with a central focus on occupational physical exposures and how they contribute to work-related musculoskeletal disorders. A major strand of his research evaluates wearable assistive systems—passive back-support and lower-limb exoskeletons—quantifying their effects on muscle activity, posture, postural control, heart rate, performance, usability, and wearer discomfort across simulated and real industrial, logistics, surgical, and nursing tasks. He works extensively with surface electromyography (EMG), muscle-fatigue and muscle-twitch-force measurement, biomechanical posture analysis, and physiological strain indices, applied in controlled laboratory experiments, randomized controlled trials, and field studies. He also investigates prolonged static postures (standing, forward bending), dual-task motor-cognitive loading, age and sex effects on muscular response, and work-break interventions for musculoskeletal prevention. Application domains span manufacturing and logistics, healthcare (laparoscopic/robotic surgery ergonomics, patient handling), and general occupational safety and health. His methodological contributions include systematic reviews and meta-analyses that consolidate the evidence on exoskeleton effectiveness for occupational use.
notes: Identity confidently confirmed (found=yes). Anchored on the six pre-verified Human Factors exoskeleton/work-physiology papers, which match exactly to Benjamin Steinhilber, PD Dr., head of the 'Work-related Exposures – Work Design' research unit at the Institute of Occupational and Social Medicine and Health Services Research, University Hospital Tübingen (confirmed via Tübingen faculty/institute pages, ResearchGate profile, and Google Scholar profile user=Rc0CQzsAAAAJ). Citation counts from Google Scholar profile retrieved 2026-06-17: total ~2,045 citations, h-index 25; top paper (2021 meta-analysis) ~200 cites; back-exoskeleton Human Factors trial ~121 cites on Scholar (the verified seed list lists 78, likely an earlier snapshot — both figures noted). All five cited DOIs (three recent + two seminal) were verified to resolve via Crossref API on 2026-06-17 with matching titles and Steinhilber in the author list: 10.1016/j.jelekin.2026.103129, 10.1016/j.apergo.2025.104621, 10.1016/j.apergo.2025.104469, 10.1016/j.apergo.2021.103385, 10.1177/00187208211007267. Note: Crossref lists the Human Factors back-exoskeleton paper as volume 65, issue 1, pp. 5-21 (2023 print issue) under DOI 10.1177/00187208211007267, the same DOI given in the seed list (online-first 2021). No citations were left unverifiable.

name: Strayer, David L.
recent_1: Sahoo, S., Strayer, D. L., & Biondi, F. (2026). An Integrated Open-Source Framework for Automated Analysis of Driver Activity, Glance, and Vehicle Automation Mode. IISE Transactions on Occupational Ergonomics and Human Factors, 1-16. DOI: 10.1080/24725838.2026.2642716
recent_1_synopsis: This methods paper presents an open-source pipeline that automatically extracts and synchronizes driver activity, eye-glance behavior, and the engagement state of vehicle automation from naturalistic video and vehicle data. By replacing labor-intensive manual coding with reproducible automated analysis, it lowers the barrier to large-scale studies of driver attention and behavior under partial automation. It matters for the field because scalable, transparent measurement of glance and automation-mode transitions is a persistent bottleneck in distraction and automation research.
recent_2: Sanbonmatsu, D. M., Crabtree, K. W., McDonnell, A. S., Cooper, J. M., & Strayer, D. L. (2024). Automated driving experiences, attention, and intentions following extensive on-road usage of a level 2 automation vehicle. Journal of Safety Research, 90, 199-207. DOI: 10.1016/j.jsr.2024.05.002
recent_2_synopsis: Drawing on drivers' experiences after extended real-world use of a Level 2 partially automated vehicle, this study examines how prolonged exposure shapes attention allocation, trust, and future intentions to use automation. It links subjective experience to attentional engagement, showing how habitual use of automation can reshape vigilance and reliance. The work is important for understanding the longer-term behavioral adaptation and complacency risks that emerge as partial automation becomes commonplace.
recent_3: Biondi, F., McDonnell, A. S., Cooper, J. M., & Strayer, D. L. (2024). Using the ISO Detection response task to measure the cognitive load of driving four separate vehicles on two distinct highways. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 102, 260-269. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2024.02.013
recent_3_synopsis: This on-road study applies the standardized ISO Detection Response Task (DRT) to quantify the cognitive load imposed by driving four different vehicles across two highway environments. It demonstrates that vehicle and roadway context systematically modulate mental workload, validating the DRT as a sensitive field measure of cognitive demand. The findings matter for designing in-vehicle systems and for benchmarking workload across real driving conditions rather than only in simulators.
seminal_1: Strayer, D. L., Drews, F. A., & Crouch, D. J. (2006). A Comparison of the Cell Phone Driver and the Drunk Driver. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 48(2), 381-391. DOI: 10.1518/001872006777724471
seminal_1_synopsis: Using a high-fidelity driving simulator, this landmark study directly compared drivers conversing on a cell phone (handheld or hands-free) with drivers legally intoxicated at a 0.08% blood alcohol concentration, finding that cell-phone drivers were as impaired as drunk drivers. With roughly 2,000+ citations (Google Scholar, approximate, 2026), it is among Strayer's most-cited works and is foundational because it reframed distracted driving as a cognitive (not merely manual/visual) impairment, directly informing public policy and hands-free legislation debates.
seminal_2: Strayer, D. L., & Johnston, W. A. (2001). Driven to Distraction: Dual-Task Studies of Simulated Driving and Conversing on a Cellular Telephone. Psychological Science, 12(6), 462-466. DOI: 10.1111/1467-9280.00386
seminal_2_synopsis: This early dual-task study demonstrated that conversing on a cellular phone disrupts simulated driving performance even when the phone is hands-free, isolating the conversation (attentional) component rather than the manual manipulation of the device. With roughly 2,000+ citations (Google Scholar, approximate, 2026), it is foundational for establishing that cell-phone distraction stems from central attentional limitations and inattention blindness, launching a major line of cognitive-distraction research.
expertise_overview: Research centers on human attention and the limits of multitasking, with a primary focus on driver distraction and the cognitive workload imposed by secondary tasks such as cell-phone conversation, voice interfaces, and in-vehicle infotainment systems. A major strand examines partially automated (Level 2) driving, studying driver engagement, vigilance decrement, complacency, and the neural and behavioral indices of workload during automated operation. Methodologically, the work combines high-fidelity driving simulators and on-road instrumented-vehicle studies with electroencephalography (EEG/ERP), eye-tracking and glance analysis, and standardized workload measures such as the ISO Detection Response Task. A secondary line of research investigates attention restoration through exposure to nature and the cognitive benefits of time spent in natural environments. Across these domains, the unifying questions concern how attention is allocated under demand, why concurrent tasks produce inattention blindness, and how to measure mental workload objectively. Application domains include traffic safety, automotive human factors, public policy on distracted driving, and the design of in-vehicle technologies.
notes: High-confidence identification. David L. Strayer, Department of Psychology, University of Utah, confirmed via University of Utah faculty profile (faculty.utah.edu / psych.utah.edu), the APA career profile, and ResearchGate; identity anchored by exact match of the provided hf_papers (e.g., the CarPlay/Android Auto infotainment paper, DOI 10.1177/0018720819836575, lists Strayer as first author with co-authors Cooper, McCarty, etc.). name: Su, Bingyi
recent_1: Jung, S., Qing, L., Su, B., & Xu, X. (2026). Toward a Fully Automated NIOSH Lifting Equation Using Computer Vision. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261418858
recent_1_synopsis: Develops a computer-vision pipeline that automatically extracts the geometric and temporal task variables needed to compute the Revised NIOSH Lifting Equation from ordinary video, removing the need for manual measurement of lifting tasks. It matters because it pushes a widely used but labor-intensive occupational risk-assessment standard toward scalable, contactless, automated deployment in real workplaces.
recent_2: Su, B., et al. (2025). Enhancing Data Privacy in Human Factors Studies with Federated Learning. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251348025
recent_2_synopsis: Applies federated learning so that human factors models can be trained across distributed datasets without sharing raw, privacy-sensitive participant data such as biometric or wearable-sensor recordings. It addresses a growing barrier to data-driven ergonomics research, showing how predictive performance can be retained while protecting human-subject privacy.
recent_3: Lu, L., Su, B., Jung, S., Qing, L., & Xu, X. (2025). Effects of anthropomorphic features of co-robots on users' perception and emotional responses during human–robot collaboration. Ergonomics, 69(6), 937-951. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2025.2497523
recent_3_synopsis: Experimentally examines how anthropomorphic design features of collaborative robots shape workers' perceptions and emotional responses during shared tasks. The findings inform co-robot interface and embodiment design choices that influence trust, comfort, and acceptance in human–robot collaboration settings.
seminal_1: Su, B., Jung, S., Lu, L., Wang, H., Qing, L., & Xu, X. (2024). Exploring the impact of human-robot interaction on workers' mental stress in collaborative assembly tasks. Applied Ergonomics, 116, 104224. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2024.104224
seminal_1_synopsis: First-authored study that manipulates human–robot interaction presence, complexity, and modality (button press, hand gestures, verbal commands) in a collaborative Lego-assembly task and measures resulting operator mental stress, finding that gesture and verbal modalities raised stress relative to button presses. With roughly 34 citations (Crossref, June 2026), it is Su's most-cited work and a foundational empirical reference on how interaction design drives psychological load in human–robot collaboration.
seminal_2: Xie, Z., Lu, L., Wang, H., Su, B., Liu, Y., & Xu, X. (2023). Improving Workers' Musculoskeletal Health During Human-Robot Collaboration Through Reinforcement Learning. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society. DOI: 10.1177/00187208231177574
seminal_2_synopsis: Uses reinforcement learning together with computer-vision-based human pose reconstruction to optimize the robot's hand-over position so as to reduce workers' musculoskeletal disorder risk during collaboration. With about 12 citations (per the provided ground-truth anchor), it is foundational for the lab's line of work coupling RL/CV with occupational biomechanics to proactively design safer, ergonomically optimized human–robot collaboration.
expertise_overview: Bingyi Su works at the intersection of computer vision, machine learning, and occupational ergonomics, with a central focus on human–robot collaboration. Core methods include deep-learning-based human pose estimation and action recognition from RGB video, reinforcement learning for optimizing robot behavior, and generative/diffusion and federated-learning approaches for privacy-preserving synthesis of physiological and time-series data (e.g., EMG). A recurring application domain is occupational biomechanics and manual materials handling, including automating the Revised NIOSH Lifting Equation and assessing musculoskeletal disorder risk. He also studies the psychological side of collaboration—how interaction modality, robot anthropomorphism, and task complexity affect workers' mental stress, perception, and emotional response. Methodologically he combines controlled human-subject experiments with computational modeling, and his work emphasizes non-invasive, video-based, and data-efficient sensing for real workplaces.

name: Szalma, James L.
recent_1: Szalma, J. L., Teo, G., Schmidt-Daly, T., & Hancock, P. A. (2025). Training for vigilance on the move using knowledge of results: The effects of feedback type on performance and subjective response. Ergonomics, 1-19. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2025.2588167
recent_1_synopsis: Using a dynamic, first-person video-game vigilance paradigm, this study manipulated the type of knowledge of results (KR) feedback (e.g., feedback on hits, misses, and/or false alarms) to test how different forms of performance feedback shape sustained-attention performance, perceived workload, and stress. It extends Szalma's long program on training for vigilance by showing that the informational content of feedback, not merely its presence, drives improvement. It matters because it offers actionable guidance for designing training and feedback regimes for operationally relevant, moving-observer detection tasks.
recent_2: Garibaldi, A. E., Waldfogle, G. E., & Szalma, J. L. (2023). Motivational underpinnings of social media use. Psychology of Popular Media, 12(4), 434-449. DOI: 10.1037/ppm0000431
recent_2_synopsis: Drawing on motivation theory, this paper examines the motivational and individual-difference factors that drive engagement with social media. It applies Szalma's interest in motivation and individual-task interactions to a contemporary technology-use domain. The work matters because it connects basic motivational constructs to patterns of everyday digital-technology use and potential problematic use.
recent_3: Davidson, S. S., Keebler, J. R., Zhang, T., Chaparro, B., Szalma, J. L., & Frederick, C. M. (2023). The development and validation of a universal enjoyment measure: The enjoy scale. Current Psychology, 42(21), 17733-17745. DOI: 10.1007/s12144-022-02967-6
recent_3_synopsis: This paper develops and psychometrically validates the ENJOY scale, a domain-general self-report instrument for measuring enjoyment across activities and contexts. It reflects Szalma's methodological emphasis on individual differences and affective/motivational states relevant to task engagement. It matters as a reusable measurement tool for researchers studying enjoyment, engagement, and motivation in human-technology interaction and beyond.
seminal_1: Schaefer, K. E., Chen, J. Y. C., Szalma, J. L., & Hancock, P. A. (2016). A Meta-Analysis of Factors Influencing the Development of Trust in Automation: Implications for Understanding Autonomy in Future Systems. Human Factors, 58(3), 377-400. DOI: 10.1177/0018720816634228
seminal_1_synopsis: This meta-analysis quantitatively synthesizes the human-, automation-, and environment-related factors that shape the development of trust in automated systems, providing one of the most comprehensive empirical frameworks for trust calibration. With approximately 1,106 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is by a wide margin Szalma's most-cited work and is foundational because it organized a fragmented literature into a tractable factor structure that has guided a decade of human-automation and human-robot trust research.
seminal_2: Szalma, J. L., & Hancock, P. A. (2011). Noise effects on human performance: A meta-analytic synthesis. Psychological Bulletin, 137(4), 682-707. DOI: 10.1037/a0023987
seminal_2_synopsis: This large-scale meta-analysis synthesizes decades of research on how acoustic noise affects human performance, identifying task and noise characteristics that moderate impairment versus facilitation. With approximately 658 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is foundational because it is a definitive, high-visibility quantitative review in a top psychology outlet (Psychological Bulletin) that established moderator effects (task type, noise properties) as central to understanding stressor-performance relationships, a core theme of Szalma's stress-and-performance research.
expertise_overview: Szalma's research centers on how task demands interact with individual differences, such as cognitive abilities, personality, emotion, and motivation, to shape performance, perceived workload, and stress in cognitively demanding tasks. A primary focus is vigilance and sustained attention, including the vigilance decrement, signal and threat detection, and training methods (e.g., knowledge-of-results feedback) to improve detection performance. He is a leading practitioner of meta-analytic synthesis, having quantitatively reviewed the effects of noise, thermal and time-pressure stressors, and the factors driving trust in automation and robots. His work spans human-automation and human-robot interaction, examining trust calibration, automation dependence, and operator individual differences. He also studies motivation and engagement, including measurement of affective states such as enjoyment and the motivational drivers of technology use. Methodologically he combines controlled laboratory experiments, psychometric scale development, and meta-analysis, often in collaboration with P. A. Hancock, Joel S. Warm, and Gerald Matthews.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as James L. Szalma, Professor in the Human Factors and Cognitive Psychology Ph.D. program, Department of Psychology, University of Central Florida, director of the Performance Research Laboratory (PeRL). Sources confirming identity: UCF Psychology faculty page (sciences.ucf.edu/psychology/person/james-l-szalma/), UCF PeRL publications page, his Google Scholar profile (scholar.google.com/citations?user=YybdujUAAAAJ), and co-author profile pages (peterhancock.ucf.edu). name: Tatasciore, Monica
recent_1: Tatasciore, M., & Loft, S. (2025). Adaptable Automation Transparency: Should Humans Be Provided Flexibility to Self-Select Transparency Information? Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251349269
recent_1_synopsis: Investigates whether giving operators the flexibility to self-select the level/type of automation transparency information improves outcomes compared to fixed transparency. Addresses the trade-off between adaptable interfaces and the cognitive cost of managing transparency, extending the author's program on how transparency should be delivered rather than merely whether it helps.
recent_2: Tatasciore, M., & Loft, S. (2025). Calibrating Reliance on Automated Advice: Transparency and Trust Calibration Feedback. International Journal of Human-Computer Interaction, 41(23), 14723-14733. DOI: 10.1080/10447318.2025.2487861
recent_2_synopsis: Examines how automation transparency combined with explicit trust-calibration feedback can help users appropriately calibrate reliance on automated advice. The work targets the persistent problem of disuse and misuse of automation, testing interventions that align reliance behavior with actual automation reliability.
recent_3: Tatasciore, M., Strickland, L., & Loft, S. (2025). Deciphering Automation Transparency: Do the Benefits of Transparency Differ Based on Whether Decision Recommendations Are Provided? Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251318465
recent_3_synopsis: Tests whether the benefits of automation transparency depend on whether the automation also supplies an explicit decision recommendation. Disentangles the contribution of transparency from the recommendation itself, clarifying when added transparency information actually improves decision accuracy and reliance. Published online 2025-02-03; cited 6 times.
seminal_1: Tatasciore, M., Bowden, V. K., Visser, T. A. W., Michailovs, S. I. C., & Loft, S. (2019). The Benefits and Costs of Low and High Degree of Automation. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/0018720819867181
seminal_1_synopsis: Foundational empirical study (approx. 18 citations per Crossref, 2026-06-17) establishing that increasing the degree of automation yields performance benefits during normal operation but imposes costs when automation fails, in a simulated submarine track-management task. It is foundational to Tatasciore's research line because it operationalizes the degree-of-automation framework that motivates her subsequent transparency work, and anchors the benefit/cost trade-off central to her PhD.
seminal_2: Gegoff, I., Tatasciore, M., Bowden, V. K., McCarley, J., & Loft, S. (2023). Transparent Automated Advice to Mitigate the Impact of Variation in Automation Reliability. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208231196738
seminal_2_synopsis: Highly cited study (approx. 15 citations per Crossref, 2026-06-17) showing that increased automation transparency can mitigate the negative effects of both low and high automation reliability on disuse, misuse, and trust in automated advice. It is foundational to her transparency program because it demonstrates transparency as a buffer against variation in reliability, a recurring theme across her later Human Factors and IJHCI papers.
expertise_overview: Tatasciore studies human-automation interaction, with a focus on how the degree of automation and the design of automation transparency affect operator performance, situation awareness, workload, trust, and reliance. A core interest is calibrating appropriate reliance: ensuring transparency and feedback reduce both disuse and misuse of automated advice, especially when automation reliability varies. She investigates whether transparency benefits depend on accompanying decision recommendations and whether operators should be given flexibility to self-select transparency information. Her empirical work uses simulated command-and-control settings such as uninhabited-vehicle management and submarine track management, relevant to safety-critical domains like air traffic control and defense. She also examines automation confidence information and trust-calibration feedback as design levers. Methodologically she emphasizes controlled cognitive experiments measuring decision accuracy, response time, perceived workload, trust, and usability.

name: van Paassen, M. M.

recent_1_synopsis: This study used multilevel confirmatory factor analysis on data from simulated flight scenarios to validate the psychometric structure of the Startle and Surprise Inventories. recent_2: Vlaskamp, D., Pollitt, A., Blundell, J., Landman, A., Groen, E. L., van Paassen, M. M., Stroosma, O., & Mulder, M. (2025). recent_2_synopsis: A survey of 234 helicopter pilots found that 96% had experienced impactful startle or surprise events, with disorientation, tail-rotor incidents, and inadvertent entry into instrument meteorological conditions rated most stressful. The study assesses current mitigation strategies and the usability of the "Aviate, Breathe, Check" startle-management procedure. It extends the flight-deck startle/surprise program into rotary-wing operations.
recent_3: Evertsen, F. W., Landman, A., Groen, E. L., Stroosma, O., van Paassen, M. M., & Mulder, M. (2025). Quantifying the Impact of Spatial Disorientation on Pilot Mental Workload and Attentional Focus. Human Factors, 67(10), 997-1010. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251323116
recent_3_synopsis: This study empirically quantified how spatial disorientation elevates pilot mental workload and reshapes attentional allocation during simulated flight. By linking a perceptual illusion to objective workload and attention measures, it clarifies the cognitive cost of disorientation events. The findings inform training and display design aimed at helping pilots detect and recover from disorientation.
seminal_1: Lam, T. M., Boschloo, H. W., Mulder, M., & van Paassen, M. M. (2009). Artificial Force Field for Haptic Feedback in UAV Teleoperation. IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics - Part A: Systems and Humans, 39(6), 1316-1330. DOI: 10.1109/TSMCA.2009.2028239
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper introduced an artificial-force-field method that maps environmental obstacle constraints to repulsive haptic forces on the operator's control device, offloading the visual channel during UAV teleoperation. With roughly 230+ citations (Google Scholar, approximate, 2026-06-17), it is his single most-cited work. It is foundational because it became a widely cited template for haptic shared-control and collision-avoidance feedback in unmanned and teleoperated vehicle research.
seminal_2: Amelink, M. H. J., Mulder, M., van Paassen, M. M., & Flach, J. (2005). Theoretical Foundations for a Total Energy-Based Perspective Flight-Path Display. The International Journal of Aviation Psychology, 15(3), 205-231. DOI: 10.1207/s15327108ijap1503_1
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper derived the total-energy reference profile and energy-angle constructs and applied Ecological Interface Design and the Abstraction Hierarchy to the aircraft energy-management and manual-control task. With roughly 130+ citations (Google Scholar, approximate, 2026-06-17), it is an early, heavily cited demonstration of Ecological Interface Design applied to aircraft display design, anchoring a long program on ecological flight-deck interfaces.
expertise_overview: Research studies human-machine interaction in aerospace and vehicle control, spanning perceptual and cognitive processes, human manual control modeling, and cybernetics. Methods combine human-in-the-loop flight simulation (including the SIMONA research simulator and real-time/distributed simulation software), control-theoretic and genetic maximum-likelihood identification of operator behavior, and controlled experimental studies of pilot performance. A major thread applies Ecological Interface Design and the Abstraction Hierarchy to flight-deck and air-traffic displays, including energy-management, separation-assistance, and decision-support interfaces. A second major line concerns haptic and shared control—artificial force fields, haptic gas pedals, and haptic feedback for teleoperation and driving. More recent work addresses pilot training for startle and surprise, spatial disorientation, mental workload, and attentional focus under unexpected in-flight events. Application domains center on aviation (fixed- and rotary-wing piloting, flight simulation, air traffic control) and extend to UAV teleoperation and automotive driver support.

name: Visser, Troy A. W.
recent_1: Greenwell-Barnden, J. N., Visser, T. A. W., Loft, S., Whitney, S. J., & Bowden, V. K. (2026). How multi-tasking ability impacts performance, workload, situation awareness, stress and trust with simulated imperfect automation. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261431968

recent_3: McLellan, R., Bowden, V. K., Visser, T. A. W., & Kelley, J. L. (2025). Camouflage using surface disruption: The importance of corners versus edges. Ecology and Evolution, 15(8), e72052. DOI: 10.1002/ece3.72052
recent_3_synopsis: Bridging Visser's visual-perception expertise with biology, this paper used artificial visual-search stimuli to test how disruptive surface patterning at object corners versus edges affects detectability. It clarifies which contour features best break up an object's outline, contributing to the perceptual theory of camouflage and edge/corner processing in visual attention.
seminal_1: Visser, T. A. W., Bischof, W. F., & Di Lollo, V. (1999). Attentional switching in spatial and nonspatial domains: Evidence from the attentional blink. Psychological Bulletin, 125(4), 458–469. DOI: 10.1037/0033-2909.125.4.458
seminal_1_synopsis: This review synthesized attentional-blink findings to argue that a common attentional-switching mechanism operates across both spatial and nonspatial domains, integrating disparate temporal-attention results under a single framework. With roughly 388 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2026), it is Visser's most-cited work and a foundational reference in the temporal attention / attentional-blink literature.
seminal_2: English, M. C. W., Gignac, G. E., Visser, T. A. W., Whitehouse, A. J. O., & Enns, J. T. (2021). DOI: 10.1186/s13229-021-00445-7
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper developed and validated the Comprehensive Autistic Trait Inventory (CATI), a multidimensional self-report measure of autistic traits in the general population, addressing limitations of earlier single-score instruments. With approximately 186 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2026), it has become a widely adopted assessment tool, making it Visser's most influential contribution outside the attention literature.
expertise_overview: Visser studies visual and temporal attention, with longstanding work on the attentional blink, attentional switching, and the dynamics of perception across rapidly presented sequences of objects. A second major strand applies this cognitive-psychology foundation to human-automation interaction—examining trust calibration, automation transparency, levels and degrees of automation, situation awareness, and workload in simulated supervisory-control settings such as submarine command-and-control, air traffic control, and aviation. He frequently focuses on how individual differences (e.g., multi-tasking ability) moderate performance with imperfect automation. Methodologically he relies on controlled behavioral experiments, psychophysics, simulated task environments, and psychometric scale development and validation. His application domains span defense and safety-critical supervisory systems, driving and video-game training, and clinical/educational populations including autism, ADHD, and dyslexia, where he studies attention-related individual differences and trait measurement.

name: Warm, Joel S.
recent_1: Dillard, M. B., Warm, J. S., Funke, G. J., Nelson, W. T., Finomore, V. S., McClernon, C. K., Eggemeier, F. T., Tripp, L. D., & Funke, M. E. (2019). recent_1_synopsis: This study tested whether the subjective sense that time is passing quickly (time "flying") moderates the well-documented negative affective and workload reactions to vigilance tasks. It found that even when observers perceived time to pass quickly, sustained monitoring remained unpleasant, mentally demanding, and stressful. The result reinforces the resource-depletion view that vigilance is genuinely hard cognitive work rather than a passive, low-effort activity.
recent_2: Funke, M. E., Warm, J. S., Matthews, G., Funke, G. J., Chiu, P. Y., Shaw, T. H., & Greenlee, E. T. (2017). The Neuroergonomics of Vigilance: Effects of Spatial Uncertainty on Cerebral Blood Flow Velocity and Oculomotor Fatigue. Human Factors, 59(1), 62-75. DOI: 10.1177/0018720816683121
recent_2_synopsis: This paper used transcranial Doppler sonography and oculomotor measures to examine how uncertainty about where critical signals will appear affects cerebral blood flow velocity (an index of attentional resource expenditure) and eye-movement fatigue during sustained monitoring. Declining cerebral hemovelocity tracked the behavioral vigilance decrement. The work advances a neuroergonomic account that links sustained-attention failures to measurable physiological depletion of resources.
recent_3: Matthews, G., Warm, J. S., & Smith, A. P. (2017). Task Engagement and Attentional Resources: Multivariate Models for Individual Differences and Stress Factors in Vigilance. Human Factors, 59(1), 44-61. DOI: 10.1177/0018720816673782
recent_3_synopsis: This study built and tested multivariate models relating subjective task engagement, individual differences, and stress factors to vigilance performance. It demonstrated that engagement and resource availability jointly predict sustained-attention outcomes. The work integrates personality and stress dimensions into resource theory, showing why some observers and states are more vulnerable to the vigilance decrement.
seminal_1: Warm, J. S., Parasuraman, R., & Matthews, G. (2008). Vigilance Requires Hard Mental Work and Is Stressful. Human Factors, 50(3), 433-441. DOI: 10.1518/001872008X312152
seminal_1_synopsis: This is the definitive statement overturning the long-held assumption that vigilance is a passive, low-effort activity. It marshals behavioral, subjective-workload (NASA-TLX), and cerebral-blood-flow evidence to show that sustained monitoring imposes heavy cognitive-resource demands and genuine stress. With roughly 1,700+ citations (Google Scholar, approximate, 2026-06-17), it is among his most-cited works and is foundational because it reframed the entire field around resource theory and is the canonical citation for the cost of sustained attention.
seminal_2: See, J. E., Warm, J. S., Dember, W. N., & Howe, S. R. (1997). Vigilance and Signal Detection Theory: An Empirical Evaluation of Five Measures of Response Bias. Human Factors, 39(1), 14-29. DOI: 10.1518/001872097778940704
seminal_2_synopsis: This methodological cornerstone evaluated five signal-detection-theory response-bias indices to determine which best captures an observer's willingness to respond in vigilance tasks. With roughly 200+ citations (Google Scholar, approximate, 2026-06-17), it standardized how researchers measure and report criterion shifts and helped separate sensitivity decline from bias change in characterizing the vigilance decrement across the sustained-attention literature.
expertise_overview: Research centered on vigilance and sustained attention—the human capacity to maintain detection performance over prolonged monitoring tasks—and the cognitive and physiological mechanisms underlying its decline. A central contribution was resource-depletion theory, demonstrating that vigilance imposes high mental workload and stress rather than being a passive, low-effort activity, and characterizing the vigilance decrement using signal detection theory measures of sensitivity and response bias. Methods spanned psychophysics, subjective workload assessment (NASA-TLX), stress-state and task-engagement questionnaires, and neuroergonomic techniques such as transcranial Doppler measurement of cerebral blood flow velocity and oculomotor indices. The work was multimodal, examining attention across visual, auditory, tactile, and temporal (time-perception) domains, and addressed individual differences, signal salience, training, knowledge of results, and co-action effects. Application domains included military and defense monitoring, warfighter-interface and air-traffic-control-style tasks, security screening, and other operational settings demanding prolonged human surveillance.
notes: High-confidence identification. Joel S. Warm (1933-2017), Professor Emeritus of Psychology, University of Cincinnati, and Senior Scientist at the Air Force Research Laboratory (Wright-Patterson AFB); identity confirmed via the FABBS memorial, ResearchGate and Legacy.com obituaries, and the HFES tribute (Hancock & Szalma, Human Factors, 2019), and anchored by exact match of the provided hf_papers (vigilance/sustained-attention co-authored work). He is deceased (2017), so the most recent genuinely-his papers are 2017-2019 (some late-career/posthumous), which is expected. name: Wiggins, Mark W.
recent_1: Renshaw, P., Wiggins, M. W., & Morrison, B. W. (2026). A cue-based processing approach to differentiating levels of safety performance in remotely piloted aircraft operations. Applied Ergonomics, 130, 104650. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2025.104650
recent_1_synopsis: Drawing on cue-based processing theory, this study examined whether domain-specific cue utilisation, independent of self-reported experience, predicts safety performance among 48 small Visual Line-Of-Sight remotely piloted aircraft (drone) operators. Higher cue utilisation was associated with fewer drone safety events even after controlling for experience. It matters because it extends EXPERTise-style cue assessment to an emerging, rapidly proliferating operational domain where conventional sensory feedback is constrained, offering a selection and training lever for safer uncrewed operations.
recent_2: Auton, J., Deidun, L., Sturman, D., Churruca, K., Morrison, B., Molesworth, B. R. C., & Wiggins, M. (2026). Adapting to renewable energy: A mixed methods exploration of safety culture and training needs in Australia's electricity distribution industry. Journal of Safety Research, 97, 197-206. DOI: 10.1016/j.jsr.2026.01.022
recent_2_synopsis: Using a mixed-methods design, the team investigated how the transition to distributed renewable generation is reshaping safety culture and training requirements within Australia's electricity distribution sector. The work characterises emerging hazards and competency gaps that arise as the grid becomes more dynamic and decentralised. It matters because it links Wiggins's situation-assessment and expertise framework to the safety implications of energy-system transformation, a high-consequence applied context.
recent_3: Morrison, B. W., Graf, E., Bayl-Smith, P., & Wiggins, M. W. (2025). Like Shooting Phish in a Barrel: Cue Utilization and Cognitive Reflection Aid Performance in Controlled, but Not Naturalistic Phishing Tasks. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making, 19(1), 32-53. DOI: 10.1177/15553434241296170
recent_3_synopsis: This study tested whether individual differences in phishing cue utilisation and cognitive reflection predict the ability to discriminate genuine from fraudulent emails, comparing a controlled discrimination task against a more naturalistic email-management setting. Both cue utilisation and cognitive reflection aided performance in the controlled task but not under naturalistic conditions, revealing a gap between assessed competence and real-world behaviour. It matters for cybersecurity training because it shows that diagnostic ability does not automatically transfer to ecologically realistic, multitasking contexts.
seminal_1: Wiggins, M., & O'Hare, D. (1995). Expertise in aeronautical weather-related decision making: A cross-sectional analysis of general aviation pilots. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Applied, 1(4), 305-320. DOI: 10.1037/1076-898X.1.4.305
seminal_1_synopsis: This cross-sectional study compared the information-acquisition strategies and decision performance of inexperienced, intermediate, and experienced general-aviation pilots using computer-based weather scenarios, information-search tracking, and verbal protocols, showing that expert pilots sample and integrate more diagnostic cues. With approximately 254 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is foundational because it established the empirical case that weather-related decision expertise is grounded in cue acquisition and use, seeding Wiggins's entire cue-utilisation/EXPERTise research program.
seminal_2: Wiggins, M., & O'Hare, D. (2003). Weatherwise: Evaluation of a Cue-Based Training Approach for the Recognition of Deteriorating Weather Conditions during Flight. Human Factors, 45(2), 337-345. DOI: 10.1518/hfes.45.2.337.27246
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper designed and evaluated Weatherwise, a computer-based, cue-based training system intended to help visual pilots recognise and respond to cues signalling deteriorating in-flight weather, testing it against a control group of 66 pilots at self-report and performance levels. With approximately 136 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is foundational because it demonstrated that cue-based competence can be deliberately trained rather than only acquired through raw experience, providing the applied training counterpart to Wiggins's diagnostic-expertise theory and a template for later domain-specific cue interventions.
expertise_overview: Wiggins studies the assessment and development of expert cognition in high-consequence operational settings, centred on cue utilisation, the largely non-conscious capacity to associate environmental features with meaningful events that underpins diagnosis, sensemaking, and situation assessment. His EXPERTise theoretical and software framework operationalises this construct, and he relates cue utilisation to outcomes such as cognitive load, cognitive resource consumption, skill acquisition, interruption management, and sustained vigilance. Methodologically he favours behaviour-based and performance measures, computer-based simulation and scenario tasks, eye-tracking and fixation analysis, verbal protocols, and individual-differences/psychometric designs. His domains span aviation and remotely piloted aircraft, medicine (radiology, pathology, anaesthesia/clinical simulation), electrical power and process control, driving, water safety and lifeguarding, marksmanship, and cybersecurity/phishing detection. A recurring theme is differentiating genuine cue-based expertise from mere experience and translating that distinction into selection, training, and interface-design interventions.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Mark W. Wiggins, Professor of Organisational Psychology at Macquarie University (Sydney), developer of the cue-utilisation/EXPERTise framework. Confirmed via the Macquarie University researcher profile (researchers.mq.edu.au/en/persons/mark-wiggins), ResearchGate profile, ORCID 0000-0002-6422-9475, and his Google Scholar profile (user SnqbFVAAAAAJ); affiliation, EXPERTise software, and co-author network (O'Hare, Morrison, Auton, Sturman, Molesworth) all align with the ground-truth Human Factors papers. name: Xu, Xu
recent_1: Jung, S., Qing, L., Su, B., & Xu, X. (2026). Toward a Fully Automated NIOSH Lifting Equation Using Computer Vision. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261418858
recent_1_synopsis: This work develops a computer-vision pipeline that automatically extracts the geometric and load inputs required by the revised NIOSH Lifting Equation directly from video of manual lifting, removing the manual measurement burden that limits field ergonomic assessment. It moves automated lifting-risk evaluation toward a turnkey tool deployable on ordinary camera footage.

recent_2_synopsis: This paper applies federated learning to human factors data so that models can be trained across distributed worker datasets without centralizing sensitive personal or biometric information. It addresses a growing tension between data-hungry machine-learning methods and the privacy expectations of human-subjects research in occupational settings.
recent_3: Jung, S., Su, B., Lu, L., Qing, L., & Xu, X. (2024). Video-Based Lifting Action Recognition Using Rank-Altered Kinematic Feature Pairs. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208241309748
recent_3_synopsis: This study introduces a video-based action-recognition method that uses rank-altered pairs of kinematic features to identify and classify lifting actions, improving robustness for automated lifting analysis. It supports unobtrusive, marker-less monitoring of manual material handling for musculoskeletal-disorder risk assessment.
seminal_1: Xu, X., McGorry, R. W., Chou, L.-S., Lin, J.-H., & Chang, C.-C. (2015). Accuracy of the Microsoft Kinect™ for measuring gait parameters during treadmill walking. Gait & Posture, 42(2), 145–151. DOI: 10.1016/j.gaitpost.2015.05.002
seminal_1_synopsis: This study quantified how accurately the low-cost, marker-less Microsoft Kinect captures spatiotemporal gait parameters and lower-limb joint angles relative to gold-standard motion capture during treadmill walking. seminal_2: Xu, X., & McGorry, R. W. (2015). The validity of the first and second generation Microsoft Kinect™ for identifying joint center locations during static postures. Applied Ergonomics, 49, 47–54. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2015.01.005
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper compared the Kinect v1 and v2 sensors for estimating joint-center locations in seated and standing postures, reporting average position errors on the order of 76–87 mm and showing the older generation was somewhat more accurate. With roughly 225 citations, it is a foundational validation study that guided ergonomists in adopting depth cameras for postural assessment.
expertise_overview: Occupational biomechanics and ergonomics with an emphasis on automatically measuring and assessing physical work exposure to prevent musculoskeletal disorders. A central thread is computer vision and machine learning applied to video and depth-camera data—posture estimation, lifting-action recognition, and automated risk scoring (e.g., RULA, the NIOSH Lifting Equation)—as marker-less, low-cost alternatives to laboratory motion capture. He also works with wearable and inertial sensors for activity and distraction recognition, including driving-safety applications. More recent work studies human-robot collaboration, using reinforcement learning and mental-stress modeling to improve workers' musculoskeletal health and safety, and applies privacy-preserving methods such as federated learning to human factors data. Methodologically he draws on optimization-based biomechanical modeling, simulation, and data mining of human daily activities.
notes: Identification confidence: high. name: Yang, Jing
recent_1: Yan, B., Zhou, X., Lin, J.-H., & Yang, J. (2025). A Sensing Based Approach to Assessing and Predicting Musculoskeletal Symptoms of Work-From-Home Employees. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting, 69. DOI: 10.1177/10711813251381733
recent_1_synopsis: This study collected wearable-sensor data (heart rate, metabolic, and activity metrics) from 36 work-from-home employees and trained machine-learning models—including Temporal Convolutional Networks compared against Informer, LSTM, SVM, and logistic regression—to predict musculoskeletal symptoms, reaching high classification accuracy when metabolic and heart-rate features were combined. It demonstrates how unobtrusive physiological sensing can flag ergonomic risk in remote-work settings where traditional workplace assessment is unavailable.
recent_2: Anton, N. E., Obuseh, M., Lim, C., Chen, H., Yang, J., Stefanidis, D., & Yu, D. (2024). Nontechnical Skills Assessment in Acute Care Trauma Simulations: A Mixed Methods Approach Using Eye Tracking and Behavioral Marker Systems. Military Medicine, 189(Suppl. 3), 719–727. DOI: 10.1093/milmed/usae240
recent_2_synopsis: Using a mixed-methods design that paired eye tracking with structured behavioral-marker (T-NOTECHS) ratings, this work objectively quantified team leaders' and trauma teams' nontechnical skills during simulated acute-care trauma scenarios. recent_3: Yang, J., Liang, N., Pitts, B. J., Prakah-Asante, K. O., Curry, R., & Yu, D. (2023). recent_3_synopsis: This paper developed a fixation-related EEG band-power (FRBP) technique that links eye-fixation-locked neural activity to situation awareness during automated driving, finding that longer scene viewing and more dispersed visual attention were associated with better SA. seminal_1: Zhang, T., Yang, J., Liang, N., Pitts, B. J., Prakah-Asante, K. O., Curry, R., Duerstock, B. S., Wachs, J. P., & Yu, D. (2020). Physiological Measurements of Situation Awareness: A Systematic Review. Human Factors, 65(5), 737–758. DOI: 10.1177/0018720820969071
seminal_1_synopsis: This systematic review synthesized the evidence relating indirect physiological measures (eye tracking, EEG, cardiac, and other signals) to direct measures of situation awareness, concluding that eye-tracking metrics are the most widely used and EEG, though less common, is consistently informative. With roughly 270 citations (Google Scholar), it is her most-cited work and serves as a foundational reference and roadmap for physiological/biobehavioral SA measurement across driving, aviation, and healthcare.
seminal_2: Ahmadi, N., Sasangohar, F., Yang, J., Yu, D., Danesh, V., Klahn, S., & Masud, F. (2022). Quantifying Workload and Stress in Intensive Care Unit Nurses: Preliminary Evaluation Using Continuous Eye-Tracking. Human Factors, 66(3), 714–728. DOI: 10.1177/00187208221085335
seminal_2_synopsis: This study used continuous eye tracking to quantify mental workload and stress in intensive care unit nurses across demanding shifts, linking ocular metrics to fluctuations in cognitive demand during real patient-care work. With approximately 60 citations (Google Scholar), it is an influential demonstration of unobtrusive, field-based physiological workload monitoring in high-stress clinical environments.
expertise_overview: Studies real-time sensing and modeling of human cognitive states—situation awareness, mental workload, and stress—using physiological and behavioral signals such as eye tracking, EEG/fixation-related neural measures, electrodermal activity, and cardiac and wearable-sensor data. Combines these measures with machine-learning prediction models to infer operator state and to drive adaptive automation and human-in-the-loop interventions. Application domains span automated and semi-automated driving (driver state monitoring), healthcare (ICU nursing workload, trauma-team nontechnical skills, neonatal and pre-cardiac-arrest simulations), and robotic-assisted surgery, including adaptive human-robot interaction architectures that adjust autonomy based on sensed workload. A recurring theme is replacing subjective, intermittent assessments of cognitive demand with objective, continuous, multimodal physiological measurement. More recent work extends this sensing-based approach to occupational and ergonomic risk, predicting musculoskeletal symptoms in remote workers.

name: Yang, Shiyan

recent_3: Yang, S., McKerral, A., Mulhall, M. D., Lenné, M. G., Reimer, B., & Gershon, P. (2023). recent_3_synopsis: Using a naturalistic multimodal dataset of GM Super Cruise and Tesla Autopilot driving, this paper characterizes the situational context surrounding control takeovers in Level 2 systems. seminal_1: Wilson, K. M., Yang, S., Roady, T., Kuo, J., & Lenné, M. G. (2020). Driver trust & mode confusion in an on-road study of level-2 automated vehicle technology. Safety Science, 130, 104845. DOI: 10.1016/j.ssci.2020.104845

seminal_2_synopsis: This on-road experiment measured how secondary-task distraction during Level 2 automated driving degrades glance behavior and subsequent takeover performance. expertise_overview: Shiyan Yang studies driver state monitoring at the intersection of human factors and machine learning, with a focus on detecting driver distraction, cognitive workload, drowsiness, and readiness to take over control. His work spans manual, Level 2, and conditionally automated driving, examining glance and gaze behavior, peripheral vision, takeover performance, and trust and mode confusion in partially automated vehicles. Methodologically he combines naturalistic and on-road driving studies with driving-simulator experiments and multimodal physiological sensing—including ECG-based workload detection and eye-tracking—and applies probabilistic, deep-learning, and generative modeling to noisy real-world driver data. A recurring theme is sensor fusion that connects driver-facing signals with the external driving context to make attention assessment context-aware. His applied domains include automotive-grade driver monitoring systems, regulatory protocols such as European NCAP DSM, and connected/automated truck platooning.
notes: Identification confidence: high. name: Zahabi, Maryam
recent_1: Kashyap, L.D., Wang, Z., Chang, Y., Zahabi, M., & Garcia, A. (2026). Inferring Hidden Attentional States in Driving: A Bayesian Approach to Modeling Distraction and Secondary Task Engagement. Human Factors, 68(8), 1098-1123. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261430069
recent_1_synopsis: This paper develops a Bayesian computational framework to infer latent attentional states of drivers—specifically distraction and secondary task engagement—from observable behavioral signals. By modeling hidden cognitive states probabilistically, the approach offers a principled method for real-time driver monitoring without requiring explicit secondary task declarations. The work advances human performance modeling for intelligent transportation systems by enabling inference of unobservable mental states.
recent_2: Basnet, N., Allahvirdi, S., Nadri, C., Park, J., & Zahabi, M. (2025). Evaluating the Feasibility of EMG-Based Human–Machine Interfaces for Driving. Human Factors, 68(1), 123-141. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251367179
recent_2_synopsis: This study examines whether electromyography (EMG) signals can serve as a viable input modality for human–machine interfaces in driving contexts. The findings establish feasibility benchmarks for EMG-based HMI in automotive applications, with implications for both assistive technology and driver state monitoring.
recent_3: Basnet, N., & Zahabi, M. (2025). Real-time cognitive workload assessment using non-intrusive methods: a systematic review. Ergonomics, 1-26. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2025.2606779
recent_3_synopsis: This systematic review synthesizes findings from 50 peer-reviewed studies examining non-intrusive physiological and behavioral methods for real-time cognitive workload assessment, including ECG, PPG, EDA, EEG, eye-tracking, and skin temperature. The review identifies current methodological trends, technological advances, and gaps in the literature regarding unobtrusive workload monitoring in operational settings. It provides a consolidated evidence base for practitioners designing cognitive state-aware adaptive systems.
seminal_1: Zahabi, M., Kaber, D., & Swangnetr, M. (2015). Usability and Safety in Electronic Medical Records Interface Design: A Review of Recent Literature and Guideline Formulation. Human Factors, 57(5), 805-834. DOI: 10.1177/0018720815576827
seminal_1_synopsis: This highly cited review (~383 citations as of June 2026, per Google Scholar) examined usability and safety issues in electronic medical record (EMR) interface design, surveying human factors literature and safety incident reports. The authors synthesized design guidelines targeting diagnosis and documentation workflows, providing a foundational reference for HCI research in health information technology. Its breadth and practical guideline output have made it a standard citation in EMR usability and patient safety research.
seminal_2: Zahabi, M., & Abdul Razak, A.M. (2020). Adaptive virtual reality-based training: a systematic literature review and framework. Virtual Reality, 24(4), 725-752. DOI: 10.1007/s10055-020-00434-w
seminal_2_synopsis: This widely cited work (~321 citations as of June 2026, per Google Scholar) presents a systematic review and integrative framework for adaptive VR-based training, identifying key components: performance measures, adaptive logic, and adaptive variables across multiple domains. The framework has become a standard reference for researchers and practitioners designing VR training systems that respond dynamically to learner performance. Its cross-domain applicability—from aviation to rehabilitation—explains its rapid and sustained citation uptake.
expertise_overview: Maryam Zahabi's research centers on human systems integration, with particular emphasis on human performance modeling, cognitive workload assessment, and human–machine interface design in high-stakes operational environments. Her work addresses driver behavior and in-vehicle technology, including law enforcement mobile computer terminals, advanced driver-assistance systems, and EMG-based control interfaces for driving and prosthetic devices. She employs physiological measurement (EMG, EEG, ECG, eye-tracking), cognitive performance modeling, and adaptive systems design to evaluate usability and safety. A significant thread of her research examines police officer cognition and performance under dual-task and pursuit-driving conditions, combining applied field studies with computational modeling approaches. Additional research directions include adaptive virtual reality for training, automated vehicles for older adults with cognitive impairment, and assistive technology usability for persons with disabilities. Methodologically, she integrates human factors experimentation, systematic reviews, Bayesian and computational modeling, and user-centered design evaluation.
